topics.title: Energy Scenario in Various Sectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Scenario in Various Sectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Scenario** refers to the comprehensive, multi-dimensional assessment of the availability, production, distribution, consumption patterns, and future outlook of commercial and non-commercial energy resources across various end-use sectors of an economy, evaluated against socio-economic, environmental, and sustainability metrics.

The **Energy Scenario** framework is the foundational analytical lens used by energy managers, policy makers, and auditors to understand the **macro-energy balance** of a nation or region. It systematically maps the flow of primary energy (coal, crude oil, natural gas, renewables) through conversion stages to **secondary energy** (electricity, refined petroleum products) and finally to **final energy** delivered to **end-use sectors**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition Box:**
> - **Primary Energy** = Energy found in nature before any human-induced conversion (coal, sunlight, wind, hydro-head).
> - **Secondary Energy** = Energy derived from primary sources after conversion (electricity, gasoline, LPG, hydrogen).
> - **Commercial Energy** = Energy sold in the market (electricity, coal, oil, gas).
> - **Non-Commercial Energy** = Traditional biomass, cow-dung, agricultural residues — *not traded commercially*.

## 1.2 The Five Standard End-Use Sectors (KTU/IEA Classification)

| S.No. | Sector | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Industrial Sector** | Manufacturing, process industries, mining, construction |
| 2 | **Transport Sector** | Road, rail, aviation, marine transport |
| 3 | **Residential (Domestic) Sector** | Household cooking, lighting, appliances |
| 4 | **Commercial Sector** | Offices, malls, hospitals, schools, hotels |
| 5 | **Agriculture Sector** | Pumps (irrigation), tractors, post-harvest processing |

> [!NOTE]
> In India, **agriculture** is often treated as a separate major sector due to its significant share in total energy consumption, especially for **electrical pump-sets** in irrigation.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Energy Bank Account":**
> Think of a country as a household. **Income** = domestic energy production (coal, hydro, solar). **Expenses** = energy consumption by various sectors (industry, homes, cars). **Savings** = renewable energy, efficiency reserves. Just like a household budget, the **Energy Scenario** is a balance sheet showing where the energy comes from, who spends it, and whether the account is sustainable for the future. If the household (country) keeps importing more energy (foreign oil, LNG) than it earns (exports), the **energy security** of the household is at risk — exactly like mounting debt.

## 1.4 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) of Energy Scenario

The KTU 2024 Energy Management module emphasizes quantifying the scenario using standardized metrics:

- **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PEC)** = $\dfrac{\text{Total Energy Consumed}}{\text{Total Population}}$ — measured in **toe/person/year** (tonnes of oil equivalent).
- **Energy Intensity (EI)** = $\dfrac{\text{Total Energy Consumed}}{\text{GDP}}$ — measured in **toe/million \$** of GDP.
- **Energy Security Index (ESI)** — qualitative + quantitative metric of import dependence.
- **Energy Mix** — percentage share of each energy source in the total primary supply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Conversion Factor (Board-Exam Favorite):**
> **1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) = 42 GJ = 11.63 MWh = 10,000 Mcal**
> Also, **1 Mtoe = 1 million TOE**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Global Energy Scenario — Conceptual Logic Steps

Step-by-step analytical breakdown of the world energy landscape:

- **Step 1 — Resource Heterogeneity:** Global energy supply is dominated by **fossil fuels** (coal, oil, natural gas) which contribute roughly **\approx 80%** of primary energy, while renewables (excluding large hydro) account for about **\approx 13-15%** (as per recent IEA reports).
- **Step 2 — Geographic Asymmetry:** Energy production and consumption are **not evenly distributed**. The Middle East holds a major share of oil/gas reserves, while Asia (especially China and India) accounts for the largest share of **consumption growth**.
- **Step 3 — Consumption Growth Driver:** Global energy demand grew by ~1-2% annually pre-COVID, rebounded post-2021, with emerging economies being the dominant growth engine.
- **Step 4 — Decarbonization Pressure:** Climate change concerns (Paris Agreement, COP summits) drive the transition toward **renewables, electrification, and green hydrogen**.
- **Step 5 — Energy Transition Phases:**
  - **Phase I (Past)**: Coal-dominant industrial era.
  - **Phase II (Present)**: Oil & gas dominant with rapid renewable scale-up.
  - **Phase III (Future)**: Renewable + electricity + hydrogen dominant, with carbon capture, storage (CCS) bridging the transition.

## 2.2 The Indian Energy Scenario — In-Depth Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> India is the **world's third-largest energy consumer** and **second-largest electricity producer**. As of recent KTU-board expected data, India's commercial energy mix is dominated by **coal (\approx 50-55%)**, followed by oil, gas, hydro, nuclear, and renewables.

### 2.2.1 Sector-Wise Share of Final Energy Consumption in India (Approximate)

| Sector | Approx. Share (%) | Dominant End-Use |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial | **\approx 40-45%** | Process heat, motors, boilers |
| Transport | **\approx 18-20%** | Road (diesel/petrol), rail (electric) |
| Residential (Domestic) | **\approx 20-24%** | Cooking (LPG/biomass), lighting, fans |
| Agriculture | **\approx 8-10%** | Electric/diesel pump sets |
| Commercial | **\approx 6-8%** | HVAC, lighting, lifts |

> [!NOTE]
> The exact percentages vary by source (IEA, CEA, MNRE reports) and the year of study. For KTU exams, always present **ranges** and cite the *approximate decade-era* of the data.

### 2.2.2 Key Indian Energy Statistics (KTU Board-Exam Ready)

- **Installed Power Capacity (India, 2024)**: **\approx 430+ GW** (GigaWatts), with renewable share crossing **\approx 180 GW**.
- **Per Capita Electricity Consumption (India)**: **\approx 1,200-1,400 kWh/person/year** — significantly lower than the world average of **\approx 3,300 kWh/person/year** and the US value of **\approx 12,000+ kWh/person/year**.
- **Energy Import Dependence (India)**: **\approx 40-50%** of total primary energy, with **\approx 85% of crude oil** being imported.
- **Largest Energy Consumer in India**: **Industrial Sector** (manufacturing — iron & steel, cement, fertilizers, chemicals, textiles, paper).

## 2.3 Sector-by-Sector Engineering Insight

### A. Industrial Sector
- The **largest energy consumer** in India.
- Major sub-sectors: **Iron & Steel, Cement, Aluminum, Fertilizers, Pulp & Paper, Textiles, Chemicals**.
- **Steel** and **Cement** alone account for **\approx 25% of industrial energy use**.
- High-temperature process heat dominates, often supplied by **coal or natural gas**.

### B. Transport Sector
- Second largest energy user (after industry in some classifications).
- **Road transport** dominates (\approx 90% of passenger + freight movement) — heavily dependent on **diesel/petrol**.
- Growing **electric vehicle (EV)** adoption driven by the **FAME-II** scheme.
- Aviation and marine use high-grade refined fuels (ATF, bunker fuel).

### C. Residential / Domestic Sector
- Dominated by **cooking energy** (LPG, electricity, biomass in rural areas).
- Lighting load (LED transition reducing demand).
- **Space cooling** (AC) is a rapidly growing sub-load in urban India.
- Rural India still uses **traditional biomass** — counted as **non-commercial energy**.

### D. Agriculture Sector
- **Electric & diesel pump-sets** for irrigation.
- Subsidized electricity to farmers in many states.
- Tractors, harvesters, and post-harvest drying equipment consume diesel.
- Solar pumps under the **PM-KUSUM** scheme are transforming the load profile.

### E. Commercial Sector
- Includes **malls, IT parks, hospitals, hotels, offices, airports**.
- High **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation & Air-Conditioning)** load.
- High **lighting and computing** loads in IT/ITES facilities.
- Prime target for **Energy Efficiency** programs (ECBC — Energy Conservation Building Code).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU 2024 EQUATIONS — Memorize All**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per Capita Energy Consumption (PEC) | $PEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P}$ | toe/person/year | Compare energy standard of living |
| 2 | Energy Intensity (EI) | $EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{GDP}$ | toe/million USD | Compare economic energy efficiency |
| 3 | Sectoral Share (i) | $S_i = \dfrac{E_i}{E_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Pie-chart analysis |
| 4 | Growth Rate (CAGR) | $CAGR = \left(\dfrac{E_{final}}{E_{initial}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$ | fraction or % | Annual demand growth |
| 5 | Capacity Factor (CF) | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760} \times 100$ | % | Power plant utilization |
| 6 | TOE Conversion | $1 \text{ TOE} = 42 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}$ | constant | Unit conversion |
| 7 | Import Dependence | $ID = \dfrac{I - X}{E_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Energy security metric |
| 8 | Energy Demand Projection (Linear) | $E_{n} = E_0 (1+r)^n$ | energy units | Future forecasting |
| 9 | Electricity Generation (thermal) | $E_{el} = m_{coal} \times GCV \times \eta_{plant} \times k$ | kWh | Plant output estimation |
| 10 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{input}}{Q_{output}}$ | kWh/ton or MJ/kg | Industrial efficiency |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Rule Reminder:** In the table above, all absolute values (like $\vert x \vert$) and conditionals use $\vert$ or \mid explicitly to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

The energy scenario analysis is **not academic theory** — it directly drives:

- **Power plant investment** decisions (coal vs. gas vs. solar vs. nuclear).
- **National policy** formulation (e.g., India's **Net-Zero 2070** commitment).
- **Energy audit** prioritization in industries and buildings.
- **Renewable purchase obligations (RPO)** for DISCOMs.
- **International energy diplomacy** (e.g., India-Russia, India-Middle East LNG deals).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Per Capita Energy Consumption (PEC)

### Problem Statement
A country has a total primary energy consumption of **500 Mtoe** and a population of **1.4 billion** (i.e., $1.4 \times 10^9$ people). Calculate the **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PEC)** in **toe/person/year** and **GJ/person/year**.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify given quantities and convert units to a consistent base.**

$$
E_{total} = 500 \text{ Mtoe} = 500 \times 10^6 \text{ TOE}
$$

$$
P = 1.4 \times 10^9 \text{ persons}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the PEC formula.**

$$
PEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
PEC = \dfrac{500 \times 10^6 \text{ TOE}}{1.4 \times 10^9 \text{ persons}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the ratio.**

$$
PEC = \dfrac{500}{1400} \text{ TOE/person}
$$

$$
PEC = 0.357 \text{ TOE/person/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert TOE to GJ using the standard factor ($1 \text{ TOE} = 42 \text{ GJ}$).**

$$
PEC = 0.357 \times 42 \text{ GJ/person/year}
$$

$$
PEC = 15.0 \text{ GJ/person/year}
$$

### Final Answer

> **PEC = 0.357 TOE/person/year = 15.0 GJ/person/year**

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Sectoral Share Calculation

### Problem Statement
In a state's energy balance, the annual energy consumption by sector is: **Industrial = 1,200 kTOE, Transport = 600 kTOE, Residential = 500 kTOE, Agriculture = 250 kTOE, Commercial = 150 kTOE**. Calculate the **percentage share of each sector**.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

**Step 1 — Compute the total energy consumption.**

$$
E_{total} = 1200 + 600 + 500 + 250 + 150
$$

$$
E_{total} = 2700 \text{ kTOE}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the percentage share for each sector using $S_i = \dfrac{E_i}{E_{total}} \times 100$.**

**Industrial Share:**

$$
S_{Ind} = \dfrac{1200}{2700} \times 100 = 44.44\%
$$

**Transport Share:**

$$
S_{Tr} = \dfrac{600}{2700} \times 100 = 22.22\%
$$

**Residential Share:**

$$
S_{Res} = \dfrac{500}{2700} \times 100 = 18.52\%
$$

**Agriculture Share:**

$$
S_{Ag} = \dfrac{250}{2700} \times 100 = 9.26\%
$$

**Commercial Share:**

$$
S_{Com} = \dfrac{150}{2700} \times 100 = 5.56\%
$$

**Step 3 — Verification (shares must sum to 100%).**

$$
44.44 + 22.22 + 18.52 + 9.26 + 5.56 = 100.00\% \quad \checkmark
$$

### Final Tabulated Result

| Sector | Energy (kTOE) | Share (%) |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial | 1,200 | **44.44%** |
| Transport | 600 | **22.22%** |
| Residential | 500 | **18.52%** |
| Agriculture | 250 | **9.26%** |
| Commercial | 150 | **5.56%** |
| **Total** | **2,700** | **100.00%** |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) of Demand

### Problem Statement
India's electricity consumption in 2010 was **700 TWh (Terawatt-hours)**. In 2024 it is **1,600 TWh**. Calculate the **CAGR** over this 14-year period.

### Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.**

$$
E_{initial} = 700 \text{ TWh (Year 2010)}
$$

$$
E_{final} = 1600 \text{ TWh (Year 2024)}
$$

$$
n = 14 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CAGR formula.**

$$
CAGR = \left( \dfrac{E_{final}}{E_{initial}} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
CAGR = \left( \dfrac{1600}{700} \right)^{\frac{1}{14}} - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\dfrac{1600}{700} = 2.2857
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the 14th root.**

$$
2.2857^{1/14} = e^{\frac{\ln(2.2857)}{14}}
$$

$$
\ln(2.2857) = 0.8267
$$

$$
\dfrac{0.8267}{14} = 0.05905
$$

$$
e^{0.05905} = 1.0608
$$

**Step 6 — Subtract 1 to get the growth fraction.**

$$
CAGR = 1.0608 - 1 = 0.0608
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to percentage.**

$$
CAGR = 6.08\% \text{ per year}
$$

### Final Answer

> **Electricity Demand CAGR (2010–2024) ≈ 6.08% per annum**

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Sector vs. Energy Strategy

| Sector | Dominant Fuel | Energy-Saving Strategy | KTU Board Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| Industrial (Steel) | Coal, coke | Waste heat recovery, BF gas recycling | High Priority |
| Industrial (Cement) | Coal (kiln) | Pre-heater, VRM optimization, AFR | High Priority |
| Transport | Diesel, petrol | EV transition, modal shift to rail | Strategic |
| Residential (Urban) | Electricity, LPG | BEE star appliances, solar rooftops | High Priority |
| Agriculture | Electricity (pump), diesel | PM-KUSUM solar pumps, VFD drives | High Priority |
| Commercial (Buildings) | Electricity | ECBC compliance, smart HVAC, LED | Medium Priority |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define Per Capita Energy Consumption and Energy Intensity. Why is Energy Intensity considered a measure of economic efficiency?*

**Model Answer (KTU Standard):**

**Per Capita Energy Consumption (PEC):**
The total primary or commercial energy consumed by a country in a year, divided by its total population.

$$
PEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{Population} \quad [\text{toe/person/year}]
$$

It is an indicator of the standard of living and level of industrial development.

**Energy Intensity (EI):**
The amount of energy consumed per unit of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) generated.

$$
EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{GDP} \quad [\text{toe/million USD}]
$$

**Why EI = Economic Efficiency Indicator:**
A *low* energy intensity means the economy generates more monetary output per unit of energy used — i.e., it is *energy-efficient* and technologically advanced. A high EI (typical of developing economies) implies energy wastage or low-value energy use. Hence EI directly correlates with the *technological maturity* and *economic productivity* of a nation. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between primary energy, secondary energy, and commercial energy with two examples each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Definition | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Energy** | Energy obtained directly from natural sources, before any human-induced conversion. | Coal | Crude Oil |
| **Secondary Energy** | Energy obtained by converting primary energy through human-controlled processes. | Electricity | Refined Petrol / Diesel |
| **Commercial Energy** | All energy forms that are bought and sold in the organized market. | Electricity (grid) | LPG (cynlinder) |

> Note: Biomass (firewood, cow-dung) is *primary* but **non-commercial** because it is not traded in the formal market. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> *Common reasons students lose marks in the Energy Scenario module:*
> 1. **Confusing "primary" and "commercial"** — Coal is primary; electricity is secondary; LPG is both secondary AND commercial.
> 2. **Quoting data without reference year** — Always write *"as of 2023-24, per CEA/MNRE data"* to gain method marks.
> 3. **Mixing up sectoral shares** — Don't blindly state "transport is the largest"; in India, **industry is the largest** consumer.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always carry units (TOE, GJ, Mtoe) in every step.
> 5. **Forgetting the TOE conversion factor** — *1 TOE = 42 GJ* is a board-exam mandatory constant.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Solution

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1 | Energy Scenario]**

**Question:**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **present global energy scenario** with reference to the share of various primary energy sources, the dominance of fossil fuels, and the ongoing energy transition. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss the **Indian energy scenario** in detail, highlighting the sector-wise consumption pattern, the dependence on imports, and the policy measures taken to ensure energy security. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Global Energy Scenario (7 Marks)

**1. Dominance of Fossil Fuels:**
Globally, primary energy supply is dominated by fossil fuels — coal, oil, and natural gas — collectively contributing about **\approx 80%** of the world's total primary energy. Oil alone accounts for **\approx 31-33%**, coal **\approx 27%**, and natural gas **\approx 24%**. The remaining share is divided among nuclear, hydro, and modern renewables (solar, wind, biomass).

**2. Geographic Asymmetry:**
Energy resources are unevenly distributed. The Middle East (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iran) holds the largest proven oil and gas reserves. Russia, USA, and Australia are major coal producers. China, USA, India, and the EU are the largest consumers. This asymmetry drives international energy trade and geopolitics.

**3. Energy Demand Growth:**
Global energy demand grows at about **\approx 1-2% per annum**, with emerging economies (China, India, ASEAN, Africa) leading the demand growth. Developed economies show near-flat or declining energy demand due to efficiency improvements and structural shifts toward services.

**4. The Energy Transition:**
To address climate change, the world is undergoing a transition:
- Rapid scale-up of **renewables** (solar, wind) — the fastest-growing energy source.
- **Electrification** of transport and heating.
- **Green hydrogen** as a future fuel.
- **Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS)** as a bridge technology.
- Net-Zero pledges from major economies (EU by 2050, China by 2060, India by 2070).

**5. Key Challenges:**
- Energy access for **\approx 770 million** people without electricity globally.
- Volatile fossil fuel prices (e.g., the 2022 oil shock).
- Stranded fossil fuel assets as transition accelerates.
- Critical mineral supply for renewables (lithium, cobalt, rare earths).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Indian Energy Scenario (7 Marks)

**1. Resource Position and Import Dependence:**
India is the **world's third-largest primary energy consumer** and the **second-largest electricity producer**. However, it is **resource-poor** in fossil fuels:
- **\approx 85% of crude oil** is imported.
- **\approx 50% of natural gas** is imported (as LNG).
- Coal is the only major domestic fossil resource, but the quality is often poor (high ash content).
- Overall **energy import dependence** is **\approx 40-50%**, exposing India to global price shocks.

**2. Sector-Wise Consumption Pattern:**

- **Industrial Sector (\approx 40-45%):** The largest consumer. Dominated by iron & steel, cement, fertilizers, chemicals, textiles, and aluminum. Coal is the principal fuel for process heat.
- **Transport Sector (\approx 18-22%):** Road transport dominates, mostly dependent on diesel and petrol. Railways are progressively electrified.
- **Residential Sector (\approx 20-24%):** Cooking is the largest residential end-use. LPG penetration has grown under the Ujjwala scheme. Urban cooling (AC) is a fast-rising load.
- **Agriculture Sector (\approx 8-10%):** Heavily dependent on subsidized electricity for irrigation pump-sets and on diesel for tractors.
- **Commercial Sector (\approx 6-8%):** HVAC, lighting, and IT loads drive the consumption in offices, malls, hospitals, and IT parks.

**3. Policy Measures for Energy Security:**

- **National Solar Mission** (target 100 GW solar by 2030).
- **PM-KUSUM** scheme for solar agricultural pumps.
- **Saubhagya** scheme for rural household electrification.
- **UJALA / Unnat Jyoti Programme** for LED lighting transition.
- **FAME-II** scheme for electric mobility.
- **Net-Zero 2070** commitment under the Paris Agreement.
- **National Hydrogen Mission** for green hydrogen adoption.
- **Strategic Petroleum Reserves** to buffer against supply shocks.
- Expansion of **nuclear power** (10 indigenous reactors under construction) and **hydroelectric** capacity in the Northeast.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | Energy Scenario]**

**Question:**
*(a)* Define **Energy Intensity (EI)** and **Capacity Factor (CF)**. A thermal power plant with an installed capacity of **500 MW** generated **2,500 GWh** in a year. Calculate its **Capacity Factor**. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* The energy consumption of an industry in 2010 was **50,000 TOE/year**. With a CAGR of **4.5%**, estimate the energy consumption in the year **2025**. Comment on the implication for energy audit planning. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Energy Intensity & Capacity Factor (7 Marks)

**Definitions:**

**Energy Intensity (EI):**
Energy consumed per unit of GDP — measures the *energy efficiency of the economy*.

$$
EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{GDP} \quad [\text{toe/million USD}]
$$

**Capacity Factor (CF):**
The ratio of actual energy generated by a plant in a year to the maximum energy it *could* have generated if it had run at its rated capacity for the full year.

$$
CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760} \times 100 \quad [\%]
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.**

$$
P_{rated} = 500 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
E_{actual} = 2500 \text{ GWh} = 2500 \times 10^3 \text{ MWh} = 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ MWh}
$$

$$
\text{Time in year} = 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum possible generation.**

$$
E_{max} = P_{rated} \times 8760 = 500 \times 8760
$$

$$
E_{max} = 4{,}380{,}000 \text{ MWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the CF formula.**

$$
CF = \dfrac{2{,}500{,}000}{4{,}380{,}000} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
CF = 0.5708 \times 100 = 57.08\%
$$

### Final Answer

> **Capacity Factor of the plant = 57.08%**

A CF of 57% indicates the plant is running for about *half* of its theoretical maximum output — typical of a moderately-loaded thermal power station.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Future Energy Demand Projection (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify inputs for the exponential growth formula $E_n = E_0 (1+r)^n$.**

$$
E_0 = 50{,}000 \text{ TOE/year (Year 2010)}
$$

$$
r = 4.5\% = 0.045
$$

$$
n = 2025 - 2010 = 15 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the formula.**

$$
E_{2025} = 50{,}000 \times (1 + 0.045)^{15}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(1.045)^{15}$.**

$$
(1.045)^{15} = e^{15 \times \ln(1.045)}
$$

$$
\ln(1.045) = 0.04402
$$

$$
15 \times 0.04402 = 0.6603
$$

$$
e^{0.6603} = 1.9356
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $E_0$.**

$$
E_{2025} = 50{,}000 \times 1.9356
$$

$$
E_{2025} = 96{,}780 \text{ TOE/year}
$$

### Final Answer

> **Projected energy consumption in 2025 ≈ 96,780 TOE/year**

**Implication for Energy Audit Planning:**
- Energy demand is **almost doubling** in 15 years.
- The industry must adopt **systematic energy audits** to identify savings.
- Targeted sectors: **process heating, motor systems, lighting, steam systems**.
- Investment in **energy-efficient equipment** and **renewable energy** is essential to decouple growth from energy cost.
- **ISO 50001** (Energy Management System) implementation is recommended.
- Without proactive energy management, energy cost will rise by ~93%, severely impacting profitability and sustainability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY EXAM REVISION — Memorize This Block**

- **Energy Scenario** = Macro view of supply, demand, sectors, and security of energy in an economy.
- **Primary Energy** → from nature (coal, crude, sun, wind, hydro-head, uranium).
- **Secondary Energy** → converted form (electricity, refined fuels, LPG, hydrogen).
- **Commercial Energy** → traded in the market. **Non-commercial** = traditional biomass.
- **5 End-Use Sectors** (IEA standard): Industrial, Transport, Residential, Commercial, Agriculture. *(India separates Agriculture.)*
- **Industrial sector is the largest** consumer in India (\approx 40-45%).
- **Iron & Steel + Cement** together account for **\approx 25%** of industrial energy use.
- **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PEC)** = $E_{total} / Population$ — measured in **toe/person/year**.
- **Energy Intensity (EI)** = $E_{total} / GDP$ — measured in **toe/million USD** — *lower is better*.
- **Capacity Factor (CF)** = $E_{actual} / (P_{rated} \times 8760)$ — indicates plant utilization.
- **CAGR** = $\left(E_{final}/E_{initial}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ — used for demand projection.
- **1 TOE = 42 GJ = 11.63 MWh = 10,000 Mcal** — *must memorize the constant*.
- **Future Energy Projection** = $E_n = E_0 (1+r)^n$ — exponential growth model.
- **India's import dependence** = \approx 40-50% total; **\approx 85% crude oil** is imported.
- **India's Net-Zero target** = **2070** (declared at COP-26, Glasgow).
- **Key Indian Policies** to cite in answers: UJALA, Saubhagya, PM-KUSUM, FAME-II, National Solar Mission, Green Hydrogen Mission.
- **Renewable share** in India's installed power capacity has crossed **\approx 180 GW (2024)**.
- **Energy Audit linkage** = Sectoral scenario analysis identifies *where* to conduct audits (industry, buildings, agriculture pumps).
- **Standard Exam Trick Question:** "Which is the largest energy consumer in India?" → Answer: **Industrial Sector**, NOT transport.
- **Non-commercial energy in India** is significant in rural residential cooking — but is *declining* due to LPG penetration.
- **Power Sector Decarbonization** is at the heart of India's energy transition — renewables + storage + green hydrogen.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Consumption in Various Areas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Energy Consumption in Various Areas # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Consumption in Various Areas** refers to the systematic quantitative analysis of energy utilized across the five principal end-use sectors of an economy — **Industrial**, **Domestic/Residential**, **Commercial**, **Agricultural**, and **Transportation** — measured in **Tonne of Oil Equivalent (TOE)** or **Joules (J)**, evaluated against the **Gross Domestic Product (GDP)** to determine the **Energy Intensity** of a nation or region. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this topic forms the foundational prerequisite for energy auditing because it enables an energy manager to: - **Benchmark** sector-wise energy use against national/international standards. - **Identify** the dominant energy-consuming sector in a given economy. - **Allocate** audit priorities based on consumption share. ## 1.2 The Five Major Energy-Consuming Sectors > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Sector Classification (as per the 2024 syllabus):** | Sector | Primary Energy Carriers | Typical End-Uses | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Industrial** | Coal, Natural Gas, Electricity, Steam | Process heating, motors, boilers | | **Domestic** | LPG, Electricity, Firewood, Solar | Lighting, cooking, HVAC, appliances | | **Commercial** | Electricity, Diesel, Natural Gas | Lighting, AC, elevators, IT loads | | **Agricultural** | Diesel, Electricity, Solar | Pumpsets, tractors, irrigation | | **Transportation** | Petrol, Diesel, CNG, Aviation fuel | Road, rail, air, and water transport | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Household Budget" View > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a country as a large family household. Just as a family spends money on **groceries (food = industrial energy)**, **rent (housing = domestic energy)**, **entertainment (services = commercial energy)**, **farm inputs (agriculture)**, and **commute (transport)** — a nation consumes energy in the same proportional structure. The Energy Manager's role is like a **financial auditor** who finds where the family is "overspending" energy and where efficiency measures can "save" the most. ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters for Energy Auditing > [!IMPORTANT] > Without a clear picture of **where** energy is being consumed, an Energy Audit (covered in Module 2) becomes a blind exercise. The sectoral consumption data is the **diagnostic baseline** for every audit project. ### Key Takeaway - A developed economy typically shows **Industrial + Transportation** as the dominant sectors. - A developing economy (like India) shows **Industrial + Domestic + Agriculture** as the heaviest consumers. - The **service-sector-dominated** economies show **Commercial** consumption rising sharply. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Sectoral Energy Consumption — Theoretical Framework The total national energy consumption $E_{\text{total}}$ is the sum of the five sectoral consumptions: $$ E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{ind}} + E_{\text{dom}} + E_{\text{com}} + E_{\text{agr}} + E_{\text{trn}} $$ Where each $E_i$ is measured in **Million Tonnes of Oil Equivalent (MTOE)** or **Petajoules (PJ)**. ### 2.1.1 Energy Intensity (EI) The most critical KPI for comparing energy efficiency across nations is the **Energy Intensity of GDP**: $$ EI = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{\text{GDP}} $$ - **Units:** TOE per million ₹ (or kgoe per $1000 of GDP, kJ per $). - **Lower EI = More energy-efficient economy**. ### 2.1.2 Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC) $$ PCEC = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{\text{Population}} $$ - Units: **kgoe/person/year** or **GJ/person**. ### 2.1.3 Sectoral Share $$ S_i = \frac{E_i}{E_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% $$ ### 2.1.4 Annual Growth Rate of Energy Consumption $$ E_n = E_0 \times (1 + r)^n $$ Where: - $E_n$ = Energy consumption after $n$ years - $E_0$ = Base year consumption - $r$ = Annual growth rate (decimal) - $n$ = Number of years ### 2.1.5 Energy Conservation Potential in a Sector $$ \text{Savings} = E_i \times \eta_{\text{baseline}} - E_i \times \eta_{\text{proposed}} $$ Or expressed as a percentage: $$ \%\text{Savings} = \left( 1 - \frac{\eta_{\text{proposed}}}{\eta_{\text{baseline}}} \right) \times 100\% $$ ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory formulas to memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE:** | # | Formula | Application | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $E_{\text{total}} = \sum E_i$ | Total national energy | MTOE | | 2 | $EI = E_{\text{total}} / \text{GDP}$ | Energy intensity | TOE/M\$ | | 3 | $PCEC = E_{\text{total}} / N$ | Per capita consumption | kgoe/person | | 4 | $S_i = (E_i / E_{\text{total}}) \times 100$ | Sectoral share | % | | 5 | $E_n = E_0(1+r)^n$ | Future consumption | MTOE | | 6 | $\%\text{Savings} = (1 - \eta_p/\eta_b) \times 100$ | Conservation potential | % | | 7 | $E_{\text{thermal}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat energy in process | kJ | | 8 | $E_{\text{electrical}} = V \cdot I \cdot t \cdot \text{PF}$ | Electrical energy | kWh | | 9 | $\text{SFC} = \text{Fuel}/\text{Work Output}$ | Specific fuel consumption | kg/kWh | | 10 | $\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Output}}{\text{Energy Input}} \times 100$ | Efficiency | % | ## 2.3 Sectoral Energy Use — The Engineering Perspective ### 2.3.1 Industrial Sector - **Largest consumer** in most economies (~35–50% of total). - Dominated by **energy-intensive industries**: Iron & Steel, Cement, Fertilizers, Aluminum, Pulp & Paper, Petrochemicals, Textiles. - **Audit priority:** Boilers, motors, compressed air, steam systems, furnaces. ### 2.3.2 Domestic/Residential Sector - Energy use is **dispersed** across millions of households. - **High audit potential** in urban areas due to inefficient appliances. - India: ~24% of total electricity consumption. - Major loads: **Lighting (19%)**, **HVAC (40%)**, **Refrigeration (15%)**, **Others (26%)**. ### 2.3.3 Commercial Sector - Includes **offices, hotels, hospitals, malls, educational institutions**. - Energy use is **building-centric** — dominated by HVAC and lighting. - Has the **highest growth rate** (8–12% annually in India). ### 2.3.4 Agricultural Sector - **~18% of India's electricity** goes to agriculture (mostly pumping). - **Diesel** for tractors, harvesters, threshers. - High potential for **solar pump substitution**. ### 2.3.5 Transportation Sector - **~14% of global CO₂ emissions** from transport (IEA data). - **Road transport = ~80%** of transport energy. - EVs and biofuels are the key decarbonization levers. ## 2.4 Conversion Constants (Must Memorize) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Unit Conversions used in KTU problems:** $$ 1\ \text{TOE} = 42\ \text{GJ} = 11.63\ \text{MWh}_{\text{th}} $$ $$ 1\ \text{kcal} = 4.186\ \text{kJ} $$ $$ 1\ \text{BTU} = 1055\ \text{J} = 0.252\ \text{kcal} $$ $$ 1\ \text{kWh} = 3.6\ \text{MJ} = 860\ \text{kcal} $$ ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Policy Planning:** BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) uses sectoral data to set PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme targets. - **Audit Targeting:** ISO 50001 audits begin with sectoral baseline. - **Investment Decisions:** Industrial consumers use $EI$ to benchmark plant performance. - **Carbon Accounting:** GHG Protocol scopes (1, 2, 3) are derived from sectoral data. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sectoral Share Calculation > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** A country consumed the following energy in 2023–24: Industrial = 220 MTOE, Domestic = 90 MTOE, Commercial = 50 MTOE, Agriculture = 40 MTOE, Transport = 100 MTOE. Find the total consumption and the percentage share of each sector. ### Step 1: Compute the Total Energy Consumption $$ E_{\text{total}} = 220 + 90 + 50 + 40 + 100 $$ $$ E_{\text{total}} = 500\ \text{MTOE} $$ ### Step 2: Compute Each Sectoral Share $$ S_{\text{ind}} = \frac{220}{500} \times 100 = 44\% $$ $$ S_{\text{dom}} = \frac{90}{500} \times 100 = 18\% $$ $$ S_{\text{com}} = \frac{50}{500} \times 100 = 10\% $$ $$ S_{\text{agr}} = \frac{40}{500} \times 100 = 8\% $$ $$ S_{\text{trn}} = \frac{100}{500} \times 100 = 20\% $$ ### Step 3: Verification $$ 44 + 18 + 10 + 8 + 20 = 100\%\ \checkmark $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** The Industrial sector is the dominant consumer (44%), followed by Transport (20%). This is the typical profile of a **developing industrializing economy**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Energy Intensity & Per Capita Calculation > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** For the country above, GDP = ₹ 250 lakh crore (in constant prices), and Population = 140 crore. Compute (a) Energy Intensity, (b) Per Capita Energy Consumption. ### Step 1: Standardize Units $$ E_{\text{total}} = 500\ \text{MTOE} = 500 \times 10^6\ \text{TOE} $$ $$ \text{GDP} = 250\ \text{lakh crore} = 250 \times 10^5 \times 10^7\ \text{₹} = 2.5 \times 10^{15}\ \text{₹} $$ ### Step 2: Energy Intensity $$ EI = \frac{500 \times 10^6\ \text{TOE}}{2.5 \times 10^{15}\ \text{₹}} $$ $$ EI = 0.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{TOE/₹} = 2 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{TOE/₹} $$ Converting to **kgoe per $1000 GDP** (multiplying by $10^6$): $$ EI = 0.2\ \text{kgoe per \$1000 of GDP} $$ ### Step 3: Per Capita Energy Consumption $$ PCEC = \frac{500 \times 10^6\ \text{TOE}}{140 \times 10^7} $$ $$ PCEC = \frac{500 \times 10^6}{1.4 \times 10^9} = 0.357\ \text{TOE/person} $$ $$ PCEC = 357\ \text{kgoe/person} $$ > [!TIP] > **Comparison:** The world average PCEC is ~1870 kgoe, USA ~6800, EU ~3000, India ~600. The calculated value (357) suggests a **developing economy baseline**. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Forecasting Future Energy Consumption > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** India's total energy consumption in 2020 was 750 MTOE. If the annual growth rate is 4.5%, what will be the consumption in 2030? ### Step 1: Apply the Compound Growth Formula $$ E_n = E_0 \times (1+r)^n $$ $$ E_{2030} = 750 \times (1 + 0.045)^{10} $$ ### Step 2: Compute the Growth Factor $$ (1.045)^{10} = 1.5530 $$ (Using $\ln(1.045) \times 10 = 0.044 \times 10 = 0.440$ and $e^{0.440} = 1.553$.) ### Step 3: Final Answer $$ E_{2030} = 750 \times 1.5530 = 1164.75\ \text{MTOE} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse the base year. Always confirm whether $n$ is measured from the **base year** or **current year**. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Energy Savings in a Sector > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** A 1000-unit residential complex consumes 5.0 kWh/day per unit. After an energy audit, consumption drops to 3.8 kWh/day. Find the daily energy saved, annual savings, and percentage reduction. ### Step 1: Daily Savings per Unit $$ \Delta E_{\text{unit}} = 5.0 - 3.8 = 1.2\ \text{kWh/day} $$ ### Step 2: Total Daily Savings $$ \Delta E_{\text{total}} = 1.2 \times 1000 = 1200\ \text{kWh/day} $$ ### Step 3: Annual Savings $$ \Delta E_{\text{annual}} = 1200 \times 365 = 438{,}000\ \text{kWh/year} $$ ### Step 4: Percentage Reduction $$ \%\text{Savings} = \frac{1.2}{5.0} \times 100 = 24\% $$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sectoral Energy Drivers > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision-Making Matrix for Audit Priority:** | Sector | Dominant Energy Form | Audit Priority Equipment | Typical Energy Share (India) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Industrial** | Thermal + Electrical | Boilers, Motors, Compressors | 44% | | **Transport** | Petroleum products | Engines, Fleet logistics | 20% | | **Domestic** | Electrical (LPG) | HVAC, Lighting, Appliances | 18% | | **Commercial** | Electrical | HVAC, Lighting, IT Loads | 10% | | **Agriculture** | Electrical + Diesel | Pumpsets, Tractors | 8% | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sectoral Energy Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Primary Energy Sources
Coal, Oil, Gas, Hydro, Solar, Wind, Nuclear] --> B[Energy Conversion
Power Plants, Refineries] B --> C[Energy Distribution Grid
Electricity, Pipelines, Road/Rail Network] C --> D1[Industrial Sector
44% Share] C --> D2[Transport Sector
20% Share] C --> D3[Domestic Sector
18% Share] C --> D4[Commercial Sector
10% Share] C --> D5[Agricultural Sector
8% Share] D1 --> E1[End Uses
Process Heat, Motors, Steam] D2 --> E2[End Uses
Road, Rail, Air, Water] D3 --> E3[End Uses
Lighting, HVAC, Cooking] D4 --> E4[End Uses
AC, Lighting, IT, Elevators] D5 --> E5[End Uses
Pumping, Tractor Operation] E1 --> F1[Energy Loss and Waste Heat] E2 --> F2[Exhaust and Friction Losses] E3 --> F3[Building Envelope Losses] E4 --> F4[Ventilation and Standby Losses] E5 --> F5[No Load and Pump Inefficiency] ``` ## 4.2 Sectoral Audit Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR Start([Energy Audit Initiated]) --> Q1{Sector Identified?} Q1 -->|Industrial| Ind[Step 1: Walk-through Survey] Q1 -->|Commercial| Com[Step 1: Building Energy Modeling] Q1 -->|Domestic| Dom[Step 1: Appliance Inventory] Q1 -->|Transport| Trn[Step 1: Fleet Analysis] Q1 -->|Agriculture| Agr[Step 1: Pump and Tractor Audit] Ind --> Ind2[Step 2: Motor and Boiler Audit] Com --> Com2[Step 2: HVAC and Lighting Audit] Dom --> Dom2[Step 2: Star Rating Check] Trn --> Trn2[Step 2: Fuel Efficiency Check] Agr --> Agr2[Step 2: Pump Efficiency Test] Ind2 --> End([Recommendations Report]) Com2 --> End Dom2 --> End Trn2 --> End Agr2 --> End ``` ## 4.3 Energy Intensity Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Developed["Developed Economies (Low EI)"] D1[Japan: 0.10 kgoe/dollar] D2[Germany: 0.13 kgoe/dollar] D3[UK: 0.14 kgoe/dollar] end subgraph Developing["Developing Economies (Medium EI)"] M1[China: 0.30 kgoe/dollar] M2[Brazil: 0.25 kgoe/dollar] M3[India: 0.20 kgoe/dollar] end subgraph Undeveloped["Undeveloped Economies (Variable EI)"] U1[Sub-Saharan Average: 0.45 kgoe/dollar] U2[Developing Asia: 0.35 kgoe/dollar] end Developed --> Compare{Energy Efficient?} Developing --> Compare Undeveloped --> Compare Compare -->|Lower EI Wins| Result[Best Practice Benchmarks] ``` ## 4.4 Domestic Sector Energy End-Use Breakdown ```mermaid pie title Domestic Sector Energy End-Use Share India "HVAC and Cooling" : 40 "Lighting" : 19 "Refrigeration" : 15 "Water Heating" : 10 "Cooking" : 8 "Entertainment and IT" : 5 "Others" : 3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]` **Define Energy Intensity. Mention its significance in national energy planning.** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Definition (2 marks):** Energy Intensity is defined as the ratio of total energy consumption of a country to its Gross Domestic Product (GDP): > $$EI = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{\text{GDP}}$$ > Units: TOE per million rupees (or kgoe per \$1000 of GDP). > > **Significance (1 mark):** A lower energy intensity indicates higher energy efficiency, better technology adoption, and a more sustainable economy. It is a key benchmarking parameter used by IEA and BEE for cross-country comparison. --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **List the five major energy-consuming sectors and state the dominant energy form in each.** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > 1. **Industrial Sector** — Dominated by thermal energy (coal, gas, steam). > 2. **Domestic Sector** — Dominated by electrical energy. > 3. **Commercial Sector** — Dominated by electrical energy. > 4. **Agricultural Sector** — Dominated by electrical (pumps) and diesel (tractors). > 5. **Transportation Sector** — Dominated by petroleum products (petrol/diesel). > > *(1 mark for naming sectors, 1 mark for grouping, 1 mark for energy forms)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the classification of energy consumption in the five major sectors of an economy. Mention the typical percentage share of each sector in a developing country like India. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The total energy consumption of a state in 2022–23 was 150 MTOE. If the population is 35 million and the GDP is ₹ 18 lakh crore, calculate: - (i) Per capita energy consumption - (ii) Energy intensity in kgoe per ₹1000 of GDP **(7 marks)** > [!NOTE] > **Model Solution to Q(a) (7 marks):** > > 1. **Flowchart (3 marks):** Draw the block diagram showing the 5 sectors connected to primary energy sources (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes). > 2. **Sector Explanation (2 marks):** Briefly describe each sector's end use: > - Industrial: Process heating, motors > - Transport: Road, rail, air > - Domestic: HVAC, lighting > - Commercial: Buildings, IT > - Agriculture: Pumping, tractors > 3. **Typical Share (2 marks):** India approximate share: Industrial 44%, Transport 20%, Domestic 18%, Commercial 10%, Agriculture 8%. > [!NOTE] > **Model Solution to Q(b) (7 marks):** > > **Given:** $E = 150\ \text{MTOE} = 150 \times 10^6\ \text{TOE}$, Population $N = 35 \times 10^6$, GDP $= 18 \times 10^{12}\ \text{₹}$. > > **(i) Per Capita Energy Consumption (3 marks):** > $$PCEC = \frac{E}{N} = \frac{150 \times 10^6}{35 \times 10^6} = 4.286\ \text{TOE/person}$$ > Converting: $4.286 \times 1000 = 4286\ \text{kgoe/person}$. > > **(ii) Energy Intensity (4 marks):** > $$EI = \frac{150 \times 10^6\ \text{TOE}}{18 \times 10^{12}\ \text{₹}} = 8.33 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{TOE/₹}$$ > $$EI = 8.33 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6\ \text{kgoe/₹1000} = 8.33\ \text{kgoe/₹1000 of GDP}$$ --- ### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the concept of Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC). How does it vary between developed and developing nations? Provide two examples with numerical values. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A country consumed 850 MTOE in 2010 and 1180 MTOE in 2020. Determine: - (i) The compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of energy consumption - (ii) The projected energy consumption in 2030 at the same growth rate **(7 marks)** > [!NOTE] > **Model Solution to Q(a) (7 marks):** > > 1. **Definition (2 marks):** PCEC is the average energy available per person: > $$PCEC = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumption}}{\text{Total Population}}$$ > 2. **Developed vs Developing (3 marks):** Developed nations have higher PCEC (3000–7000 kgoe/person) due to industrial activity, AC use, car ownership. Developing nations have lower PCEC (300–800 kgoe/person) due to limited industrial access and lower appliance penetration. > 3. **Numerical Examples (2 marks):** USA: ~6800 kgoe/person; India: ~600 kgoe/person; Norway: ~7000 kgoe/person; Bangladesh: ~250 kgoe/person. > [!NOTE] > **Model Solution to Q(b) (7 marks):** > > **Given:** $E_0 = 850\ \text{MTOE}$ (2010), $E_n = 1180\ \text{MTOE}$ (2020), $n = 10$ years. > > **(i) CAGR (3 marks):** > $$E_n = E_0(1+r)^n$$ > $$1180 = 850(1+r)^{10}$$ > $$(1+r)^{10} = 1.3882$$ > Taking the 10th root: > $$1+r = (1.3882)^{1/10} = 1.0334$$ > $$r = 0.0334 = 3.34\%$$ > > **(ii) Projected 2030 Consumption (4 marks):** > $$E_{2030} = 1180 \times (1.0334)^{10} = 1180 \times 1.3882$$ > $$E_{2030} = 1638.1\ \text{MTOE}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Failing to convert MTOE → TOE or kgoe correctly. Always show the unit conversion step explicitly. > 2. **Wrong Base Year in CAGR:** Confusing $n$ (number of years from base year). For 2010 → 2020, $n = 10$, NOT 9. > 3. **GDP Unit Confusion:** Conflating lakh crore, billion, trillion. Always write units as $\times 10^n$ for clarity. > 4. **Missing Flowchart Labels:** Examiners award marks only for **labeled** diagrams. Unlabeled boxes get zero. > 5. **Formula Substitution Errors:** In the $EI$ formula, students often write TOE/₹ without converting. Show the unit cancellation explicitly. > 6. **No Verification Step:** Always verify: total sectoral shares must sum to 100%. Examiners reward this habit. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:** > > - **Five sectors:** Industrial, Domestic, Commercial, Agriculture, Transport. > - **Industrial sector** is the largest consumer in India (~44%); Transport is the second (~20%). > - **Energy Intensity (EI):** Lower is better; India = ~0.20 kgoe/\$1000 GDP. > - **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC):** India ~600 kgoe/person; Global average ~1870 kgoe. > - **Energy Unit Conversions:** $1\ \text{TOE} = 42\ \text{GJ} = 11.63\ \text{MWh}_{\text{th}}$. > - **CAGR Formula:** $E_n = E_0(1+r)^n$ — used for forecasting. > - **Sectoral Share Formula:** $S_i = (E_i/E_{\text{total}}) \times 100\%$. > - **Audit Priority:** Industrial → Transport → Commercial → Domestic → Agriculture. > - **Domestic End-Use Split (India):** HVAC 40%, Lighting 19%, Refrigeration 15%, Others 26%. > - **Conversion factor:** $1\ \text{kWh} = 3.6\ \text{MJ} = 860\ \text{kcal}$. > - **Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = (\text{Output}/\text{Input}) \times 100\%$. > - **HVAC** is the **single largest** end-use in both commercial and domestic sectors — hence the top audit target. > - **Always** verify numerical sums (sectoral share = 100%, mass balance, energy balance). > - **Developed economies** have **lower EI** but **higher PCEC**; developing economies have the **opposite profile**.

topics.title: Energy Security and Its Conservation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Security and Its Conservation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Energy Security — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Security (KTU 2024 Definition):** Energy Security is the uninterrupted availability of energy sources at an affordable price, ensuring a nation, organization, or household can meet its current and future energy demand without suffering from supply disruptions, price shocks, geopolitical threats, or infrastructural failures.

Energy security operates across **four inter-locking dimensions** that examiners frequently test:

1. **Availability** — Adequate physical supply of energy resources (coal, crude oil, natural gas, uranium, renewables).
2. **Accessibility** — The infrastructure (grids, pipelines, refineries) to deliver the energy to end-users.
3. **Affordability** — The cost must be sustainable for consumers and competitive for industries.
4. **Acceptability / Sustainability** — Energy must be sourced and consumed with minimal environmental, social, and geopolitical cost.

## 1.2 Energy Conservation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Conservation:** The practice of reducing the amount of energy consumed by using it more efficiently, eliminating wastage, and modifying consumption patterns, *without necessarily sacrificing the level of service or output*.

A frequently confused twin concept is **Energy Efficiency**:
- **Conservation** = Using *less* energy by changing behavior or processes (e.g., switching off lights).
- **Efficiency** = Getting *more output* per unit of energy (e.g., using a BLDC fan instead of an induction fan).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍲 The Kitchen Analogy (Energy Security):**
> Imagine a household kitchen. Energy Security is like ensuring the kitchen has a **steady, affordable, and clean** supply of LPG cylinders and electricity every day — the cylinder vendor is reliable, the price doesn't suddenly double, and the supply isn't cut off during a crisis (like a cyclone). A country with energy security sleeps peacefully; a country without it worries daily about its next "meal" of energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **💡 The Phone Battery Analogy (Energy Conservation):**
> Your phone at 20% battery. Energy Conservation is like switching off Wi-Fi, lowering brightness, and closing background apps. You're not changing the battery (energy source) — you're *reducing waste* so it lasts longer. Energy Efficiency is like upgrading to a phone with a more efficient processor — same battery lasts twice as long.

## 1.4 Key Global & National Metrics

| Metric | Value (Indicative) |
|---|---|
| Global primary energy consumption | **~606 EJ/year** (Exa-Joules) |
| India's primary energy consumption | **~36 EJ/year** (~6% of world) |
| India's import dependency (crude oil) | **~85%** |
| Standard unit (energy) | **1 TOE = 41.868 GJ** |
| Barrel of crude oil equivalent | **~6.12 GJ** |
| Standard BEE Star rating range | **1-star to 5-star** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillars of Energy Security — Structured Logic

Energy security is built on **four strategic pillars**. Understanding these is critical because KTU exam questions often ask students to "explain the dimensions of energy security" for 7 marks.

- **Pillar 1 — Resource Adequacy (Supply Side):**
  - *Why:* A nation must have proven reserves or assured import contracts to meet demand.
  - *How:* Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPRs), long-term LNG contracts, domestic exploration (ONGC, OIL).
  - *Example:* India maintains SPRs at Visakhapatnam, Mangalore, and Padur (total 5.33 MMT).

- **Pillar 2 — Demand-Side Management (DSM):**
  - *Why:* Reducing demand reduces dependence on imports.
  - *How:* Energy efficiency standards, BEE star labelling, ECBC (Eco Niwas Samhita) for buildings.
  - *Example:* India's PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme for industries.

- **Pillar 3 — Diversification of Sources:**
  - *Why:* Avoid over-reliance on a single fuel or supplier.
  - *How:* Renewable portfolio (solar, wind, hydro, nuclear), fuel-mix balancing.
  - *Example:* India's target of **500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030** (Panchamrit commitment, COP26).

- **Pillar 4 — Geopolitical & Strategic Stability:**
  - *Why:* Energy routes (e.g., Strait of Hormuz, Malacca Strait) are chokepoints.
  - *How:* Pipeline diplomacy, international partnerships (IAEA, IEA, OPEC+ dialogues).
  - *Example:* India–Russia crude trade, Chabahar Port (Iran), India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC).

## 2.2 Principles of Energy Conservation (The 5 R's)

Energy conservation in industry and households rests on five actionable principles:

1. **Reduce** — Minimize energy use at the source (e.g., turn off idle equipment).
2. **Reuse** — Use waste energy for another process (e.g., waste heat recovery).
3. **Recycle** — Convert waste energy into usable form (e.g., biogas from organic waste).
4. **Recover** — Capture lost energy (e.g., regenerative braking in EVs).
5. **Replace** — Substitute inefficient technologies with efficient ones (e.g., LED replacing incandescent).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Energy (PE) to Useful Energy | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100\%$ | % | Efficiency |
| Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{Energy\ Consumed}{Output\ Produced}$ | kWh/ton, MJ/kg | Lower is better |
| Energy Cost | $C_E = P \times t \times R$ | ₹, \$ | $P$=power, $t$=time, $R$=rate |
| Energy Payback Period | $EPP = \dfrac{Embodied\ Energy\ of\ System}{Annual\ Energy\ Saved}$ | years | Decision metric |
| Simple Payback | $SPP = \dfrac{Investment}{Annual\ Savings}$ | years | Financial metric |
| Annual Energy Savings | $\Delta E = E_{baseline} - E_{proposed}$ | kWh, MJ | Conservation outcome |
| Power from Renewables | $P = \eta \times A \times I$ | W, kW | For solar: $I$ = irradiance |
| Energy Security Index (qualitative) | $ESI = f(Availability, Affordability, Accessibility, Acceptability)$ | dimensionless | Composite indicator |
| Barrel of Oil Equivalent | $1\ bbl \approx 6.12\ GJ \approx 1700\ kWh$ | — | Standard conversion |
| TOE Conversion | $1\ TOE = 41.868\ GJ$ | — | Tonne of Oil Equivalent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the cornerstone of any **Energy Audit** (covered in Module 4). KTU often frames Module 1 questions on energy conservation with these same metrics as a foundation. Memorize the units carefully — a wrong unit in a $SEC$ calculation costs full marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Industrial Sector:** Energy conservation in motors (VFDs), boilers (air-fuel ratio optimization), compressors (cascade control).
- **Buildings:** ECBC compliance, green building ratings (GRIHA, LEED-India).
- **Transport:** EVs, modal shift to rail, fuel-efficient vehicle standards (BS-VI, CAFE norms).
- **Power Sector:** Reducing AT&C (Aggregate Technical & Commercial) losses from the current **~18–20%** to global benchmarks of <8%.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This topic is predominantly conceptual and management-oriented. However, KTU 2024 examiners expect students to perform **quantitative energy conservation calculations** that link Module 1 theory with later modules (audit, accounting). The most common calculation is **Energy Savings Estimation** and **Simple Payback Period** derivation.

## 3.1 Derivation: Annual Energy Savings from an Energy Conservation Measure

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem (KtU 2024 Model Style):**
> A factory operates a 30 kW motor for 4,000 hours/year. The motor's efficiency is 88%. An energy auditor proposes replacing it with a premium-efficiency IE3 motor (efficiency 93.5%). The electricity tariff is ₹7.50/kWh. Calculate:
> (a) Annual energy saved (kWh and GJ)
> (b) Annual monetary savings (₹)
> (c) Cost of the new motor = ₹1,80,000. Compute Simple Payback Period.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Input energy to the existing motor (baseline):**

The mechanical output is 30 kW. The electrical input equals the output divided by efficiency.

$$
E_{input,old} = \frac{P_{output}}{\eta_{old}} = \frac{30\ \text{kW}}{0.88}
$$

$$
E_{input,old} = 34.0909\ \text{kW (electrical)}
$$

**Step 2 — Input energy to the proposed IE3 motor:**

$$
E_{input,new} = \frac{P_{output}}{\eta_{new}} = \frac{30\ \text{kW}}{0.935}
$$

$$
E_{input,new} = 32.0856\ \text{kW (electrical)}
$$

**Step 3 — Power input reduction (per hour of operation):**

$$
\Delta P = E_{input,old} - E_{input,new} = 34.0909 - 32.0856
$$

$$
\Delta P = 2.0053\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual energy saved (kWh):**

The motor runs for 4,000 hours/year.

$$
\Delta E = \Delta P \times t = 2.0053 \times 4000
$$

$$
\Delta E = 8021.39\ \text{kWh/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to GJ (since 1 kWh = 0.0036 GJ):**

$$
\Delta E_{GJ} = 8021.39 \times 0.0036
$$

$$
\Delta E_{GJ} = 28.877\ \text{GJ/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Annual monetary savings (₹):**

$$
S_{annual} = \Delta E \times R = 8021.39 \times 7.50
$$

$$
S_{annual} = \text{₹}\ 60{,}160.43/\text{year}
$$

**Step 7 — Simple Payback Period (SPP):**

$$
SPP = \frac{Investment}{Annual\ Savings} = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{60{,}160.43}
$$

$$
SPP \approx 2.99\ \text{years} \approx 3\ \text{years}
$$

### Result Summary:

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Power reduction | **2.0053 kW** |
| Annual energy saved | **8,021.39 kWh** (or **28.88 GJ**) |
| Annual cost saving | **₹ 60,160/year** |
| Simple Payback | **~3 years** |
| **Verdict** | **Justified (SPP < 5 years is industry standard)** |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Energy Conservation Opportunity (ECO) Hierarchy

| Priority | ECO Type | Typical Saving | Typical Payback | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Highest) | Housekeeping / No-cost | 5–10% | Immediate | Switching off idle loads, insulation repair |
| 2 | Low-cost / Procedural | 10–20% | < 1 year | Timer-based lighting, capacitor bank for PF |
| 3 | Medium-cost Retrofit | 20–30% | 1–3 years | VFD on pump, LED retrofit, ECBC windows |
| 4 (Longest) | High-cost Capital | 30–50% | 3–5+ years | Cogeneration, waste heat boiler, solar PV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When answering Module 1 questions on conservation, always classify the opportunity into one of the four tiers above. It demonstrates "engineer's judgement" — a frequently assessed KTU 2024 Outcome (CO1: Engineering Knowledge).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Security Framework — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Primary Energy Sources] --> B[Energy Conversion & Refining]
    B --> C[Transmission and Distribution Network]
    C --> D[End Use Sectors]
    D --> D1[Industrial]
    D --> D2[Domestic]
    D --> D3[Commercial]
    D --> D4[Transport]
    D --> D5[Agriculture]

    E[Strategic Reserves] --> B
    F[Geopolitical Agreements] --> A
    G[Diversification Policy] --> A
    H[Demand Side Management] --> D

    I[External Threats] --> I1[Price Volatility]
    I --> I2[Supply Disruption]
    I --> I3[Climate Risk]
    I --> I4[Geopolitical Conflict]

    A -.-> I
    B -.-> I
    C -.-> I

    J[Energy Security Outcomes] --> J1[Uninterrupted Supply]
    J --> J2[Affordable Tariff]
    J --> J3[Environmental Sustainability]
    J --> J4[Economic Stability]

    D1 --> J
    D2 --> J
    D3 --> J
    D4 --> J
    D5 --> J
    H --> J
    G --> J
    F --> J
    E --> J
```

## 4.2 Energy Conservation Methodology — 5-Stage Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Stage 1: Energy Audit] --> A2[Stage 2: Baseline Energy Analysis]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3: Identify Conservation Opportunities]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 4: Implementation and Monitoring]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 5: Verification and Reporting]

    A1 -.Feedback Loop.-> A5
    A5 -.Continuous Improvement.-> A1
```

## 4.3 Energy Security vs. Energy Conservation — Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ES[ENERGY SECURITY]
        ES1[Supply Assurance]
        ES2[Affordable Pricing]
        ES3[Infrastructure Resilience]
        ES4[Strategic Reserves]
        ES5[Geopolitical Stability]
    end

    subgraph EC[ENERGY CONSERVATION]
        EC1[Reduce Consumption]
        EC2[Improve Efficiency]
        EC3[Behavioural Change]
        EC4[Technology Upgrade]
        EC5[Policy and Standards]
    end

    ES -.Supports.-> EC
    EC -.Reduces Pressure On.-> ES
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for Exam:** The bidirectional arrow between the two subgraphs is a high-value diagram for KTU 2024 answers. Conservation *eases* the security burden, while a secure supply *enables* planned conservation. Examiners reward students who visually depict this interplay.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between Energy Conservation and Energy Efficiency. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Energy Conservation** is the practice of *reducing energy consumption* by changing habits or processes, without changing the service output. (1 mark)
  - *Example:* Switching off fans and lights when not in use; turning off a vehicle's engine at traffic signals. (1 mark)
- **Energy Efficiency** is the practice of *obtaining more useful output per unit of energy input* by upgrading technology. (1 mark)
  - *Example:* Replacing a 60 W incandescent bulb with a 9 W LED bulb providing the same illumination.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**List any four dimensions (pillars) of Energy Security.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Availability** of energy resources. (0.75 mark)
2. **Accessibility** through infrastructure. (0.75 mark)
3. **Affordability** for consumers. (0.75 mark)
4. **Acceptability / Environmental sustainability.** (0.75 mark)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For 14-mark answers, the model answer format expects clear headings, bullet points, and at least 2–3 examples or numerical illustrations. Writing a "wall of text" without structure will cost you 3–4 marks. Always support definitions with at least one Indian-specific example (BEE, ECBC, SPR, PAT scheme, etc.).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of **Energy Security** and discuss its **four major dimensions** with suitable examples from the Indian context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Energy Conservation**. Discuss the **"5R principles"** of energy conservation and explain how each contributes to industrial energy savings. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Energy Security — Concept & Dimensions (7 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Energy security is the uninterrupted availability of energy at affordable prices, ensuring the nation's economic, social, and strategic stability.
- **Dimension 1 — Availability (1.5 marks):** India imports ~85% of crude oil, ~50% of natural gas, and 100% of certain critical minerals. To enhance availability, India operates **Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPRs)** at Visakhapatnam (1.33 MMT), Mangalore (1.5 MMT), and Padur (2.5 MMT), providing ~9.5 days of net import cover.
- **Dimension 2 — Accessibility (1.5 marks):** Even with reserves, energy must reach consumers. India has built the **"One Nation One Grid"** (national grid of 400+ GW capacity) and is investing in **city gas distribution (CGD)** networks covering 295+ geographical areas. The **UJALA** programme distributed 36+ crore LED bulbs, improving household access to efficient lighting.
- **Dimension 3 — Affordability (1.5 marks):** Subsidy management (LPG DBTL — Direct Benefit Transfer for LPG), PMUY (Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana) for free LPG connections, and the **SAUBHAGYA scheme** for 100% household electrification.
- **Dimension 4 — Acceptability / Sustainability (1.5 marks):** India's commitment at COP26 (Glasgow) — **Panchamrit goals** — targeting **500 GW of non-fossil fuel installed capacity by 2030** and net-zero by 2070. Promoting green hydrogen, electric mobility (FAME-II), and ethanol blending (E20 target by 2025).

**(b) Energy Conservation — 5R Principles (7 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Energy conservation is the conscious practice of reducing energy use through behavioural, procedural, and technological interventions without compromising output.
- **Reduce (1.2 marks):** Switching off idle equipment, optimizing HVAC set-points, using natural daylighting. *Industrial example:* Shutting down boilers during non-production hours in a textile mill can save up to 8% of annual fuel cost.
- **Reuse (1.2 marks):** Reusing waste heat from compressor exhaust to pre-heat boiler feed water. *Example:* In a dairy plant, hot water from pasteurization is reused for CIP (clean-in-place) operations.
- **Recycle (1.2 marks):** Converting organic waste to biogas for cooking/steam generation. *Example:* Sugar industries using bagasse to generate captive power and export surplus to the grid.
- **Recover (1.2 marks):** Capturing lost energy. *Example:* Regenerative braking in metro rail systems recovers up to **30%** of traction energy.
- **Replace (1.2 marks):** Substituting inefficient equipment. *Example:* Replacing conventional T8 fluorescent lamps with LED tubes (saves 50–60% energy).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** "Energy Conservation is the first fuel for a developing nation." Discuss this statement in the context of India's energy demand and import dependency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 22 kW induction motor operates 5,000 hours/year at 90% efficiency. A VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) is installed reducing average motor loading to 75% of rated, with motor efficiency improving to 93%. The electricity tariff is ₹8/kWh. The VFD cost is ₹1,20,000. Calculate **(i)** annual energy saved, **(ii)** annual cost saved, and **(iii)** simple payback period. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Energy Conservation as the First Fuel (7 Marks):**
- **Opening Context (1 mark):** India is the world's third-largest energy consumer but imports ~85% of its crude oil and ~50% of natural gas. Every unit of energy conserved directly reduces import burden and forex outflow.
- **Demand-Supply Gap (2 marks):** India's peak electricity demand is projected to reach **~366 GW by 2030** (CEA estimates). To bridge this gap, India must add generation capacity — every 1 GW of new capacity costs ~₹5,000–6,000 crore. Conservation can defer this capex.
- **Conservation as "Virtual Capacity" (2 marks):** The **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** estimates India's energy efficiency programmes have saved **~62 MTOE** cumulatively — equivalent to ~12 GW of avoided generation capacity.
- **Policy Reinforcement (1 mark):** The **Energy Conservation Act 2001** (amended 2010), the **PAT scheme** (mandatory for 13 energy-intensive sectors), and **ECBC / Eco Niwas Samhita** for buildings are institutional pillars.
- **Conclusion (1 mark):** Therefore, energy conservation is the *cheapest, fastest, and cleanest* "fuel" — it costs less than new power plants, takes less time to deploy, and emits zero CO₂. It is rightly called the "first fuel" in national policy.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $P_{rated} = 22\ \text{kW}$, $t = 5000\ \text{hr/yr}$, $R = ₹8/\text{kWh}$, VFD cost = ₹1,20,000, $\eta_{old} = 90\%$, $\eta_{new} = 93\%$.

**Assumption:** The motor's output mechanical power remains 22 kW (full load equivalent). The VFD reduces input power by improving efficiency (no speed/load reduction in this problem — efficiency gain is the only saving).

**Step 1 — Baseline input power:**

$$
P_{in,old} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_{old}} = \frac{22}{0.90} = 24.444\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — New input power with VFD + improved motor:**

$$
P_{in,new} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_{new}} = \frac{22}{0.93} = 23.656\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Power saving:**

$$
\Delta P = 24.444 - 23.656 = 0.788\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual energy saved:**

$$
\Delta E = 0.788 \times 5000 = 3{,}940\ \text{kWh/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual cost saved:**

$$
S = 3{,}940 \times 8 = \text{₹}\ 31{,}520/\text{year}
$$

**Step 6 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$
SPP = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{31{,}520} \approx 3.81\ \text{years}
$$

**Verdict (0.5 mark):** With an SPP of ~3.8 years (acceptable industry threshold of <5 years), the VFD investment is **justified**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist (Exam Day Lifesaver):**

- **Definition clarity:** Conservation = *use less*; Efficiency = *use smarter*; Security = *assured supply*.
- **Four pillars of Energy Security:** Availability, Accessibility, Affordability, Acceptability — memorize in order.
- **5 R's of Conservation:** Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover, Replace.
- **India's key security statistics:** ~85% crude oil import dependency, SPR cover of ~9.5 days (expanding to ~22 days), 500 GW non-fossil target by 2030.
- **Key institutions to remember:** BEE, CEA, MNRE, EESL, SPRL (Strategic Petroleum Reserves Limited), PCRA (Petroleum Conservation Research Association).
- **Key schemes:** UJALA, UJJWALA, SAUBHAGYA, FAME-II, PAT, ECBC, ENS (Energy Norms & Standards).
- **Standard conversions to memorize:** $1\ \text{TOE} = 41.868\ \text{GJ}$; $1\ \text{kWh} = 3.6\ \text{MJ} = 0.0036\ \text{GJ}$; $1\ \text{bbl oil} \approx 6.12\ \text{GJ}$.
- **Two flagship formulas:** $SEC = \dfrac{E}{Output}$ (lower is better) and $SPP = \dfrac{Investment}{Annual\ Savings}$ (lower is better).
- **One-line exam opener for any Module 1 question:** *"Energy is the primary driver of economic growth, yet it is finite — therefore, security and conservation are the two pillars of sustainable development."* (This sentence fetches an immediate 1 mark for "context setting" in KTU valuations.)
- **Avoid the common pitfall:** Confusing "renewable energy" with "energy conservation" — they are related but distinct. Renewables are a *supply-side* answer; conservation is a *demand-side* answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy and Its Various Forms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy and Its Various Forms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Energy** is the fundamental scalar physical quantity that represents the capacity of a system to perform **work** or to transfer **heat**. In the framework of classical mechanics and thermodynamics, energy quantifies the ability of a system to produce changes in its own state or in the state of its surroundings. The SI unit of energy is the **Joule (J)**, defined as the work done when a force of one **Newton** displaces a body by one **metre** along the direction of the force.

Mathematically, the differential form of energy is expressed as:

$$dE = F \cdot dx$$

where $F$ is the applied force in **Newtons** and $dx$ is the elemental displacement in **metres**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Energy is the primary fuel of an industrial economy and a key factor in the sustained economic growth of a country. In physical terms, it is defined as the capacity of a system to perform work or produce change.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine energy as the **"money of the physical universe"**. Just as money is required to buy goods, energy is required to cause any physical change — to lift a weight, to heat water, to light a bulb, or to power a factory.

- A **stored battery** is like a wallet full of cash (potential chemical energy).
- A **moving car** is like cash being spent (kinetic energy).
- A **hot cup of coffee** is like a hot potato — it has stored thermal energy that dissipates over time.

The First Law of Thermodynamics (energy conservation) tells us that this "money" can neither be created nor destroyed, only **converted** from one form to another — much like exchanging dollars for euros or rupees.

### 1.3 Standard Energy Units & Conversions (Must Memorize)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key SI and Industrial Energy Units:**
>
> | Unit Name | Symbol | Equivalent in Joules | Common Use |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Joule | J | 1 J (base unit) | SI scientific unit |
> | Calorie | cal | 4.184 J | Food energy, chemistry |
> | Kilo-calorie | kcal | 4 184 J | Nutrition, dietetics |
> | Watt-hour | Wh | 3 600 J | Electricity billing |
> | kiloWatt-hour | kWh | 3.6 × 10⁶ J | Domestic & industrial electricity |
> | British Thermal Unit | BTU | 1 055 J | HVAC, air-conditioning industry |
> | Therm | thm | 1.055 × 10⁸ J | Natural gas billing (UK) |
> | Electron-volt | eV | 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J | Atomic & nuclear physics |
> | Tonne of Oil Equivalent | TOE | 4.187 × 10¹⁰ J | National energy accounting |

**Fundamental Physical Constants Used:**

- Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J}\cdot\text{s}$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Mechanical equivalent of heat: **1 cal = 4.184 J**
- Avogadro's number: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
