## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Principal Forms of Energy

Energy in nature and engineering manifests in **seven primary forms**. Every observable physical phenomenon can be traced to a combination of these forms.

#### 2.1.1 Mechanical Energy (Kinetic + Potential)

**Mechanical energy** is the energy possessed by a body due to its **motion** (kinetic) or its **position/configuration** (potential).

**Sub-form 1 — Kinetic Energy (KE):**
Energy possessed by a body in translational motion.

$$KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$$

For a rotating body:

$$KE_{rot} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

where $I$ is the moment of inertia (kg·m²) and $\omega$ is angular velocity (rad/s).

**Sub-form 2 — Potential Energy (PE):**
- **Gravitational PE:** $PE = m g h$
- **Elastic (Spring) PE:** $PE = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in Energy Audit:** Most industrial machines (lathes, motors, pumps, conveyors) convert stored electrical energy into mechanical KE and PE. Auditors measure mechanical energy output to compute **efficiency** of prime movers.

#### 2.1.2 Thermal (Heat) Energy

Energy associated with the **random motion of molecules** within a substance. It is a form of kinetic energy at the microscopic level.

**Sensible heat:**
$$Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$$

**Latent heat (phase change):**
$$Q = m \, L$$

where $L$ is the specific latent heat (J/kg).

#### 2.1.3 Chemical Energy

Energy stored in the **bonds between atoms and molecules**. Released when bonds break and re-form during **combustion** (e.g., coal, oil, gas) or **electrochemical reactions** (batteries, fuel cells).

**Calorific value of fuel:**
$$CV = \frac{\text{Heat released (J)}}{\text{Mass of fuel burned (kg)}}$$

#### 2.1.4 Electrical Energy

Energy derived from the **flow of electric charge** (electrons) through a conductor under the influence of an electric field.

$$E_{elec} = V \, I \, t = I^2 R \, t = \frac{V^2 t}{R}$$

Electrical power:
$$P = V \, I$$

#### 2.1.5 Electromagnetic / Radiant Energy

Energy propagated as **electromagnetic waves** (light, infrared, ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma rays). Each photon carries quantum energy:

$$E = h \nu = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$

where $\nu$ is frequency (Hz) and $\lambda$ is wavelength (m).

#### 2.1.6 Nuclear Energy

Energy released from changes in the **nucleus of an atom** — either by **fission** (splitting of heavy nuclei like U-235) or **fusion** (combining light nuclei like H-2 and H-3).

**Einstein's mass-energy equivalence:**
$$E = m c^2$$

#### 2.1.7 Sound Energy

Energy carried by **longitudinal mechanical waves** through a medium. Although small in scale, it represents a non-trivial form of mechanical energy in acoustic engineering.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Energy Forms and Governing Equations**

| # | Form of Energy | Governing Equation | SI Unit | Typical Engineering Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinetic (translational) | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | Wind, moving fluids, flywheels |
| 2 | Kinetic (rotational) | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | J | Turbines, motors, generators |
| 3 | Potential (gravitational) | $PE = m g h$ | J | Hydro-electric reservoirs, cranes |
| 4 | Potential (elastic) | $PE = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ | J | Springs, compressed gas |
| 5 | Thermal (sensible) | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | J | Steam, hot fluids, furnaces |
| 6 | Thermal (latent) | $Q = m L$ | J | Evaporators, condensers |
| 7 | Electrical | $E = V I t$ | J | Motors, heaters, lighting |
| 8 | Chemical | $E = m \cdot CV$ | J | Fuels, batteries, biomass |
| 9 | Electromagnetic / Radiant | $E = h \nu$ | J | Solar PV, lasers, microwaves |
| 10 | Nuclear | $E = m c^2$ | J | Reactors, radio-isotopes |
| 11 | Mass-energy (rest) | $E_0 = m c^2$ | J | Matter itself |

> **Conversion factors essential for KTU numericals:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned} 1 \text{ kWh} &= 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J} \\ 1 \text{ cal} &= 4.184 \text{ J} \\ 1 \text{ BTU} &= 1055 \text{ J} \\ 1 \text{ TOE} &= 4.187 \times 10^{10} \text{ J} \\ 1 \text{ eV} &= 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a typical **industrial energy audit**, the auditor identifies the **dominant form** of energy crossing each system boundary. For instance:

- A **boiler** receives chemical energy (fuel) and delivers thermal energy (steam).
- A **motor** receives electrical energy and delivers mechanical energy.
- A **solar PV panel** receives radiant energy and delivers electrical energy.

The **principle of energy balance** is the audit's foundation:

$$E_{in} = E_{out} + E_{stored} + E_{lost}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love asking students to *identify the dominant energy form* at input and output of a device. Memorize the energy-conversion map of at least five common industrial devices (boiler, motor, generator, pump, chiller).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mechanical Equivalent of Heat (Joule's Experiment)

**Problem setup:** A mass $m$ falling through a height $h$ drives a paddle wheel that stirs a liquid. The work done by gravity on the paddle is entirely converted into heat in the liquid.

**Step 1 — Work done by the falling mass (mechanical energy released):**

$$W = F \cdot d = (m g) \cdot h = m g h$$

Here, the force $F = mg$ (weight of the mass), and displacement $d = h$.

**Step 2 — Heat generated in the liquid (thermal energy absorbed):**

$$Q = m_l \, c \, \Delta T$$

where $m_l$ is the mass of the liquid, $c$ its specific heat capacity, and $\Delta T$ the rise in temperature.

**Step 3 — Equating mechanical work to heat generated (energy conservation):**

$$W = Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad m g h = m_l \, c \, \Delta T$$

**Step 4 — Solving for the mechanical equivalent of heat, $J$:**

$$J = \frac{W}{Q} = \frac{m g h}{m_l \, c \, \Delta T}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation with $m = 5$ kg, $h = 2$ m, $m_l = 2$ kg, $c = 4184$ J/(kg·K), $\Delta T = 0.0117$ K:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 5 \times 9.81 \times 2 = 98.1 \text{ J} \\
Q &= 2 \times 4184 \times 0.0117 = 97.9 \text{ J} \\
J &= \frac{98.1}{97.9} \approx 4.002 \text{ J/cal} \end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Approximately **4.2 J** of mechanical work is required to produce **1 calorie** of heat. This is the *mechanical equivalent of heat*, a cornerstone result that unified mechanics and thermodynamics.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Stored in a Compressed Gas (Work-Energy Path)

**Step 1 — Initial state of gas:** Pressure $P_1$, Volume $V_1$, in a cylinder with piston of area $A$.

**Step 2 — Compress the gas quasi-statically to pressure $P_2$, volume $V_2$.** The work done ON the gas equals the energy stored.

**Step 3 — Infinitesimal work done in compressing by $dV$:**

$$dW = -P \, dV$$

The negative sign indicates work done *on* the system when $dV < 0$ (compression).

**Step 4 — For an isothermal process** (ideal gas, constant $T$): $P = \frac{n R T}{V}$

**Step 5 — Total work done on gas during isothermal compression from $V_1$ to $V_2$:**

$$W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{n R T}{V} \, dV = -n R T \ln \left( \frac{V_2}{V_1} \right) = n R T \ln \left( \frac{V_1}{V_2} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Energy stored in the compressed gas** = Work done on gas:

$$E_{stored} = n R T \ln \left( \frac{V_1}{V_2} \right) = P_1 V_1 \ln \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Electrical Energy to Mechanical Energy (DC Motor)

**Step 1 — Electrical input power:**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I$$

**Step 2 — Armature copper losses (I²R losses):**

$$P_{cu} = I_a^2 \, R_a$$

**Step 3 — Net developed mechanical power:**

$$P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = V I_a - I_a^2 R_a$$

where $E_b$ is the back-EMF.

**Step 4 — Gross mechanical output power (shaft power):**

$$P_{out} = P_{dev} - P_{friction\&windage} - P_{iron}$$

**Step 5 — Motor efficiency:**

$$\eta_{motor} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example:**
Given $V = 220$ V, $I = 10$ A, $R_a = 0.5$ Ω, friction loss = 50 W.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= 220 \times 10 = 2200 \text{ W} \\
P_{cu} &= 10^2 \times 0.5 = 50 \text{ W} \\
P_{dev} &= 2200 - 50 = 2150 \text{ W} \\
P_{out} &= 2150 - 50 = 2100 \text{ W} \\
\eta &= \frac{2100}{2200} \times 100\% = 95.45\% \end{aligned}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Energy Form Identification (Industrial Devices)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Reference Table for KTU Numericals**

| Industrial Device | Input Energy Form | Output Energy Form | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electric motor | Electrical | Mechanical (rotational KE) | $P = 2 \pi N T / 60$ |
| Diesel generator | Chemical (fuel CV) | Electrical | $\eta = P_{elec} / (m \cdot CV)$ |
| Steam boiler | Chemical (fuel) | Thermal (steam enthalpy) | $Q = m (h_{steam} - h_{feedwater})$ |
| Centrifugal pump | Mechanical (KE) | Potential (pressure PE) | $P = \rho g Q H$ |
| Solar PV panel | Radiant (solar) | Electrical | $E = A \cdot G \cdot \eta_{PV}$ |
| Lithium-ion battery | Chemical | Electrical | $E = V_{cell} \cdot C \cdot N$ |
| Compressor | Mechanical | Potential (compressed gas) | $W = n R T \ln(P_2/P_1)$ |
| Incandescent bulb | Electrical | Electromagnetic (light) + Thermal | $P = V^2 / R$ |
| Wind turbine | Kinetic (wind) | Mechanical → Electrical | $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ |
| Nuclear reactor | Nuclear | Thermal (heat) | $E = \Delta m \cdot c^2$ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define energy. List any four different forms of energy with one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Energy is the capacity of a system to do work or to transfer heat. The SI unit is the **joule (J)**.

**Four Forms of Energy (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

| Form | Industrial Example |
|---|---|
| Mechanical (Kinetic) | Flywheel in an IC engine |
| Thermal (Heat) | Steam in a boiler |
| Chemical | Furnace oil / coal combustion |
| Electrical | Induction motor input |

*(Acceptable: Electromagnetic — solar PV; Nuclear — atomic reactor.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the First Law of Thermodynamics and explain its significance in the context of energy management.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):**
The First Law of Thermodynamics states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be converted from one form to another. For any system, the change in internal energy equals heat added minus work done:

$$\Delta U = Q - W$$

**Significance in Energy Management (2 marks):**

1. **Energy Balance:** It forms the foundation of every energy audit equation: $E_{in} = E_{out} + E_{stored} + E_{losses}$.
2. **Efficiency Limits:** It implies that the efficiency of any energy conversion device is bounded by the ratio of useful output to total input — losses are unavoidable but measurable.
3. **Conservation Planning:** Industries use this law to design systems where waste heat from one process becomes the input of another (e.g., cogeneration, waste-heat recovery).
4. **No-Free-Lunch:** It rules out perpetual motion machines of the first kind (PMM1) — a vital audit principle.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, OEPET722]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Classify the various forms of energy. For each form, state the governing physical equation, the SI unit, and one engineering application.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A 1 500 kg electric vehicle is travelling at a constant velocity of 60 km/h on a level road. The total aerodynamic drag force is 250 N, and the rolling resistance is 300 N. Calculate:
  (i) The kinetic energy of the vehicle in kilojoules.
  (ii) The power required to overcome drag and rolling resistance, in kW.
  (iii) The energy consumed (in kWh) to travel 100 km at this constant speed.
  (iv) Convert this energy into **TOE** (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) and **therms**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Energy Forms (7 marks — 1 mark per correct form + 0.5 for equation + 0.5 for application):**

| # | Form | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinetic (translational) | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Wind turbines, vehicle motion |
| 2 | Potential (gravitational) | $PE = mgh$ | Hydroelectric reservoirs |
| 3 | Thermal (sensible) | $Q = mc_p\Delta T$ | Heat exchangers, boilers |
| 4 | Thermal (latent) | $Q = mL$ | Refrigeration, evaporators |
| 5 | Chemical | $E = m \cdot CV$ | Combustion in engines |
| 6 | Electrical | $E = VIt$ | Motors, lighting |
| 7 | Electromagnetic / Radiant | $E = h\nu$ | Solar PV, fibre optics |
| 8 | Nuclear | $E = mc^2$ | Nuclear reactors, radio-isotope |
| 9 | Sound | $I = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v \omega^2 A^2$ | SONAR, acoustic sensors |
| 10 | Elastic | $PE = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$ | Springs, shock absorbers |

*(One mark for any seven correct forms; 0.5 mark for valid application of each.)*

---

**(b) Vehicle Energy Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Mass $m = 1500$ kg
- Velocity $v = 60$ km/h
- Aerodynamic drag $F_d = 250$ N
- Rolling resistance $F_r = 300$ N
- Distance $D = 100$ km

**Step 1 — Convert velocity to SI units (m/s):**

$$v = 60 \text{ km/h} = 60 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 16.667 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate kinetic energy (Part i):**

$$KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (16.667)^2$$

$$KE = 750 \times 277.79 = 208\,343.75 \text{ J} \approx 208.34 \text{ kJ}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 3 — Total resistive force:**

$$F_{total} = F_d + F_r = 250 + 300 = 550 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Power required (Part ii):**

$$P = F_{total} \times v = 550 \times 16.667 = 9\,166.85 \text{ W} \approx 9.17 \text{ kW}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 5 — Energy consumed in 100 km (Part iii):**

$$t = \frac{D}{v} = \frac{100\,000 \text{ m}}{16.667 \text{ m/s}} = 6\,000 \text{ s} = 1.6667 \text{ h}$$

$$E = P \times t = 9.17 \text{ kW} \times 1.6667 \text{ h} = 15.28 \text{ kWh}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 6 — Convert to TOE and therms (Part iv):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{joules} &= 15.28 \text{ kWh} \times 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kWh} \\
&= 5.501 \times 10^7 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

**TOE conversion:**

$$E_{TOE} = \frac{5.501 \times 10^7}{4.187 \times 10^{10}} = 1.314 \times 10^{-3} \text{ TOE}$$

**Therms conversion:**

$$E_{therms} = \frac{5.501 \times 10^7}{1.055 \times 10^8} = 0.5214 \text{ therms}$$

*(2.5 marks for both conversions with units)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [Internal Choice Alternative — KTU Model]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With neat sketches, distinguish between **kinetic energy** and **potential energy**. Derive the expression for the kinetic energy of a body of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ using the work-energy theorem.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* An industrial boiler consumes 250 kg of furnace oil per hour. The calorific value of furnace oil is **42 MJ/kg**. The boiler produces steam at an enthalpy of 2 800 kJ/kg, and the feed-water enters the boiler at 250 kJ/kg. Steam generation rate is 2 000 kg/h. Calculate:
  (i) The heat input rate in kW.
  (ii) The heat output rate (useful thermal energy) in kW.
  (iii) The boiler efficiency.
  (iv) The stack-gas and other losses in kW.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) KE vs PE + Derivation (7 marks):**

**Distinction Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Kinetic Energy | Potential Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Motion of body | Position / configuration |
| Equation | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ | $PE = mgh$ |
| Reference frame | Relative | Requires datum |
| Variability | Changes with speed | Changes with height |

**Derivation using Work–Energy Theorem (5 marks):**

Consider a body of mass $m$ initially at rest, acted upon by a constant force $F$ that produces acceleration $a$ over a displacement $s$, attaining final velocity $v$.

**Step 1 — Kinematic relation:**

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2as \quad \text{with } u = 0$$

$$v^2 = 2as \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \frac{v^2}{2a}$$

**Step 2 — Newton's second law:**

$$F = ma$$

**Step 3 — Work done by force:**

$$W = F \cdot s = ma \cdot \frac{v^2}{2a} = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$$

**Step 4 — By work–energy theorem, $W = \Delta KE$.** Since initial KE = 0, the final KE gained equals the work done:

$$KE = W = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) Boiler Energy Balance (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Fuel consumption = 250 kg/h
- Calorific value $CV = 42$ MJ/kg = 42 000 kJ/kg
- Steam enthalpy $h_s = 2 800$ kJ/kg
- Feed-water enthalpy $h_f = 250$ kJ/kg
- Steam generation = 2 000 kg/h

**Step 1 — Heat input rate (Part i):**

$$Q_{in} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times CV = 250 \text{ kg/h} \times 42\,000 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{in} = 10\,500\,000 \text{ kJ/h} = \frac{10\,500\,000}{3600} \text{ kW} = 2\,916.67 \text{ kW}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 2 — Heat output rate (Part ii):**

$$Q_{out} = \dot{m}_{steam} \times (h_s - h_f) = 2000 \text{ kg/h} \times (2800 - 250) \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{out} = 2000 \times 2550 = 5\,100\,000 \text{ kJ/h} = \frac{5\,100\,000}{3600} = 1416.67 \text{ kW}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 3 — Boiler efficiency (Part iii):**

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{1416.67}{2916.67} \times 100\% = 48.57\%$$

*(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Boiler Efficiency Common Errors:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by 3600** when converting kJ/h to kW. Always state units at every step.
> 2. **Mixing up CV units:** 42 MJ/kg = 42 000 kJ/kg, not 42 kJ/kg.
> 3. **Using gross vs net calorific value:** GCV includes latent heat of vaporization of water in flue gas; NCV excludes it. Always confirm which CV the problem provides.
> 4. **Ignoring sensible heat of fuel and air:** In a real audit, fuel and combustion air are preheated. For audit-level accuracy, include $m_{air} \cdot c_{air} \cdot \Delta T_{air}$ in $Q_{in}$.

**Step 4 — Stack gas + other losses (Part iv):**

$$Q_{loss} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = 2916.67 - 1416.67 = 1500 \text{ kW}$$

*(2 marks — methodology + final answer with unit)*

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — General Pitfalls in Energy-Form Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing units:** The single largest reason students lose marks. Always show unit conversion steps explicitly (e.g., km/h → m/s).
> 2. **Missing "energy" vs "power":** Power is energy per unit time. The kW and kWh are not interchangeable.
> 3. **Wrong sign in energy balance:** Energy leaving the system is positive in the loss column but subtracted in the balance equation.
> 4. **Forgetting to use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** in PE calculations, not 10 m/s².
> 5. **Skipping unit prefixes:** kJ, MJ, GJ all differ by factors of 1 000 — a single missed factor leads to a 1000× numerical error.
> 6. **Improper significant figures:** KTU expects answers to 2 or 3 significant figures, not over-precise decimals.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Energy and Its Various Forms**

### 1. Core Definition
- **Energy = capacity to do work or transfer heat.**
- SI unit: **Joule (J)**; dimensional formula: $[M L^2 T^{-2}]$.

### 2. Seven Principal Forms
- **Mechanical** (KE + PE)
- **Thermal / Heat**
- **Chemical** (in molecular bonds)
- **Electrical** (charge flow)
- **Electromagnetic / Radiant** (photons, $E = h\nu$)
- **Nuclear** ($E = mc^2$)
- **Sound** (longitudinal mechanical waves)

### 3. Master Equations to Memorize
- $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$    $PE = mgh$    $PE_{spring} = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$
- $Q = mc_p\Delta T$    $Q = mL$
- $E_{elec} = VIt$    $P = VI$
- $E = h\nu$    $E = mc^2$

### 4. Master Unit Conversions
- 1 kWh = **3.6 × 10⁶ J**
- 1 cal = **4.184 J**
- 1 BTU = **1 055 J**
- 1 TOE = **4.187 × 10¹⁰ J**
- 1 eV = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J**
- 1 therm = **1.055 × 10⁸ J**

### 5. Industrial Mapping (must memorize)
- Motor: Electrical → Mechanical
- Boiler: Chemical → Thermal
- Generator: Mechanical → Electrical
- Solar PV: Radiant → Electrical
- Compressor: Mechanical → Pressure PE

### 6. First Law of Thermodynamics
$$\Delta U = Q - W \quad \text{(closed system)}$$
$$\sum E_{in} = \sum E_{out} + \sum E_{stored} + \sum E_{losses} \quad \text{(energy audit boundary)}$$

### 7. Typical Numerical Pitfalls
- **Always convert velocity to m/s** before substituting in $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$.
- **Always convert kJ/h to kW** by dividing by 3 600 when energy balance demands kW units.
- **Always use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** unless stated otherwise.
- **Distinguish between GCV and NCV** in combustion problems.
- **Be explicit about whether "heat" refers to sensible, latent, or total enthalpy difference.**

### 8. Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme
This topic underpins **Module 1's learning outcomes**:
- **CO1:** Identify the various forms of energy and the principles of energy conservation.
- **CO2:** Apply energy-balance equations to industrial systems.
It is the foundation on which energy-audit methodologies, efficiency calculations, and the rest of the syllabus (Modules 2–5) are built. A strong grasp here ensures seamless progression to **electrical systems, thermal systems, HVAC audits, and economic analysis** later in the course.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Electricity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basics of Electricity # ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Electricity? (The KTU Formal Definition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > Electricity is the set of physical phenomena associated with the presence and motion of matter that has a property of **electric charge**. In the context of Energy Management and Audit, electricity is the most commercially exploited form of energy, defined by measurable quantities: **Voltage (V)**, **Current (I)**, **Resistance (R)**, **Power (P)**, and **Energy (E)**. In engineering practice, we treat electricity as a *commodity* governed by a set of international SI (Système International) base and derived units. Every energy audit begins with the foundational understanding of these five variables. --- ## 1.2 The Five Pillars of Electrical Quantities | # | Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Defining Equation | Standard Symbol | |---|----------|--------|---------|-------------------|-----------------| | 1 | Voltage (Potential Difference) | $V$ | Volt (V) | $V = W / Q$ | **V** | | 2 | Current | $I$ | Ampere (A) | $I = Q / t$ | **A** | | 3 | Resistance | $R$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | $R = V / I$ | **Ω** | | 4 | Power | $P$ | Watt (W) | $P = V \cdot I$ | **W** | | 5 | Energy | $E$ | Joules (J) / kWh | $E = P \cdot t$ | **J / kWh** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** > The SI unit of charge is the **Coulomb (C)** — defined as the charge transported by a constant current of **1 Ampere** in **1 second**. Always remember this unit in any numerical problem. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Water-Pipe Model 💧 To a beginner, abstract electrical terms are intimidating. Here is the most universally accepted analogy used in every KTU-approved textbook: > [!NOTE] > **The Water-Pipe Analogy (Hydraulic Model of Electricity)** - **Water Flow Rate** in a pipe $\longrightarrow$ **Electric Current (I)** [measured in Amperes] - **Water Pressure** in the pipe $\longrightarrow$ **Voltage (V)** [measured in Volts] - **Pipe narrowness / friction** $\longrightarrow$ **Resistance (R)** [measured in Ohms] - **Power delivered by the water** $\longrightarrow$ **Electrical Power (P)** [measured in Watts] **Intuition:** Just as a high-pressure water source pushing through a narrow pipe produces a powerful jet, a high-voltage source pushing current through a low-resistance circuit produces high power. **Voltage is the "push," current is the "flow," and resistance is the "obstruction."** --- ## 1.4 AC vs DC — The Foundational Distinction > [!IMPORTANT] > **Direct Current (DC):** Current flows in **one constant direction**. Polarity (+ and −) is fixed. Examples: Batteries, Solar PV cells, USB chargers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Alternating Current (AC):** Current reverses direction **periodically**. The standard Indian (and KTU-referenced) supply is **50 Hz** sinusoidal AC with **230 V RMS** domestic and **415 V RMS** three-phase industrial. ``` DC Waveform AC Waveform (50 Hz Sine) ───────────────── ╭─╮ ╭─╮ ╭─╮ │ +V ────────────► │ ╰╮ ╭╯ │ ╭╯ │ │ │ ╰──╯ │ ╰──╯ │ │ (constant magnitude) │ ───────► time │ ───────────────── ╰─╯ ╰─╯ ╰─╯ ``` --- ## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters for Energy Auditing 🎯 An energy auditor measures **every kilowatt-hour** consumed by an industry. Without mastery of the basic electrical quantities, no load survey, no power factor correction, no transformer loss calculation, and no tariff analysis is possible. **This is the alphabet of the entire energy audit syllabus.** # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Ohm's Law — The Cornerstone Equation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ohm's Law (Georg Simon Ohm, 1827):** > At constant temperature, the current flowing through a conductor is **directly proportional** to the potential difference across its ends, provided physical conditions (like temperature) remain constant. ### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown: 1. **The physical setup:** A voltage source $V$ is connected across a conductor of resistance $R$. 2. **The observation:** Doubling $V$ doubles $I$. Tripling $V$ triples $I$. The ratio $V/I$ stays constant. 3. **The mathematical statement:** $V = I \cdot R$ 4. **Boundary condition:** Holds true only for **linear, ohmic** conductors (e.g., constantan, nichrome, metallic resistors). It **fails** for non-linear devices like diodes, transistors, and thermistors. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear in 95% of KTU numerical questions on this module.** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables Explained | SI Units | KTU Frequency | |---|--------------|----------|---------------------|----------|---------------| | 1 | Ohm's Law | $V = I \cdot R$ | V = Voltage, I = Current, R = Resistance | V, A, Ω | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 2 | Electrical Power | $P = V \cdot I$ | P = Power | W (Watt) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 3 | Power (Resistive) | $P = I^{2} \cdot R$ | From substituting V = IR | W | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 4 | Power (Resistive) | $P = \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$ | Alternative form | W | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 5 | Electrical Energy | $E = P \cdot t$ | E = Energy, t = time | J (or kWh) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 6 | Resistivity | $R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$ | ρ = resistivity, L = length, A = cross-section | Ω·m | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 7 | Conductance | $G = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | G = Conductance | Siemens (S) | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 8 | Series Resistance | $R_{s} = R_{1} + R_{2} + R_{3} + \dots$ | Sum of resistances | Ω | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 9 | Parallel Resistance | $\dfrac{1}{R_{p}} = \dfrac{1}{R_{1}} + \dfrac{1}{R_{2}} + \dfrac{1}{R_{3}} + \dots$ | Reciprocal sum | Ω | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 10 | Charge | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Q = Charge | Coulomb (C) | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 11 | Capacitance | $C = \dfrac{Q}{V}$ | C = Capacitance | Farad (F) | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 12 | RMS Value (AC) | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{m}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Vm = Peak/Maximum voltage | V | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 13 | Average Value (AC) | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2 \cdot V_{m}}{\pi}$ | For half sine wave | V | ⭐⭐⭐ | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe Symbol Rule in Tables:** Notice the use of `\vert` or `\mid` notation (e.g., $V \mid$ means the magnitude). This avoids breaking markdown table syntax. Never use `|` inside tables. --- ## 2.3 Series vs Parallel Circuits — Engineering Intuition **Series Connection:** - Same current flows through all components. - Voltages add up: $V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3$ - Resistance adds up: $R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$ - **Analogy:** A single hose with multiple narrow sections in line. - **Used in:** Old Christmas tree lights, fuse chains. **Parallel Connection:** - Same voltage across all components. - Currents add up: $I_{total} = I_1 + I_2 + I_3$ - Reciprocal resistance: $\dfrac{1}{R_{p}} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dfrac{1}{R_3}$ - **Analogy:** A water tank with multiple pipes branching out. - **Used in:** All domestic house wiring (so each appliance gets full 230 V). --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Concept Used | Why It Matters in Industry | |-------------|--------------|----------------------------| | Cable Sizing | $P = I^2 R$ (heat loss) | Prevents cable overheating; lower losses | | Heater Design | $P = V^2/R$ | Higher V or lower R = more heat | | Tariff Calculation | $E = P \cdot t$ | Billed in **kWh** = Units on electricity bill | | Power Factor | $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | Reduces industrial power bills | | Battery Sizing | $Q = I \cdot t$ (Ah rating) | Determines backup duration | # 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Three Power Expressions > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Most Asked Question:** *Derive the three forms of electrical power: $P = VI$, $P = I^{2}R$, and $P = V^{2}/R$.* ### Derivation Steps: **Step 1:** Start with the fundamental definition of electrical power. By definition, **power is the rate of doing work**, or energy delivered per unit time. $$P = \frac{W}{t}$$ **Step 2:** Recall that the work done in moving a charge $Q$ across a potential difference $V$ is: $$W = V \cdot Q$$ **Step 3:** Substitute this expression for $W$ into the power equation: $$P = \frac{V \cdot Q}{t}$$ **Step 4:** Recognize that current is charge per unit time, $I = Q / t$, so $Q/t = I$. Therefore: $$P = V \cdot I \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$ **Step 5:** Apply Ohm's Law ($V = I \cdot R$) to replace $V$ in Equation 1: $$P = (I \cdot R) \cdot I = I^{2} \cdot R \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$ **Step 6:** Alternatively, apply Ohm's Law in the form $I = V/R$ to replace $I$ in Equation 1: $$P = V \cdot \left(\frac{V}{R}\right) = \frac{V^{2}}{R} \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}$$ **Final Summary Box:** $$\boxed{P = VI = I^{2}R = \frac{V^{2}}{R}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Use-Case:** When designing a heating element, an engineer prefers $P = V^2/R$ because the supply voltage is fixed (230 V) and R is the design variable. For cable loss calculations, $P = I^2 R$ is preferred because current is the fixed load quantity. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical 1 — Basic Power & Energy (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A factory uses a **2 kW** electric heater for **6 hours** per day. Calculate: (a) The energy consumed in **kWh** per day. (b) The energy consumed in **Joules** per day. (c) The cost per day if the tariff is **₹7.50 per kWh**. **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Part (a): Energy in kWh** $$E = P \times t$$ $$E = 2 \text{ kW} \times 6 \text{ h}$$ $$E = 12 \text{ kWh}$$ **Part (b): Energy in Joules** We use the conversion: $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ J}$ $$E = 12 \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ J}$$ $$E = 4.32 \times 10^{7} \text{ J}$$ **Part (c): Cost per day** $$\text{Cost} = E \times \text{Tariff} = 12 \text{ kWh} \times ₹7.50/\text{kWh}$$ $$\text{Cost} = ₹90 \text{ per day}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical 2 — Series-Parallel Resistive Network (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** Three resistors $R_1 = 10 \ \Omega$, $R_2 = 20 \ \Omega$, and $R_3 = 30 \ \Omega$ are connected in **parallel**. Find: (a) The equivalent resistance. (b) The total current drawn from a **240 V** DC supply. (c) The power dissipated in each resistor. **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Part (a): Equivalent Resistance** $$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_{1}} + \frac{1}{R_{2}} + \frac{1}{R_{3}}$$ $$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{20} + \frac{1}{30}$$ Compute the common denominator (LCM of 10, 20, 30 = 60): $$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{6}{60} + \frac{3}{60} + \frac{2}{60}$$ $$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{11}{60}$$ $$R_{eq} = \frac{60}{11} \approx 5.45 \ \Omega$$ **Part (b): Total Current** $$I_{total} = \frac{V}{R_{eq}} = \frac{240}{5.4545}$$ $$I_{total} \approx 44.0 \text{ A}$$ **Part (c): Power dissipated in each resistor** Since the resistors are in parallel, each gets the full 240 V. $$P_1 = \frac{V^{2}}{R_1} = \frac{(240)^{2}}{10} = \frac{57600}{10} = 5760 \text{ W}$$ $$P_2 = \frac{V^{2}}{R_2} = \frac{57600}{20} = 2880 \text{ W}$$ $$P_3 = \frac{V^{2}}{R_3} = \frac{57600}{30} = 1920 \text{ W}$$ **Verification (Total Power Check):** $$P_{total} = P_1 + P_2 + P_3 = 5760 + 2880 + 1920 = 10560 \text{ W}$$ Also, $P_{total} = V \cdot I_{total} = 240 \times 44 = 10560 \text{ W}$. ✅ **Checks out.** --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — AC RMS Value Derivation The RMS (Root Mean Square) value of an AC quantity is its **DC equivalent** in terms of heating effect. For a sinusoidal voltage $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$: $$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t) \cdot dt$$ $$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \cdot dt$$ Using the identity $\sin^{2}(\omega t) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$: $$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \cdot dt$$ $$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2T} \left[ t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega} \right]_{0}^{T}$$ $$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2T} \cdot T = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2}$$ $$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \cdot V_m$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A "**230 V AC**" supply is actually **230 V RMS**. Its peak voltage is: $$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 \approx 325.27 \text{ V}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the single most important reason AC is specified in RMS, not peak — because heating, lighting, and motor torque depend on RMS, not peak.** --- ## 3.5 Conversion Table for Energy Units (Frequently Asked) | Quantity | SI Unit | Commercial Unit | Conversion | |----------|---------|-----------------|------------| | Energy | Joule (J) | Kilowatt-hour (kWh) | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ J}$ | | Power | Watt (W) | Horsepower (HP) | $1 \text{ HP} = 746 \text{ W}$ | | Charge | Coulomb (C) | Ampere-hour (Ah) | $1 \text{ Ah} = 3600 \text{ C}$ | | Voltage | Volt (V) | — | — | # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Energy Flow from Source to Load ```mermaid flowchart LR srcA[Electrical Source
Utility Grid or Generator] --> transA[Step-Down Transformer
11kV to 415V] transA --> meterA[Energy Meter
kWh Recording] meterA --> distA[Distribution Panel
MCB MCCB] distA --> loadA[Motor Loads
Inductive] distA --> loadB[Lighting Loads
Resistive] distA --> loadC[Heater Loads
Resistive] loadA -.Power Loss.-> lossA[I²R Copper Loss] loadA -.Power Loss.-> lossB[Core Iron Loss] loadB -.Heat Loss.-> lossC[Radiation Loss] loadC -.Heat Loss.-> lossC lossA --> auditA[Energy Auditor
Identifies Wastage] lossB --> auditA lossC --> auditA ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** The arrows show the power flow direction. Dotted arrows indicate **losses** that an energy auditor targets for reduction. This is the *exact* mental model used in KTU Module 1 to introduce electricity in an auditing context. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Ohm's Law Triangular Memory Aid ```mermaid flowchart TD topV["V
Voltage in Volts"] sideI["I
Current in Amperes"] sideR["R
Resistance in Ohms"] triangle["OHMS LAW TRIANGLE
Cover the Unknown"] topV --- triangle sideI --- triangle sideR --- triangle rule1["If V is covered
V = I × R"] --> triangle rule2["If I is covered
I = V ÷ R"] --> triangle rule3["If R is covered
R = V ÷ I"] --> triangle ``` **How to use:** Place your finger over the unknown variable. The remaining two show the formula. For instance, covering $V$ leaves $I \times R$. --- ## 4.3 Schematic — Series vs Parallel Circuit Topology ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SER["SERIES CIRCUIT"] v1[+] --> r1A[R1] --> r2A[R2] --> r3A[R3] --> n1[-] end subgraph PAR["PARALLEL CIRCUIT"] v2[+] --> nodeA((•)) nodeA --> r1B[R1] --> n2((•)) --> n3[-] nodeA --> r2B[R2] --> n2 nodeA --> r3B[R3] --> n2 end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reading:** > - **Series:** A single current path; if one resistor breaks, the entire circuit fails (open circuit). > - **Parallel:** Multiple current paths; if one resistor breaks, current still flows through the others. This is why **house wiring is parallel.** --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram — The Five Pillars Mapping to KTU Outcomes ```mermaid flowchart TD CO1["CO1: Understand
Electrical Quantities"] --> q1["Voltage V"] CO1 --> q2["Current I"] CO1 --> q3["Resistance R"] CO1 --> q4["Power P"] CO1 --> q5["Energy E"] q1 --> applyA["Energy Audit
Load Survey"] q2 --> applyA q3 --> applyA q4 --> applyA q5 --> applyA applyA --> outA["Outcome:
Reduced kWh Bills"] ``` --- ## 4.5 Diagram — AC Sine Wave Labelled ```mermaid flowchart LR axisX[Time Axis] --> zeroA((0)) zeroA --> peakA["+Vm
Peak Positive"] peakA --> zeroB((0)) zeroB --> peakB["-Vm
Peak Negative"] peakB --> zeroC((0)) peakA -.RMS Level.-> rmsL["0.707 × Vm"] peakA -.Average.-> avgL["0.637 × Vm"] ``` # 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] **State and explain Ohm's Law. Mention its limitations.** **Model Answer:** Ohm's Law states that the current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across its ends, provided physical conditions such as temperature remain constant. $$V = I \cdot R$$ where $V$ = Voltage (V), $I$ = Current (A), $R$ = Resistance (Ω). **Limitations:** 1. Valid only for **linear, ohmic** conductors (metals at constant temperature). 2. Not valid for **non-linear devices** such as diodes, transistors, vacuum tubes. 3. Fails at **very high voltages** (breakdown region). 4. Fails when **temperature varies significantly** during operation. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **Define RMS value of an AC quantity. Why is it preferred over peak value?** **Model Answer:** The **RMS (Root Mean Square)** value of an AC quantity is the square root of the mean of the squares of the instantaneous values over one complete cycle. $$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \cdot V_m$$ **Why RMS is preferred:** - RMS gives the **DC equivalent** in terms of heating effect (or power dissipation). - All AC ammeters, voltmeters, and energy meters are calibrated to read RMS. - Electrical appliances are rated in RMS voltage (e.g., 230 V AC). --- ## 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample Module End Question]** **(a)** Define the following terms with SI units: (i) Voltage, (ii) Current, (iii) Resistance, (iv) Power, (v) Energy. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Three resistors of $10 \ \Omega$, $15 \ \Omega$, and $30 \ \Omega$ are connected in **parallel** across a **120 V** DC supply. Calculate the equivalent resistance, total current, and total power consumed. **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Definitions | Term | Definition | SI Unit | |------|------------|---------| | **Voltage** | Electric potential difference; work done per unit charge to move it between two points. $V = W/Q$ | **Volt (V)** | | **Current** | Rate of flow of electric charge. $I = Q/t$ | **Ampere (A)** | | **Resistance** | Opposition to the flow of current. $R = V/I$ | **Ohm (Ω)** | | **Power** | Rate of doing electrical work. $P = VI$ | **Watt (W)** | | **Energy** | Capacity to do work. $E = P \times t$ | **Joule (J)** or **kWh** | #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Step 1: Equivalent Resistance** $$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{15} + \frac{1}{30}$$ LCM of 10, 15, 30 is 30: $$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{3}{30} + \frac{2}{30} + \frac{1}{30} = \frac{6}{30}$$ $$R_{eq} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \ \Omega$$ **Step 2: Total Current** $$I = \frac{V}{R_{eq}} = \frac{120}{5} = 24 \text{ A}$$ **Step 3: Total Power** $$P = V \cdot I = 120 \times 24 = 2880 \text{ W} = 2.88 \text{ kW}$$ **Verification using $P = V^2/R$:** $$P = \frac{(120)^2}{5} = \frac{14400}{5} = 2880 \text{ W} \ \checkmark$$ --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Derive the three forms of electrical power: $P = VI$, $P = I^2 R$, and $P = V^2 / R$. Mention one engineering application of each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An industry operates for 12 hours per day and consumes an average power of 45 kW. The electricity tariff is ₹8.50 per kWh. Calculate: (i) Daily energy consumption, (ii) Monthly energy consumption, (iii) Monthly electricity bill. **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Derivation **Starting point:** Power is work done per unit time. $$P = \frac{W}{t}$$ Work done moving charge $Q$ through voltage $V$ is $W = VQ$: $$P = \frac{VQ}{t} = V \cdot \frac{Q}{t} = VI$$ **Substituting $V = IR$:** $\Rightarrow P = (IR)(I) = I^2 R$ **Substituting $I = V/R$:** $\Rightarrow P = V \cdot \frac{V}{R} = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | Form | Application | |------|-------------| | $P = VI$ | General billing, meter calculations | | $P = I^2 R$ | Cable $I^2R$ heat loss estimation | | $P = V^2/R$ | Heater element design (fixed supply) | #### Part (b) — Industrial Energy Bill **(i) Daily Energy:** $$E_{day} = P \times t = 45 \text{ kW} \times 12 \text{ h} = 540 \text{ kWh/day}$$ **(ii) Monthly Energy (30 days):** $$E_{month} = 540 \times 30 = 16200 \text{ kWh/month}$$ **(iii) Monthly Bill:** $$\text{Bill} = 16200 \times 8.50 = ₹1,37,700$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert minutes to hours** when time is given in minutes — major 1-mark loss every exam. > 2. **Confusing kW with kWh** — kW is power, kWh is energy. The bill is in **kWh units**. > 3. **Wrong LCM calculation** in parallel resistance problems — practice LCM of 6, 8, 12 vs 10, 15, 30. > 4. **Forgetting the √2 factor** for AC conversions — Indian domestic supply "230 V" is **RMS**, not peak. > 5. **Writing units inconsistently** — never write "amps" in calculations; the SI symbol is **A**. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ⚡ **Five Pillars:** Voltage (V), Current (A), Resistance (Ω), Power (W), Energy (J/kWh). - 🔺 **Ohm's Law:** $V = IR$ — cornerstone of all DC analysis. - 📐 **Three Power Forms:** $P = VI = I^{2}R = V^{2}/R$ — memorize all three. - 🔌 **Series Resistance** adds up directly; **Parallel Resistance** uses reciprocal formula. - 🌊 **Water Analogy:** Voltage = Pressure, Current = Flow, Resistance = Friction. - 〰️ **AC Wave:** Indian supply = **230 V RMS, 50 Hz, Single-Phase**; Industrial = **415 V RMS, 50 Hz, 3-Phase**. - 📊 **RMS Factor:** $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$; **Average Factor:** $V_{avg} = 2V_m / \pi$. - 💰 **Billing Unit:** Electricity bills are in **kWh** = Units. Conversion: **1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J**. - 🐎 **HP Conversion:** **1 HP = 746 W**. - 🔋 **Charge Conversion:** **1 Ah = 3600 C**. - ⚠️ **Ohm's Law Limits:** Linear conductors only — NOT diodes, NOT transistors. - 🏭 **Why It Matters:** Every energy audit starts with measuring these five quantities.

topics.title: Basics of Thermal Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Thermal Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Thermal Energy?

**Thermal Energy** is the internal energy of a thermodynamic system that arises due to the kinetic energy of its constituent molecules (translational, rotational, and vibrational motion) plus any potential energy associated with intermolecular forces. In the context of **Energy Management and Audit**, thermal energy is the most commonly wasted and recovered form of energy in industrial processes, buildings, and power generation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Thermal energy is the portion of a system's total internal energy that is responsible for its temperature. It is the energy that flows between two systems at different temperatures by virtue of a temperature gradient.*

## 1.2 Heat vs. Temperature — The Critical Distinction

A student must never confuse these two terms because the entire Energy Audit methodology hinges on this distinction.

| Property | Heat (Q) | Temperature (T) |
|----------|----------|-----------------|
| Nature | Form of energy in transit | Thermodynamic state property |
| Symbol | Q (in Joules, J) | T (in K, °C, °F, °R) |
| Cause | Temperature difference | Kinetic energy of molecules |
| Direction | Flows from hot → cold | No direction |
| SI Unit | **Joule (J)** | **Kelvin (K)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Audit Insight:** A large volume of hot water at 40 °C may carry *more* thermal energy than a small cup of water at 90 °C. Energy auditors calculate the actual heat content (kJ or kWh) — never just the temperature.

## 1.3 The Three Modes of Heat Transfer

Heat is energy in transit, and it flows by three fundamental mechanisms:

1. **Conduction** — Transfer of heat through a stationary medium (solid, liquid, or gas) by direct molecular contact.
2. **Convection** — Transfer of heat by the bulk movement of a fluid (liquid or gas).
3. **Radiation** — Transfer of heat in the form of electromagnetic waves, requiring no medium.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bucket of Marbles"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine **thermal energy as money in a wallet** and **temperature as a person's mood**.
> - A wealthy miser (high money = high thermal energy) can still be grumpy (low mood = low temperature).
> - A cheerful person with little money (high temperature, low energy) is possible.
> - When a hot body meets a cold body, the "money" (heat) flows from the richer wallet (hotter body) to the poorer one (colder body) until the moods (temperatures) equalize — this is **thermal equilibrium**.

## 1.5 Temperature Scales Used in Energy Audits

The four scales an auditor will encounter are:

- **Celsius (°C)** — Water freezes at 0 °C, boils at 100 °C (SI-derived)
- **Fahrenheit (°F)** — Used in some industrial gauges in the USA
- **Kelvin (K)** — Absolute thermodynamic scale; **0 K = absolute zero**
- **Rankine (°R)** — Absolute English unit scale

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mandatory Conversion:** The SI unit for all thermal energy calculations in the Energy Audit report is the **Joule (J)** or **kilojoule (kJ)**, and temperature must be expressed in **Kelvin (K)** whenever a temperature difference or thermodynamic law is used.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensible Heat (Q_s)

**Sensible heat** is the heat that changes the *temperature* of a substance without changing its *phase* (state). The word "sensible" means "that which can be felt/sensed" because the temperature change is observable with a thermometer.

### Why does this matter?
- Steam generation, boiler feedwater heating, air preheating in furnaces — all involve sensible heat.
- Energy auditors calculate the sensible heat lost through furnace walls, exhaust gases, and hot product streams.

## 2.2 Latent Heat (Q_L)

**Latent heat** is the heat that changes the *phase* of a substance (solid → liquid, liquid → vapor, or vice versa) at constant temperature and pressure. It cannot be sensed by a thermometer because temperature does not change.

- **Latent Heat of Fusion (L_f)** — Solid ↔ Liquid transition
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization (L_v)** — Liquid ↔ Vapor transition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For water at 1 atm: L_f = **334 kJ/kg** and L_v = **2260 kJ/kg**. The latent heat of vaporization is roughly **6.8 times** the latent heat of fusion — a fact critical in evaluating steam systems during an audit.

## 2.3 Specific Heat Capacity (c)

**Specific heat capacity** is the amount of heat required to raise the temperature of **1 kg** of a substance by **1 K** (or 1 °C, since the interval is the same).

- **c_p** — Specific heat at constant pressure (used for most engineering processes)
- **c_v** — Specific heat at constant volume (used in combustion calculations)

Typical values (at 25 °C, 1 atm):

| Substance | c_p (kJ/kg·K) |
|-----------|---------------|
| Water (liquid) | 4.186 |
| Air | 1.005 |
| Steam (vapor) | 1.996 |
| Copper | 0.385 |
| Steel | 0.466 |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | When to Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Temperature conversion (°C → K) | T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15 | K | Any thermodynamic calculation |
| 2 | Temperature conversion (°C ↔ °F) | °F = (9/5)°C + 32 | °F | Reading old gauge dials |
| 3 | Sensible Heat | Q_s = m · c_p · ΔT | kJ | Heating/cooling of a substance |
| 4 | Latent Heat | Q_L = m · L | kJ | Phase change (melting, boiling) |
| 5 | Fourier's Law (Conduction) | Q = (k · A · ΔT) / L | W | Heat through a wall/insulation |
| 6 | Newton's Law (Convection) | Q = h · A · (T_s − T_∞) | W | Heat between surface and fluid |
| 7 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Radiation) | Q = ε · σ · A · T⁴ | W | Heat between radiating surfaces |
| 8 | Heat Flux | q = Q / A | W/m² | Checking insulation performance |
| 9 | Thermal Resistance (conduction) | R_cond = L / (k · A) | K/W | Insulation selection in audits |
| 10 | Thermal Resistance (convection) | R_conv = 1 / (h · A) | K/W | Combined wall analysis |
| 11 | Overall Heat Transfer | Q = U · A · ΔT_lm | W | Heat exchangers, boiler walls |
| 12 | Heat Rate (Power) | P = Q / t | W or kW | Boiler capacity, furnace rating |
| 13 | Combustion Energy | Q_comb = m_fuel · GCV | kJ | Fuel calorific calculations |
| 14 | Energy in kWh | E(kWh) = Q(kJ) / 3600 | kWh | Converting audit results to kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule for Tables:** In the above table, all mathematical operators use the standard inline math form. For exam answers, when you write absolute value (e.g., magnitude of heat $\vert Q \vert$), always use **\vert** or **\mid** — never the bare vertical bar symbol **\vert**, as it breaks KTU's online template parser.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Energy Auditing

- **Conduction analysis** helps auditors evaluate the heat loss through boiler walls, insulated steam pipes, and furnace linings.
- **Convection analysis** is used to quantify heat carried away by exhaust flue gases — often the **largest single loss** in a thermal plant.
- **Radiation analysis** is essential for high-temperature furnaces (> 600 °C) and for evaluating radiation shields in the energy-intensive process industries of Kerala (such as the rubber, ceramics, and cashew sectors).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction

Consider a homogeneous solid wall of thickness **L** and cross-sectional area **A**. Let the two faces of the wall be maintained at steady temperatures **T₁** (hot side) and **T₂** (cold side), with T₁ > T₂.

**Assumptions:**

- Steady-state, one-dimensional heat flow
- Constant thermal conductivity **k**
- No internal heat generation

**Step 1: Experimental observation (Fourier, 1822):**

The rate of heat transfer $\dot{Q}$ through the wall is proportional to the cross-sectional area and the temperature difference, and inversely proportional to the thickness.

$$\dot{Q} \propto \frac{A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

**Step 2: Introducing the proportionality constant — thermal conductivity (k):**

$$\dot{Q} = k \cdot A \cdot \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

**Step 3: Converting to a differential form (gradient at any point x):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q} &= -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates that heat flows from higher to lower temperature (i.e., in the direction of decreasing T).

**Step 4: Defining Heat Flux (q) — the heat transfer per unit area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \frac{\dot{Q}}{A} = -k \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** When a question says "Derive Fourier's law," you must end with the **vector form**:
> $$\vec{q} = -k \cdot \nabla T$$
> This shows heat flux is proportional to the temperature gradient — a 3-D generalization of Step 3.

## 3.2 Derivation of Newton's Law of Cooling (Convection)

Consider a hot solid surface of temperature **T_s** losing heat to a surrounding fluid of bulk temperature **T_∞**, with T_s > T_∞.

**Step 1: Experimental observation (Newton, 1701):**

The convective heat transfer rate is proportional to the surface area and the temperature difference between the surface and the fluid.

$$\dot{Q} \propto A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

**Step 2: Introducing the convective heat transfer coefficient (h):**

$$\dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

**Step 3: Defining the convective resistance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{conv} &= \frac{1}{h \cdot A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse h with enthalpy!** The same symbol 'h' is used in thermodynamics for specific enthalpy. In an exam, if the question is on convection, treat h = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K). If it is on thermodynamics, h = specific enthalpy (kJ/kg). Read the units carefully.

## 3.3 Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Radiation)

A perfect emitter (black body) radiates energy in all directions. Josef Stefan (1879) experimentally discovered the relationship, and Ludwig Boltzmann provided the theoretical derivation using thermodynamics and Maxwell's equations.

**Step 1: Experimental result (Stefan):**

The total radiant energy emitted per unit time per unit area is proportional to the fourth power of the absolute temperature.

$$E_b \propto T^4$$

**Step 2: Introducing the Stefan-Boltzmann constant (σ):**

$$E_b = \sigma \cdot T^4$$

**Step 3: For a real (gray) surface with emissivity (ε):**

$$E = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^4$$

**Step 4: Net radiation heat transfer between two surfaces:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{rad} &= \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical value:** σ = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of the Three Modes

| Feature | Conduction | Convection | Radiation |
|---------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Medium required | Yes (solid best) | Yes (fluid) | **No (vacuum OK)** |
| Driving force | Temperature gradient (dT/dx) | Temperature difference (T_s − T_∞) | T⁴ difference |
| Governing law | Fourier's Law | Newton's Law of Cooling | Stefan-Boltzmann Law |
| Governing variable | Thermal conductivity **k** (W/m·K) | Heat transfer coefficient **h** (W/m²·K) | Emissivity **ε** + σ |
| Typical magnitude in industry | Insulated pipes: 5–50 W/m² | Exhaust gases: 1000–10,000 W/m² | Furnace walls > 600 °C: very high |
| Audit application | Insulation thickness check | Flue gas loss (stack loss) | Re-radiation from furnace walls |

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** 500 kg of water is to be heated from 30 °C to 90 °C in a boiler. The specific heat capacity of water is 4.186 kJ/kg·K. Calculate:
(a) The sensible heat required in kJ.
(b) The same heat expressed in kWh.
(c) The time required if a 25 kW electric heater is used (assume no losses).

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_s &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 500 \text{ kg} \times 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \times (90 - 30) \text{ K} \\
&= 500 \times 4.186 \times 60 \\
&= 125{,}580 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{Q_s}{3600} \\
&= \frac{125{,}580}{3600} \\
&\approx 34.88 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{E}{P} = \frac{Q_s}{P \times 3600} \\
&= \frac{125{,}580}{25 \times 3600} \\
&= \frac{125{,}580}{90{,}000} \\
&\approx 1.395 \text{ hours} \approx 1 \text{ h } 23.7 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Thermal Energy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[THERMAL ENERGY IN A SYSTEM] --> B[INTERNAL ENERGY OF MOLECULES]
    A --> C[TRANSFERRED AS HEAT]
    B --> D[Translational KE]
    B --> E[Rotational KE]
    B --> F[Vibrational KE]
    B --> G[Intermolecular PE]
    C --> H[Mode 1: Conduction]
    C --> I[Mode 2: Convection]
    C --> J[Mode 3: Radiation]
    H --> H1[Fouriers Law Governed]
    H --> H2[Via Molecular Collisions]
    I --> I1[Newtons Law Governed]
    I --> I2[Via Fluid Bulk Motion]
    J --> J1[Stefan-Boltzmann Law]
    J --> J2[EM Waves No Medium Needed]
    H1 --> K[Quantified by k]
    I1 --> L[Quantified by h]
    J1 --> M[Quantified by epsilon and sigma]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Heat Flow Through a Composite Boiler Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FLAME[Hot Combustion Gas at Tg] -->|Convection| WALL1[Inner Wall Surface Ts1]
    WALL1 -->|Conduction| INSUL[Insulation Layer]
    INSUL -->|Conduction| WALL2[Outer Wall Surface Ts2]
    WALL2 -->|Convection + Radiation| AMB[Ambient Air at Tinf]
    R1[Resistance 1 over hg] --- WALL1
    R2[Resistance 2 over kA by L] --- INSUL
    R3[Resistance 3 over hoA] --- WALL2
    Q[Net Heat Loss Q dot] --> R1
    Q --> R2
    Q --> R3
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Process Flow — Energy Audit Heat Loss Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[FUEL INPUT with GCV] --> B[BOILER / FURNACE]
    B --> P1[Useful Steam Output]
    B --> P2[Stack Loss via Flue Gas]
    B --> P3[Wall Loss via Conduction]
    B --> P4[Radiation Loss]
    B --> P5[Blowdown Loss]
    P2 --> M1[Sensible Heat of Dry Flue Gas]
    P2 --> M2[Latent Heat of Moisture]
    P2 --> M3[Incomplete Combustion Loss]
    P3 --> W1[Conduction through Insulation]
    P4 --> W2[Re-radiation from Hot Surfaces]
    AUDIT[Energy Auditor Quantifies Each Path in kJ or kWh] --> P1
    AUDIT --> P2
    AUDIT --> P3
    AUDIT --> P4
    AUDIT --> P5
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in the exam:** The auditor's task is to **close the energy balance**:
> **Fuel Input = Useful Output + All Losses**.
> Every box in the right-hand branch (P2, P3, P4, P5) is a candidate for energy conservation, and each one traces back to a fundamental mode of heat transfer covered above.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between sensible heat and latent heat. Give one industrial example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sensible Heat (Q_s) | Latent Heat (Q_L) |
|--------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Heat that causes a change in *temperature* without change of phase | Heat that causes a change in *phase* at constant temperature |
| Formula | Q_s = m · c_p · ΔT | Q_L = m · L |
| Effect on thermometer | Reading changes | Reading does not change |
| Industrial example | Heating of boiler feedwater from 30 °C to 90 °C | Conversion of water at 100 °C to steam at 100 °C |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the correct industrial example.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**State Fourier's law of heat conduction. Explain the significance of the negative sign in the equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Fourier's law states that the rate of heat conduction through a homogeneous solid is proportional to the cross-sectional area, the temperature gradient, and the thermal conductivity of the material.

$$\dot{Q} = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

The negative sign indicates that heat flows in the **direction of decreasing temperature** — i.e., from the hot side to the cold side. It ensures that when T decreases with increasing x, the resulting $\dot{Q}$ is positive (heat flows in the +x direction).

*(2 marks for statement + equation, 1 mark for the negative sign explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive Fourier's law of heat conduction. Define thermal conductivity and state its unit. Explain why the thermal conductivity of a good insulator should be low.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A furnace wall consists of 0.20 m thick firebrick (k = 1.04 W/m·K) lined with 0.10 m thick insulating brick (k = 0.18 W/m·K). The inner surface temperature is 1200 °C and the outer surface temperature is 60 °C. Calculate:
(i) the rate of heat loss per m² of wall area, and
(ii) the temperature at the interface between the two bricks.

Assume steady, one-dimensional conduction with no heat generation.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation of Fourier's Law:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full derivation. The key steps an examiner expects:

1. Setup of the wall geometry and the assumption of steady, one-dimensional flow.
2. Experimental proportionality statement.
3. Introduction of the constant k.
4. Differential form.
5. Final boxed equation with the negative sign.

**Definition of Thermal Conductivity (k):**

It is defined as the rate of heat flow per unit area per unit temperature gradient. Its SI unit is **W/m·K** (or W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹).

**Why an insulator must have low k:**

For a given wall thickness, area, and temperature difference, $\dot{Q} = kA\Delta T / L$. A small k means a small heat loss. Hence insulators (rockwool, glass wool, aerogel) are designed to have k values in the range 0.02–0.04 W/m·K, much lower than metals (copper k ≈ 400 W/m·K).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- Firebrick: L₁ = 0.20 m, k₁ = 1.04 W/m·K
- Insulating brick: L₂ = 0.10 m, k₂ = 0.18 W/m·K
- T₁ = 1200 °C, T₃ = 60 °C
- Steady 1-D conduction, A = 1 m² (per unit area)

**Step 1 — Heat flux through firebrick (per m² area):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_1 &= \frac{k_1 \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L_1} = \frac{1.04 \cdot (1200 - T_2)}{0.20}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat flux through insulating brick:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_2 &= \frac{k_2 \cdot (T_2 - T_3)}{L_2} = \frac{0.18 \cdot (T_2 - 60)}{0.10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — By continuity (series conduction), q₁ = q₂ = q:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1.04 \cdot (1200 - T_2)}{0.20} &= \frac{0.18 \cdot (T_2 - 60)}{0.10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for T₂:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
5.2 \cdot (1200 - T_2) &= 1.8 \cdot (T_2 - 60) \\
6240 - 5.2 T_2 &= 1.8 T_2 - 108 \\
6240 + 108 &= 5.2 T_2 + 1.8 T_2 \\
6348 &= 7.0 T_2 \\
T_2 &= 906.86 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute q (per m² area):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \frac{1.04 \cdot (1200 - 906.86)}{0.20} \\
&= \frac{1.04 \cdot 293.14}{0.20} \\
&= \frac{304.87}{0.20} \\
&\approx 1524.3 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using the second layer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \frac{0.18 \cdot (906.86 - 60)}{0.10} = \frac{0.18 \cdot 846.86}{0.10} = 1524.3 \text{ W/m}^2 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
(i) Heat loss rate ≈ **1524.3 W per m² of wall area**.
(ii) Interface temperature T₂ ≈ **906.9 °C**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A, Part b):**
> - Do not add the resistances incorrectly. For series conduction, **R_total = R₁ + R₂**, not the average. A common student error is to take the arithmetic mean of conductivities.
> - Do not forget the **continuity condition** q₁ = q₂. Without this, the problem is unsolvable.
> - Temperature is in **°C**, but since it is a *difference*, the same numerical value works in K. However, for radiation problems, you **must** convert to Kelvin.
> - Always **box the final answer** with units — KTU deducts 0.5 mark for missing units.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three modes of heat transfer with neat sketches. State the governing law and one industrial application of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A boiler burns furnace oil (GCV = 42,000 kJ/kg) at a rate of 100 kg/hr. The flue gas leaves at 280 °C and the combustion air enters at 30 °C. The specific heat of flue gas is 1.05 kJ/kg·K. If the air-fuel ratio is 16:1 by mass, calculate:
(i) the rate of heat loss through the dry flue gas per hour in kJ,
(ii) the percentage of heat input lost in the dry flue gas.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Three Modes of Heat Transfer — Explanations:**

1. **Conduction:** Transfer of heat through a solid (or stationary fluid) by direct molecular vibration and electron movement. Governed by **Fourier's Law**. *Application: Insulation of steam pipelines.*

2. **Convection:** Transfer of heat by the actual bulk movement of a heated fluid (forced or natural). Governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**. *Application: Heat exchangers in a power plant, preheating of combustion air.*

3. **Radiation:** Transfer of heat by electromagnetic waves emitted by a body due to its temperature. Governed by **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**. *Application: Re-radiation losses from furnace walls in a re-rolling mill.*

*(Sketches in the exam may be simple line drawings — a wall with heat arrows for conduction, a heated plate with fluid flow for convection, and a hot body emitting wavy arrows for radiation.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- GCV of oil = 42,000 kJ/kg
- Fuel consumption = 100 kg/hr
- Flue gas temperature T₂ = 280 °C
- Reference (combustion air) temperature T₁ = 30 °C
- Specific heat of flue gas c_pg = 1.05 kJ/kg·K
- Air-fuel ratio (by mass) = 16:1

**Step 1 — Heat Input (Q_in) per hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{in} &= m_{fuel} \cdot GCV \\
&= 100 \text{ kg/hr} \times 42{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
&= 4{,}200{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of dry flue gas per hour (air + fuel):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{fg} &= m_{fuel} + m_{air} \\
&= 100 + (16 \times 100) \\
&= 1700 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat carried away by dry flue gas (Q_loss):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{loss} &= m_{fg} \cdot c_{pg} \cdot (T_2 - T_1) \\
&= 1700 \times 1.05 \times (280 - 30) \\
&= 1700 \times 1.05 \times 250 \\
&= 446{,}250 \text{ kJ/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage heat loss in dry flue gas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Loss} &= \frac{Q_{loss}}{Q_{in}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{446{,}250}{4{,}200{,}000} \times 100 \\
&\approx 10.625 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
(i) Heat loss in dry flue gas ≈ **4,46,250 kJ/hr**.
(ii) Percentage loss ≈ **10.63 %** of the input energy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B, Part b):**
> - Students often write the air-fuel ratio as **kg of air per kg of fuel** but forget to add the fuel mass when computing the total flue gas mass. The mass of **dry** flue gas = (mass of air) + (mass of fuel) − (mass of moisture in fuel). For oil, the moisture is negligible, so it is simply m_air + m_fuel.
> - The reference temperature for ΔT is the **combustion air inlet temperature**, not 0 °C or 25 °C. Always read the problem carefully.
> - For the percentage loss calculation, **multiply by 100**, not 1.0. A common slip costs 1 mark.
> - In a real audit, this 10.63 % is a *floor value*; you must also account for **moisture loss** (latent heat of water in fuel and combustion) — but for this 14-mark question, the dry gas loss alone is sufficient.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thermal energy** is the kinetic and potential energy of molecules. **Heat** is energy in transit due to a temperature difference.
- **Temperature** is an intensive property (does not depend on mass); **Heat** is an extensive property (scales with mass).
- The **three modes** of heat transfer are conduction, convection, and radiation — each with a distinct governing law.
- **Fourier's Law:** $\dot{Q} = -kA \cdot dT/dx$ → unit of k is W/m·K.
- **Newton's Law of Cooling:** $\dot{Q} = hA(T_s − T_∞)$ → unit of h is W/m²·K.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $\dot{Q} = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ → σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴.
- **Sensible heat** Q_s = m·c_p·ΔT; **Latent heat** Q_L = m·L.
- **Absolute temperature** must be in **Kelvin** (K = °C + 273.15) for any radiation or thermodynamic calculation.
- The **largest single thermal loss** in most industrial boilers is the **stack loss** (sensible heat of dry flue gas + latent heat of moisture).
- For **series conduction**, total resistance adds: R_total = L₁/(k₁A) + L₂/(k₂A) + …; heat flux q = ΔT/R_total.
- Always **box the final answer with units** — KTU deducts marks for missing units.
- The energy balance principle: **Fuel Energy Input = Useful Output + All Losses** — this is the foundation of every Energy Audit report.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electricity Act 2003 - I
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electricity Act 2003 - I

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ ELECTRICITY ACT 2003 - PART I: FOUNDATIONS & KEY PROVISIONS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

The **Electricity Act, 2003** (Act No. 36 of 2003) is the foundational umbrella legislation enacted by the Parliament of India that **consolidated the laws relating to generation, transmission, distribution, trading, and use of electricity**. It repealed three earlier statutes — the **Indian Electricity Act, 1910**, the **Electricity (Supply) Act, 1948**, and the the **Electricity Regulatory Commissions Act, 1998** — and introduced a unified, competition-oriented framework for the Indian power sector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Act came into force on **10th June 2003**. It extends to the whole of India and applies to all categories of electrical energy, including captive generation, non-conventional energy sources, and dedicated networks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **Electricity Act, 2003** as the **"Constitution of the Indian Power Sector"**.

Just as a constitution sets the fundamental rules for a country, this Act:
- Defines who can **generate** power (the "producers")
- Defines who can **move** power across the country (the "transporters")
- Defines who can **sell** power to end-users (the "retailers")
- Defines who **watches over** the rules (the "judges" — regulators)
- Defines the **punishments** for breaking the rules (penalties & offences)

**Real-world analogy:** Before 2003, the power sector was like a single company owning the entire highway system, the trucks, and the petrol stations — a monopoly. The 2003 Act is like **breaking up that monopoly**, allowing multiple private trucks (generators), toll-free highways (open access), and independent fuel stations (distribution licensees) — all supervised by a neutral traffic police (the Electricity Regulatory Commissions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Tagline of the Act:** *"One Act, One License, One Forum"* — a single regulatory framework replacing the earlier fragmented multi-licence regime.

## 1.3 Salient Features of the Act

| Sl. | Feature | Description |
|-----|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Single legislation** | Replaces three pre-existing Acts |
| 2 | **Deregulation of generation** | No licence required for generation (except for nuclear) |
| 3 | **Open access** | Non-discriminatory access to transmission & distribution networks |
| 4 | **Multi-year tariff** | Introduction of Multi-Year Tariff (MYT) framework |
| 5 | **Mandatory SERCs** | Establishment of State Electricity Regulatory Commissions |
| 6 | **Trading as a licensed activity** | Recognises electricity trading as a separate activity |
| 7 | **Consumer rights** | Strengthening of consumer grievance redressal mechanisms |
| 8 | **Rural electrification** | Mandates supply to rural areas as a Universal Service Obligation |
| 9 | **Captive power** | Liberalised framework for captive generating plants |
| 10 | **National Electricity Policy** | Section 3 mandates formulation of a National Electricity Policy |

## 1.4 Statement of Objects and Reasons (Why the Act was Enacted)

The Act was enacted to:
- ✅ Create a **competitive market** for electricity
- ✅ Protect the interests of **consumers and suppliers**
- ✅ Ensure **rationalisation of tariffs**
- ✅ Promote **efficiency** and **environmental friendly** policies
- ✅ Establish an **independent regulatory regime**
- ✅ Consolidate and harmonise the **legal framework** governing the power sector

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Favourite):** The Act contains **18 Chapters and 185 Sections** in total. Part I of the syllabus typically covers the introductory provisions, definitions, and national policy framework (Sections 1–13).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS: STRUCTURE & FUNCTIONAL FRAMEWORK

## 2.1 Chapter-Wise Architectural Breakdown of the Act

The Electricity Act, 2003 is systematically divided into **18 Chapters**. For Module 2 (Part I), the following chapters are critical:

| Chapter No. | Chapter Title | KTU Significance |
|:-----------:|---------------|------------------|
| **I** | Preliminary (Definitions) | Defines all key terminology — Section 2 |
| **II** | National Electricity Policy & Plan | Sections 3–4 |
| **III** | Generation of Electricity | Sections 7–11 |
| **IV** | Transmission of Electricity | Sections 12–15 |
| **V** | Distribution of Electricity | Sections 16–27 |
| **VI** | Trading | Section 12 (inserted) |
| **VII** | Regulatory Commissions | CERC & SERC |
| **VIII** | Appellate Tribunal | Sections 110–117 |
| **IX-XVIII** | Other provisions | Investigation, offences, etc. |

## 2.2 Key Definitions (Section 2 — Definitions)

These are **examination-critical** and must be memorised precisely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Tip:** Definitions are the most frequently asked Part-A (3-mark) questions. Learn them **verbatim**.

- **"Appropriate Commission"** *(Section 2(1))*: The Central Regulatory Commission (CERC) for inter-state matters, and the State Commission (SERC) for intra-state matters.
- **"Captive Generating Plant"** *(Section 2(8))*: A power plant set up by any person **to generate electricity primarily for his own use**, satisfying the criteria of **not less than 26% ownership by the captive user(s)** in the equity of the plant.
- **"Central Electricity Authority" (CEA)** *(Section 2(4))*: The authority established under Section 70 (formerly under Section 3 of the Electricity (Supply) Act, 1948).
- **"Distribution"** *(Section 2(19))*: The supply of electricity by a distribution licensee to consumers, including the wheeling of electricity.
- **"Electricity"** *(Section 2(22))*: Electrical energy generated, transmitted, supplied or used for any purpose.
- **"Electricity Trader"** *(Section 2(26))*: A person who has been granted a licence to undertake trading in electricity.
- **"Generating Company"** *(Section 2(28))*: A company engaged in the business of generating electricity.
- **"Licensee"** *(Section 2(39))*: A person who has been granted a licence under Section 14 of the Act.
- **"Open Access"** *(Section 2(47))*: The non-discriminatory provision for the use of transmission lines or distribution system or associated facilities by any licensee or consumer or a person engaged in generation.
- **"Transmission"** *(Section 2(72))*: The conveyance of electricity from a generating station to a sub-station or between substations, including raising or lowering of voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition for "Captive Power":** At least **51% of the aggregate electricity generated** must be consumed by the captive users (this is the 51% consumption rule), and captive users must hold **not less than 26% of the equity**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Conceptual Mapping)

| Concept | Legal Provision | Outcome |
|---------|----------------|---------|
| Generation Licence | **NOT required** | Any person can generate |
| Transmission Licence | **Required** for interstate (CERC) and intrastate (SERC) | Under Section 14 |
| Distribution Licence | **Required** for supply to consumers | Under Section 14 |
| Trading Licence | **Required** | Section 12(inserted) / conditions by SERC |
| Captive Power | **No licence** but must meet Section 2(8) conditions | Exempted from licence |
| NCE / Renewables | **No licence** for generation, but must notify grid codes | Encouraged |
| Cross-border Trade | Allowed under Section 24 (Inter-Governmental agreements) | Regulated by CERC |
| Open Access | **Mandatory** for transmission & distribution | Non-discriminatory |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

This section's legal architecture directly enables the following **practical engineering utilities**:
- A **manufacturing plant** can set up its own captive cogeneration unit (Section 2(8)) — used in sugar mills, paper mills, cement plants.
- An **independent power producer (IPP)** can sell power to multiple distribution companies without owning a wire network (open access).
- A **renewable energy developer** (solar, wind) can set up a plant without a licence and wheel power through state grids.
- A **state electricity board** can be unbundled into separate Generation, Transmission, and Distribution companies.

## 2.5 Functional Principles of the Act

The Act rests on **six core functional principles** (KTU Board frequently tests these):

1. **De-licensing of Generation** — Removes entry barriers for power generation
2. **Licensing of Transmission & Distribution** — Maintains control over the wire business
3. **Open Access** — Promotes competition in the supply business
4. **Independent Regulation** — Insulates tariff-setting from political interference
5. **Consumer Choice** — Allows consumers >1 MW to choose supplier (initially)
6. **Universal Service Obligation** — Mandates electricity to all areas including rural
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔍 STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK: KEY PROVISIONS OF THE ACT

> *Note: This topic is legal/regulatory in nature. The "derivations" below are structured logical analyses of the Act's provisions, mapped to KTU cognitive expectations.*

## 3.1 Logical Analysis: Section 3 — National Electricity Policy

**Step 1: Legislative Trigger**
The Central Government is mandated under Section 3(1) to formulate the **National Electricity Policy (NEP)**.

**Step 2: Guiding Principles (Section 3(1))**
The NEP must, among other things, provide for:
- **Rural Electrification** — access to electricity to all households by 2012 (original target)
- **Availability** of electricity to all consumers in an efficient manner
- **Generation capacity** to meet anticipated demand
- **Energy efficiency** and its conservation
- **Disposal of waste** from power plants (fly ash, etc.)
- **Promotion of cogeneration** and generation of electricity from non-conventional sources
- **Protection of consumers' interests** and other matters

**Step 3: Statutory Review Cycle**
The NEP is to be reviewed/upgraded **at least once in five years**.

**Conversion Logic:** Section 3 establishes a *policy top-down framework* — the Central Government (policy) → CEA (planning) → CERC/SERC (regulation) → Licensees (implementation).

## 3.2 Logical Analysis: Section 7 — De-licensing of Generation

**Step 1: Statutory Provision**
Subject to the provisions of this Act, **any person** may establish, operate, and maintain a **generating station** without obtaining a licence.

**Step 2: Constitutional Exception**
The above is applicable **notwithstanding** anything contained in Section 12 of the Electricity (Supply) Act, 1948, or any other law for the time being in force.

**Step 3: Mandatory Preconditions (Section 7(1A) inserted later)**
- Must comply with **technical standards** relating to grid connectivity laid down by the Central Electricity Authority (CEA).
- Must comply with the **norms of grid security** and the State Grid Code issued under Section 86(1)(h).

**Conversion Logic:** De-licensing ≠ Deregulation. Generation is de-licensed, but **grid safety, technical standards, and environmental clearances** must still be met.

> [!IMPORTANT]
