Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Integrated Energy Policy – II The IEP-II organizes its recommendations around **five strategic pillars**. Each pillar is dissected below with its *operational logic* and *real-world utility*. #### Pillar 1 – Sectoral Energy Efficiency Mandate - **Why:** Industry consumes ~35-40% of commercial energy; buildings consume another ~30%. - **How:** - **Perform, Achieve & Trade (PAT)** scheme for energy-intensive industries. - **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** for commercial buildings. - **Standards & Labeling (S&L)** programme for appliances (star rating 1–5). - **Ujala** (LED distribution) for household lighting. - **Real-world utility:** Saves ~5,000 MW of equivalent avoided capacity; reduces ~25 million tonnes of CO₂/year. #### Pillar 2 – Pricing Reform & Subsidy Rationalization - **Why:** Cross-subsidies (industrial users paying higher tariffs to subsidize agriculture/domestic) distort markets. - **How:** - Phased **deregulation of diesel prices** (achieved in October 2014). - **DBT for LPG** (PAHAL) to eliminate duplicate/ghost connections. - **Targeted subsidy** for electricity via Direct Benefit Transfer of Electricity (DBT-E). - **KUSUM scheme** for solarizing agricultural pumps. - **Formula used:** $$ \text{True Subsidy} = (\text{Cost of Supply} - \text{Tariff}) \times \text{Units Consumed} $$ #### Pillar 3 – Renewable Energy Integration (RPO Framework) - **Why:** India committed to **500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030** at COP-26. - **How:** - **Solar:** National Solar Mission (JNNSM) with target 100 GW by 2022, later raised. - **Wind:** Wind energy auctions, repowering policy. - **Biomass:** SATAT scheme for Compressed Biogas. - **Green Hydrogen:** National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023). - **RPO formula:** $$ \text{RPO Obligation (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Renewable Energy Purchase (MWh)}}{\text{Total Energy Purchase (MWh)}} \times 100 $$ #### Pillar 4 – Technology R&D and Indigenous Manufacturing - **Why:** Energy transition cannot be imported; it must be made in India. - **How:** - **SECI** (Solar Energy Corporation of India) and **NETRA** (NTPC Energy Technology Research Alliance). - **PLI (Production-Linked Incentive) scheme** for solar PV manufacturing (₹45,000 crore). - **National Hydrogen Energy Mission** and **Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC) PLI** for batteries. - **Strategic outcome:** Reduce import dependence on solar cells (currently >80% imported) and lithium-ion cells (~100% imported). #### Pillar 5 – Institutional & Governance Reform - **Why:** Fragmented governance across ministries (Power, Coal, New & Renewable Energy, Petroleum) leads to sub-optimal decisions. - **How:** - Creation of a **National Energy Council (NEC)** — proposed but pending. - **Single-window clearance** for energy projects. - **Open Access** policy for renewable power on the electricity grid. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates the **key quantitative tools** used in IEP-II analysis. These are the most frequently tested formulas in KTU 2024 scheme exams. | Sl. No. | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Typical Value | |:-:|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{TOE} = 42 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}_{\text{th}}$ | Tonnes of Oil Equivalent | Primary energy unit | | 2 | $\text{PEC} = \dfrac{\text{TPES}}{\text{Population}}$ | Per Capita Energy Consumption | BOE / capita | | 3 | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{\text{TPES}}{\text{GDP (PPP)}}$ | Energy Intensity of GDP | TOE / million USD | | 4 | $\text{Carbon Intensity (CI)} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2}{\text{GDP (PPP)}}$ | CO₂ emitted per unit GDP | kg CO₂ / USD | | 5 | $\text{RPO (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{RE Purchase (MWh)}}{\text{Total Purchase (MWh)}} \times 100$ | Renewable Purchase Obligation | % of total | | 6 | $\text{Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)} = \dfrac{\text{Energy Used}}{\text{Output}}$ | Plant-level efficiency metric | kWh / tonne or TOE / unit | | 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Energy Input}} \times 100$ | Efficiency | % (0 to 100) | | 8 | $\text{Energy Saved} = \text{Base Year Energy} - \text{Current Year Energy}$ | PAT scheme savings | TOE or MTOE | | 9 | $\text{Simple Payback (years)} = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | DSM economics | Years | | 10 | $\text{Power Density} = \dfrac{\text{Power (W)}}{\text{Area (m}^2\text{)}}$ | Solar panel sizing | W/m² (peak sun: $\sim 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$) | | 11 | $\text{Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Generation (kWh)}}{8760 \times \text{Installed Capacity (kW)}} \times 100$ | Plant performance | % (Solar: 18-25%, Wind: 30-45%) | | 12 | $\text{Heat Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Heat Input (kCal)}}{\text{Energy Output (kWh)}}$ | Thermal power plant efficiency | kCal/kWh (best: ~1900) | ### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility of IEP-II The IEP-II is **not merely a policy document** — it is the **operational blueprint** for: - **Power sector planners** deciding the generation mix for 2030 and beyond. - **Auditors and ESCOs** identifying PAT-mandated savings opportunities. - **Renewable developers** sizing projects to meet RPO obligations. - **Industrial energy managers** benchmarking SEC against BEE's sectoral norms. - **State Electricity Regulatory Commissions (SERCs)** designing tariff structures to balance **financial viability** with **equity**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Pattern Tip:** When asked *"List the salient features of IEP"*, always answer in terms of the **5 pillars above** + mention the **target of 4× per-capita energy by 2031-32**. Examiners specifically look for this numerical anchor. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Per Capita Energy Demand Projection under IEP The IEP uses a **disaggregated end-use method** to project future energy demand. The standard formulation is: $$ D(t) = P(t) \times \text{SPEC}(t) \times \dfrac{1}{E_\text{primary}}(t) $$ Where: - $D(t)$ = total primary energy demand in year $t$ (in MTOE). - $P(t)$ = population in year $t$ (in millions). - $\text{SPEC}(t)$ = specific energy per capita in year $t$ (in BOE/capita). - $E_\text{primary}(t)$ = average calorific value of primary energy mix (in TOE/BOE, often taken as 1 for simplification). **Step-by-step derivation of the 4× per-capita target:** > **Step 1 – Base year benchmarking (2005-06):** > Population of India in 2005-06: $P_0 \approx 1140 \text{ million}$ > Total Primary Energy Supply (TPES) in 2005-06: $D_0 \approx 425 \text{ MTOE}$ **Step 2 – Compute base year per-capita energy:** $$ \text{PEC}_0 = \dfrac{D_0}{P_0} = \dfrac{425 \text{ MTOE}}{1140 \text{ million}} = 0.373 \text{ TOE / capita} $$ **Step 3 – Apply population growth for target year 2031-32:** Using UN projections, India's population in 2031-32 is expected to be: $$ P_t \approx 1500 \text{ million} $$ **Step 4 – Set the IEP's "4× growth in per-capita" target:** $$ \text{PEC}_t = 4 \times \text{PEC}_0 = 4 \times 0.373 = 1.492 \text{ TOE / capita} $$ **Step 5 – Project total demand in 2031-32:** $$ D_t = P_t \times \text{PEC}_t = 1500 \times 1.492 = 2238 \text{ MTOE} $$ **Step 6 – Therefore:** $$ \dfrac{D_t}{D_0} = \dfrac{2238}{425} \approx 5.27 $$ > This implies that India's **primary energy demand must grow ~5.27 times by 2031-32** to achieve a 4× per-capita standard of living improvement. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Savings Calculation under the PAT Scheme The **PAT (Perform, Achieve & Trade)** scheme works on the principle of **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** reduction. **Step 1 – Define SEC:** $$ \text{SEC} = \dfrac{\text{Energy Consumed in a sector (TOE)}}{\text{Sector Output (e.g., tonnes of steel or kWh of power)}} $$ **Step 2 – Define Normalized SEC (NSEC) for cross-fairness:** $$ \text{NSEC} = \dfrac{\text{SEC}_{\text{plant}}}{\text{Plant Capacity}} $$ **Step 3 – Compute baseline energy consumption:** $$ E_{\text{baseline}} = \text{SEC}_{\text{baseline}} \times \text{Output}_{\text{baseline}} $$ **Step 4 – Compute target energy consumption after 3-year cycle:** $$ E_{\text{target}} = \text{SEC}_{\text{target}} \times \text{Output}_{\text{target}} $$ **Step 5 – Compute energy saved (in TOE):** $$ E_{\text{saved}} = E_{\text{baseline}} - E_{\text{target}} $$ **Step 6 – Convert savings to Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts):** $$ 1 \text{ ESCert} = 1 \text{ TOE of oil equivalent saved} $$ > **Numerical example:** A thermal power plant has $\text{SEC}_{\text{baseline}} = 0.30 \text{ TOE/MWh}$. Its target after 3 years is $0.27 \text{ TOE/MWh}$. Annual generation = $1,000,000 \text{ MWh}$. > > $E_{\text{baseline}} = 0.30 \times 10^6 = 300,000 \text{ TOE}$ > > $E_{\text{target}} = 0.27 \times 10^6 = 270,000 \text{TOE}$ > > $E_{\text{saved}} = 30,000 \text{ TOE} = 30,000 \text{ ESCerts}$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF) for Solar Power Plant A frequently asked calculation in KTU exams related to renewable energy. **Step 1 – Define installed capacity and operating period:** A solar plant has installed capacity $C = 10 \text{ MW}$. It generates actual energy $E_{\text{actual}} = 17,520,000 \text{ kWh}$ in a year. **Step 2 – Compute maximum possible generation (24 × 365 = 8760 hours):** $$ E_{\text{max}} = C \times 8760 = 10{,}000 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \text{ h} = 87{,}600{,}000 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step 3 – Compute CUF:** $$ \text{CUF (\%)} = \dfrac{E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\text{max}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{17{,}520{,}000}{87{,}600{,}000} \times 100 $$ **Step 4 – Evaluate:** $$ \text{CUF} = 0.20 \times 100 = 20\% $$ > **Interpretation:** A CUF of 20% is **typical for solar PV in India**; wind achieves 30-45%; coal achieves 60-80%. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Sectoral Energy Demand under IEP > [!NOTE] > The following matrix presents the projected **sectoral share of total commercial energy demand in India by 2031-32**, as projected by the IEP. This is a **favourite 7-mark question** topic in KTU 2024. | Sector | Share in 2005-06 (%) | Projected Share in 2031-32 (%) | Key Driver of Growth | |---|:-:|:-:|---| | **Industry** | 45 | 35 | Energy efficiency via PAT | | **Transport** | 19 | 25 | Urbanization & freight | | **Households** | 14 | 22 | Appliance penetration | | **Agriculture** | 8 | 7 | Solar pumps (KUSUM) | | **Services & Commercial** | 9 | 8 | ECBC adoption | | **Others (T&D losses, non-energy)** | 5 | 3 | Smart grid / efficiency | > **KTU 2024 Insight:** The biggest **relative increase** is in **households (+57% in share)** and **transport (+32%)**, while **industry's share declines** despite its absolute energy demand doubling — this is the **structural shift** that IEP-II is built to manage. ### 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example: Sizing a Solar Plant to Meet RPO > **Problem (14-mark type):** A textile industry in Kerala consumes 50 million units (kWh) of electricity per year. As per SERC regulations, the RPO is **15%** for the upcoming year. The industry wants to install a rooftop solar PV plant to meet the RPO. The location has **5 peak sun hours/day** and expected **CUF = 18%**. Compute: > (a) The annual energy required from solar (in kWh). > (b) The required installed capacity (in kWp). > (c) The approximate area needed at $100 \text{ W/m}^2$ specific yield. **Step (a) – Annual solar energy required:** $$ E_{\text{solar}} = 0.15 \times 50{,}000{,}000 = 7{,}500{,}000 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step (b) – Required installed capacity:** First, compute maximum possible annual generation per kWp: $$ E_{\text{max per kWp}} = 8760 \text{ h} \times 1 \text{ kWp} = 8760 \text{ kWh/kWp} $$ Apply CUF: $$ E_{\text{actual per kWp}} = 8760 \times 0.18 = 1576.8 \text{ kWh/kWp/year} $$ Solve for required kWp: $$ \text{Required kWp} = \dfrac{7{,}500{,}000}{1576.8} \approx 4757 \text{ kWp} \approx 4.76 \text{ MWp} $$ **Step (c) – Approximate area required:** $$ \text{Area} = \dfrac{\text{Capacity (W)}}{100 \text{ W/m}^2} = \dfrac{4{,}757{,}000}{100} = 47{,}570 \text{ m}^2 \approx 4.76 \text{ hectares} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion for the industry:** Installing a 4.76 MWp rooftop solar plant will fully meet the 15% RPO, displacing ~7.5 million kWh of grid electricity annually and saving ~6,000 tonnes of CO₂ per year (at ~0.82 kg CO₂/kWh grid emission factor). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Architectural Flow – Integrated Energy Policy Framework The following Mermaid block renders the **functional architecture** of the Integrated Energy Policy – II, showing how the policy levers interact with the **macro-level targets** and **sectoral implementation mechanisms**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[IEP Vision
Sustainable Energy Security] --> B[Five Strategic Pillars] B --> P1[Pillar 1
Sectoral Energy Efficiency] B --> P2[Pillar 2
Pricing and Subsidy Reform] B --> P3[Pillar 3
Renewable Energy Integration] B --> P4[Pillar 4
Technology R and D] B --> P5[Pillar 5
Institutional Governance] P1 --> I1[PAT Scheme] P1 --> I2[ECBC Code] P1 --> I3[Standards and Labelling] P1 --> I4[Ujala LED Programme] P2 --> J1[DBT for LPG PAHAL] P2 --> J2[Diesel Deregulation] P2 --> J3[DBT Electricity] P2 --> J4[KUSUM Solar Pumps] P3 --> K1[Solar Mission JNNSM] P3 --> K2[Wind Energy Auctions] P3 --> K3[SATAT Biogas] P3 --> K4[Green Hydrogen Mission] P3 --> K5[RPO Enforcement] P4 --> L1[SECI Solar Manufacturing] P4 --> L2[NETRA Research] P4 --> L3[PLI Scheme for PV] P4 --> L4[ACC Battery PLI] P5 --> M1[National Energy Council Proposal] P5 --> M2[Open Access Policy] P5 --> M3[Single Window Clearance] I1 --> OUT1[Industrial Energy Savings] I2 --> OUT1 I3 --> OUT1 I4 --> OUT1 J1 --> OUT2[Subsidy Rationalization] J2 --> OUT2 J3 --> OUT2 J4 --> OUT2 K1 --> OUT3[Renewable Capacity Addition] K2 --> OUT3 K3 --> OUT3 K4 --> OUT3 K5 --> OUT3 L1 --> OUT4[Indigenous Manufacturing] L2 --> OUT4 L3 --> OUT4 L4 --> OUT4 M1 --> OUT5[Coordinated Governance] M2 --> OUT5 M3 --> OUT5 OUT1 --> T[Target 2031-32
Per Capita Energy 4x
Renewable Share 50 percent] OUT2 --> T OUT3 --> T OUT4 --> T OUT5 --> T ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block – RPO Compliance Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[State DISCOM or Bulk Consumer] --> B[Compute Annual Energy Purchase] B --> C[Apply RPO Percentage as per SERC Order] C --> D[Option 1: Buy Renewable Energy from RE Generator] C --> E[Option 2: Install Own Captive RE Plant] C --> F[Option 3: Purchase REC Renewable Energy Certificate] D --> G[Submit Compliance Report to SERC] E --> G F --> G G --> H{Compliant} H -->|Yes| I[Certificate Issued] H -->|No| J[Penalty under EA 2003] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block – PAT Scheme Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Bureau of Energy Efficiency BEE] --> B[Identify DCs Designated Consumers] B --> C[Set Baseline SEC per Sector] C --> D[Issue Notification of Target SEC] D --> E[3 Year Compliance Cycle Begins] E --> F{Annual Performance Assessment} F -->|Achieved Target| G[Continue Operations] F -->|Exceeded Target| H[Issue ESCerts Energy Saving Certificates] F -->|Underperformed| I[Buy ESCerts from Market] H --> J[ESCerts Traded on Power Exchanges] I --> J J --> K[Annual Energy Savings MTOE reported] ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture – National Energy Governance under IEP ```mermaid flowchart TB NITI[NITI Aayog
Policy Think Tank] --> PM[Prime Ministers Office] PM --> ME[Ministry of Power] PM --> MNRE[Ministry of New and Renewable Energy] PM --> MOC[Ministry of Coal] PM --> MOPNG[Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas] PM --> DAH[Department of Atomic Energy] ME --> CEA[Central Electricity Authority] ME --> BEE[Bureau of Energy Efficiency] ME --> CERC[Central Electricity Regulatory Commission] BEE --> SDA[State Designated Agencies] BEE --> DCs[Designated Consumers in Industry] MNRE --> SECI[Solar Energy Corporation of India] MNRE --> IREDA[Indian Renewable Energy Dev Agency] SECI --> STU[State Transmission Utilities] IREDA --> RE[Renewable Energy Developers] CEA --> NLDC[National Load Despatch Centre] NLDC --> RLDC[Regional Load Despatch Centres] RLDC --> SLDC[State Load Despatch Centres] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Each node uses purely alphanumeric IDs (e.g., `node1`, `OUT1`) to comply with Mermaid syntax rules. Labels are kept clean, uppercase, and free of markdown formatting. These diagrams map the **operational flow** of IEP-II from policy design to field-level implementation. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** > **List the five strategic pillars of the Integrated Energy Policy (IEP) of India and explain any one in detail.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The five strategic pillars of IEP are: > 1. **Sectoral Energy Efficiency** — Mandatory PAT scheme for energy-intensive industries; ECBC for buildings. > 2. **Pricing & Subsidy Reform** — DBT for LPG, KUSUM for solar pumps, phased diesel deregulation. > 3. **Renewable Energy Integration** — RPO enforcement, JNNSM, Green Hydrogen Mission. > 4. **Technology R&D and Indigenous Manufacturing** — PLI scheme, SECI, NETRA, ACC battery programme. > 5. **Institutional Governance** — Proposed National Energy Council, single-window clearance, open access. > > **Detailed explanation of Pillar 1:** The PAT scheme covers **DCs (Designated Consumers)** in sectors like thermal power, aluminium, cement, fertilizer, iron & steel, and pulp & paper. The BEE sets a **baseline Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** for each plant, and the plant must reduce its SEC over a 3-year cycle. Underperformers must **purchase ESCerts** from the market, while over-achievers can **sell their ESCerts**, creating a market-based incentive for energy efficiency. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** > **What is the Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO)? Explain with formula and typical values for India.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO)** is a regulatory mandate issued by **State Electricity Regulatory Commissions (SERCs)** under the **Electricity Act, 2003**, requiring every **distribution licensee and certain bulk consumers** to purchase a minimum percentage of their total energy consumption from renewable energy sources. > > **Formula:** > > $$ > \text{RPO (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Renewable Energy Purchase (MWh)}}{\text{Total Energy Purchase (MWh)}} \times 100 > $$ > > **Typical values:** The Ministry of Power has notified a **uniform RPO trajectory** starting from ~10.5% in 2010-11 and rising to **29.9% by 2029-30**, comprising **Solar RPO**, **Non-Solar RPO**, and **Hydro Purchase Obligation (HPO)**. Non-compliance attracts **penalty under Section 142 of the Electricity Act, 2003**. --- ### 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain in detail the salient features and objectives of the **Integrated Energy Policy (IEP) of India**. Discuss the **target of four-fold increase in per-capita energy consumption by 2031-32** with a numerical illustration. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Perform, Achieve & Trade (PAT) scheme** in detail. With a suitable example, show the calculation of **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)** for a designated consumer. **(7 marks)** --- **MODEL SOLUTION:** **Part (a) – 7 marks:** The Integrated Energy Policy (IEP) was formulated by the **Planning Commission in 2006** to provide a comprehensive roadmap for India's energy future. Its salient features are: 1. **Holistic approach:** Considers all primary energy carriers (coal, oil, gas, nuclear, hydro, renewables) in an integrated framework rather than sector-by-sector. 2. **Energy security focus:** Reduces import dependence by diversifying the energy mix; targets 25% reduction in import dependency by 2031-32. 3. **Sustainable development:** Balances economic growth with environmental protection and equity. 4. **Demand-side management:** Emphasizes energy efficiency, Ujala programme, ECBC, S&L. 5. **Strategic R&D:** Funds indigenous technology development for renewables, nuclear, hydrogen, and storage. **Numerical illustration of 4× per-capita target:** - Base year (2005-06): Population = 1140 million, TPES = 425 MTOE. - Per-capita energy = 425 / 1140 = **0.373 TOE/capita**. - Target year (2031-32): Per-capita = 4 × 0.373 = **1.492 TOE/capita**. - Population = 1500 million. - Total demand = 1500 × 1.492 = **2238 MTOE** (a 5.27-fold increase in absolute terms). **Part (b) – 7 marks:** The **PAT scheme** is India's flagship **market-based energy efficiency** mechanism managed by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**. **Working mechanism:** - **Step 1:** BEE identifies **DCs (Designated Consumers)** in energy-intensive sectors. - **Step 2:** BEE sets a **baseline SEC** for each DC based on historical data. - **Step 3:** BEE notifies a **target SEC** to be achieved within a **3-year cycle**. - **Step 4:** DCs that **exceed** their target are issued **ESCerts** (1 ESCert = 1 TOE of oil equivalent saved). - **Step 5:** DCs that **underperform** must purchase ESCerts from the market. - **Step 6:** ESCerts are traded on **power exchanges** (IEX, PXIL). **Numerical example:** A thermal power plant has baseline SEC = 0.30 TOE/MWh, target SEC = 0.27 TOE/MWh, and annual generation = 1,000,000 MWh. - Baseline energy = 0.30 × 1,000,000 = 300,000 TOE. - Target energy = 0.27 × 1,000,000 = 270,000 TOE. - Energy saved = 30,000 TOE = **30,000 ESCerts**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Discuss the **pricing reforms and subsidy rationalization** strategies recommended by the IEP. Explain the **Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT)** mechanism for LPG with its impact. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A commercial building in Kochi consumes 800,000 kWh of electricity per year. The DISCOM has notified an **RPO of 12%** for the current year. The building owner wishes to install a **rooftop solar plant** with a **CUF of 18%**. Calculate: **(i)** the annual energy to be generated from solar, **(ii)** the required installed capacity in kWp, and **(iii)** the area required in m² assuming $100 \text{ W/m}^2$. **(7 marks)** --- **MODEL SOLUTION:** **Part (a) – 7 marks:** The IEP identifies that **unsustainable subsidies and cross-subsidies** distort energy markets and impose heavy fiscal burdens. Key reforms include: 1. **Diesel price deregulation** (achieved in October 2014) — eliminated the Rs. 18/litre subsidy. 2. **DBT for LPG (PAHAL scheme)** — subsidy directly transferred to beneficiary's bank account linked to Aadhaar. 3. **DBT for Electricity (proposed)** — DBT-E to replace free/subsidized power with targeted cash transfers. 4. **KUSUM scheme** — solarizing agricultural pumps to reduce the agricultural subsidy burden on DISCOMs. 5. **Targeted subsidy for BPL households** while removing subsidies for the affluent. **PAHAL (DBT for LPG) impact:** - **Coverage:** 30+ crore LPG consumers. - **Savings:** Eliminated ~3.5 crore duplicate/ghost connections, saving ~Rs. 30,000 crore per annum. - **Mechanism:** Consumer buys cylinder at market price, subsidized amount (Rs. 200-300) transferred via Aadhaar-linked bank account. - **Environmental benefit:** Reduced household fuel-wood usage and indoor air pollution. **Part (b) – 7 marks:** **Given data:** - Total annual consumption = 800,000 kWh. - RPO = 12%, CUF = 18%. - Specific yield = 100 W/m². **Step 1: Annual energy to be generated from solar:** $$ E_{\text{solar}} = 0.12 \times 800{,}000 = 96{,}000 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step 2: Required installed capacity:** Maximum possible generation per kWp in one year = $8760 \text{ h} \times 1 \text{ kWp} = 8760 \text{ kWh}$. Actual generation per kWp at CUF 18%: $$ E_{\text{actual per kWp}} = 8760 \times 0.18 = 1576.8 \text{ kWh/kWp} $$ Required capacity: $$ C_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{96{,}000}{1576.8} \approx 60.9 \text{ kWp} \approx 61 \text{ kWp} $$ **Step 3: Area required:** $$ \text{Area} = \dfrac{60{,}900 \text{ W}}{100 \text{ W/m}^2} = 609 \text{ m}^2 $$ > **Conclusion:** The building owner must install a **61 kWp rooftop solar plant occupying ~609 m²** of unshaded roof area to meet the 12% RPO. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls in IEP-II Questions (Where Students Lose Marks):** > > 1. **Confusing IEP-I and IEP-II:** Part-I deals with macro-level demand-supply projections; Part-II focuses on **operational levers**. Students often blur the two and lose 2-3 marks. > > 2. **Forgetting units:** Always write TOE, MTOE, kWh, or kCal/kWh with the numerical value. Marks are deducted if units are missing. > > 3. **Ignoring the "calorific value" conversion:** When using BOE (barrel of oil equivalent) and TOE, remember $1 \text{ TOE} \approx 7.45 \text{ BOE}$. > > 4. **Missing the Aadhaar linkage in DBT answers:** If asked about DBT for LPG or electricity, **always mention Aadhaar-based bank account transfer** — without this, the answer is incomplete. > > 5. **PAT vs RPO confusion:** PAT is a **mandatory efficiency programme for industries**; RPO is a **renewable purchase mandate for DISCOMs and bulk consumers**. Examiners mark these as **separate concepts**. > > 6. **Numericals without formula:** Always write the formula before substituting the numbers. Marks are awarded for the **formula statement (1 mark) + substitution (1 mark) + final answer with unit (1 mark)**. > > 7. **Not stating the CUF assumption:** In solar sizing problems, the CUF must be explicitly given or assumed; otherwise the answer is incomplete. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist – Integrated Energy Policy II** - ⭐ **IEP – 2006** (Planning Commission); **IEP Review – 2014**; **NEP (National Energy Policy)** still under draft. - ⭐ **Five Pillars:** Sectoral Efficiency, Pricing Reform, Renewable Integration, R&D, Institutional Governance. - ⭐ **Per-capita target:** 4× growth by 2031-32, translating to ~5.27× absolute demand growth. - ⭐ **PAT scheme** covers **DCs in 13 sectors** (aluminium, cement, chlor-alkali, fertilizer, iron & steel, paper, petrochemicals, refineries, thermal power, textiles, etc.). - ⭐ **1 ESCert = 1 TOE** of energy saved; tradable on **IEX and PXIL**. - ⭐ **RPO trajectory:** rises to **29.9% by 2029-30** (Solar RPO + Non-Solar RPO + HPO). - ⭐ **JNNSM target:** 100 GW solar (60 GW large-scale + 40 GW rooftop). - ⭐ **500 GW non-fossil** target by 2030 (announced at COP-26, Glasgow). - ⭐ **PLI scheme:** ₹45,000 crore for solar PV manufacturing; ₹18,100 crore for ACC batteries. - ⭐ **DBT for LPG (PAHAL):** ~Rs. 30,000 crore saved; ~3.5 crore ghost connections eliminated. - ⭐ **KUSUM scheme:** solarizing 3.5 million agricultural pumps. - ⭐ **CUF benchmarks:** Solar 18-25%, Wind 30-45%, Coal 60-80%. - ⭐ **Key conversion:** $1 \text{ TOE} = 42 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}_{\text{th}}$. - ⭐ **Energy Intensity target:** ~2% annual reduction in TOE/million USD PPP. - ⭐ **Institutional bodies to remember:** NITI Aayog, BEE, CEA, CERC, SERCs, MNRE, SECI, IREDA, NETRA. - ⭐ **Electricity Act, 2003** and **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** are the **enabling legislative instruments** for IEP-II implementation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Board-Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions on IEP-II, structure your answer using the **"5W1H" framework** — *What (definition), Why (rationale), Who (ministries/agencies), When (timelines), Where (sectors covered), and How (instruments/mechanisms)*. This 6-point structure has been shown to fetch **90%+ marks** in KTU valuation.

topics.title: Objectives of Energy Management and Energy Audit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Objectives of Energy Management and Energy Audit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Energy Management?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Energy Management):**
> **Energy Management** is the systematic, proactive, and well-documented process of planning, operating, monitoring, and controlling energy usage in a building, industrial facility, or organization to achieve maximum energy productivity, minimize waste, reduce operational costs, and ensure sustainable utilization of natural energy resources while complying with statutory and environmental norms.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, Energy Management is treated as the *strategic umbrella* under which all technical, administrative, and financial decisions regarding energy are coordinated. It is not a one-time activity but a **continuous improvement cycle** that aligns closely with the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** philosophy of NEP 2020.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Budget" Idea

Imagine a household that receives a fixed monthly income but has unpredictable bills for electricity, water, and gas. The head of the family sits down, **lists** every expense, **identifies** where money leaks (e.g., lights left on, AC running with windows open), **plans** a monthly cap, **monitors** daily usage via an app, and **reviews** it weekly. That disciplined oversight of resources is exactly what Energy Management does for a factory or a campus — except the "currency" is **kWh, kilolitres of fuel, and rupees**, and the stakes are far higher.

### Core Pillars of Energy Management

| Pillar | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Policy** | Top management commitment to energy efficiency |
| **Planning** | Setting SMART energy targets and KPIs |
| **Implementation** | Deploying efficient technologies and practices |
| **Verification** | Monitoring, Measurement & Verification (M&V) |
| **Review** | Management review and continual improvement |

---

## 1.2 What is an Energy Audit?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Energy Audit):**
> An **Energy Audit** is a structured, on-site, technical assessment of energy-using equipment, processes, and systems in a facility, conducted by a qualified Energy Auditor, to identify the quantity, source, and cost of energy flows, quantify energy waste, and recommend economically viable Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) for improving energy efficiency.

The Energy Audit is the **diagnostic instrument** of Energy Management — the stethoscope that the manager places on the "body" of the facility to hear where energy is bleeding.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Check-up"

If Energy Management is "staying healthy", then an Energy Audit is the **annual full-body check-up**. The doctor checks the heart (boilers), lungs (HVAC), blood (electrical network), and reflexes (motors), then prescribes medicine (ECMs) with a dosage (cost) and expected benefit (savings). Just as a check-up reveals hidden conditions like high BP before a stroke, an energy audit reveals hidden losses like compressed air leaks before a financial hemorrhage.

### Types of Energy Audits (KTU Standard Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India** guidelines that KTU follows:

| Type | Scope | Investment Level | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Walk-through Audit** (Preliminary) | Visual inspection, quick wins | Very Low | Small establishments |
| **Mini Audit** (Targeted) | Single system deep-dive | Moderate | Boilers, HVAC, Lighting |
| **Comprehensive Audit** (Detailed) | All systems, M&V, instrumentation | High | Large industries, mandatory for PAT scheme |

### Key Standard Metrics Used (Highlighted for KTU)

- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** = Energy used / Unit of production — measured in **kWh/tonne**, **MJ/kg**, etc.
- **Energy Intensity (EI)** = Total energy / Total built-up area — measured in **kWh/m²/year**.
- **Power Factor (PF)** = Real Power (kW) / Apparent Power (kVA) — dimensionless, ideally **> 0.95**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Objectives of Energy Management

Energy Management is governed by a **pyramid of objectives**, moving from broad organizational goals down to operational targets. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this hierarchy because exam questions frequently test the *relationship* between corporate strategy and shop-floor action.

### Objective Layer 1 — Strategic (Top-Down)

- **Cost Reduction:** Decrease energy spend (typically **20–40%** of manufacturing cost in energy-intensive units).
- **Environmental Compliance:** Meet **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** cycle targets, **CPCB/SPCB** norms, and **ESG** mandates.
- **Corporate Sustainability:** Achieve net-zero / carbon-neutral pledges committed under NEP 2020's sustainability framework.

### Objective Layer 2 — Tactical (Mid-Level)

- **Demand Side Management (DSM):** Shift loads to off-peak hours to reduce **kVA demand charges**.
- **Energy Substitution:** Replace fossil fuels with renewables (solar, biomass, wind).
- **Procurement Optimization:** Negotiate better **PPA (Power Purchase Agreement)** tariffs, optimize ToU (Time-of-Use) billing.

### Objective Layer 3 — Operational (Bottom-Up)

- **Loss Minimization:** Reduce distribution losses in **compressed air, steam, water, and electrical networks**.
- **Equipment Efficiency Upgrades:** Replace standard motors with **IE3/IE4 premium efficiency motors**.
- **Behavioral Change:** Operator training, signage, and incentive schemes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this hierarchy matters for KTU:** The examiner often frames a 14-mark question like *"Discuss the objectives of energy management in an industrial facility."* A high-scoring answer must *explicitly* cover all three layers, not just state "to save energy."

---

## 2.2 The Hierarchical Objectives of an Energy Audit

The Energy Audit has **four sequential objectives** that correspond to the four phases of the audit methodology:

1. **Quantification Objective:** Measure actual energy consumption at each end-use and establish a credible baseline.
2. **Diagnostic Objective:** Identify the magnitude, source, and root cause of energy wastage.
3. **Prescriptive Objective:** Recommend specific Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) with quantified savings.
4. **Economic Objective:** Estimate implementation cost, payback period, ROI, and prioritize ECMs.

---

## 2.3 The Energy Audit Methodology — 7-Stage Logical Framework

| Stage | Activity | Tool Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Pre-Audit Planning | Collect bills, drawings, process data | Utility bill analysis |
| 2. Walk-through Survey | Visual inspection, interviews | Checklist, thermal camera |
| 3. Detailed Measurement | kWh, kg/hr, °C, m³/hr logging | Power analyzer, flue gas analyzer |
| 4. Data Analysis | Balance energy inputs vs outputs | Sankey diagram, energy balance sheet |
| 5. Identification of ECMs | Brainstorming + benchmarking | BEE best-practice database |
| 6. Economic Evaluation | Calculate payback, NPV, IRR | Excel/spreadsheet models |
| 7. Reporting | Prepare audit report with action plan | Standard BEE format |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **high-frequency** in KTU 2024 ESE and internal exams. Memorize units and typical values.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical / Ideal Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P}$ | $\text{kWh/tonne}$ or $\text{MJ/kg}$ | Depends on industry |
| 2 | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | $EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{A}$ | $\text{kWh/m}^2\text{/yr}$ | Commercial: 100–250 |
| 3 | **Energy Cost Share** | $\%EC = \dfrac{C_{energy}}{C_{total}} \times 100$ | % | 5–10% (general), 30–40% (energy-intensive) |
| 4 | **Energy Savings (kWh)** | $\Delta E = E_{baseline} - E_{actual}$ | kWh | Always positive |
| 5 | **Annual Monetary Savings** | $S = \Delta E \times R$ | ₹/yr | $R$ = unit rate in ₹/kWh |
| 6 | **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** | $SPP = \dfrac{I}{S}$ | years | Acceptable if < 3 yrs |
| 7 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \dfrac{S}{I} \times 100$ | % | Higher is better |
| 8 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{S_t}{(1+i)^t} - I$ | ₹ | Must be > 0 |
| 9 | **Cogeneration Efficiency** | $\eta_{cogen} = \dfrac{E_{elec} + E_{thermal}}{E_{fuel}}$ | % | 70–85% |
| 10 | **Power Factor Correction** | $kVAR_c = kW \left(\tan\theta_1 - \tan\theta_2\right)$ | kVAR | Target $\theta_2 \le 18.2°$ (PF ≥ 0.95) |
| 11 | **Heat Loss in Flue Gas** | $Q_{fg} = m_{fg} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ/hr | Should be < 8% of input |
| 12 | **Lighting Energy Saving** | $\%S = \dfrac{W_{old} - W_{new}}{W_{old}} \times 100$ | % | LED retrofit: 50–70% |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Trap:** In the formula table, every `tan`, `ΔT`, `c_p`, etc. that resembles a vertical bar has been carefully avoided inside table cells. KTU examiners deduct marks if symbols break table rendering in answer sheets.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial Sector:** Cement, steel, aluminium, fertilizers — all covered under **PAT scheme** of BEE, where SEC reduction is a legal obligation.
- **Commercial Buildings:** Mandatory **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** compliance under KTU 2024 civil/architecture electives.
- **Renewable Integration:** Energy audit identifies feasibility of rooftop solar, reducing grid dependency.
- **Academic Projects:** Final-year B.Tech projects in Kerala often use energy audit methodology for hostel/college energy studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Simple Payback Period (SPP)

The Simple Payback Period is the most frequently tested economic indicator in KTU exams. Let us derive it from first principles so the logic is permanently embedded.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

- Let $I$ = Initial investment required for the Energy Conservation Measure (ECM), measured in **₹**.
- Let $S$ = Annual monetary savings achieved by implementing the ECM, measured in **₹/yr**.
- Let $n$ = Payback period, measured in **years** — the unknown we wish to find.

### Step 2 — Core Logical Assumption

The payback period is defined as the time required for the **cumulative savings** to exactly equal the **initial investment**.

$$\text{Cumulative Savings} = \text{Initial Investment}$$

### Step 3 — Express the Cumulative Savings

Assuming a **constant annual savings** $S$ (which is the standard KTU assumption unless compound savings are mentioned), the cumulative savings over $n$ years is:

$$C_{cum} = S \times n$$

### Step 4 — Equate to Investment

$$S \times n = I$$

### Step 5 — Solve for n

$$\boxed{n = \frac{I}{S}}$$

### Step 6 — Add Correction for Mid-Year (KTU Board Favourite)

If the ECM is implemented mid-year, the first year's savings are pro-rated. The corrected payback becomes:

$$n_{corr} = \frac{I}{S} + \frac{t_{mid}}{12}$$

where $t_{mid}$ is the number of months into the year when commissioning happens. This nuanced formula is frequently asked in **Part B (14 mark)** questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Annual Energy Savings from Power Reduction

Consider a motor operating at load $P_1$ (kW) for $H$ hours/year at a unit rate $R$ (₹/kWh). After retrofit with an efficient motor, the load reduces to $P_2$.

### Step 1 — Energy Before Retrofit

$$E_1 = P_1 \times H \quad \text{[kWh/yr]}$$

### Step 2 — Energy After Retrofit

$$E_2 = P_2 \times H \quad \text{[kWh/yr]}$$

### Step 3 — Energy Saved

$$\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot H$$

### Step 4 — Annual Cost Saved

$$\boxed{S = \Delta E \cdot R = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot H \cdot R}$$

### Worked Numerical (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 15 kW motor runs for 5000 hours/year at ₹7/kWh. After Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) installation, average power drops to 11 kW. Find annual savings and payback if VFD cost is ₹1,50,000.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta E = (15 - 11) \times 5000 = 4 \times 5000 = 20{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

$$S = 20{,}000 \times 7 = \text{₹}1{,}40{,}000 \text{ /yr}$$

$$n = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}40{,}000} \approx 1.07 \text{ years}$$

Since payback is under **2 years**, the VFD is a **high-priority ECM**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Energy Audit Report Mapping

| Section of Audit Report | Purpose | Source Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Executive Summary** | Top-level findings for management | Computed KPIs |
| **Facility Description** | Process map, equipment list | Site survey |
| **Energy Consumption Profile** | Utility bill analysis | Electricity bill, fuel log |
| **Energy Balance Sheet** | Input = Output + Losses | Measured flows |
| **ECMs Identified** | List of recommendations | Engineering analysis |
| **Economic Analysis** | Payback, NPV, ROI table | Cost data |
| **Implementation Schedule** | Gantt chart of actions | Project planning |
| **M&V Plan** | How savings will be verified | IPMVP Protocol |

> [!NOTE]
> **M&V Protocol** for KTU 2024 follows the international **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol)** with four options A/B/C/D, of which **Option B — Retrofit Isolation** is the most common in Indian industrial audits.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Management ↔ Energy Audit Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Top Management Energy Policy] --> node2[Energy Manager Appointed]
    node2 --> node3[Energy Management Program Initiated]
    node3 --> node4[Energy Audit Conducted]
    node4 --> node5[Baseline Established]
    node5 --> node6[ECMs Identified]
    node6 --> node7[Economic Prioritization]
    node7 --> node8[Implementation of ECMs]
    node8 --> node9[Measurement and Verification]
    node9 --> node10[Performance Compared to Baseline]
    node10 -->|Gap Found| node4
    node10 -->|Target Achieved| node11[New Targets Set]
    node11 --> node4
    node1 --> node12[Corporate Sustainability Goals]
    node12 --> node3
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Audit Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Stage 1: Pre-Audit Planning] --> stepB[Stage 2: Walk-through Survey]
    stepB --> stepC[Stage 3: Detailed Measurement]
    stepC --> stepD[Stage 4: Data Analysis]
    stepD --> stepE[Stage 5: ECM Identification]
    stepE --> stepF[Stage 6: Economic Evaluation]
    stepF --> stepG[Stage 7: Reporting and Implementation]
    stepG -->|Feedback Loop| stepC
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — PDCA Cycle for Energy Management

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    planNode[PLAN: Set energy objectives and KPIs] --> doNode[DO: Implement ECMs and train staff]
    doNode --> checkNode[CHECK: Monitor via M and V]
    checkNode --> actNode[ACT: Review and improve]
    actNode -->|Continual Improvement| planNode
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Objectives Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topNode[Strategic: Cost, Compliance, Sustainability] --> midNode[Tactical: DSM, Substitution, Procurement]
    midNode --> botNode[Operational: Loss Reduction, Efficiency, Behavior]
    botNode --> baseNode[Shop Floor: Motors, Boilers, Lighting, HVAC]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define Energy Audit. List any four objectives of conducting an energy audit in an industrial facility.

**Model Answer:**
Energy Audit is a systematic study of energy use in a facility to identify wastage and recommend efficiency improvements. The four objectives are:
1. To establish the baseline of energy consumption for each end-use.
2. To identify sources of energy wastage and quantify the losses.
3. To recommend economically viable Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs).
4. To comply with statutory requirements like the **PAT scheme** and **ECBC** norms.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Differentiate between Energy Management and Energy Audit.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Energy Management | Energy Audit |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Continuous, strategic process | Periodic, diagnostic activity |
| **Scope** | Organization-wide | Specific facility or system |
| **Frequency** | Ongoing | Annual or biennial |
| **Output** | Sustained efficiency improvements | Audit report with ECMs |
| **Owner** | Energy Manager / Steering Committee | Certified Energy Auditor |
| **Relationship** | Umbrella activity | Tool within Energy Management |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the strategic, tactical, and operational objectives of Energy Management in an industrial facility.

**Model Answer:**

**Strategic Objectives (Top-Down):**
- Reduce energy cost which forms **20–40%** of manufacturing cost in energy-intensive units like cement, steel, aluminium.
- Achieve regulatory compliance with **BEE-PAT**, **ECBC**, and **CPCB** norms.
- Meet corporate ESG commitments and align with India's **net-zero by 2070** pledge.

**Tactical Objectives (Mid-Level):**
- Implement **Demand Side Management (DSM)** to shift loads to off-peak hours, reducing demand charges.
- Substitute conventional fuels with **renewable energy sources** like solar, biomass, and wind.
- Optimize energy procurement via competitive bidding, group captive, and **ToU tariff** arbitrage.

**Operational Objectives (Bottom-Up):**
- Minimize distribution losses in **compressed air, steam, water, and electrical networks** (typical savings 20–30%).
- Replace standard motors with **IE3/IE4 premium efficiency** motors (savings 3–8% per motor).
- Drive behavioral change through operator training, awareness drives, and incentive schemes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A factory installs a capacitor bank costing ₹2,00,000 to improve power factor from 0.78 to 0.96. The connected load is 500 kW operating 6000 hours/year. The tariff is ₹7/kWh. Calculate the kVAR installed, annual kWh savings from reduced line losses (assume 5% loss reduction in distribution), and simple payback period.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate kVAR installed.**

For PF improvement from 0.78 to 0.96:

$$\cos\theta_1 = 0.78 \Rightarrow \tan\theta_1 = 0.8023$$

$$\cos\theta_2 = 0.96 \Rightarrow \tan\theta_2 = 0.2917$$

$$kVAR_c = kW(\tan\theta_1 - \tan\theta_2) = 500(0.8023 - 0.2917) = 500 \times 0.5106$$

$$kVAR_c = 255.3 \text{ kVAR (approx)}$$

**Step 2 — Annual energy consumption.**

$$E_{annual} = 500 \times 6000 = 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Energy saved by reducing distribution losses by 5%.**

$$\Delta E = 0.05 \times 30{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 4 — Monetary savings.**

$$S = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 7 = \text{₹}10{,}50{,}000 \text{ /yr}$$

**Step 5 — Simple Payback Period.**

$$n = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{10{,}50{,}000} \approx 0.19 \text{ years} \approx 2.3 \text{ months}$$

The investment recovers in under **3 months**, making it an **excellent ECM** with priority ranking "A".

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the objectives of an Energy Audit. Explain the different types of audits with their applicability.

**Model Answer:**

**Objectives of Energy Audit:**
1. **Quantification** — Measure actual energy consumption at each end-use point.
2. **Diagnosis** — Identify sources, magnitudes, and root causes of energy wastage.
3. **Prescription** — Recommend specific Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs).
4. **Economic Prioritization** — Rank ECMs by payback period, NPV, and ROI.

**Types of Energy Audits:**

- **Walk-through Audit:** Visual inspection of the plant to identify obvious losses (leaks, insulation gaps). Low cost, takes 1–2 days. Suitable for small offices, retail outlets.
- **Mini / Targeted Audit:** Deep-dive into one system like boilers, HVAC, compressed air, or lighting. Involves portable measurements. Duration: 1 week.
- **Comprehensive Audit:** Full-scale instrumentation with calibrated meters, **IPMVP-compliant** M&V plan, detailed engineering and economic analysis. Mandatory under **PAT scheme**. Duration: 2–4 weeks.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 100 HP (74.6 kW) induction motor operates at 85% loading for 4500 hours/year at ₹6.5/kWh. Replacing with a premium efficiency motor reduces losses by 5% of input. Calculate annual energy savings, monetary savings, and payback if the premium motor costs ₹1,20,000 extra.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Original motor input power.**

Standard motor efficiency at 85% load ≈ 88%.

$$P_{in,std} = \frac{P_{output}}{\eta_{std}} = \frac{63.4}{0.88} \approx 72.05 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Loss reduction (5% of input).**

$$\Delta P = 0.05 \times 72.05 = 3.60 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy saved.**

$$\Delta E = 3.60 \times 4500 = 16{,}200 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary savings.**

$$S = 16{,}200 \times 6.5 = \text{₹}1{,}05{,}300 \text{ /yr}$$

**Step 5 — Simple Payback Period.**

$$n = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{1{,}05{,}300} \approx 1.14 \text{ years}$$

Since payback is just over **1 year**, this is a **high-priority ECM** and should be implemented immediately.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - Writing only "Energy Management means saving energy" without the **PDCA** structure → loses 3–4 marks.
> - Forgetting the **units** (kWh, kVAR, ₹/yr) in numerical problems → loses 1 mark per error.
> - Confusing **SPP with Discounted Payback** → examiner expects the simple version unless specified.
> - In the 14-mark question, students often skip the **economic justification** for ECMs → remember to always tie recommendations to **payback/ROI**.
> - Never write vague phrases like "improve efficiency"; always quantify ("reduce SEC from 850 to 720 kWh/tonne").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Energy Management** is a **continuous, strategic umbrella**; **Energy Audit** is a **periodic, diagnostic tool** within it.
- The two are linked through the **PDCA cycle**: Plan (set targets), Do (audit + implement), Check (M&V), Act (review).
- Objectives of Energy Management follow a **three-tier hierarchy** — Strategic, Tactical, Operational.
- Objectives of an Energy Audit are **four-fold** — Quantification, Diagnosis, Prescription, Economic Prioritization.
- Three audit types per **BEE norms** — Walk-through, Mini, Comprehensive — with progressively deeper instrumentation.
- **Simple Payback Period** $n = I/S$ is the most-tested economic formula; always quote in **years or months**.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is the most important KPI for industry; **Energy Intensity (EI)** for buildings.
- Always include **monetary savings** along with energy savings in numerical answers.
- The KTU 2024 syllabus aligns with **BEE-PAT, ECBC, IPMVP**, and India's **net-zero 2070** commitments.
- Remember standard efficiency classes: **IE2 (standard), IE3 (premium), IE4 (super premium)** for motors.
- Typical compressor leak losses account for **20–30%** of compressed air generation — a frequent audit finding.
- Lighting retrofit from **FTL/CFL to LED** gives **50–70%** energy saving — a quick-win ECM.
- Steam trap failures cause **5–15%** fuel wastage in boiler systems — check during audits.
- Always **verify savings post-implementation** through the IPMVP protocol — claim without proof is rejected.
- For KTU answers, structure with **headings, sub-points, and tables** — examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Audit and Its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Energy Audit and Its Types # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Energy Audit > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)** > An **Energy Audit** is the systematic, documented verification, inspection, monitoring, and analysis of the energy use of a building, process, or system with the objective of identifying energy conservation opportunities (ECOs), quantifying the energy savings potential, and recommending cost-effective retrofit measures while ensuring comfort, productivity, and operational reliability are not compromised. In the language of the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India**, an Energy Audit is essentially a *"systematic review of energy use and consumption aimed at identifying, quantifying, and reporting energy wastage and inefficiencies, followed by the recommendation of corrective actions."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Health Check-Up" Imagine you go to a doctor for a routine health check-up. The doctor does not immediately prescribe medicines. Instead, he/she: 1. **Asks questions** about your lifestyle and symptoms (Pre-audit data collection). 2. **Measures vital signs** — blood pressure, heart rate, cholesterol, sugar (On-site measurements and instrumentation). 3. **Diagnoses the problem** — identifies what is wrong and how serious it is (Energy analysis and benchmarking). 4. **Prescribes a treatment plan** — medicines, diet, exercise, and follow-up (Energy conservation recommendations and implementation report). An **Energy Audit** is *exactly* the same process, but applied to a **factory, building, or system** instead of a human body. The "patient" is your plant, the "doctor" is the **Certified Energy Auditor (CEA)**, and the "medicines" are the **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)**. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Used in Energy Audits > [!NOTE] > **Glossary of Standard KTU Energy Audit Terms** | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **ECoS** | Energy Conservation Studies | | **ECMs** | Energy Conservation Measures (the "cures" recommended) | | **ECMs vs ECOs** | ECOs = Opportunities (broad); ECMs = Measures (specific actions) | | **M&V** | Measurement and Verification | | **SBD** | Standard Baseline Data | | **CUSUM** | Cumulative Sum Analysis for trend detection | | **Sankey Diagram** | Visual representation of energy flows | | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | Energy used per unit of output (e.g., kWh/ton, MJ/kg) | | **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** | A measurable value that tracks energy performance | | **ktoe / Mtoe** | kilo-tonnes / million tonnes of oil equivalent | ## 1.4 Why is an Energy Audit Conducted? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Objectives of an Energy Audit** > 1. To identify **where**, **how much**, and **why** energy is being used. > 2. To quantify **energy wastage and inefficiencies** in terms of rupees, kWh, and tonnes of CO₂. > 3. To recommend **technically feasible and economically viable** energy-saving measures. > 4. To establish a **baseline** for ongoing energy management and M&V (Measurement & Verification). > 5. To comply with **regulatory requirements** such as the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** (India) and **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme of BEE. ## 1.5 Scope of Energy Auditing Energy audits can be applied to virtually any **energy-consuming entity**, including: - Industrial plants (Cement, Steel, Textile, Chemical, Paper, etc.) - Commercial buildings (Offices, Hotels, Hospitals, Malls) - Institutional facilities (Universities, Airports, Data Centres) - Utility systems (Water pumping stations, HVAC plants, Compressed air systems) - Transport fleets and process heating/cooling systems # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Energy Audits The **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** and international standards such as **ISO 50002** classify energy audits into three primary types based on **depth, scope, and investment intent**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Main Types of Energy Audits (as per KTU/BEE/ISO 50002)** ### Type 1: Preliminary / Walk-Through Energy Audit A quick, low-cost assessment that provides an initial "snapshot" of the facility's energy performance. ### Type 2: Detailed / Comprehensive Energy Audit A rigorous, in-depth engineering study that quantifies every significant energy stream. ### Type 3: Investment-Grade Energy Audit (IGEA) A bankable, high-precision audit used to justify large capital investments. ## 2.2 Type 1 — Preliminary Energy Audit (Walk-Through Audit) **Purpose:** A short-duration (typically 1–3 days) on-site visit to identify *obvious* energy savings and to decide whether a detailed audit is justified. **Methodology:** - Walk-through of the facility, visual inspection. - Review of utility bills (electricity, diesel, gas) for the past 12–24 months. - Brief interviews with operating staff. - Identification of "low-hanging fruit" — no-cost and low-cost measures (e.g., switching off idle motors, fixing steam leaks, optimizing compressor pressure setpoints). **Deliverables:** - A short report with **major energy-consuming equipment** listed. - An indicative list of potential **Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs)**. - A recommendation on whether to proceed to a **Detailed Audit**. **Key Features:** | Attribute | Specification | |-----------|---------------| | **Duration** | 1 to 3 days | | **Cost** | Low (typically 0.5–1% of annual energy cost) | | **Data collected** | Utility bills, nameplate data, walk-through observations | | **Instruments** | Mostly visual + portable clamp meter | | **Accuracy of savings estimate** | ± 30% to ± 50% | | **M&V rigor** | Minimal | ## 2.3 Type 2 — Detailed Energy Audit **Purpose:** A complete engineering study that produces accurate, actionable, and quantified energy-saving recommendations. **Methodology:** - Detailed **mass and energy balance** for each major process. - Use of calibrated instruments: power analyzers, flue gas analysers, lux meters, ultrasonic flow meters, thermographic cameras. - Sampling of operating data over 7–14 days (or longer for batch processes). - Calculation of **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** for every process and benchmarking with best-practice values. - Development of a detailed **Energy Audit Report** with cost-benefit analysis. **Deliverables:** - A comprehensive report (typically 50–150 pages) with: - Plant energy baseline. - Sankey diagrams of energy flow. - Quantified ECMs with cost, savings, and payback. - Implementation roadmap. **Key Features:** | Attribute | Specification | |-----------|---------------| | **Duration** | 2 to 6 weeks | | **Cost** | Moderate to high (3–8% of annual energy cost) | | **Data collected** | Detailed measurements, mass/energy balances | | **Instruments** | Calibrated power loggers, flue gas analyser, ultrasonic flow meter, tachometer, IR camera, etc. | | **Accuracy of savings estimate** | ± 10% to ± 20% | | **M&V rigor** | Substantial | ## 2.4 Type 3 — Investment-Grade Energy Audit (IGEA) **Purpose:** A high-precision audit commissioned to support **major capital investment decisions** (e.g., installation of a new boiler, cogeneration plant, or complete HVAC retrofit). The output of an IGEA is considered "**bankable**" — financial institutions accept it for project funding. **Methodology:** - All features of a detailed audit, **plus**: - Engineering design and equipment specifications. - Detailed **capital cost** estimates (vendor quotes, civil works, installation). - Full financial analysis: **NPV, IRR, payback period, sensitivity analysis**. - Risk assessment. - Sometimes a **pilot test** or M&V protocol design. **Key Features:** | Attribute | Specification | |-----------|---------------| | **Duration** | 2 to 6 months | | **Cost** | High (often 5–15% of the project's capital cost) | | **Accuracy of savings estimate** | ± 5% to ± 10% | | **M&V rigor** | Engineering-grade, with project finance compliance | | **Output** | Bankable feasibility report | ## 2.5 Comparison Table of the Three Types > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Comparison Table for KTU 2024 Exam** | Parameter | Preliminary Audit | Detailed Audit | Investment-Grade Audit | |-----------|------------------|----------------|----------------------| | **Also called** | Walk-through, Mini, Level-1 | Comprehensive, Level-2 | IGEA, Level-3 | | **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks | 2–6 months | | **Scope** | Broad, high-level | Comprehensive, process-level | Bankable, finance-ready | | **Instruments** | Visual + basic | Full instrumented | Full + engineering design | | **Accuracy of estimates** | ± 30–50% | ± 10–20% | ± 5–10% | | **Cost of audit** | Low | Medium | High | | **Output use** | Decide "should we go deeper?" | Implement ECMs | Secure project finance | | **Mass/Energy balance** | Not done | Yes | Yes, very detailed | | **NPV / IRR analysis** | No | Optional | Yes, mandatory | | **Done by** | In-house engineer / junior auditor | Certified Energy Auditor | Senior CEA + engineering team | ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Energy Audit Economics > [!NOTE] > **These formulas are EXTREMELY high-yield for KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | |---|-----------|---------|-------| | 1 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Consumed}}{\text{Production Output}}$ | kWh/ton, MJ/kg, etc. | | 2 | **Annual Energy Cost Saving (₹)** | $S = \Delta E \times C_e \times H$ | ₹/year | | 3 | **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** | $SPP = \dfrac{\text{Capital Cost (₹)}}{\text{Annual Savings (₹/year)}}$ | Years | | 4 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Capital Cost}} \times 100$ | % | | 5 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{S_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | ₹ | | 6 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $0 = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{S_t}{(1+IRR)^t}$ | % | | 7 | **Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) Index** | $CUSUM_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (E_i - \overline{E})$ | kWh | | 8 | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | $EI = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{Floor Area (or Production)}}$ | kWh/m² | | 9 | **% Energy Savings** | $\%\text{Saving} = \dfrac{E_{before} - E_{after}}{E_{before}} \times 100$ | % | | 10 | **CO₂ Emission Reduction** | $\Delta CO_2 = \Delta E \times EF$ | tonnes CO₂ | | 11 | **Standard Energy** | $E_{std} = \text{SEC}_{best} \times \text{Actual Production}$ | kWh | Where: - $\Delta E$ = Energy saved per hour (kWh/h) - $C_e$ = Energy unit cost (₹/kWh) - $H$ = Annual operating hours - $C_0$ = Initial capital cost (₹) - $S_t$ = Net cash flow in year $t$ (₹) - $i$ = Discount rate (fraction) - $n$ = Project life (years) - $EF$ = Emission factor (tonnes CO₂ per kWh) ## 2.7 Energy Audit Methodology — The Standard 3-Phase Approach > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard 3-Phase Energy Audit Procedure (BEE/ISO 50002)** ### **Phase 1: Pre-Audit Phase (Planning & Preparation)** 1. Define audit scope, boundaries, and objectives. 2. Form the audit team (lead auditor, process experts, instrumentation experts). 3. Collect historical data: utility bills, drawings, production data. 4. Conduct a preliminary walk-through. 5. Identify major energy-consuming centres. 6. Plan instrumentation and data collection schedule. ### **Phase 2: Audit Phase (On-Site Measurements & Analysis)** 1. Install portable instruments (power loggers, flow meters, temperature sensors). 2. Conduct spot and continuous measurements (24–72 hours typical). 3. Perform mass and energy balances for each subsystem. 4. Compute Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and benchmark. 5. Identify inefficiencies (e.g., oversized motors, air leakage, poor insulation). 6. Develop quantified Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs). ### **Phase 3: Post-Audit Phase (Reporting & Implementation)** 1. Prepare the Energy Audit Report. 2. Cost-benefit analysis of each ECM. 3. Prioritise ECMs based on payback, ROI, NPV, IRR. 4. Present findings to top management. 5. Support implementation, M&V, and follow-up audits. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Simple Payback Period (SPP) Calculation > [!NOTE] > **A common 7-mark or 14-mark KTU Part B question. Mastering this is essential.** **Problem Statement:** A textile mill is evaluating a proposed Energy Conservation Measure (ECM): installing a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) on a 30 kW motor driving a pump. **Given Data:** - Power rating of motor: $P = 30 \text{ kW}$ - Average load factor before VFD installation: $L_1 = 80\%$ - Expected load factor after VFD installation: $L_2 = 60\%$ - Annual operating hours: $H = 5000 \text{ hr/year}$ - Cost of electricity: $C_e = \text{₹} \, 7 \text{ per kWh}$ - Capital cost of VFD + installation: $C_0 = \text{₹} \, 1{,}50{,}000$ **Find:** (a) Annual energy savings in kWh. (b) Annual monetary savings in ₹. (c) Simple Payback Period in years. --- ### **Step 1: Calculate the Power Consumption BEFORE the VFD Installation** The actual electrical power drawn is the rated power multiplied by the load factor: $$ P_1 = P \times L_1 $$ $$ P_1 = 30 \text{ kW} \times 0.80 = 24 \text{ kW} $$ **Explanation:** At 80% load factor, the motor draws 80% of its rated 30 kW, i.e., 24 kW of electrical power continuously. ### **Step 2: Calculate the Power Consumption AFTER the VFD Installation** $$ P_2 = P \times L_2 $$ $$ P_2 = 30 \text{ kW} \times 0.60 = 18 \text{ kW} $$ **Explanation:** Once the VFD is installed, the same pump meets the process demand using only 60% motor loading, drawing 18 kW. ### **Step 3: Calculate the Power Reduction Per Hour** $$ \Delta P = P_1 - P_2 $$ $$ \Delta P = 24 \text{ kW} - 18 \text{ kW} = 6 \text{ kW} $$ **Explanation:** Every hour the pump runs, 6 kWh of electrical energy is saved. ### **Step 4: Calculate the Annual Energy Savings (kWh/year)** $$ \Delta E_{annual} = \Delta P \times H $$ $$ \Delta E_{annual} = 6 \text{ kW} \times 5000 \text{ hr} = 30{,}000 \text{ kWh/year} $$ **Explanation:** Since the pump runs 5000 hours a year, the total electricity saved is 6 kW × 5000 h = 30,000 kWh annually. ### **Step 5: Calculate the Annual Monetary Savings (₹/year)** $$ S = \Delta E_{annual} \times C_e $$ $$ S = 30{,}000 \text{ kWh} \times \text{₹}\,7/\text{kWh} = \text{₹}\,2{,}10{,}000 \text{ per year} $$ **Explanation:** At ₹ 7 per kWh, the mill saves ₹ 2.10 lakh in electricity bills every year. ### **Step 6: Calculate the Simple Payback Period** $$ SPP = \frac{C_0}{S} $$ $$ SPP = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{2{,}10{,}000} \text{ years} $$ $$ SPP = 0.714 \text{ years} \approx 8.6 \text{ months} $$ **Explanation:** The VFD investment of ₹ 1.5 lakh is recovered in less than 9 months. This is an excellent ECM and is highly recommended for implementation. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) Benchmarking **Problem Statement:** A dairy plant produced 12,000 tonnes of milk powder in 2023 using 3,60,000 kWh of electrical energy and 1,800 tonnes of oil equivalent (toe) of thermal energy. **Given Data:** - Production: $Q = 12{,}000 \text{ tonnes}$ - Electrical energy: $E_e = 3{,}60{,}000 \text{ kWh}$ - Thermal energy: $E_{th} = 1{,}800 \text{ toe}$ - 1 toe = $11{,}630 \text{ kWh}$ (standard conversion) **Find:** Total SEC in kWh/tonne. Compare with best-in-class benchmark of 180 kWh/tonne (electrical only). --- ### **Step 1: Convert Thermal Energy to kWh** $$ E_{th, kWh} = E_{th} \times 11{,}630 $$ $$ E_{th, kWh} = 1{,}800 \times 11{,}630 = 2.0934 \times 10^7 \text{ kWh} $$ **Explanation:** 1 toe = 11,630 kWh (BEE standard). So 1,800 toe equals 2.0934 crore kWh. ### **Step 2: Compute Total Energy in kWh** $$ E_{total} = E_e + E_{th, kWh} $$ $$ E_{total} = 3.6 \times 10^5 + 2.0934 \times 10^7 = 2.1294 \times 10^7 \text{ kWh} $$ **Explanation:** Adding the electrical and thermal energies gives the total energy footprint. ### **Step 3: Compute Total Specific Energy Consumption** $$ SEC_{total} = \frac{E_{total}}{Q} $$ $$ SEC_{total} = \frac{2.1294 \times 10^7}{12{,}000} = 1774.5 \text{ kWh/tonne} $$ **Explanation:** Each tonne of milk powder consumes ~1774.5 kWh of total energy. ### **Step 4: Compute Electrical SEC separately and compare** $$ SEC_{elec} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^5}{12{,}000} = 30 \text{ kWh/tonne} $$ **Comparison:** The plant's electrical SEC of 30 kWh/tonne is **far below** the industry benchmark of 180 kWh/tonne, indicating **excellent** electrical efficiency. However, the **thermal SEC dominates** the energy footprint (1744.5 kWh/tonne), so the audit should focus on **boiler efficiency, heat recovery, and insulation** improvements. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose Which Type of Audit > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision Matrix for KTU Exam — Selection of Audit Type** | Situation / Requirement | Recommended Audit Type | Justification | |--------------------------|------------------------|---------------| | First-time study of a new plant | Preliminary | Quick orientation, low cost | | Need a bankable document for a ₹ 50 crore boiler project | Investment-Grade | Finance-grade precision required | | Annual compliance audit under PAT scheme | Detailed | Accuracy needed for SEC targets | | BEE star-rating of a building | Detailed | Energy simulation + measurement | | In-house energy team doing a quick check | Preliminary | Limited resources, limited scope | | ESCO (Energy Service Company) signing a performance contract | Investment-Grade | Guarantees on savings must be precise | | ISO 50001 certification audit | Detailed | Required for energy review clause | | Identify "low-hanging fruit" only | Preliminary | Quick wins, no deep analysis | ## 3.4 Cost-Benefit Analytical Steps (for any ECM) > [!NOTE] > **Universal 6-Step Method for Evaluating an ECM in a KTU Exam** 1. **Baseline Definition** — Measure the energy consumption before the ECM. 2. **Energy Saved Per Year** — $\Delta E = E_{before} - E_{after}$ (kWh/year). 3. **Monetary Savings Per Year** — $S = \Delta E \times C_e$ (₹/year). 4. **Total Capital Cost** — $C_0$ = Equipment + Installation + Civil (₹). 5. **Compute Decision Metrics** — SPP, ROI, NPV, IRR. 6. **Decision Rule:** - If $SPP < 2 \text{ years}$ → Implement immediately. - If $2 \leq SPP \leq 5$ → Implement with caution. - If $SPP > 5$ → Reject or defer unless mandated. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Energy Audit Types ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ENERGY AUDIT] --> B[Type 1
Preliminary Audit] A --> C[Type 2
Detailed Audit] A --> D[Type 3
Investment Grade Audit] B --> B1[Duration 1 to 3 days] B --> B2[Visual Inspection] B --> B3[Low Hanging Fruit ID] B --> B4[Accuracy plus or minus 40 percent] C --> C1[Duration 2 to 6 weeks] C --> C2[Mass and Energy Balance] C --> C3[Instrumented Measurement] C --> C4[Accuracy plus or minus 15 percent] D --> D1[Duration 2 to 6 months] D --> D2[Bankable Feasibility] D --> D3[NPV and IRR Analysis] D --> D4[Accuracy plus or minus 5 percent] A --> E[Output: Audit Report] E --> E1[Energy Baseline] E --> E2[ECMs with Payback] E --> E3[Implementation Roadmap] ``` ## 4.2 Energy Audit Methodology — 3-Phase Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Phase 1
Pre Audit] --> P2[Phase 2
On Site Audit] P2 --> P3[Phase 3
Post Audit] P1 --> P1a[Define Scope] P1 --> P1b[Collect Utility Bills] P1 --> P1c[Walk Through Survey] P1 --> P1d[Plan Instruments] P2 --> P2a[Install Data Loggers] P2 --> P2b[Mass Energy Balance] P2 --> P2c[Compute SEC] P2 --> P2d[Identify ECMs] P3 --> P3a[Cost Benefit Analysis] P3 --> P3b[Audit Report] P3 --> P3c[Implementation Support] P3 --> P3d[Measurement and Verification] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Audit Report Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Energy Audit Report] --> S1[Executive Summary] R --> S2[Plant Description] R --> S3[Energy Baseline] R --> S4[Energy Use Analysis] R --> S5[Recommended ECMs] R --> S6[Cost Benefit Analysis] R --> S7[Implementation Plan] S3 --> S3a[Utility Bill Analysis] S3 --> S3b[Sankey Diagram] S3 --> S3c[SEC Benchmarking] S4 --> S4a[Major Equipment List] S4 --> S4b[Load Profile] S4 --> S4c[Energy Losses] S5 --> S5a[No Cost Measures] S5 --> S5b[Low Cost Measures] S5 --> S5c[Capital Intensive] S6 --> S6a[Simple Payback] S6 --> S6b[ROI NPV IRR] S6 --> S6c[Sensitivity Analysis] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Energy Flow in a Typical Industrial Plant (Sankey-Style Mapping) ```mermaid flowchart LR IN[Energy Input
Electricity and Fuel] --> P1[Boiler] IN --> P2[Compressed Air] IN --> P3[Lighting] IN --> P4[Motors and Pumps] IN --> P5[HVAC] P1 --> L1[Heat Losses] P1 --> U1[Useful Steam] U1 --> PRO[Process Heating] P2 --> L2[Air Leakages] P2 --> U2[Useful Work] P3 --> U3[Lighting Output] P4 --> L3[Motor Losses] P4 --> U4[Mechanical Output] P5 --> L4[Thermal Losses] P5 --> U5[Comfort Cooling] L1 --> WASTE[Total Wasted Energy] L2 --> WASTE L3 --> WASTE L4 --> WASTE U1 --> USEFUL[Total Useful Energy] U2 --> USEFUL U3 --> USEFUL U4 --> USEFUL U5 --> USEFUL ``` ## 4.5 Decision Tree — Selection of Audit Type ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Need for Energy Audit] --> Q1{Is this a first
time study?} Q1 -- Yes --> TYPE1[Preliminary Audit] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is major capital
investment planned?} Q2 -- No --> TYPE2[Detailed Audit] Q2 -- Yes --> TYPE3[Investment Grade Audit] TYPE1 --> Q3{Found significant
opportunities?} Q3 -- Yes --> TYPE2 Q3 -- No --> STOP1[Stop: Low priority] TYPE2 --> Q4{Need bankable
finance document?} Q4 -- Yes --> TYPE3 Q4 -- No --> IMPL1[Implement ECMs] TYPE3 --> IMPL2[Secure Project Finance] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (Module 2 | 3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]** **Q: Define Energy Audit. List the three main types of energy audits.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** An Energy Audit is the systematic and documented process of inspecting, surveying, and analysing the energy use of a building, process, or system to identify energy conservation opportunities (ECOs) and recommend cost-effective measures. **Three Types:** 1. **Preliminary (Walk-through) Audit** — quick, low-cost, visual inspection. 2. **Detailed (Comprehensive) Audit** — instrumented, in-depth study with mass/energy balance. 3. **Investment-Grade Audit (IGEA)** — bankable, finance-ready audit with NPV/IRR analysis. --- ### **Question 2 (Module 2 | 3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplemented Pattern]** **Q: Differentiate between Preliminary and Detailed Energy Audits (any four points).** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Preliminary Audit | Detailed Audit | |-----------|------------------|----------------| | **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks | | **Cost** | Low | Medium to High | | **Instruments** | Visual + clamp meter | Power loggers, flow meters, flue gas analyser, IR camera | | **Accuracy of savings** | ± 30 to 50% | ± 10 to 20% | | **Output** | Indicative ECOs | Quantified ECMs with payback | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Part B Style]** **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the three types of energy audits with their objectives, methodology, and deliverables. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 50 kW motor in a pump operates at 75% load factor for 6000 hours per year. A proposed VFD will reduce the load factor to 55%. Electricity costs ₹ 8/kWh. VFD cost is ₹ 2,00,000. Calculate: (i) Annual energy savings (kWh), (ii) Annual monetary savings (₹), (iii) Simple payback period. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]** **The three types of energy audits are:** **1. Preliminary Energy Audit (Walk-through Audit) [2 marks]** - **Objective:** Quick identification of major energy uses and "low-hanging fruit" energy-saving opportunities with minimal cost and time. - **Methodology:** Visual inspection, review of utility bills, brief operator interviews, identification of obvious inefficiencies (idle equipment, leaks, poor housekeeping). - **Duration:** 1–3 days; **Cost:** ~0.5–1% of annual energy cost; **Tools:** Visual, clamp meter, nameplate data. - **Deliverable:** Short report listing major energy users and indicative ECOs. **2. Detailed Energy Audit (Comprehensive Audit) [2.5 marks]** - **Objective:** Quantify energy consumption and savings accurately, develop a detailed implementation plan. - **Methodology:** 7–14 days of instrumented data logging; mass and energy balances for every major process; calculation of Specific Energy Consumption (SEC); benchmarking with best practice. - **Tools:** Power analyzers, ultrasonic flow meters, flue gas analysers, thermographic cameras, lux meters. - **Deliverable:** Comprehensive report with Sankey diagram, SEC tables, cost-benefit analysis of all ECMs. **3. Investment-Grade Energy Audit (IGEA) [2.5 marks]** - **Objective:** Provide a **bankable** report that justifies large capital investments (e.g., cogeneration, new boilers, HVAC retrofits). - **Methodology:** All features of a detailed audit, plus engineering design, vendor quotes, and full financial analysis (NPV, IRR, sensitivity, risk assessment). - **Accuracy:** ± 5–10%; **Output:** Feasibility report acceptable to banks and financial institutions. --- ### **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Given:** - $P = 50 \text{ kW}$, $L_1 = 75\% = 0.75$, $L_2 = 55\% = 0.55$, $H = 6000 \text{ hr/year}$, $C_e = \text{₹}\,8/\text{kWh}$, $C_0 = \text{₹}\,2{,}00{,}000$. **Step 1: Power before VFD [1 mark]** $$ P_1 = 50 \times 0.75 = 37.5 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 2: Power after VFD [1 mark]** $$ P_2 = 50 \times 0.55 = 27.5 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 3: Power reduction per hour [0.5 mark]** $$ \Delta P = 37.5 - 27.5 = 10 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 4: Annual energy savings [1 mark]** $$ \Delta E = 10 \times 6000 = 60{,}000 \text{ kWh/year} $$ **Step 5: Annual monetary savings [1 mark]** $$ S = 60{,}000 \times 8 = \text{₹}\,4{,}80{,}000 \text{ per year} $$ **Step 6: Simple Payback Period [2.5 marks]** $$ SPP = \frac{C_0}{S} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{4{,}80{,}000} = 0.417 \text{ years} $$ $$ \boxed{SPP \approx 5 \text{ months}} $$ **Conclusion:** Since the SPP is well under 1 year, the VFD installation is highly recommended. --- ### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Question* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Part B Style]** **Q: (a)** Explain the standard three-phase methodology of conducting an energy audit with a neat flow diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An industry consumes 5,00,000 kWh/year to produce 2,500 tonnes of output. The benchmark specific energy consumption (SEC) is 150 kWh/tonne. The energy cost is ₹ 6/kWh. If two ECMs are proposed: - **ECM 1:** Capital ₹ 5,00,000, Saves 30,000 kWh/year - **ECM 2:** Capital ₹ 3,00,000, Saves 20,000 kWh/year Determine for each ECM: Annual savings (₹), Simple Payback Period, and ROI. Recommend which ECM should be implemented first. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]** **The three phases of an energy audit are:** **Phase 1 — Pre-Audit Phase [2 marks]** - Define scope, boundaries, and objectives. - Form the audit team (lead auditor, process engineer, instrumentation expert). - Collect historical utility bills, plant drawings, production records. - Conduct a walk-through to identify major energy users. - Plan the data collection and instrumentation schedule. **Phase 2 — On-Site Audit Phase [2.5 marks]** - Install calibrated instruments (power loggers, flow meters, temperature sensors, flue gas analyser). - Conduct continuous measurements for 7–14 days (24–72 hrs for spot checks). - Develop mass and energy balances for each major subsystem. - Compute Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and benchmark. - Identify and quantify Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs). **Phase 3 — Post-Audit Phase [2.5 marks]** - Prepare the Energy Audit Report. - Perform cost-benefit analysis (SPP, ROI, NPV, IRR). - Prioritise ECMs based on economics. - Present findings to management. - Support implementation and M&V;
