```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Level 1 Field Sensors and Meters] --> L2[Level 2 Data Acquisition DAQ and Loggers]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Data Validation and Outlier Rejection]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Baseline Model and CUSUM Engine]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 MIS Reports and Dashboards]
    L5 --> L6[Level 6 Management Review and Action]
    L6 -.Feedback.-> L1
```

### 4.3 CUSUM Interpretation Map

The following schematic illustrates how a CUSUM chart is interpreted at a glance, a frequently tested concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[CUSUM Trend Flat] --> R1[Performance matches target]
    P2[CUSUM Trend Rising] --> R2[Performance deteriorating: act now]
    P3[CUSUM Trend Falling] --> R3[Performance improving: lock in gains]
    P4[Slope Inflection Point] --> R4[Probable intervention timestamp]
```

### 4.4 M&T Information Hierarchy

The diagram below clarifies the **information pyramid** of an M&T system. Each level adds value but adds latency.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Raw[Raw Meter Pulses] --> Hourly[Hourly Averages]
    Hourly --> Daily[Daily Energy Report]
    Daily --> Weekly[Weekly SEC and EnPI]
    Weekly --> Monthly[Monthly Variance Report]
    Monthly --> Annual[Annual M&T Audit and PAT Submission]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Drawings?** Industrial M&T systems are inherently *informational*. The physical hardware (meters, CTs, PTs) is well covered in instrumentation courses. What is uniquely tested in the KTU OEPET722 syllabus is the **flow of information and decisions**, which is best represented as a functional block architecture.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEPET722]`

**Define Monitoring and Targeting. List its four essential stages.**

**Model Answer:**

Monitoring and Targeting (M&T) is a systematic technique of measuring, recording, and analyzing energy consumption in an organization, comparing it against a predetermined target (or baseline), and taking corrective action when deviations occur.

The four essential stages are:

1. **Measurement** — continuous recording of energy and production at sub-metered levels.
2. **Comparison with Target** — comparing actual consumption to the baseline.
3. **Analysis of Deviation** — using CUSUM, variance analysis, and root cause tools.
4. **Corrective Action** — operational and engineering interventions to close the gap.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "M&T is a one-time audit" — this loses full marks. M&T is a *continuous* process, not a single event. Use the word "continuous" or "ongoing" in your answer.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEPET722]`

**What is a CUSUM chart? How is it used in an M&T system?**

**Model Answer:**

A **CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) chart** is a statistical tool that plots the running cumulative difference between actual energy consumption and the target (or baseline) consumption over successive time periods.

The formula is:
$$C_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (E_{actual,i} - E_{target,i})$$

In an M&T system, the CUSUM chart is used to:

- Detect **small, persistent drifts** in performance that a normal bar chart would hide.
- Identify the **inflection point** — the time at which performance changed, often corresponding to a specific event (e.g., equipment failure, change in raw material, new shift in-charge).
- Provide a **visual alarm**: a rising slope means performance is worsening; a falling slope means it is improving.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse CUSUM with a simple cumulative *consumption* plot. CUSUM is the cumulative *deviation* from target. This distinction is commonly tested.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions in the 2024 scheme are **modular internal choice** — within a module, the student may answer *either* of the two alternatives offered. Both questions below are designed to be of equal difficulty and cover the same Learning Outcomes (CO3 of OEPET722).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEPET722]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **methodology of establishing an energy baseline** for an industrial plant using simple linear regression. State the underlying assumptions, formula, and goodness-of-fit criterion. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing unit recorded the following monthly data for a furnace:

| Month | Production (tonnes) | Energy (MWh) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 80 | 600 |
| 2 | 100 | 690 |
| 3 | 70 | 560 |
| 4 | 120 | 780 |
| 5 | 90 | 640 |

Build the regression equation $E = a + bP$. If next month's planned production is 110 tonnes, calculate the **target energy** and the **expected SEC**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
An energy baseline (EnB) is a quantitative reference that quantifies the energy consumption of a plant (or system) over a defined period, used as the basis for setting targets and comparing actual performance.

**Step 2 — Model Assumption:**
The energy consumption $E$ is assumed to be linearly related to production $P$:
$$E = a + bP + \epsilon$$
where $a$ is the fixed (no-load) consumption and $b$ is the variable (specific) consumption per unit of production. The error term $\epsilon$ is assumed to be normally distributed with zero mean and constant variance.

**Step 3 — Estimation (OLS):**
The slope and intercept are obtained by minimizing the sum of squared residuals:
$$b = \dfrac{n \sum P_i E_i - \sum P_i \sum E_i}{n \sum P_i^2 - (\sum P_i)^2}, \qquad a = \bar{E} - b\bar{P}$$

**Step 4 — Goodness of Fit:**
The coefficient of determination $R^2$ is computed as:
$$R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (E_i - \hat{E_i})^2}{\sum (E_i - \bar{E})^2}$$

A value of $R^2 \geq 0.80$ is generally required for the baseline to be considered reliable in an industrial M&T context.

**Step 5 — Caveats:**
The model is valid only for the *range of production* used in fitting, and within stable operating conditions (no major changes in equipment, fuel quality, or product mix).

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Tabulate Sums:**

- $n = 5$
- $\sum P_i = 80 + 100 + 70 + 120 + 90 = 460$
- $\sum E_i = 600 + 690 + 560 + 780 + 640 = 3270$
- $\sum P_i^2 = 6400 + 10000 + 4900 + 14400 + 8100 = 43{,}800$
- $\sum P_i E_i = (80 \times 600) + (100 \times 690) + (70 \times 560) + (120 \times 780) + (90 \times 640)$
- $\sum P_i E_i = 48{,}000 + 69{,}000 + 39{,}200 + 93{,}600 + 57{,}600 = 307{,}400$

**Step 2 — Compute Slope $b$:**
$$b = \dfrac{5 \times 307{,}400 - 460 \times 3270}{5 \times 43{,}800 - (460)^2}$$

Numerator:
$$1{,}537{,}000 - 1{,}504{,}200 = 32{,}800$$

Denominator:
$$219{,}000 - 211{,}600 = 7{,}400$$

$$b = \dfrac{32{,}800}{7{,}400} = 4.432 \;\text{MWh/tonne}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Intercept $a$:**
$$\bar{P} = 460/5 = 92, \quad \bar{E} = 3270/5 = 654$$
$$a = 654 - 4.432 \times 92 = 654 - 407.74 = 246.26 \;\text{MWh}$$

**Step 4 — Regression Equation:**
$$\boxed{E = 246.26 + 4.432 \cdot P}$$

**Step 5 — Target for $P = 110$ tonnes:**
$$E_{target} = 246.26 + 4.432 \times 110 = 246.26 + 487.52 = 733.78 \;\text{MWh}$$

**Step 6 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):**
$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{733.78 \;\text{MWh}}{110 \;\text{tonnes}} = 6.67 \;\text{MWh/tonne}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget that the **target** is a *predicted total energy* (intercept + slope × production), not just the variable component. The intercept represents the no-load consumption that *will still occur* even at zero production. Marking schemes explicitly allocate marks for $a + bP$, not just $bP$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEPET722]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop Monitoring and Targeting system** as applied to an industrial energy management program. Highlight the role of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The following CUSUM data is available for a chiller plant, with target energy = 50 MWh/day. Calculate the CUSUM values and **comment on the trend**.

| Day | Actual Energy (MWh) | Target (MWh) | CUSUM |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 52 | 50 | — |
| 2 | 53 | 50 | — |
| 3 | 55 | 50 | — |
| 4 | 54 | 50 | — |
| 5 | 56 | 50 | — |
| 6 | 57 | 50 | — |
| 7 | 55 | 50 | — |

*(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Block Diagram Description (mark allocation: 3 marks for diagram, 4 for explanation):**

1. **Energy Boundary Definition Block:** Establishes the physical and operational scope of the M&T system. For a chiller plant, the boundary includes chillers, pumps, cooling tower, AHUs, and associated electrical panels.

2. **Sub-Metering and Data Acquisition Block:** Installs dedicated kWh meters on each major load. Data is captured at intervals of 15 min to 1 hour by a data logger.

3. **Production / Driver Variable Block:** Records the independent variable (e.g., cooling demand in TR, or ambient temperature) that drives energy use.

4. **Baseline Model Block:** Stores the regression equation (e.g., $E = a + b \cdot TR$) and the target for the period.

5. **Variance Computation Block:** Daily comparison of $E_{actual}$ vs. $E_{target}$; calculates $\Delta E$ and CUSUM.

6. **Decision / Alarm Block:** Triggers an alarm when $\Delta E$ exceeds the threshold (typically 2–5%) or CUSUM slope changes sign.

7. **Corrective Action and Feedback Block:** Engineers implement actions; new data feeds back into the model.

**Explanation:** The system is a classic **closed-loop negative feedback** controller. Any deviation from the target generates an alarm, which triggers a corrective action, which in turn modifies the actual consumption, bringing the system back to the target. Over time, the model itself is updated, and the target is tightened, leading to **continuous improvement** — the cornerstone of ISO 50001.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

Compute the daily deviation $(E_{actual} - E_{target})$ and accumulate:

| Day | Actual (MWh) | Target (MWh) | Deviation | CUSUM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 52 | 50 | +2 | +2 |
| 2 | 53 | 50 | +3 | +5 |
| 3 | 55 | 50 | +5 | +10 |
| 4 | 54 | 50 | +4 | +14 |
| 5 | 56 | 50 | +6 | +20 |
| 6 | 57 | 50 | +7 | +27 |
| 7 | 55 | 50 | +5 | +32 |

**Final CUSUM at end of Day 7 = +32 MWh.**

**Trend Comment:**

- The CUSUM is **continuously and steeply rising** throughout the 7-day period.
- This indicates that the chiller plant is **consistently over-consuming** energy relative to the target — performance is **deteriorating**.
- The deviations are not random noise (which would oscillate around zero); they are **systematically positive**, pointing to a real, persistent problem.
- **Probable root causes** to investigate: refrigerant undercharge, condenser fouling, chilled water pump wear, sensor drift, or a recent change in cooling load profile.
- The **slope** of the CUSUM chart is approximately $+32/7 \approx +4.6$ MWh/day, meaning the chiller plant is losing about 4.6 MWh/day more than expected. At an industrial tariff of ₹7/kWh, this is ≈ ₹32,200/day of avoidable cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When computing CUSUM, students often add the deviation *to the previous CUSUM* but **omit the day-1 deviation**. The first CUSUM is **not** zero; it equals the day-1 deviation. This is a frequently penalized error. Also, marks are reserved for **interpretation of the trend**, not just calculation — always write 2–3 lines of qualitative conclusion.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **M&T is continuous**, not a one-time audit. State this in every definition question.
- ✅ The four stages are **Measurement → Target → Comparison → Action** (a closed loop).
- ✅ The **regression equation** $E = a + bP$ is the workhorse of M&T. Memorize both formulas for slope $b$ and intercept $a$.
- ✅ Always check **$R^2 \geq 0.80$** before accepting a regression baseline. A low $R^2$ means the model is not trustworthy.
- ✅ **CUSUM** = cumulative *deviation*, not cumulative consumption. A rising CUSUM = worsening; falling = improving.
- ✅ **SEC** is a per-unit metric; **EnPI** is the broader performance indicator; **EnB** is the baseline.
- ✅ The **intercept $a$** represents fixed (no-load) losses. The **slope $b$** represents variable consumption.
- ✅ A **variance > 2–5%** triggers investigation; < 2% is normal statistical noise.
- ✅ The PAT scheme (BEE) and **ISO 50001:2018** *legally* require M&T for Designated Consumers — this is a frequently asked 1-mark/2-mark question.
- ✅ **Sign convention for CUSUM:** Positive = over-consumption. Many students lose marks by inverting the sign.
- ✅ **Regression validity range:** Never extrapolate a baseline beyond the production range used in fitting.
- ✅ A **block diagram** with 6–7 blocks (boundary, meters, baseline, variance, alarm, action, feedback) is the KTU-expected answer for full-mark Q-B(a) questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Information and Data with Management Information System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Information and Data with Management Information System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Management Information System (MIS)** is a formalized, computer-based (or hybrid paper-and-digital) framework that collects, processes, stores, analyzes, and disseminates **information** derived from raw **data** to support managerial decision-making, operational control, and strategic planning within an organization. In the specific context of **Energy Management and Audit**, MIS refers to the integrated system used to monitor, record, evaluate, and report **energy-related data** (electricity, fuel, steam, compressed air, water, etc.) so that energy performance can be benchmarked, deviations diagnosed, and corrective actions initiated.

**Information**, in the strict MIS sense, is **data that has been processed, contextualized, and organized into a meaningful form** such that it reduces uncertainty for the decision-maker. **Data**, by contrast, are raw, unprocessed facts and figures (e.g., a kilowatt-hour reading on a meter).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Students must clearly distinguish **Data** (raw measurement), **Information** (processed data), and **Knowledge** (information applied to action). The MIS is the *engine* that performs this transformation automatically.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Car Dashboard Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car. The **fuel gauge**, **speedometer**, **RPM meter**, and **engine temperature indicator** are all displaying *processed* numbers. Those numbers came from raw sensor signals (data) inside the engine. The **dashboard** (analogous to MIS) takes those signals, processes them, and shows you only what you need to know — speed, fuel left, engine health — so you can *drive safely*.
>
> Now extend this to a **factory**. Hundreds of energy meters are scattered across machines. A MIS in an energy audit acts as the *factory dashboard*: it gathers the raw readings (kWh, m³ of gas, tonnes of steam) every minute, converts them into useful information (SEC, power factor, peak demand, wastage %), and presents them to the **Energy Manager** so they can "drive" the plant toward efficiency — just as a driver uses a dashboard to drive safely.

### 1.3 The Data–Information–Knowledge–Wisdom (DIKW) Hierarchy

This is one of the most frequently examined concepts in KTU Module 3.

| Level | Definition | Energy Audit Example |
|-------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Data** | Raw, unprocessed facts | "1472 kWh" recorded at 10:00 AM |
| **Information** | Data given context and meaning | "1472 kWh consumed by Boiler-2 between 9–10 AM, 18% above average" |
| **Knowledge** | Information applied to understand patterns | "Boiler-2 spikes correlate with morning startup of Unit-3" |
| **Wisdom** | Knowledge used to make strategic decisions | "Reschedule Unit-3 startup to 11 AM to avoid peak tariff and reduce stress" |

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics in Energy MIS

Key standard metrics highlighted in KTU syllabus:

- **SEC (Specific Energy Consumption)** — measured in **kWh/tonne** or **MJ/kg** of product
- **EUI (Energy Use Intensity)** — measured in **kWh/m²/year** for buildings
- **Power Factor (pf)** — dimensionless, ideally **> 0.95**
- **Load Factor** — dimensionless, ideally **> 0.75**
- **CUSUM (Cumulative Sum)** — dimensionless statistical tool
- **Baseline Year Reference** — typically the **base year** against which savings are computed

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components / Architecture of an Energy MIS

A complete Energy MIS has **five inter-linked functional blocks**:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer** — Sensors, smart meters, SCADA, manual logbooks. Captures raw energy and process variables.
2. **Data Storage Layer** — Centralized database / data historian (e.g., SQL, OSIsoft PI). Stores time-stamped data.
3. **Data Processing / Analysis Layer** — Performs calculations: SEC, regression, CUSUM, normalization (TMD, production volume).
4. **Reporting & Visualization Layer** — Dashboards, Sankey diagrams, trend charts, KPI scorecards.
5. **Decision / Control Layer** — Alarms, automated control loops, management reports for ISO 50001 compliance.

### 2.2 Types of Information Systems Relevant to Energy Audit

- **Transaction Processing System (TPS)** — handles meter reading entries, billing.
- **Management Information System (MIS)** — generates scheduled reports (daily, monthly energy use).
- **Decision Support System (DSS)** — "what-if" modeling for retrofit scenarios.
- **Executive Information System (EIS)** — top-level KPIs for plant head.
- **Expert System** — rules-based diagnostic for fault detection (e.g., compressed air leaks).

### 2.3 Data Analysis Techniques Used in Energy MIS

The MIS does not merely store numbers — it *analyzes* them. The principal techniques demanded by KTU Module 3 are:

#### (a) Normalization
Raw energy consumption is meaningless without normalization against a **driving variable** (production tonnage, degree-days, occupancy).

#### (b) Baseline Establishment (Regression)
A mathematical model linking energy use (E) to drivers (X₁, X₂ …):

$$E = a_0 + a_1 X_1 + a_2 X_2 + \cdots + \epsilon$$

#### (c) CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) Analysis
Detects small but persistent drifts in performance that are invisible in raw charts.

#### (d) Sankey Diagram
Visualizes energy *flows* and *losses* through a system; mandatory for energy audit reports.

#### (e) Benchmarking
Compares SEC against industry best practice or own past performance.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear regularly in KTU Part B questions.**

| # | Formula | Variables / Meaning | Units | Use Case |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------|----------|
| 1 | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P}$ | $E_{total}$ = total energy, $P$ = production | $\text{MJ/kg}$ or $\text{kWh/t}$ | Energy intensity per unit output |
| 2 | $EUI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A}$ | $A$ = floor area | $\text{kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$ | Building benchmarking |
| 3 | $CUSUM_i = \sum_{k=1}^{i} (x_k - \mu_0)$ | $x_k$ = reading, $\mu_0$ = target mean | dimensionless | Drift detection |
| 4 | $E_{norm} = E_{actual} \times \dfrac{P_{base}}{P_{actual}}$ | $P_{base}$ = reference production | $\text{kWh}$ | Production normalization |
| 5 | $LF = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}}$ | Load Factor | dimensionless | Demand analysis |
| 6 | $PF = \dfrac{P}{S} = \cos\phi$ | Real power / Apparent power | dimensionless | Power quality |
| 7 | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu}$ | Coefficient of Variation | dimensionless | Variability check |
| 8 | $E_{savings} = E_{baseline} - E_{reported}$ | Energy saved | $\text{kWh}$ | M&V (IPMVP) |
| 9 | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | Goodness of fit | dimensionless | Regression validation |
| 10 | $EnPI = \dfrac{E}{X_{driver}}$ | Energy Performance Indicator | variable | ISO 50001 KPI |

> [!NOTE]
> In LaTeX tables, always use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. The pipe character is reserved as a column separator by markdown.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

In **ISO 50001:2018 Energy Management Systems**, an Energy MIS is the *backbone*. Without it:
- **Baseline** cannot be established → no savings claim is valid.
- **EnPIs** cannot be tracked → certification fails.
- **Deviations** cannot be detected → savings erode silently.

Industries relying heavily on Energy MIS: **Cement, Steel, Aluminum, Pulp & Paper, Textile, Data Centers, Commercial Buildings**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) with Normalization

**Problem Context:** A steel plant produced 1,200 tonnes of crude steel using 4,80,000 kWh in April. In May, production rose to 1,500 tonnes due to a new order, and consumption rose to 5,40,000 kWh. Is performance *better* or *worse*?

#### Step 1: Compute actual SEC for both months

$$\text{SEC}_{\text{April}} = \frac{4{,}80{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{1{,}200 \text{ t}} = 400 \text{ kWh/t}$$

**Logic:** Divide total energy consumed by production output to get intensity.

#### Step 2: Compute actual SEC for May

$$\text{SEC}_{\text{May}} = \frac{5{,}40{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{1{,}500 \text{ t}} = 360 \text{ kWh/t}$$

**Logic:** Same arithmetic, applied to the new month's data.

#### Step 3: Compute normalized SEC for May (using April as baseline)

$$E_{\text{May,norm}} = E_{\text{May,actual}} \times \frac{P_{\text{April}}}{P_{\text{May}}}$$

$$E_{\text{May,norm}} = 5{,}40{,}000 \times \frac{1{,}200}{1{,}500} = 4{,}32{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\text{SEC}_{\text{May,norm}} = \frac{4{,}32{,}000}{1{,}200} = 360 \text{ kWh/t}$$

**Logic:** This *answers the question*: May's normalized SEC equals 360 kWh/t, which is **lower than April's 400 kWh/t** by 10%. Therefore the plant's energy performance *improved* — even though absolute kWh increased.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Many students incorrectly conclude that increased kWh = worse performance. The whole purpose of MIS-based analysis is to *normalize* and reveal the *true* trend.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) Drift Detection

**Problem:** A chiller consumes an average (target) of **500 kWh/day**. Ten days of readings are: 510, 505, 512, 508, 515, 520, 525, 522, 528, 530. Determine if drift exists.

#### Step 1: Compute daily deviation from target

$$d_k = x_k - \mu_0$$

| Day | $x_k$ | $d_k = x_k - 500$ |
|-----|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | 510 | +10 |
| 2 | 505 | +5 |
| 3 | 512 | +12 |
| 4 | 508 | +8 |
| 5 | 515 | +15 |
| 6 | 520 | +20 |
| 7 | 525 | +25 |
| 8 | 522 | +22 |
| 9 | 528 | +28 |
| 10 | 530 | +30 |

**Logic:** CUSUM analysis works on deviations, not raw values.

#### Step 2: Compute cumulative sum

$$\text{CUSUM}_i = \sum_{k=1}^{i} d_k$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_1 = 10$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_2 = 10 + 5 = 15$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_3 = 15 + 12 = 27$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_4 = 27 + 8 = 35$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_5 = 35 + 15 = 50$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_6 = 50 + 20 = 70$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_7 = 70 + 25 = 95$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_8 = 95 + 22 = 117$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_9 = 117 + 28 = 145$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_{10} = 145 + 30 = 175$$

**Logic:** The CUSUM grows monotonically — this is a *clear signature of systematic drift*, meaning the chiller's performance is degrading gradually (likely fouled condenser or refrigerant undercharge).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CUSUM and not simple average?** Average would still be ~519.5 kWh/day. CUSUM reveals the *trend* — average cannot tell you if a system is *slowly deteriorating*. This is the unique power of MIS-based statistical analysis.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Linear Regression Baseline Model

**Problem:** Establish an energy baseline for a boiler using 6 months of data. Monthly steam output (X, tonnes) and fuel consumption (E, GJ) are given.

| Month | X (tonnes) | E (GJ) |
|-------|------------|--------|
| 1 | 100 | 320 |
| 2 | 120 | 360 |
| 3 | 140 | 410 |
| 4 | 160 | 470 |
| 5 | 180 | 525 |
| 6 | 200 | 580 |

#### Step 1: Compute sums

$$\sum X = 100 + 120 + 140 + 160 + 180 + 200 = 900$$

$$\sum E = 320 + 360 + 410 + 470 + 525 + 580 = 2665$$

$$\sum X^2 = 10000 + 14400 + 19600 + 25600 + 32400 + 40000 = 142000$$

$$\sum XE = 32000 + 43200 + 57400 + 75200 + 94500 + 116000 = 418300$$

#### Step 2: Compute slope and intercept

$$a_1 = \frac{n \sum XE - \sum X \sum E}{n \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$$

$$a_1 = \frac{6 \times 418300 - 900 \times 2665}{6 \times 142000 - 900^2}$$

$$a_1 = \frac{2509800 - 2398500}{852000 - 810000} = \frac{111300}{42000} = 2.65$$

$$a_0 = \frac{\sum E - a_1 \sum X}{n} = \frac{2665 - 2.65 \times 900}{6} = \frac{2665 - 2385}{6} = \frac{280}{6} = 46.67$$

#### Step 3: Baseline equation

$$E = 46.67 + 2.65 X$$

**Logic:** This means the boiler has a **fixed baseline loss of 46.67 GJ** (radiation, blowdown) and a **variable loss of 2.65 GJ per tonne of steam**. Now in Month 7, if X = 210 t, predicted E = 46.67 + 2.65 × 210 = **603.17 GJ**.

If actual consumption is 640 GJ, savings opportunity = 640 − 603.17 = **36.83 GJ** (flag for investigation).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Data Sources to MIS Outputs

| Raw Data Source | Data Captured | MIS Processing | Information Output | Decision Triggered |
|-----------------|---------------|----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Electricity meter | kWh, kVAh, pf | Hourly load profiling | Load curve, peak demand | Shift load to off-peak |
| Steam flow meter | kg/h, T, P | Mass-energy balance | Boiler efficiency | Tune combustion |
| Compressed air | m³/min, pressure | Specific power | Leakage % | Repair leaks |
| Cooling tower | °C wet-bulb, flow | Approach & range | Evaporation loss | Trim fan speed |
| Production counter | Tonnes/day | Normalize against base | SEC trend | Energy target setting |
| Ambient sensor | °C, humidity | Degree-day adjustment | Weather-corrected EUI | Compare across seasons |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Information Hierarchy Pyramid (DIKW)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WISDOM - Apply knowledge strategically"] --> B["KNOWLEDGE - Patterns and rules"]
    B --> C["INFORMATION - Contextualized data"]
    C --> D["DATA - Raw facts and figures"]
    D --> E["Sensors and Meters in Plant"]
    E --> D

    style A fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#4d908e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style E fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Raw data flows upward. At each level, *intelligence* is added by the MIS until the energy manager receives **wisdom** — actionable insight for ISO 50001 compliance.

### 4.2 Energy MIS Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Sensors and Smart Meters"] --> L2["Data Acquisition Layer SCADA and RTUs"]
    L2 --> L3["Data Storage Layer Historian and SQL Database"]
    L3 --> L4["Analysis Layer Regression CUSUM Normalization"]
    L4 --> L5["Reporting Layer Dashboards Sankey KPI"]
    L5 --> L6["Decision Layer Energy Manager ISO 50001"]
    L6 --> L7["Control Actions Retrofit and Tuning"]
    L7 -.feedback.-> L1

    style L1 fill:#264653,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style L2 fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style L3 fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style L4 fill:#f4a261,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style L5 fill:#e76f51,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style L6 fill:#6a4c93,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style L7 fill:#1982c4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a **closed-loop energy management system**. The feedback arrow (dotted) shows that control actions change plant operation, which is sensed again — completing the loop.

### 4.3 Data Analysis Workflow Inside the MIS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Raw Energy Data Input"]) --> v1{"Data Valid and Complete?"}
    v1 -- No --> clean["Clean and Impute Missing Values"]
    clean --> v1
    v1 -- Yes --> norm["Normalize Against Driving Variables"]
    norm --> model["Apply Baseline Regression Model"]
    model --> comp["Compare Actual vs Predicted"]
    comp --> cusum["Run CUSUM Drift Test"]
    cusum --> flag{"Deviation Above Threshold?"}
    flag -- Yes --> alarm["Generate Alarm and Report"]
    flag -- No --> ok["Update KPI Dashboard"]
    alarm --> ok
    ok --> endnode(["Information Delivered to Manager"])

    style start fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style alarm fill:#e63946,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style ok fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style endnode fill:#1d3557,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Sankey Energy Flow Visualization (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    source["Energy Input 100 Percent Coal plus Electricity"] --> boiler["Boiler 70"]
    source --> motor["Motors 30"]
    boiler --> steam["Useful Steam 55"]
    boiler --> loss1["Stack Loss 10"]
    boiler --> loss2["Radiation 5"]
    motor --> useful["Useful Mechanical 24"]
    motor --> loss3["Inefficiency 6"]

    style source fill:#264653,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style boiler fill:#f4a261,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style motor fill:#f4a261,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style steam fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style loss1 fill:#e76f51,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style loss2 fill:#e76f51,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style useful fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style loss3 fill:#e76f51,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A Sankey representation of where energy enters, where it flows, and where it is lost. In an actual MIS dashboard, the width of arrows is *proportional* to energy quantity. **Loss streams (orange-red)** are the targets for energy-saving projects.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Distinguish between **Data** and **Information** with a suitable energy-audit example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Data | Information |
|--------|------|-------------|
| Definition | Raw, unprocessed facts | Processed, contextualized data |
| Form | Numbers, symbols, signals | Reports, charts, KPIs |
| Example | "1472 kWh" on a meter display | "Compressor-2 consumed 18% more energy than its baseline this week — investigate valve leakage" |

*Valuation tip:* Award **1 mark** for definition of each, **1 mark** for the distinguishing feature, **1 mark** for energy-audit example. A common error is to give a *non-energy* example (e.g., student marks) — for this paper, the example **must** relate to energy audit.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** What is a **Sankey Diagram**? Why is it important in an Energy MIS?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Sankey Diagram is a **flow visualization** in which the width of arrows is proportional to the quantity (energy, mass, cost) flowing through the system. It is important because:
1. It visually reveals **where the largest energy losses occur** (helpful for prioritizing audit recommendations).
2. It allows the energy manager to *communicate* complex mass/energy balances to non-technical management in a single image.
3. It serves as a **baseline snapshot** against which future Sankey diagrams are compared to show savings.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Part B question in Module 3 carries an internal choice. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both options are provided below for practice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five functional layers of an Energy Management Information System**. Discuss the role of each layer in enabling evidence-based energy decisions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The monthly electricity consumption (in kWh) of a workshop over 7 days is: **450, 460, 455, 470, 465, 475, 480**. The target mean (μ₀) is **450 kWh**. Apply **CUSUM analysis** and comment on the existence of any performance drift. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Layers of an Energy MIS (7 Marks):**

1. **Data Acquisition Layer (1.5 Marks):** Comprises smart meters, current transformers (CTs), flow meters, temperature sensors, and SCADA RTUs. *Role:* Captures raw energy and process variables at high frequency (typically 1-min to 15-min intervals) and transmits them to the next layer.

2. **Data Storage Layer (1 Mark):** Centralized data historian (e.g., OSIsoft PI, Wonderware) or relational database. *Role:* Stores time-stamped, validated data with redundancy and security. Forms the *audit trail* required by ISO 50001.

3. **Data Processing / Analysis Layer (1.5 Marks):** Performs mathematical operations — normalization, regression, CUSUM, energy balances. *Role:* Transforms raw data into *information* and *knowledge*. This is the analytical brain of the MIS.

4. **Reporting & Visualization Layer (1.5 Marks):** Generates dashboards, trend charts, Sankey diagrams, KPI scorecards, automated email reports. *Role:* Delivers information to the *right person at the right time* in an understandable format.

5. **Decision & Control Layer (1.5 Marks):** Triggers alarms, ISO 50001 corrective actions, M&V reports, and feeds back to operation/maintenance teams. *Role:* Closes the loop — *information becomes action*.

*(Include a block diagram similar to Section 4.2 for full marks.)*

**(b) CUSUM Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute deviations** $d_k = x_k - \mu_0 = x_k - 450$:

$$d_1 = 10, \; d_2 = 10, \; d_3 = 5, \; d_4 = 20, \; d_5 = 15, \; d_6 = 25, \; d_7 = 30$$

**Step 2 — Compute cumulative sum:**

$$\text{CUSUM}_1 = 10$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_2 = 20$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_3 = 25$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_4 = 45$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_5 = 60$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_6 = 85$$

$$\text{CUSUM}_7 = 115$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The CUSUM is **monotonically increasing** with a steepening slope. This is a **textbook signature of systematic positive drift** — the workshop is consuming *more* energy than the target every day, and the gap is *widening*. Likely causes: leaking compressed-air line, poor housekeeping (lights/equipment left on), or degrading HVAC efficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students *only* compute the average (≈ 465 kWh) and conclude "slightly above target — acceptable." This loses **3 marks** because it ignores the *trend*. The whole point of CUSUM is that average alone **hides drift**. Always plot or tabulate the cumulative sum.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **DIKW hierarchy** with energy audit examples. How does an MIS convert data into wisdom? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A factory produced the following data for **five** months. Establish a **linear regression baseline** for energy consumption and predict consumption for Month 6 when production is expected to be **260 tonnes**. Comment on the **R²** suitability. **(7 Marks)**

| Month | Production X (tonnes) | Energy E (kWh) |
|-------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | 150 | 5,000 |
| 2 | 180 | 5,800 |
| 3 | 200 | 6,300 |
| 4 | 220 | 6,950 |
| 5 | 240 | 7,500 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DIKW Hierarchy with Energy Examples (7 Marks):**

| Level | Definition | Energy Example |
|-------|-----------|----------------|
| **Data** (1 M) | Raw, isolated facts without context | Meter reads "320 kWh" at 2 PM |
| **Information** (2 M) | Data + context, meaning added | "320 kWh between 1–2 PM, 12% above the same hour last week" |
| **Knowledge** (2 M) | Information interpreted through patterns/experience | "12% excess correlates with simultaneous operation of two chillers due to cooling tower fan failure" |
| **Wisdom** (2 M) | Knowledge applied to strategic action | "Replace cooling tower fan motor now; savings ≈ ₹4 lakh/year; ROI 8 months" |

The **MIS converts data → wisdom** by automated stages: data acquisition (sensors) → processing (regression, normalization) → information output (dashboards) → knowledge inference (rule engines, expert systems) → wisdom delivery (decision-support to top management). Without an MIS, this transformation relies on slow, error-prone manual effort.

**(b) Regression Baseline (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute required sums:**

$$n = 5, \quad \sum X = 990, \quad \sum E = 31550$$

$$\sum X^2 = 150^2 + 180^2 + 200^2 + 220^2 + 240^2 = 22500 + 32400 + 40000 + 48400 + 57600 = 200900$$

$$\sum XE = 150 \cdot 5000 + 180 \cdot 5800 + 200 \cdot 6300 + 220 \cdot 6950 + 240 \cdot 7500$$

$$= 750000 + 1044000 + 1260000 + 1529000 + 1800000 = 6383000$$

**Step 2 — Slope and intercept:**

$$a_1 = \frac{n \sum XE - \sum X \sum E}{n \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$$

$$a_1 = \frac{5 \times 6383000 - 990 \times 31550}{5 \times 200900 - 990^2}$$

$$a_1 = \frac{31915000 - 31234500}{1004500 - 980100} = \frac{680500}{24400} \approx 27.89$$

$$a_0 = \frac{\sum E - a_1 \sum X}{n} = \frac{31550 - 27.89 \times 990}{5}$$

$$a_0 = \frac{31550 - 27611.1}{5} = \frac{3938.9}{5} \approx 787.78$$

**Step 3 — Baseline equation:**

$$E = 787.78 + 27.89 X$$

**Step 4 — Prediction for Month 6 (X = 260):**

$$E_{6,\text{predicted}} = 787.78 + 27.89 \times 260 = 787.78 + 7251.4 \approx 8039.2 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on R²:**
For a 5-point linear dataset with values so close to a straight line, R² will be **> 0.98**, indicating an **excellent fit**. The regression baseline is *suitable* for M&V under IPMVP Option C (whole-facility).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Errors):**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the prediction answer — write "≈ 8039 kWh", not just "8039".
> 2. **Skipping the R² discussion** — even without numerical R², students must *qualitatively* comment ("the points lie almost on a straight line, indicating strong linear correlation").
> 3. **Mis-naming the variables** — confusing slope (a₁) with intercept (a₀). Slope has units of *kWh/tonne*; intercept has units of *kWh*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Analysis of Information and Data with MIS**

- ☐ **Data ≠ Information**: Data is raw; Information is data + context. (Most-tested definition.)
- ☐ **DIKW Pyramid**: Data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom. Remember one energy example for each.
- ☐ **Five layers of Energy MIS**: Acquisition → Storage → Processing → Reporting → Decision/Control.
- ☐ **Types of IS**: TPS, MIS, DSS, EIS, Expert System — know which one *generates routine reports* (MIS) vs. *supports "what-if"* (DSS).
- ☐ **SEC formula**: $\text{SEC} = E / P$ — always normalize to a *driving variable*.
- ☐ **CUSUM**: Detects **drift** that averages miss. Compute deviations from target, then cumulatively sum.
- ☐ **Regression baseline**: $E = a_0 + a_1 X$. Compute sums first, then slope, then intercept.
- ☐ **R² interpretation**: Closer to 1 = better fit. Suitable for IPMVP M&V if R² ≥ 0.75 (industry rule).
- ☐ **Sankey Diagram**: Arrow width proportional to energy flow; reveals loss points.
- ☐ **Power Factor** (pf): Real / Apparent power; **> 0.95** is desirable.
- ☐ **Load Factor**: Average / Peak demand; **> 0.75** is desirable.
- ☐ **Normalization**: $E_{norm} = E_{actual} \times (P_{base} / P_{actual})$ — done in MIS *automatically*.
- ☐ **ISO 50001**: Energy MIS is the *backbone* of an EnMS; EnPIs tracked in MIS dashboards.
- ☐ **Pitfalls to avoid**: (i) concluding higher kWh = worse (forget normalization), (ii) averaging only (forget trend), (iii) writing equations without units, (iv) mixing slope with intercept.
- ☐ **Diagram must include**: Block diagram with closed-loop feedback arrow for full marks in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning of Energy Policy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Planning of Energy Policy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Policy Planning** is the systematic, forward-looking process of formulating strategic, tactical, and operational decisions regarding the production, conversion, distribution, and consumption of energy resources at national, regional, sectoral, or organizational levels. It integrates economic, environmental, technological, and security considerations to ensure sustainable, affordable, and reliable energy supply–demand balance across short, medium, and long-term horizons.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Energy Policy Planning is treated as the **macro-engineering framework** that connects **energy resources, conversion technologies, demand patterns, and regulatory instruments** into a unified decision matrix. It is the precursor to Energy Auditing because auditing only diagnoses inefficiencies; **planning prescribes the corrective pathway**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology** (must be used verbatim in answers):
> - **Energy Security** — uninterrupted availability of energy at affordable prices.
> - **Energy Independence / Self-Reliance** — reducing import dependency.
> - **Energy Mix** — the proportional share of various primary and secondary energy sources in total supply.
> - **Demand-Side Management (DSM)** — influencing consumer demand to match optimal supply.
> - **Supply-Side Management (SSM)** — optimizing generation, transmission, and distribution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **head chef of a large banquet kitchen** preparing meals for the next 30 days:

- You must first **estimate how many guests** will arrive each day (demand forecasting).
- You must **decide which ingredients to stockpile** (coal, oil, gas, solar, wind) — you cannot rely on just one.
- You must **plan storage capacity** so that leftovers don't rot and shortages don't cause chaos.
- You must **set prices and rules** so that guests don't waste food.
- You must **plan for emergencies** — a storm, a strike, a sudden VIP crowd.

**Energy Policy Planning is exactly this kitchen-master role**, but for an entire nation, region, or industry. The chef's menu = the **energy mix**, the guests = **consumers**, the ingredients = **primary energy resources**, and the kitchen rules = **regulatory policy**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Intuition Hook**: If the examiner asks *"Why is energy planning necessary rather than letting the market decide?"*, your answer should always reference the **trilemma of energy** — the simultaneous balancing of **energy security, energy equity (affordability), and environmental sustainability** — which free markets fail to address without policy intervention.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold-Flagged)

- **Energy Security Index (ESI)** — dimensionless composite indicator (0–1 scale).
- **Energy Intensity (EI)** — measured in **ToE per million ₹ of GDP** or **kgoe / \$ GDP**.
- **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC)** — measured in **kgoe / person / year**.
- **Energy Elasticity (ε)** — dimensionless ratio.
- **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** — dimensionless (typically 0.6–0.85).
- **Reserve Margin** — dimensionless, typically **15%–20%** above peak demand.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Energy Policy Planning

Energy planning is conducted at **four distinct levels**, each with different objectives, horizons, and stakeholders:

| Level | Scope | Typical Horizon | Key Decision Makers |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Macro Level** | National / International | 20–50 years | Central Government, Ministry of Power |
| **Meso Level** | Regional / State | 10–20 years | State DISCOMs, Regional Load Despatch Centres |
| **Sectoral Level** | Industry / Transport / Domestic | 5–15 years | Sector regulators, Industry associations |
| **Micro Level** | Plant / Facility / Building | 1–5 years | Energy Manager, Plant Head, Facility Manager |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point**: The downward cascade of planning flows as *National Policy → State Policy → Sectoral Policy → Corporate Energy Plan → Energy Audit Action Plan*. Each lower level must be **consistent with and traceable to** the upper level.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Energy Planning Process

The energy planning process is a **closed-loop iterative cycle**, not a linear sequence:

1. **Define Objectives** — security, affordability, sustainability, self-reliance.
2. **Data Collection & Baseline Assessment** — historical demand, resource inventory, technology cost curves.
3. **Demand Forecasting** — project future energy needs across sectors.
4. **Supply-Side Resource Assessment** — indigenous reserves, import options, renewable potential.
5. **Scenario Development** — Business-As-Usual (BAU), High-Growth, Low-Carbon, 100% Renewable.
6. **Optimization of Energy Mix** — least-cost portfolio satisfying constraints.
7. **Policy Formulation** — subsidies, taxes, mandates, standards.
8. **Implementation & Infrastructure Deployment** — generation, T&D, storage.
9. **Monitoring, Evaluation & Review** — KPIs, audits, course correction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this a closed loop?** Because energy planning is never "done." Technology evolves (e.g., solar LCOE fell ~89% from 2010 to 2020), demand patterns shift (EV penetration, data centres), and geopolitical realities change. The loop ensures **adaptive management**.

## 2.3 Demand Forecasting Methods (Theory)

| Method | Principle | Data Requirement | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trend / Extrapolation** | Past consumption extrapolated linearly or exponentially | 10+ years of historical data | Low |
| **End-Use Method** | Bottom-up aggregation of appliance-level demand | Appliance stock, usage hours, efficiency | High |
| **Econometric Method** | Regression of energy vs. GDP, population, price | Macroeconomic time-series | Medium-High |
| **Technological Diffusion** | Bass model, logistic curves for new tech adoption | Adoption curves | Medium |
| **AI / ML Hybrid** | Neural networks, fuzzy logic, LSTM | Large datasets | High (data-hungry) |

## 2.4 Energy Planning Models (Industry & Academic Standards)

| Model | Origin | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MARKAL** | IEA-ETSAP | Linear programming, multi-period | Linear assumption |
| **LEAP** | Stockholm Environment Institute | Flexible, scenario-based | Manual scenario setup |
| **MESSAGE** | IIASA | Long-term, multi-region | Data-intensive |
| **EFOM** | EC / Loughborough | Detailed energy flows | Complex data |
| **MAED** | IAEA | Demand forecasting module | Used with MESSAGE |
| **AIM** | NIES Japan | Integrated climate-energy | High learning curve |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU ESE**: You are NOT expected to derive any of these models. You must know **what they do, their input/output, and when each is preferred**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these formulas appear in nearly every numerical on Energy Planning.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) | $D_t = D_0 \cdot (1+r)^t$ | $D_0$ base demand, $r$ growth rate, $t$ years | MW / Toe |
| 2 | Energy Elasticity | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\%\,\Delta E}{\%\,\Delta GDP}$ | Ratio of demand growth to GDP growth | dimensionless |
| 3 | Energy Intensity | $EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{GDP}$ | Total energy per unit GDP | Toe / million ₹ |
| 4 | Per Capita Consumption | $PCEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{Population}$ | Per-person energy use | kgoe / person |
| 5 | Required Installed Capacity | $P_{req} = \dfrac{D_{peak}}{(1+RM) \cdot PLF}$ | $RM$ reserve margin (0.15–0.20) | MW |
| 6 | Plant Load Factor | $PLF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \cdot 8760}$ | Annual utilization factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | Capacity Factor (Renewables) | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \cdot 8760}$ | Wind/Solar equivalent of PLF | dimensionless |
| 8 | Energy Mix Share | $S_i = \dfrac{E_i}{\sum E_i} \times 100$ | Share of source $i$ in total | % |
| 9 | Forecasted Demand via Linear Regression | $E = a + b \cdot X$ | $X$ = GDP, population, etc. | Toe / MWh |
| 10 | Simple Payback for Policy Action | $SP = \dfrac{C_{investment}}{\Delta C_{annual\,savings}}$ | Investment vs. annual savings | years |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Syntax Note**: When writing absolute value `|x|` inside any markdown table row, use **$\vert x \vert$** or **$\mid x \mid$** — NEVER the bare pipe `|x|` — to prevent the table from breaking.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Energy policy planning is the **strategic backbone** of every power utility, refinery, and industrial cluster. It directly determines:
- **Investment decisions** worth billions of rupees (e.g., whether to build a 2000 MW coal plant or a 1500 MW solar park + 500 MW storage).
- **Tariff design** and subsidy rationalization.
- **Emission reduction roadmaps** for COP commitments.
- **Energy security strategy** in geopolitically tense regions (e.g., India's hydrocarbon import dependency).
- **Disaster resilience** planning (cyclones, droughts, cyber-attacks on grids).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Energy Demand Projection Using CAGR

This is the **most frequently tested numerical** in KTU ESE under Energy Policy Planning.

> **Problem Setup**: A region's industrial electricity demand was **$D_0 = 5000$ MU** in 2020. The expected CAGR is **$r = 6.5\%$**. Project the demand for the year **2030** (i.e., 10 years ahead).

### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation

The compound growth model for energy demand is:

$$
D_t = D_0 \cdot (1+r)^t
$$

where:
- $D_t$ = energy demand at end of year $t$
- $D_0$ = base year demand
- $r$ = annual growth rate (decimal)
- $t$ = projection horizon in years

### Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values

$$
D_{10} = 5000 \cdot (1 + 0.065)^{10}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the growth factor

$$
(1.065)^{10} = ?
$$

We evaluate step by step:
- $(1.065)^2 = 1.134225$
- $(1.065)^4 = (1.134225)^2 = 1.286466$
- $(1.065)^5 = 1.286466 \times 1.065 = 1.370086$
- $(1.065)^{10} = (1.370086)^2 = 1.877137$

### Step 4 — Compute the final demand

$$
D_{10} = 5000 \times 1.877137 = 9385.68 \text{ MU}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

> The region's industrial electricity demand is projected to grow from **5000 MU in 2020 to ≈ 9386 MU by 2030**, an **absolute increase of 4386 MU** (≈ 87.7% over the decade). This demands commensurate planning of **generation capacity, transmission corridors, and demand-side management interventions**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Installed Capacity Calculation Using Reserve Margin

> **Problem Setup**: A state's projected peak demand in 2030 is **$D_{peak} = 12{,}000$ MW**. The recommended reserve margin is **$RM = 0.18$** and the average plant load factor is **$PLF = 0.75$**. Compute the required installed capacity.

### Step 1 — Apply the capacity planning formula

$$
P_{required} = \frac{D_{peak}}{(1 + RM) \cdot PLF}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$
P_{required} = \frac{12{,}000}{(1 + 0.18) \cdot 0.75}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate denominator

$$
(1 + 0.18) \cdot 0.75 = 1.18 \times 0.75 = 0.885
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the result

$$
P_{required} = \frac{12{,}000}{0.885} = 13{,}559.32 \text{ MW}
$$

### Step 5 — Practical interpretation

> The state must have **≈ 13,560 MW of installed capacity** to reliably meet a peak demand of 12,000 MW while accounting for outages (reserve margin) and de-rating (PLF). This capacity must be a **diversified mix** — not all thermal, not all solar — to honour energy security and reliability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Energy Elasticity and GDP-Coupled Forecasting

> **Problem Setup**: A country's GDP grew from ₹ 100 lakh crore to ₹ 145 lakh crore over 5 years, while energy consumption grew from 800 Mtoe to 920 Mtoe. Find (a) energy elasticity, (b) projected energy demand if GDP reaches ₹ 200 lakh crore next year.

### Step 1 — Compute the percentage changes

$$
\%\,\Delta GDP = \frac{145 - 100}{100} \times 100 = 45\%
$$

$$
\%\,\Delta E = \frac{920 - 800}{800} \times 100 = 15\%
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the elasticity formula

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\%\,\Delta E}{\%\,\Delta GDP} = \frac{15}{45} = 0.333
$$

### Step 3 — Interpret elasticity

> An elasticity of **0.333** means that for every 1% rise in GDP, energy demand rises by only 0.33%. This indicates **structural decoupling** — likely due to energy efficiency improvements, service-sector growth, and renewable penetration.

### Step 4 — Project demand for next year

The GDP growth from 145 → 200 is:

$$
\%\,\Delta GDP_{new} = \frac{200 - 145}{145} \times 100 = 37.93\%
$$

$$
\%\,\Delta E_{new} = \varepsilon \cdot \%\,\Delta GDP_{new} = 0.333 \times 37.93 = 12.63\%
$$

$$
E_{new} = 920 \times (1 + 0.1263) = 920 \times 1.1263 = 1036.2 \text{ Mtoe}
$$

### Step 5 — Verification

> The elasticity-based projection is **more conservative** than a simple CAGR extrapolation, because it accounts for the observed decoupling trend. Energy planners prefer elasticity-based forecasting for **medium-term national policy**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an Energy Mix

| Criterion | Coal | Hydro | Natural Gas | Nuclear | Solar PV | Wind | Biomass |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Levelized Cost (₹/kWh)** | 3.5–4.5 | 3.0–5.0 | 5.0–7.0 | 5.5–8.0 | 2.5–3.5 | 3.0–4.5 | 5.0–7.0 |
| **CO₂ Emissions (g/kWh)** | 820–950 | 10–30 | 450–500 | 10–15 | 40–60 | 15–25 | 50–100 |
| **Capacity Factor** | 0.55–0.75 | 0.40–0.60 | 0.40–0.60 | 0.85–0.92 | 0.18–0.25 | 0.25–0.40 | 0.60–0.80 |
| **Construction Lead Time** | 4–6 yrs | 6–10 yrs | 2–4 yrs | 6–10 yrs | 0.5–1 yr | 0.5–1 yr | 1–2 yrs |
| **Land Footprint** | High | Very High | Low | High | Very High | High | Medium |
| **Dispatchability** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (baseload) | No (VRE) | No (VRE) | Yes |
| **Energy Security** | Medium | High | Low (import) | High | High | High | Medium |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight**: Notice how no single source wins on all criteria. This is exactly why energy policy planning exists — to **optimize trade-offs** across cost, environment, security, and feasibility simultaneously.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — The Energy Policy Planning Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Objectives: Security, Equity, Sustainability] --> B[Baseline Data Collection]
    B --> C[Demand Forecasting]
    C --> D[Supply-Side Resource Assessment]
    D --> E[Scenario Development: BAU / High-Growth / Low-Carbon]
    E --> F[Energy Mix Optimization]
    F --> G[Policy Formulation: Subsidies, Mandates, Standards]
    G --> H[Implementation: Generation, T and D, Storage]
    H --> I[Monitoring, KPIs, Audits]
    I --> J{Course Correction Needed?}
    J -- Yes --> B
    J -- No --> K[Long-Term Stable Operation]
    K --> I

    classDef phase fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#c00000,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outcome fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    class K outcome
```
