<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Energy Policy. List any four key objectives of a national energy policy. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Energy Policy** is a set of governmental directives, regulations, incentives, and strategic frameworks designed to govern the production, distribution, and consumption of energy resources in a country.

**Four Key Objectives:**

1. **Energy Security** — ensuring uninterrupted supply of energy at affordable prices.
2. **Energy Affordability / Equity** — making energy accessible to all sections of society.
3. **Environmental Sustainability** — minimizing greenhouse gas emissions and pollution.
4. **Economic Competitiveness** — providing cost-competitive energy to industries.
5. *(Bonus)* Self-reliance through reduced import dependency.
6. *(Bonus)* Promoting efficient end-use and renewable integration.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for objectives — any 4 valid points)*

---

### Q2. What is Energy Elasticity? A country's energy consumption grew by 12% while GDP grew by 30%. Calculate the energy elasticity and interpret the result. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Energy Elasticity** ($\varepsilon$) is the ratio of the percentage change in energy demand to the percentage change in GDP (or any economic driver).

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\%\,\Delta E}{\%\,\Delta GDP} = \frac{12}{30} = 0.40
$$

**Interpretation:** An elasticity of **0.40** indicates **partial decoupling** — the economy is becoming more energy-efficient. For every 1% increase in GDP, energy demand rises by only 0.40%. *(3 marks: 1 definition, 1 calculation, 1 interpretation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions carry 14 marks and typically have **internal choice** between two questions on the same module. Both choices below are structured with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the hierarchical levels of energy policy planning with suitable examples. Discuss the role of long-term planning horizons in ensuring energy security. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Energy policy planning operates at **four hierarchical levels**, each with distinct scope, horizon, and stakeholders:

**Level 1 — Macro Level (National / International)**
- **Scope:** Entire country's energy strategy, international treaties, cross-border energy trade.
- **Stakeholders:** Ministry of Power, Ministry of Petroleum, NITI Aayog.
- **Horizon:** 20–50 years.
- **Example:** India's National Electricity Plan, COP commitments.

**Level 2 — Meso Level (Regional / State)**
- **Scope:** State-level generation mix, intra-state transmission, regional load dispatch.
- **Stakeholders:** State Electricity Regulatory Commissions, State DISCOMs.
- **Horizon:** 10–20 years.
- **Example:** Kerala State Energy Conservation Plan.

**Level 3 — Sectoral Level**
- **Scope:** Specific sectors — Industry, Transport, Agriculture, Domestic, Commercial.
- **Stakeholders:** Sector regulators (e.g., PNGRB for gas, BEE for appliances).
- **Horizon:** 5–15 years.
- **Example:** National Hydrogen Mission, EV adoption roadmap.

**Level 4 — Micro Level (Plant / Facility)**
- **Scope:** Single facility's energy procurement, on-site generation, audits.
- **Stakeholders:** Energy Manager, Plant Head.
- **Horizon:** 1–5 years.
- **Example:** Captive solar for a manufacturing plant.

**Role of Long-Term Planning in Energy Security:**
- Long horizons (20–50 years) allow **diversification** of the energy mix, reducing over-dependence on a single source (e.g., India's hydrocarbon import burden of >85%).
- Enable **technology lock-in** with adequate lead time — nuclear reactors take 8–10 years to build, hydro 6–10 years.
- Provide **policy stability** that attracts private and foreign direct investment.
- Allow **workforce development** and **infrastructure creation** at the pace required.

*(7 marks: 4 for hierarchical levels with examples, 3 for long-term planning's role)*

---

**(b)** The base-year industrial electricity demand of a region is **4500 MU** in 2024. Project the demand for the year 2034 assuming a CAGR of **7.2%**. If the peak demand corresponds to **55%** of total energy at a **PLF of 0.72** and reserve margin of **17%**, compute the required installed capacity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Demand Projection using CAGR**

$$
D_{10} = D_0 \cdot (1+r)^t = 4500 \times (1.072)^{10}
$$

Compute $(1.072)^{10}$ step-by-step:
- $(1.072)^2 = 1.149184$
- $(1.072)^4 = (1.149184)^2 = 1.320624$
- $(1.072)^5 = 1.320624 \times 1.072 = 1.415709$
- $(1.072)^{10} = (1.415709)^2 = 2.004231$

$$
D_{10} = 4500 \times 2.004231 = 9019.04 \text{ MU}
$$

**Step 2 — Peak Demand**

$$
D_{peak} = 0.55 \times 9019.04 = 4960.47 \text{ MW-equivalent units}
$$

**Step 3 — Required Installed Capacity**

$$
P_{required} = \frac{D_{peak}}{(1 + RM) \cdot PLF} = \frac{4960.47}{1.17 \times 0.72} = \frac{4960.47}{0.8424}
$$

$$
P_{required} = 5888.84 \text{ MW}
$$

**Conclusion:** The region must plan for an installed capacity of **≈ 5889 MW** by 2034 to reliably meet the projected industrial peak demand, **assuming all energy is supplied by grid power**. *(7 marks: 3 for projection, 2 for peak demand, 2 for installed capacity)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the major steps involved in the formulation of a national energy policy. Discuss the role of energy forecasting in this process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Major Steps in National Energy Policy Formulation:**

1. **Vision and Goal Setting** — defining the policy goals: security, affordability, sustainability, self-reliance.
2. **Baseline Assessment** — inventory of resources (coal, oil, gas, hydro, solar, wind, nuclear), existing installed capacity, demand patterns.
3. **Demand Forecasting** — projecting demand for short, medium, and long-term horizons using end-use, econometric, and AI/ML methods.
4. **Supply-Side Resource Assessment** — domestic reserves, import dependency, technology readiness levels.
5. **Scenario Development** — constructing BAU, accelerated-renewable, and net-zero pathways.
6. **Energy Mix Optimization** — using least-cost planning models (MARKAL, LEAP, MESSAGE) to balance trade-offs.
7. **Policy Instruments Design** — tariffs, taxes, subsidies, mandates (e.g., RPO), standards (e.g., BEE star ratings).
8. **Implementation Roadmap** — phased targets, milestones, institutional responsibilities.
9. **Monitoring, Review & Adaptive Management** — KPIs, independent audits, mid-course corrections.

**Role of Energy Forecasting:**

- Forecasting **transforms guesswork into quantified decision-making** — capacity addition cannot be planned without a demand number.
- It enables **scenario analysis** — comparing "what if GDP grows at 6% vs 8%?"
- It identifies **sectoral hotspots** — where demand will surge (e.g., data centres, EVs, cooling).
- It supports **investment justification** for lenders and investors.
- It is the **single most important input** to all planning models.

*(7 marks: 5 for steps, 2 for forecasting role)*

---

**(b)** A state's energy consumption was **750 Mtoe** in 2020 when the population was **120 million**. The population is projected to grow at **1.1% per annum** and per-capita energy consumption at **2.5% per annum**. Calculate the total energy demand in **2030** and the per-capita consumption in **2030**. Comment on what this implies for energy planning. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Population in 2030 (10 years later)**

$$
P_{2030} = 120 \times (1.011)^{10}
$$

Compute $(1.011)^{10}$:
- $(1.011)^2 = 1.022121$
- $(1.011)^4 = (1.022121)^2 = 1.044731$
- $(1.011)^5 = 1.044731 \times 1.011 = 1.056223$
- $(1.011)^{10} = (1.056223)^2 = 1.115687$

$$
P_{2030} = 120 \times 1.115687 = 133.88 \text{ million}
$$

**Step 2 — Per-Capita Energy Consumption in 2030**

$$
PCEC_{2030} = PCEC_{2020} \times (1.025)^{10}
$$

First find $PCEC_{2020}$:

$$
PCEC_{2020} = \frac{750}{120} = 6.25 \text{ kgoe / person}
$$

Now compute $(1.025)^{10}$:
- $(1.025)^2 = 1.050625$
- $(1.025)^4 = (1.050625)^2 = 1.103813$
- $(1.025)^5 = 1.103813 \times 1.025 = 1.131408$
- $(1.025)^{10} = (1.131408)^2 = 1.280084$

$$
PCEC_{2030} = 6.25 \times 1.280084 = 8.00 \text{ kgoe / person}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Energy Demand in 2030**

$$
E_{2030} = P_{2030} \times PCEC_{2030} = 133.88 \times 8.00 = 1071.04 \text{ Mtoe}
$$

**Step 4 — Implications for Energy Planning**

- Total energy demand grows by **≈ 42.8%** in just 10 years — from 750 to 1071 Mtoe.
- This demands aggressive **capacity addition** (~1500–1800 MW per year, depending on PLF).
- The state must invest in **diversified sources** (renewables + storage + thermal backup).
- **Demand-side management** becomes critical to flatten the per-capita growth curve.
- **Infrastructure planning** (transmission corridors, port capacity for LNG, rail for coal) must start now to be ready by 2030.

*(7 marks: 2 for population, 2 for per-capita, 1 for total demand, 2 for planning implications)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Energy Policy Planning Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units** — A numerical answer of *"≈ 5889"* without units (MW) loses 1 full mark. Always write units at every step.
> 2. **Confusing Energy (Mtoe / MU) with Power (MW)** — Energy is a stock; power is a flow. Peak demand is in MW; annual consumption is in MU or Mtoe. Mixing them up = -2 marks minimum.
> 3. **Skipping the Interpretation Step** — KTU examiners allot **1–2 marks specifically for the "what does this mean" interpretation**. A calculation without interpretation is treated as a half-answer.
> 4. **One-Sided Energy Mix Argument** — Never advocate for 100% renewable OR 100% fossil. Examiners penalize extreme views. Always argue for **balanced, diversified mix**.
> 5. **Ignoring PLF in Capacity Calculations** — A classic trap. If you forget to divide by PLF, your installed capacity will be under-estimated, and the examiner will deduct 2 marks.
> 6. **Memorizing Without Understanding** — Writing "*MARKAL is a linear programming model*" without explaining what that means for planning loses you the **application marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Energy Policy** = strategic, regulatory, and economic framework governing the energy sector.
- [ ] Four planning levels: **Macro → Meso → Sectoral → Micro**.
- [ ] Planning horizons: **Short (1–5 yrs), Medium (5–15 yrs), Long (15–50 yrs)**.
- [ ] Process is a **closed loop**, not linear — it is iterative and adaptive.
- [ ] Key demand forecasting methods: **Trend, End-Use, Econometric, Bass Diffusion, AI/ML**.
- [ ] Key planning models: **MARKAL, LEAP, MESSAGE, EFOM, MAED, AIM** — know what each does.
- [ ] **CAGR formula**: $D_t = D_0 (1+r)^t$ — the single most tested formula.
- [ ] **Capacity formula**: $P_{req} = D_{peak} / [(1+RM) \cdot PLF]$ — always remember PLF.
- [ ] **Energy Elasticity** < 1 indicates decoupling; > 1 indicates intensifying use.
- [ ] **Per-capita consumption** rising faster than GDP = alarming; falling = success.
- [ ] **Trilemma of Energy**: Security + Equity + Environment — every policy must address all three.
- [ ] **Reserve margin** typically **15%–20%** above peak demand.
- [ ] **PLF**: Thermal 0.6–0.85; Solar 0.18–0.25; Wind 0.25–0.40; Nuclear 0.85–0.92.
- [ ] Always argue for a **diversified energy mix** — never 100% one source.
- [ ] **Renewables need storage / backup** because of low capacity factor.
- [ ] Long-term planning is **essential** because energy infrastructure has multi-decade lifetimes.
- [ ] Demand-side management (DSM) is **as important as** supply-side planning.
- [ ] Policy instruments: **Subsidies, Taxes, Mandates (RPO), Standards (BEE), Tariffs**.
- [ ] Energy planning connects directly to **Energy Audit** — audits diagnose; planning prescribes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation and Implementation of Energy Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaluation and Implementation of Energy Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Energy Performance Evaluation (EPE)** is the systematic, documented process of measuring, monitoring, analyzing, and verifying the energy efficiency of a facility, process, system, or organization against established baselines and benchmarks. It is the quantitative bridge between the *energy audit findings* and the *implementation of energy conservation measures (ECMs)*.

As per the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) India** and **ISO 50001:2018** framework adopted in the KTU 2024 Energy Management & Audit syllabus, evaluation consists of three pillars:

1. **Energy Review** – Establishing the energy baseline (EnB) and Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs).
2. **Energy Performance Assessment** – Quantifying deviations using statistical tools (CUSUM, regression).
3. **Verification (M&V)** – Confirming actual savings through the **International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol (IPMVP)**.

**Implementation of Energy Performance** refers to the engineering, managerial, and financial execution of recommended ECMs — encompassing project prioritization, Measurement & Verification (M&V), and post-implementation performance tracking to ensure persistent energy savings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:**
> *Energy Performance = (Useful Energy Output) / (Energy Input)*. Anything less than 100% represents a performance gap, which is the **target** of the evaluation process.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car's Fuel Efficiency Tracker"

Imagine you drive a car and want to know how fuel-efficient it is:
- You record **kilometers driven** and **liters of fuel used** (these are your *Energy Performance Indicators*).
- Over months, you plot a graph. If your "km/l" suddenly drops, you investigate — maybe tire pressure is low, or air filter is dirty (this is the *evaluation phase*).
- You then fix the issue (this is *implementation*).
- After the fix, you re-measure to confirm the car is back to optimal efficiency (this is *M&V Verification*).

> [!NOTE]
> **In a factory**, the "fuel" is electricity/thermal energy, the "kilometers" is production output, and the "fix" is the Energy Conservation Measure (ECM). The **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** plays the role of "km per liter."

## 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics (KTU Board Frequently Tested)

| Metric | Standard Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | **kWh/ton, GJ/ton, kWh/unit** | Energy used per unit of product |
| **Energy Intensity (EI)** | **kWh/m², MJ/m²** | Energy used per unit area (buildings) |
| **Power Factor (PF)** | dimensionless (0–1) | cos φ; ideally ≥ **0.95** |
| **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** | dimensionless (0–1) | Actual energy / Max possible energy |
| **Maximum Demand (MD)** | **kVA or kW** | Peak power draw in a billing cycle |
| **CUSUM** | **kWh (cumulative)** | Cumulative deviation from baseline |
| **Energy Baseline (EnB)** | **kWh/year** | Reference energy consumption |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU board loves the formula: **% Energy Savings = [(Baseline Energy − Post-ECM Energy) / Baseline Energy] × 100**. Memorize it — it appears in nearly every Part B numerical.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Stage Evaluation–Implementation Cycle

### Stage 1: Baseline Establishment (EnB)
- Collect at least **12 months** of historical energy data (per ISO 50001).
- Normalize using independent variables: production volume, degree-days, occupancy.
- Apply **regression analysis** to model: `Energy = a + b·(Production) + c·(T_outside)`.
- The regression equation becomes your **Energy Baseline Equation**.

### Stage 2: Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs) Definition
- EnPIs must be **measurable, traceable, and relevant** to the organization's energy use.
- Examples:
  - *kWh per ton of cement produced*
  - *MJ per kWh of compressed air generated*
  - *Liters of furnace oil per ton of steam raised*

### Stage 3: Monitoring, Targeting (M&T) & CUSUM
- Set a **target** EnPI (e.g., reduce SEC by 5% in 12 months).
- Continuously track deviation using **CUSUM (Cumulative Sum of deviations)**:

$$CUSUM_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (B_i - A_i)$$

where $B_i$ = baseline energy at month $i$ (adjusted), $A_i$ = actual energy at month $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CUSUM?** A simple month-to-month comparison hides gradual drifts. CUSUM amplifies small persistent deviations — the slope of the CUSUM line **directly indicates** the rate of energy drift.

### Stage 4: Implementation of ECMs (Project Execution Ladder)
1. **Technical Feasibility** → Will it work?
2. **Economic Feasibility** → Simple Payback, NPV, IRR.
3. **Prioritization Matrix** → Rank ECMs by *Savings × Feasibility / Investment*.
4. **Detailed Engineering & Procurement**.
5. **Installation & Commissioning**.
6. **M&V Verification** (per IPMVP Option A/B/C/D).
7. **Sustain & Re-tune**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verified** and align with the **IPMVP, BEE Certified Energy Auditor (CEA) syllabus, and ISO 50001**. Every KTU Board numerical will reduce to one of these.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Typical KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{consumed}}{P_{output}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption; units **kWh/ton** | 3–7 marks |
| 2 | $\% \text{ Savings} = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{actual}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Percentage energy savings | 3–7 marks |
| 3 | $PF = \dfrac{kW}{kVA} = \cos \phi$ | Power factor; must be ≥ **0.95** | 3 marks |
| 4 | $PLF = \dfrac{\text{Actual Energy Generated}}{\text{Max Possible Energy}}$ | Plant Load Factor | 3–7 marks |
| 5 | $CUSUM_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (B_i - A_i)$ | Cumulative deviation from baseline | 7–14 marks |
| 6 | $\text{Simple Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Investment Cost}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | Years to recover investment | 3–7 marks |
| 7 | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | Net Present Value; $r$ = discount rate | 14 marks (full) |
| 8 | $IRR: NPV = 0$ | Internal Rate of Return (trial-and-error) | 14 marks |
| 9 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{steam} \times (h_g - h_{fw})}{m_{fuel} \times GCV}$ | Boiler thermal efficiency | 7–14 marks |
| 10 | $E_{saved,annual} = kW_{saved} \times \text{Operating Hours}$ | Annual energy savings (for IPMVP Option A) | 3–7 marks |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU board papers, students frequently **confuse "Maximum Demand" (kVA)** with "Energy" (kWh). MD is a *power* quantity; kWh is *energy*. Never interchange them in calculations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISO 50001 EnMS Audits**: EnPIs and CUSUM are mandatory for certification.
- **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme** in India — BEE mandates M&V for designated consumers.
- **ESCO (Energy Service Company) business model** — savings are *verified* before payment, making M&V protocols commercially critical.
- **Green Building Ratings (GRIHA, LEED)** — require documented energy performance evaluation.
- **Smart Manufacturing / Industry 4.0** — EnPIs feed digital twins and AI-based optimization loops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Savings with Operating-Hours Normalization (IPMVP Option A)

**Problem Setup:** A factory installs a 22 kW VFD on a motor that previously ran at constant speed. Motor now draws 14 kW. The motor operates **5,400 hours/year**. Electricity cost is **₹7.50/kWh**. Find annual energy saved, monetary savings, and % savings if baseline annual consumption was **118,800 kWh**.

**Step-by-step:**

$$\Delta P = P_{baseline} - P_{retrofit} = 22 \text{ kW} - 14 \text{ kW} = 8 \text{ kW}$$

*This is the power reduction at the equipment boundary — IPMVP Option A "retrofitted isolation" key measurement.*

$$\Delta E_{annual} = \Delta P \times t_{operating} = 8 \text{ kW} \times 5{,}400 \text{ h} = 43{,}200 \text{ kWh/year}$$

*This converts power-time-product (kW·h) into annual energy. Note the multiplication is valid only when load is constant — verified by M&V metering.*

$$\text{Monetary Savings} = \Delta E_{annual} \times \text{Tariff} = 43{,}200 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ } 3{,}24{,}000 \text{ /year}$$

$$\% \text{ Savings} = \frac{43{,}200}{118{,}800} \times 100 = 36.36\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Option A is the *cheapest* M&V option because it measures only the ECM boundary, not the whole facility. It is most appropriate when ECM affects **one well-defined equipment**. If the ECM affects the entire plant (e.g., chiller plant optimization), the examiner will expect **Option C — whole facility**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CUSUM Drift Detection

**Problem:** A boiler's baseline energy use (regression-adjusted) for 6 months and the actual consumption are given. Detect the drift month and the rate of energy drift.

| Month | Baseline $B_i$ (kWh) | Actual $A_i$ (kWh) | $(B_i - A_i)$ | CUSUM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10,000 | 10,200 | −200 | **−200** |
| 2 | 10,500 | 10,600 | −100 | **−300** |
| 3 | 11,000 | 11,050 | −50 | **−350** |
| 4 | 11,200 | 11,000 | +200 | **−150** |
| 5 | 11,500 | 10,900 | +600 | **+450** |
| 6 | 11,800 | 10,950 | +850 | **+1300** |

**Interpretation (Analytical Reasoning):**
- Months 1–3: CUSUM is *negative and increasing in magnitude* → energy consumption is **higher** than baseline (poor performance).
- At Month 4, the slope reverses → ECM was likely commissioned around Month 4.
- Slope from Month 4 to 6: $(1300 - (-150))/(6-4) = +725$ kWh/month **improvement**.

**Mathematical Form of the CUSUM Slope:**

$$m_{CUSUM} = \frac{\Delta \text{CUSUM}}{\Delta t} = \frac{\text{CUSUM}_n - \text{CUSUM}_{n-1}}{t_n - t_{n-1}}$$

In our case, $m_{CUSUM} = 725 \text{ kWh/month}$. This is the **monthly energy drift** — useful for projecting annual savings: $725 \times 12 = 8{,}700 \text{ kWh/year}$ recovered through the ECM.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board numericals, students are often asked: *"At which month did the CUSUM slope reverse?"* — the answer is found by identifying where the **second derivative** changes sign.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Economic Justification of an ECM (NPV Approach)

**Given:** Investment $C_0$ = ₹ 10,00,000; Annual cash flow from savings = ₹ 3,50,000; Project life $n$ = 5 years; Discount rate $r$ = 10%.

**Step 1: Discount factor** for year $t$:

$$DF_t = \frac{1}{(1+r)^t}$$

**Step 2: Compute discounted cash flows:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF_1 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^1} = 0.9091 \\
DF_2 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^2} = 0.8264 \\
DF_3 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^3} = 0.7513 \\
DF_4 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^4} = 0.6830 \\
DF_5 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^5} = 0.6209
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Discounted cash flows:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCF_1 &= 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 3{,}18{,}182 \\
DCF_2 &= 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 2{,}89{,}256 \\
DCF_3 &= 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 2{,}62{,}960 \\
DCF_4 &= 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 2{,}39{,}055 \\
DCF_5 &= 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6209 = 2{,}17{,}323
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Sum of discounted cash flows:**

$$\sum DCF = 3{,}18{,}182 + 2{,}89{,}256 + 2{,}62{,}960 + 2{,}39{,}055 + 2{,}17{,}323 = 13{,}26{,}776$$

**Step 5: Net Present Value:**

$$NPV = 13{,}26{,}776 - 10{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 3{,}26{,}776$$

**Step 6: Decision Rule:**

$$NPV > 0 \implies \text{ACCEPT the ECM}$$

**Step 7: Simple Payback (cross-check):**

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} = 2.86 \text{ years}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 boards often give a **Choice A or Choice B** of *Payback vs NPV*. Know both — NPV is preferred for long-life projects (>3 yrs) because it accounts for the **time value of money**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Energy Performance Evaluation–Implementation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Energy Audit Findings] --> B0[Stage 1: Energy Baseline EnB]
    B0 --> B1[Collect 12 months historical data]
    B1 --> B2[Regression Model E = a + bX + cY]
    B2 --> C0[Stage 2: Define EnPIs]
    C0 --> C1[SEC kWh per ton]
    C0 --> C2[Energy Intensity kWh per sqm]
    C0 --> C3[PLF Power Factor]
    C1 --> D0[Stage 3: Monitoring and Targeting]
    C2 --> D0
    C3 --> D0
    D0 --> D1[Monthly CUSUM Tracking]
    D1 --> D2{Drift Detected?}
    D2 -- No --> D3[Continue Monitoring]
    D2 -- Yes --> E0[Stage 4: Identify ECMs]
    E0 --> E1[Technical Feasibility Check]
    E1 --> E2[Economic Analysis NPV IRR]
    E2 --> F0[Prioritization Matrix]
    F0 --> F1[Ranked ECM List]
    F1 --> G0[Stage 5: Implementation]
    G0 --> G1[Detailed Engineering]
    G1 --> G2[Procurement and Installation]
    G2 --> G3[Commissioning]
    G3 --> H0[Stage 6: M and V Verification]
    H0 --> H1{IPMVP Option Selection}
    H1 --> H2[Option A Retrofit Isolation]
    H1 --> H3[Option B Retrofit Isolation with All Parameters]
    H1 --> H4[Option C Whole Facility]
    H1 --> H5[Option D Calibrated Simulation]
    H2 --> I0[Verified Savings]
    H3 --> I0
    H4 --> I0
    H5 --> I0
    I0 --> J0[Stage 7: Sustain and Re-Tune]
    J0 --> K0[Closed Loop ISO 50001 PDCA]
    K0 --> B0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This is a closed-loop **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle. The output of M&V Verification re-feeds the baseline stage — making energy performance improvement a **continuous process**, not a one-time activity.

## 4.2 IPMVP Option Selection Logic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[ECM Scope Decision] --> P1{Single Equipment?}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2[Use IPMVP Option A]
    P1 -- No --> P3{Affects Whole Facility?}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Use IPMVP Option C]
    P3 -- No --> P5{Need Detailed Parameters?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Use IPMVP Option B]
    P5 -- No --> P7{Limited Meters Available?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Use IPMVP Option D Simulation]
    P7 -- No --> P9[Re-evaluate scope]
    P2 --> Q0[KTU Exam Tip: Lowest Cost M&V]
    P6 --> Q1[KTU Exam Tip: Best for HVAC Retrofits]
    P4 --> Q2[KTU Exam Tip: Best for Plant-wide ECMs]
    P8 --> Q3[KTU Exam Tip: Used when metering impossible]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Mnemonic for IPMVP Options:** **"A B C D = All Boundaries Carefully Defined"**
> - **A** = equipment-level retrofit (cheapest)
> - **B** = equipment-level with all variable parameters measured
> - **C** = whole facility (utility bill analysis)
> - **D** = simulation / calibrated model (most expensive, used when M&V is impractical)

## 4.3 Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z0[Organization Level EnPI] --> Z1[Plant Level MJ per ton]
    Z1 --> Z2[Process Level kWh per batch]
    Z2 --> Z3[Equipment Level kWh per motor]
    Z3 --> Z4[Component Level kW per pump]
    Z0 --> Z5[Facility Level kWh per sqm per year]
    Z5 --> Z6[System Level kWh per RT kW HVAC]
    Z6 --> Z7[Sub-system Level kWh per CFM fan]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define the term "Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)" and give any two examples used in an industrial facility.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Words in Bold):**

An **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** is a **quantitative measure of energy performance** that an organization defines and uses to track and improve its energy efficiency. EnPIs form the backbone of the **ISO 50001 Energy Management System (EnMS)** and the **BEE PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme**.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in a cement plant:** kWh of electrical energy consumed per ton of clinker produced. **Typical benchmark:** 90–110 kWh/ton.
2. **Energy Intensity in a commercial building:** kWh of electricity per square meter per year (kWh/m²/year). **Typical benchmark:** 150–200 kWh/m²/year for an air-conditioned office.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award the full 3 marks only if the student gives a *quantified* example (with units), not just "kWh per unit."

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Differentiate between IPMVP Option A and Option C with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | IPMVP Option A | IPMVP Option C |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Single, well-defined equipment (retrofit isolation) | Whole facility (utility bill analysis) |
| **Measurement** | Key parameter measured at ECM boundary | Energy use of the entire facility measured |
| **Cost** | **Lowest cost** | Higher cost (whole-plant metering) |
| **When Used** | Known, predictable performance of ECM-affected equipment | Multiple interactive ECMs, uncertain individual savings |
| **Example** | Verifying savings from a **VFD on a single motor** | Verifying savings from a **plant-wide lighting + HVAC + compressed air retrofit** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU boards award full marks **only when both columns are contrasted** — a definition alone is insufficient.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **four-stage methodology for evaluating energy performance** in an industrial facility, with reference to **CUSUM analysis**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz induction motor** of rated power **37 kW** operates at **0.85 power factor (lagging)** and **88% efficiency**. Calculate:
   (i) The input current drawn by the motor. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The **annual energy consumption** if the motor runs **4,800 hours/year**. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) The **annual energy cost** at a tariff of **₹ 8.00/kWh**. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The four-stage methodology for energy performance evaluation is as follows:

**Stage 1: Energy Baseline (EnB) Establishment**
- Historical energy data (≥12 months) is collected from utility bills and sub-meters.
- A **regression model** is built: $E = a + b \cdot X_1 + c \cdot X_2 + \ldots$, where $X_i$ are independent variables (production, weather, occupancy).
- This model represents *expected* energy use under normal operating conditions — the **baseline**.

**Stage 2: Definition of Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)**
- EnPIs are selected based on the **major energy uses (MEUs)** identified during the energy audit.
- Examples: SEC (kWh/ton), Energy Intensity (MJ/m²), Compressed Air Specific Power (kW/100 cfm).

**Stage 3: Monitoring, Targeting, and CUSUM Analysis**
- Monthly actual energy is compared against the **adjusted baseline** (regression-predicted).
- **CUSUM** is computed: $CUSUM_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (B_i - A_i)$, where $B_i$ is baseline and $A_i$ is actual.
- The **slope of the CUSUM line** indicates the **rate of energy drift** (kWh/month).
- A **sustained positive slope** indicates energy performance has improved; a **negative slope** indicates deterioration.

**Stage 4: Verification and Continuous Improvement (M&V)**
- Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) are implemented.
- Post-implementation **M&V** is performed as per **IPMVP**.
- The verified savings become the **new EnB reference** for the next cycle, creating a closed-loop **PDCA** improvement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Awarding the 7 marks:** 1 mark per stage (4 marks) + 1 mark for **CUSUM equation** + 1 mark for **drift interpretation** + 1 mark for **PDCA loop closure**.

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $P_{out} = 37$ kW, $V_L = 415$ V, $\cos\phi = 0.85$, $\eta = 0.88$, $t = 4{,}800$ h/year, tariff = ₹ 8.00/kWh.

**(i) Input current (3 marks):**

$$P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{37}{0.88} = 42.05 \text{ kW}$$

For a 3-phase system:

$$P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

Solving for $I_L$:

$$I_L = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{42{,}050}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= \frac{42{,}050}{1.732 \times 415 \times 0.85} \\
&= \frac{42{,}050}{610.65} \\
&\approx 68.86 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Annual energy consumption (2 marks):**

$$E_{annual} = P_{in} \times t = 42.05 \times 4{,}800 = 2{,}01{,}840 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**(iii) Annual energy cost (2 marks):**

$$\text{Cost} = E_{annual} \times \text{Tariff} = 2{,}01{,}840 \times 8.00 = \text{₹ } 16{,}14{,}720 \text{ /year}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** `[KTU University Exam – May 2025, Model]`

**(a)** What is **IPMVP**? Describe its **four M&V options** with suitable industrial examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory replaces **150 conventional T8 fluorescent tubes (40 W each)** with **LED tubes (18 W each)** in a workshop. The workshop operates **3 shifts × 8 hours × 300 days = 7,200 hours/year**. The retrofit cost is **₹ 4,50,000**, and electricity cost is **₹ 7.00/kWh**.
   (i) Calculate the **annual energy savings (kWh)** and **annual monetary savings (₹)**. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) Calculate the **simple payback period (years)**. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) If the project life is **8 years** and the **discount rate is 9%**, determine whether the project is **economically viable using NPV**. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)** is the **globally accepted framework** developed by the **Efficiency Valuation Organization (EVO)** for determining savings achieved by Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs). It ensures that energy savings are **measured accurately, transparently, and consistently** between ECM providers (ESCOs) and consumers.

**Four M&V Options:**

| Option | Method | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **A – Retrofit Isolation (Key Parameter)** | Performance of ECM-affected equipment is measured; only the key parameter is measured (e.g., kW) at the equipment boundary. | VFD on a pump — measure input kW before/after. |
| **B – Retrofit Isolation (All Parameters)** | All relevant parameters (e.g., kW, flow, temperature) are measured; uses periodic measurements or continuous metering. | Chiller retrofit — measure kW, chilled water flow, and temperatures. |
| **C – Whole Facility** | Savings determined from **whole-facility utility meter data** (electricity, gas bills) using statistical analysis (regression). | Plant-wide HVAC + lighting + compressed air retrofit. |
| **D – Calibrated Simulation** | Savings determined by **simulating** baseline and post-ECM performance using a calibrated energy model (e.g., eQUEST, EnergyPlus). | When physical metering is impractical, e.g., new building design. |

> [!TIP]
> **Awarding the 7 marks:** 2 marks for IPMVP definition, 4 marks (1 each) for the four options with examples, 1 mark for the "whole-facility" justification.

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Annual Energy and Monetary Savings (3 marks):**

**Power reduction per tube:**

$$\Delta P = 40 - 18 = 22 \text{ W} = 0.022 \text{ kW}$$

**Total power reduction (150 tubes):**

$$\Delta P_{total} = 150 \times 0.022 = 3.3 \text{ kW}$$

**Annual energy savings:**

$$\Delta E = 3.3 \times 7{,}200 = 23{,}760 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Annual monetary savings:**

$$\text{Savings}_{money} = 23{,}760 \times 7.00 = \text{₹ } 1{,}66{,}320 \text{ /year}$$

**(ii) Simple Payback Period (2 marks):**

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{1{,}66{,}320} = 2.706 \text{ years}$$

**(iii) NPV Calculation (2 marks):**

$$NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{8} \frac{CF_t}{(1+0.09)^t}$$

The sum of discount factors for an **annuity** of 8 years at 9%:

$$PVIFA(9\%, 8) = \frac{1 - (1.09)^{-8}}{0.09} = \frac{1 - 0.5019}{0.09} = 5.5348$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -4{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}66{,}320 \times 5.5348 \\
&= -4{,}50{,}000 + 9{,}20{,}539 \\
&= \text{₹ } 4{,}70{,}539
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $NPV > 0$, so the project is **economically viable**. Accept the LED retrofit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert **Watts to Kilowatts** in power reduction calculations. This alone costs 1–2 marks per sub-part.
> 2. **Discount Factor Error:** Using `r = 0.09` in some steps and `9` in others. Always write the decimal form `0.09` explicitly.
> 3. **Neglecting the "−C₀" term:** Some students compute only the PV of cash inflows and forget to subtract the initial investment, getting the **sign of NPV wrong**.
> 4. **Payback vs. NPV Confusion:** The board explicitly tests whether you know the **limitation of simple payback** (ignores time value of money).
> 5. **CUSUM Sign Error:** Mixing up $(B_i - A_i)$ and $(A_i - B_i)$. KTU keys strictly penalize this — the **direction of slope** matters for the conclusion.
> 6. **Mixing PF with Efficiency:** $\cos\phi = 0.85$ is the **power factor**, not the **efficiency** — students often multiply it twice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Read 30 Minutes Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Energy Performance = Output / Input** (output = useful work, input = energy supplied).
- ✅ **EnB (Energy Baseline)** = regression model of historical energy vs. independent variables.
- ✅ **EnPI (Energy Performance Indicator)** = the metric that *defines* performance (SEC, EI, PLF, PF).
- ✅ **CUSUM equation:** $CUSUM_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (B_i - A_i)$. **Positive slope = improvement**, **negative slope = deterioration**.
- ✅ **% Savings Formula:** $\% S = \frac{E_b - E_a}{E_b} \times 100$.
- ✅ **SEC** = energy per unit product (kWh/ton). **EI** = energy per unit area (kWh/m²).
- ✅ **PF** = kW/kVA, ideally **≥ 0.95**. Penalty in tariff applies if PF < 0.90.
- ✅ **PLF** = actual energy / max possible energy. Closer to 1 = better utilization.
- ✅ **IPMVP** — Four options: **A** (equipment, key param), **B** (equipment, all params), **C** (whole facility), **D** (simulation).
- ✅ **Simple Payback** = Investment / Annual Savings (does NOT consider time value of money).
- ✅ **NPV** = $-C_0 + \sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$. **Accept if NPV > 0.**
- ✅ **PVIFA formula** for equal annual cash flows: $\frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$.
- ✅ **M&V Protocol** must be defined *before* ECM implementation, not after.
- ✅ **ISO 50001 PDCA Cycle** — EnB → EnPIs → M&T → ECM Implementation → M&V → Re-baseline.
- ✅ **Three-Phase Power Formula:** $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ — this is the **most-tested equation** in the entire module.
- ✅ **Energy Audit Pyramid:** Walk-through → Mini-audit → Comprehensive audit → Investment-grade audit.
- ✅ **M&V cost** typically ranges from **1% to 5%** of annual ECM savings — KTU numericals sometimes ask this.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonics for Final Recall:**
> - **EnB, EnPI, M&T, M&V** = "**B**efore **P**lanning, **M**easure and **V**erify" — the four pillars in order.
> - **IPMVP A, B, C, D** = "**A**ppliance, **B**uilding, **C**ampus, **D**esign" (equipment → equipment-detailed → whole facility → simulation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner Advice:** In KTU 2024 Energy Management & Audit papers, **70% of marks in Module 3** are awarded to numericals involving **CUSUM, M&V Savings, Payback, and NPV**. Allocate 80% of your revision time to these four topics. Conceptual questions on IPMVP and ISO 50001 PDCA are typically short (3 marks) but easy scoring — do not skip them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools for Effective Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tools for Effective Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tools for Effective Implementation** in Energy Management refer to the systematic set of **financial, technical, managerial, and procedural instruments** employed by an organization to translate its energy policy and objectives into measurable, verifiable, and sustainable energy performance improvements. These tools bridge the gap between energy audit findings and actual on-ground implementation of energy conservation measures (ECMs).

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** outcome mapping for OEPET722 (CO3: *Apply energy management tools and techniques in industrial/commercial facilities*), these tools are broadly classified into:

1. **Financial Analysis Tools** — Simple Payback, Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), Life Cycle Costing (LCC), Benefit-Cost Ratio.
2. **Planning Tools** — Energy Action Plan (EAP), Critical Path Method (CPM), Gantt Charts.
3. **Monitoring & Benchmarking Tools** — Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs), Specific Energy Consumption (SEC), CUSUM, Statistical Process Control.
4. **Systematic Frameworks** — ISO 50001 Energy Management System, PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle, Energy Review.
5. **Procurement & Procurement Specifications Tools** — Energy-efficient equipment guidelines, Star ratings.
6. **Awareness & Communication Tools** — Training modules, energy policy dissemination, suggestion schemes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Doctor's Toolkit vs. The Health Plan**
>
> Imagine a doctor (the energy manager) who has diagnosed a patient (the industry) with high cholesterol (energy waste) and prescribed lifestyle changes (ECMs). However, the doctor also needs a *stethoscope, blood test reports, a diet chart, a follow-up schedule, and financial planning for medicines*. Without these tools, even the best diagnosis is useless.
>
> Similarly, after an energy audit identifies savings opportunities, the energy manager needs **financial tools** (to justify investment), **planning tools** (to schedule implementation), **monitoring tools** (to verify savings), and **system frameworks** (to sustain improvements). This toolkit ensures the *medicine (ECMs)* is taken correctly and the *patient (industry)* stays healthy.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Standard Discount Rate (in India):** typically **12%** for BEE/PCRA studies, **10%** for many industrial evaluations.
- **Plant Economic Life:** **10–15 years** for industrial retrofits.
- **Currency Unit for KTU Numerical Problems:** Indian Rupees (₹), Lakhs (₹ 1 Lakh = 1,00,000), or Crores (₹ 1 Cr = 1,00,00,000).
- **Energy Unit Conversions:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$, $1 \text{ TOE} = 11,630 \text{ kWh}$ (approx.).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Financial Analysis Tools (The Most Tested in KTU)

#### 2.1.1 Simple Payback Period (SPP)

The **Simplest and Most Frequently Used** tool. It is the time required for the cumulative savings from an ECM to equal the initial capital investment.

**Why it is used:** Easy to compute, intuitive, used as a first-pass screening tool by management.

**Limitations:** Ignores the **time value of money**, savings after payback, and project life. Often used as a *hurdle* (e.g., "Management only approves projects with SPP < 2 years").

#### 2.1.2 Discounted Payback Period (DPP)

Improves on SPP by discounting future cash flows back to present value using a discount rate $i$.

#### 2.1.3 Net Present Value (NPV)

The present value of all cash inflows minus the present value of all cash outflows over the project life.

**Decision Rule:**
- $\text{NPV} > 0$ → **Accept** the project
- $\text{NPV} < 0$ → **Reject** the project
- $\text{NPV} = 0$ → Indifferent

#### 2.1.4 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

The discount rate at which the NPV of a project becomes **zero**. It is the "break-even" discount rate.

**Decision Rule:** Accept if $\text{IRR} \geq \text{required rate of return (hurdle rate)}$.

#### 2.1.5 Life Cycle Costing (LCC) / Total Cost of Ownership

The sum of all costs incurred over the entire life of the equipment: initial cost + operating cost + maintenance cost + disposal cost, all discounted to present value.

**LCC Formula:**
$$\text{LCC} = C_{\text{init}} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_{O,t} + C_{M,t}}{(1+i)^t} - \frac{S_v}{(1+i)^n}$$

where $S_v$ is salvage value.

#### 2.1.6 Benefit-Cost Ratio (B-C Ratio)

Ratio of discounted benefits to discounted costs. Accept if **B-C > 1**.

### 2.2 Why Each Tool Matters in Engineering Practice

| Tool | Engineering Use Case |
|------|---------------------|
| **SPP** | Quick screening of multiple ECMs in an audit report |
| **NPV** | Justifying high-investment ECMs (e.g., cogeneration, VFDs) |
| **IRR** | Comparing profitability against bank FD rates / hurdle rate |
| **LCC** | Comparing two motors/pumps with different capital and operating costs |
| **EnPIs** | Year-on-year performance tracking of a plant |

### 2.3 Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)

EnPIs are normalized metrics that allow comparison across time, plants, or processes.

$$\text{EnPI} = \frac{\text{Energy Consumed (kWh/TOE)}}{\text{Activity Level (tonnes produced, $\text{m}^2$ floor area, occupants, etc.)}}$$

Common EnPIs:
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** kWh/tonne of product
- **Energy Intensity:** TOE/₹ Crore of turnover
- **kWh/$1000$ of production output**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU-board-exam ready. Memorize the symbols and units.**

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Units |
|--------|---------------------|-------------|---------------|
| SPP | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{I}{A_s}$ | Simple Payback Period | years |
| NPV | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | Net Present Value | ₹ |
| IRR | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t} = 0$ | Internal Rate of Return | % |
| DPP | $\sum_{t=0}^{\text{DPP}} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} = 0$ | Discounted Payback | years |
| LCC | $C_{\text{init}} + \sum \dfrac{C_{O,t}+C_{M,t}}{(1+i)^t} - \dfrac{S_v}{(1+i)^n}$ | Life Cycle Cost | ₹ |
| B-C | $\dfrac{\sum B_t/(1+i)^t}{\sum C_t/(1+i)^t}$ | Benefit-Cost Ratio | dimensionless |
| PV factor | $P/A = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Uniform Series Present Worth factor | dimensionless |
| EnPI | $E_{\text{total}} / \text{Activity}$ | Specific Energy | kWh/unit |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** When using a *uniform annual savings* $A_s$ over $n$ years at discount rate $i$, the present value of savings is **$A_s \times (P/A, i, n)$**, NOT simply $A_s \times n$. Many students lose 2–3 marks by ignoring the time value of money.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the NPV Equation from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define Cash Flow at Time $t$**

Let $CF_t$ be the net cash flow in year $t$ (positive = savings, negative = investment). For an energy conservation project, the typical cash flow stream is:
- $CF_0 = -I$ (initial investment at $t = 0$)
- $CF_t = A_s$ (annual savings, constant) for $t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$

**Step 2 — Apply the Discounting Concept**

A rupee received $t$ years from now is worth less than a rupee today because today's rupee can be invested at the discount rate $i$ to grow. The discount factor is:

$$DF_t = \frac{1}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 3 — Express Present Value of Each Year's Cash Flow**

The present value of cash flow in year $t$ is:

$$PV_t = \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 4 — Sum Over the Project Life**

The NPV is the algebraic sum of all present values from $t=0$ to $t=n$:

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 5 — Expand the Series (Constant Annual Savings Case)**

Substituting $CF_0 = -I$ and $CF_t = A_s$ for $t \geq 1$:

$$\text{NPV} = -I + \frac{A_s}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{A_s}{(1+i)^2} + \cdots + \frac{A_s}{(1+i)^n}$$

**Step 6 — Use the Geometric Series Summation**

The bracketed sum is a finite geometric series with first term $\frac{1}{1+i}$, ratio $\frac{1}{1+i}$, and $n$ terms. Using the closed-form result:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^t} = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} = \left(\frac{P}{A}, i, n\right)$$

**Step 7 — Final NPV Formula**

$$\text{NPV} = -I + A_s \cdot \left(\frac{P}{A}, i, n\right) = -I + A_s \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

This is the **workhorse equation** for KTU 14-mark financial problems.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Model):**
> A textile mill in Kerala is considering installation of a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) on its boiler ID fan motor. The data is:
> - Initial Investment: ₹ 8,00,000
> - Annual Energy Savings: ₹ 2,40,000 per year
> - Annual Maintenance Cost Increase: ₹ 20,000 per year
> - Project Life: 10 years
> - Discount Rate: 12%
> - Salvage Value: ₹ 50,000
>
> Compute: (a) Simple Payback Period, (b) NPV at 12%, (c) IRR (approximate).

#### 3.2.1 Part (a): Simple Payback Period

Net annual cash flow $A_s = 2,40,000 - 20,000 = 2,20,000$ ₹/year.

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{I}{A_s} = \frac{8,00,000}{2,20,000}$$

Performing the division:

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{8,00,000}{2,20,000} = \frac{80}{22} = \frac{40}{11} = 3.636 \text{ years}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SPP} \approx 3.64 \text{ years}}$$

#### 3.2.2 Part (b): Net Present Value at $i = 12\%$

The present worth factor (P/A, 12%, 10) is computed as:

$$(P/A, 12\%, 10) = \frac{(1+0.12)^{10} - 1}{0.12 \times (1+0.12)^{10}}$$

Step-by-step:

$$(1.12)^{10} = 3.10585$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 3.10585 - 1 = 2.10585$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.12 \times 3.10585 = 0.37270$$

$$(P/A, 12\%, 10) = \frac{2.10585}{0.37270} = 5.6502$$

Present value of annual net savings:

$$PV_{\text{savings}} = 2,20,000 \times 5.6502 = 12,43,044 \text{ ₹}$$

Present value of salvage value (received at end of year 10):

$$PV_{\text{salvage}} = \frac{50,000}{(1.12)^{10}} = \frac{50,000}{3.10585} = 16,098 \text{ ₹}$$

Total present value of inflows:

$$PV_{\text{in}} = 12,43,044 + 16,098 = 12,59,142 \text{ ₹}$$

Net Present Value:

$$\text{NPV} = PV_{\text{in}} - I = 12,59,142 - 8,00,000$$

$$\boxed{\text{NPV} = 4,59,142 \text{ ₹} > 0}$$

**Decision:** Since NPV > 0, the VFD project is **financially viable and should be accepted**.

#### 3.2.3 Part (c): IRR by Interpolation

We need the discount rate $r$ such that:

$$-I + A_s \cdot (P/A, r, 10) + S_v \cdot (P/F, r, 10) = 0$$

Equivalently, the equivalent uniform annual worth must equal the annualized cost. Using the simpler Annual Worth approach:

Equivalent Annual Savings: $A_s = 2,20,000$ ₹/year, plus annualized salvage $\frac{S_v}{(F/A, r, 10)}$.

**Trial 1:** Try $r = 20\%$

$$(P/A, 20\%, 10) = \frac{(1.20)^{10} - 1}{0.20 \times (1.20)^{10}}$$

$$(1.20)^{10} = 6.1917$$

$$(P/A, 20\%, 10) = \frac{6.1917 - 1}{0.20 \times 6.1917} = \frac{5.1917}{1.2383} = 4.1925$$

$$(P/F, 20\%, 10) = \frac{1}{6.1917} = 0.1615$$

$$NPV_{20\%} = -8,00,000 + 2,20,000 \times 4.1925 + 50,000 \times 0.1615$$

$$NPV_{20\%} = -8,00,000 + 9,22,350 + 8,075 = 1,30,425 \text{ ₹}$$

Since NPV is still positive, IRR is **higher than 20%**.

**Trial 2:** Try $r = 25\%$

$$(1.25)^{10} = 9.3132$$

$$(P/A, 25\%, 10) = \frac{9.3132 - 1}{0.25 \times 9.3132} = \frac{8.3132}{2.3283} = 3.5705$$

$$(P/F, 25\%, 10) = \frac{1}{9.3132} = 0.1074$$

$$NPV_{25\%} = -8,00,000 + 2,20,000 \times 3.5705 + 50,000 \times 0.1074$$

$$NPV_{25\%} = -8,00,000 + 7,85,510 + 5,370 = -9,120 \text{ ₹}$$

NPV is slightly negative at 25%, so IRR lies between 20% and 25%.

**Linear Interpolation:**

$$\text{IRR} = 20\% + \frac{NPV_{20\%}}{NPV_{20\%} - NPV_{25\%}} \times (25\% - 20\%)$$

$$\text{IRR} = 20\% + \frac{1,30,425}{1,30,425 - (-9,120)} \times 5\%$$

$$\text{IRR} = 20\% + \frac{1,30,425}{1,39,545} \times 5\%$$

$$\text{IRR} = 20\% + 0.9345 \times 5\% = 20\% + 4.67\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{IRR} \approx 24.67\%}$$

**Decision:** Since IRR (24.67%) >> Hurdle Rate (12%), the project is **highly attractive**.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Criterion | VFD Project Value | Hurdle | Decision |
|-----------|------------------|--------|----------|
| SPP | 3.64 years | < 4 years | ✅ Accept |
| NPV @ 12% | ₹ 4,59,142 | > 0 | ✅ Accept |
| IRR | 24.67% | > 12% | ✅ Accept |
| B-C Ratio | $\frac{12,59,142}{8,00,000} = 1.57$ | > 1 | ✅ Accept |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Management Implementation Framework (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    policyStart[Energy Policy and Top Management Commitment] --> policyEnd[Set Energy Objectives and Targets]
    policyEnd --> auditStart[Conduct Detailed Energy Audit]
    auditStart --> auditEnd[Identify Energy Conservation Measures ECMs]
    auditEnd --> finStart[Apply Financial Analysis Tools]
    finStart --> finEnd[Rank ECMs by NPV IRR and SPP]
    finEnd --> planStart[Develop Energy Action Plan EAP]
    planStart --> planEnd[Schedule Implementation using Gantt Chart]
    planEnd --> implStart[Execute Implementation Phase]
    implStart --> implEnd[Commissioning and Verification]
    implEnd --> monStart[Monitor using EnPIs and SEC]
    monStart --> monEnd{Check Performance vs Target}
    monEnd --> gapYes[Gap Identified]
    monEnd --> okNo[Performance Satisfactory]
    gapYes --> corrective[Take Corrective Action PDCA]
    corrective --> monStart
    okNo --> sustain[Sustain and Review Annually]
    sustain --> auditStart
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flowchart is a **closed-loop system**. Note that after "Performance Satisfactory," the process loops back to the **energy audit** stage — this embodies the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** philosophy embedded in ISO 50001.

### 4.2 PDCA Cycle for Energy Management (ISO 50001)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    planNode[PLAN - Establish EnPIs, Objectives, Energy Review] --> doNode[DO - Implement Energy Action Plan, Train Staff, Operate Controls]
    doNode --> checkNode[CHECK - Monitor, Measure, Audit, Compare with EnPIs]
    checkNode --> actNode[ACT - Correct Deviations, Update Plan, Management Review]
    actNode --> planNode
```

### 4.3 Financial Tool Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart{Is the Project a Quick Screening Need?}
    qStart --> yesSPP[Use Simple Payback Period SPP]
    qStart --> noNPV{Is Project Life greater than 5 years and High Investment?}
    noNPV --> yesUseNPV[Use NPV IRR and LCC]
    noNPV --> noAnnual{Is Annual Cash Flow Roughly Constant?}
    noAnnual --> yesUniform[Apply Uniform Series P/A factor]
    noAnnual --> noVariable[Use Year-by-Year Discounting]
    yesSPP --> decision[Make Investment Decision]
    yesUseNPV --> decision
    yesUniform --> decision
    noVariable --> decision
```

### 4.4 Energy Action Plan (EAP) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    eapStart[Energy Action Plan Document] --> eapA[Section A - Project Description and Scope]
    eapA --> eapB[Section B - Baseline Energy and EnPI]
    eapB --> eapC[Section C - Proposed ECMs and Technical Justification]
    eapC --> eapD[Section D - Financial Analysis NPV IRR SPP]
    eapD --> eapE[Section E - Implementation Schedule Gantt]
    eapE --> eapF[Section F - Resource Allocation Budget and Manpower]
    eapF --> eapG[Section G - Monitoring Verification and M and V Plan]
    eapG --> eapH[Section H - Risk Analysis and Mitigation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Simple Payback Period. State its two major limitations as a tool for energy project evaluation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Simple Payback Period is the time required for the cumulative net cash savings from an energy conservation measure to equal the initial capital investment. Mathematically,

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{\text{Initial Investment } (I)}{\text{Annual Net Savings } (A_s)}$$

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Ignores Time Value of Money** — treats a rupee saved in year 5 the same as a rupee saved today, leading to overestimation of project desirability for long-life projects.
2. **Ignores Post-Payback Cash Flows** — two projects with identical SPP may have vastly different savings beyond the payback year, but SPP cannot distinguish them.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is an Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)? Give two examples used in industry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** An EnPI is a quantitative metric that expresses an organization's energy consumption normalized against a relevant variable (such as production output, floor area, or degree-days), enabling objective tracking of energy performance over time and across facilities.

**Examples (1 mark, any two):**
1. **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** kWh per tonne of cement produced in a cement plant.
2. **Energy Intensity:** TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) per ₹ Crore of turnover in a commercial building.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**(A) Question A (14 Marks):**
A pharmaceutical plant in Kochi is evaluating the replacement of its existing 75 kW constant-speed motor on a chilled water pump with a high-efficiency motor fitted with a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD). The following data is available:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Initial Investment (motor + VFD + installation) | ₹ 12,00,000 |
| Annual Energy Savings | ₹ 4,50,000 |
| Annual Additional Maintenance Cost | ₹ 30,000 |
| Project Life | 12 years |
| Discount Rate | 14% |
| Salvage Value at end of year 12 | ₹ 1,00,000 |

Calculate:
**(a)** Simple Payback Period and Discounted Payback Period *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Net Present Value, Benefit-Cost Ratio, and IRR (using interpolation) *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Net annual savings:
$$A_s = 4,50,000 - 30,000 = 4,20,000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

Simple Payback Period:
$$\text{SPP} = \frac{12,00,000}{4,20,000} = 2.857 \text{ years} \approx 2.86 \text{ years}$$

Discounted Payback Period — we need to find year $t$ such that cumulative discounted savings reach ₹ 12,00,000.

Present worth factor at 14%:
$$(P/A, 14\%, t) = \frac{(1.14)^t - 1}{0.14 \times (1.14)^t}$$

Year by year calculation of discounted savings:

| Year $t$ | $(1.14)^t$ | $DF_t$ | Discounted Savings | Cumulative |
|----------|------------|--------|--------------------|------------|
| 1 | 1.1400 | 0.8772 | 3,68,420 | 3,68,420 |
| 2 | 1.2996 | 0.7695 | 3,23,176 | 6,91,596 |
| 3 | 1.4815 | 0.6750 | 2,83,488 | 9,75,084 |
| 4 | 1.6890 | 0.5921 | 2,48,674 | 12,23,758 |

Cumulative discounted savings exceed ₹ 12,00,000 between year 3 and year 4.

Linear interpolation for exact DPP:

$$\text{DPP} = 3 + \frac{12,00,000 - 9,75,084}{2,48,674} = 3 + \frac{2,24,916}{2,48,674}$$

$$\text{DPP} = 3 + 0.905 = 3.90 \text{ years}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SPP} = 2.86 \text{ years}, \quad \text{DPP} = 3.90 \text{ years}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Present worth factor at 14%, $n=12$:
$$(1.14)^{12} = 4.8159$$
$$(P/A, 14\%, 12) = \frac{4.8159 - 1}{0.14 \times 4.8159} = \frac{3.8159}{0.6742} = 5.6599$$

Present value of annual savings:
$$PV_{A_s} = 4,20,000 \times 5.6599 = 23,77,158 \text{ ₹}$$

Present value of salvage:
$$PV_{S_v} = \frac{1,00,000}{4.8159} = 20,764 \text{ ₹}$$

Total PV of inflows = $23,77,158 + 20,764 = 23,97,922$ ₹

**Net Present Value:**
$$\text{NPV} = 23,97,922 - 12,00,000 = 11,97,922 \text{ ₹}$$

**Benefit-Cost Ratio:**
$$B/C = \frac{23,97,922}{12,00,000} = 1.998 \approx 2.0$$

**IRR by Interpolation:**

At 30%:
$$(1.30)^{12} = 23.298$$
$$(P/A, 30\%, 12) = \frac{22.298}{0.30 \times 23.298} = \frac{22.298}{6.989} = 3.1903$$
$$(P/F, 30\%, 12) = \frac{1}{23.298} = 0.04292$$
$$NPV_{30\%} = -12,00,000 + 4,20,000 \times 3.1903 + 1,00,000 \times 0.04292$$
$$NPV_{30\%} = -12,00,000 + 13,39,926 + 4,292 = 1,44,218 \text{ ₹}$$

At 35%:
$$(1.35)^{12} = 43.219$$
$$(P/A, 35\%, 12) = \frac{42.219}{0.35 \times 43.219} = \frac{42.219}{15.127} = 2.7910$$
$$(P/F, 35\%, 12) = \frac{1}{43.219} = 0.02314$$
$$NPV_{35\%} = -12,00,000 + 4,20,000 \times 2.7910 + 1,00,000 \times 0.02314$$
$$NPV_{35\%} = -12,00,000 + 11,72,220 + 2,314 = -25,466 \text{ ₹}$$

Interpolation:
$$\text{IRR} = 30\% + \frac{1,44,218}{1,44,218 - (-25,466)} \times 5\% = 30\% + \frac{1,44,218}{1,69,684} \times 5\%$$

$$\text{IRR} = 30\% + 0.850 \times 5\% = 30\% + 4.25\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{NPV} = ₹ 11,97,922, \quad B/C = 2.0, \quad \text{IRR} \approx 34.25\%}$$

**Conclusion:** All criteria indicate **strong acceptance**.

---

**(B) Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**

Explain in detail the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle as applied to **ISO 50001 Energy Management System (EnMS)**. How does it serve as a tool for effective implementation of energy management? Compare it with the older **ISO 14001 (Environmental Management System)** framework.

**Model Answer Outline (14 marks):**

**1. Introduction to ISO 50001 (3 marks):**
ISO 50001 is an international standard published in 2011 (revised 2018) specifying the requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and improving an **Energy Management System (EnMS)**. It follows the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** common to all ISO management system standards.

**2. PDCA Cycle Phases (8 marks, ~2 marks each phase):**

- **PLAN:** Establish energy policy, conduct **energy review**, identify **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)**, set **energy objectives and targets**, form the **Energy Management Team**.
- **DO:** Implement the **Energy Action Plan**, provide **competence and training**, ensure **operational controls** (e.g., SOPs for boiler operation), maintain **documented information** and **communication**.
- **CHECK:** **Monitor, measure, and analyze** energy performance; conduct **internal audits** of the EnMS; evaluate **compliance with legal requirements**; perform **management review** of energy data and EnPIs.
- **ACT:** Address **non-conformities and corrective actions**, undertake **continual improvement** of energy performance, update the energy policy and objectives based on review findings.

**3. ISO 50001 vs ISO 14001 Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | ISO 50001 | ISO 14001 |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Focus | Energy performance & efficiency | Overall environmental impact |
| Core Metric | EnPI (Energy Performance Indicator) | Environmental Aspect/Impact |
| Legal Basis | Energy Conservation Act, 2001 (India) | Environmental Protection Act, 1986 |
| Driver | Cost reduction, sustainability | Regulatory compliance, CSR |
| Methodology | Energy Review + EnPI | Aspect-Impact Register |
| PDCA | Same HLS structure | Same HLS structure |
| Commonality | Can be integrated with ISO 9001, 14001, 45001 | Can be integrated with ISO 50001, 9001 |

**Conclusion:** ISO 50001 provides a **systematic, audit-ready, internationally recognized framework** that converts energy management from ad-hoc projects into a **continuous, organization-wide discipline** — making it the most important single tool for long-term effective implementation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the initial investment sign in NPV:** Initial investment is a *cash outflow* at $t=0$, so $CF_0 = -I$. Many students write $CF_0 = +I$, which reverses the entire sign of NPV.
> 2. **Not using the correct present worth factor for salvage value:** Salvage is a *single future cash flow*, so use $(P/F, i, n) = \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}$, not $(P/A, i, n)$.
> 3. **Interpolation mistakes in IRR:** The formula is $\text{IRR} = r_1 + \dfrac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2}(r_2 - r_1)$ where $r_1 < r_2$ and NPV changes sign between them. Students often reverse the denominator.
> 4. **Confusing SPP with DPP:** SPP uses *undiscounted* cash flow, DPP uses *discounted* cash flow. DPP is always ≥ SPP.
> 5. **Omitting units in the final answer:** KTU examiners *will* deduct ½ mark for writing "NPV = 4,59,142" without the ₹ symbol or "years" in payback period.
> 6. **Writing ISO 50001 in detail without mapping to PDCA:** If the question is on PDCA, the four phases must be *explicitly* named with their activities, not vaguely described.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tools for Effective Implementation**

- **SPP** is the *easiest* and *most overused* tool. **NPV** is the *most academically rigorous* tool. **IRR** is the *most intuitive* for comparing with hurdle rate.
- The **$(P/A, i, n)$ factor** equals $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ — memorize this and you can solve 80% of KTU financial problems.
- **Discount rate in India:** 10% (PCRA), 12% (BEE), 15% (private industries with high cost of capital). Always **state the discount rate explicitly** before computing NPV.
- **Decision Rule Summary:** NPV > 0 ✓, IRR > hurdle ✓, SPP < cutoff ✓, B-C > 1 ✓, EnPI improving year-on-year ✓.
- **ISO 50001 + PDCA** is the *gold standard* systematic tool. It integrates energy policy → review → planning → implementation → verification → continual improvement.
- **EnPIs** must be **normalized** (e.g., kWh/tonne) — never use absolute kWh as an EnPI, because production volume variations will mask real performance changes.
- **Life Cycle Costing** considers *all* cost streams: initial, operating, maintenance, and salvage. It is mandatory for comparing equipment with *different* capital and operating cost profiles.
- **Energy Action Plan (EAP)** is the operational document that bridges the *audit report* and the *actual implementation*. It must include scope, baseline, ECMs, financials, schedule, resources, M&V plan, and risk analysis.
- **CUSUM (Cumulative Sum)** charts and **statistical process control** techniques are advanced monitoring tools for detecting gradual energy drifts in plants.
- **For KTU numericals,** always tabulate the year-by-year cash flow and present value calculation. Examiners reward **clarity of presentation** and **explicit intermediate steps**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Global Warming in Climate Change
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Impact of Global Warming in Climate Change

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Global Warming** is defined as the long-term, persistent increase in the average surface temperature of the Earth due to the accumulation of greenhouse gases (GHGs) in the atmosphere, primarily caused by anthropogenic (human) activities such as fossil fuel combustion, deforestation, and industrial processes. According to the **Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) AR6 Report (2021)**, the global mean surface temperature has risen by approximately **1.1 °C** above the pre-industrial baseline (1850–1900).

**Climate Change**, on the other hand, is a broader phenomenon encompassing the statistically significant variations in the mean state and variability of climate parameters (temperature, precipitation, wind patterns, sea level, and frequency of extreme events) that persist over decades or longer. The **United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC)** defines climate change as "*a change of climate which is attributed directly or indirectly to human activity that alters the composition of the global atmosphere.*"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** While the terms are often used interchangeably, **Global Warming** refers specifically to the rise in global mean temperature, whereas **Climate Change** includes ALL atmospheric and oceanic shifts caused by warming. Examiners frequently test this distinction for 3-mark questions.

## 1.2 The Greenhouse Effect — Intuitive Analogy

**Real-World Analogy — The Earth's Blanket:**

Imagine the Earth wrapped in a thin cotton blanket. Sunlight (shortwave radiation) passes through the blanket and warms the body underneath. The body emits heat (longwave infrared radiation), but instead of escaping freely, the blanket traps a portion and reflects it back. This is exactly how greenhouse gases work in the atmosphere.

- The **Earth's surface** = the warm body.
- The **atmosphere** = the blanket.
- **Greenhouse gases (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O, CFCs)** = the threads of the blanket that trap heat.

When humans add *more* threads (more GHGs), the blanket becomes thicker, trapping **more heat**, raising the global temperature.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Solar Constant (S₀)** | **1361 W/m²** |
| **Albedo of Earth (α)** | **0.30 (30%)** |
| **Earth's Mean Surface Temperature** | **288 K (15 °C)** |
| **Pre-Industrial CO₂ Level (1750)** | **280 ppm** |
| **Current CO₂ Level (2024)** | **~422 ppm** |
| **Stefan–Boltzmann Constant (σ)** | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **CO₂ concentration has increased by ~51%** since pre-industrial times. This single number is the most cited statistic in IPCC reports and KTU board questions on climate change.

## 1.4 Distinguishing Global Warming from Climate Change

| Aspect | Global Warming | Climate Change |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Temperature rise only | All climate parameters |
| **Timescale** | Long-term (decades) | Long-term (decades to millennia) |
| **Primary Cause** | GHG emissions | GHG + land use + aerosols |
| **Measured By** | °C temperature anomaly | Temperature, rainfall, sea level, ice mass |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Greenhouse Gases (GHGs) — Quantified

The principal greenhouse gases regulated under the **Kyoto Protocol** and the **Paris Agreement (2015)** are:

1. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — Long lifetime (~hundreds of years in climate terms)
2. **Methane (CH₄)** — Lifetime ~12 years; ~80× more potent per molecule over 20 years
3. **Nitrous Oxide (N₂O)** — Lifetime ~114 years; ~273× CO₂ over 100 years
4. **Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)** — Industrial refrigerants
5. **Perfluorocarbons (PFCs)** — Aluminum smelting by-product
6. **Sulphur Hexafluoride (SF₆)** — Used in electrical switchgear
7. **Water Vapour (H₂O)** — Most abundant, but a feedback, not a forcing

## 2.2 Global Warming Potential (GWP) — Core Concept

The **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** is a relative measure of how much heat a greenhouse gas traps compared to CO₂ over a specified time horizon (commonly 100 years).

### Formula:

$$ \text{GWP}_x = \frac{\int_0^{TH} a_x \cdot C_{x}(t) \, dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} \cdot C_{CO_2}(t) \, dt} $$

Where:
- $a_x$ = radiative efficiency of gas *x* (W/m²/ppb)
- $C_x(t)$ = atmospheric decay function (fraction remaining at time *t*)
- $TH$ = time horizon (typically 100 years)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GWP must always be stated with a time horizon.** A GWP of "25" for CH₄ means *over 100 years*. KTU questions often specify this; failing to state the horizon costs marks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Climate Change & Global Warming

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{abs} = S_0 \cdot (1 - \alpha) / 4$ | Average solar flux absorbed by Earth | W/m² |
| 2 | $T_{eq} = \left( \dfrac{I_{abs}}{4\sigma} \right)^{1/4}$ | Effective radiating temperature | K |
| 3 | $CO_2_{eq} = m_x \times GWP_x$ | CO₂ equivalent emissions | tonnes CO₂eq |
| 4 | $\Delta T = \lambda \cdot \Delta F$ | Temperature change from forcing | °C |
| 5 | $F = 5.35 \cdot \ln(C/C_0)$ | Radiative forcing from CO₂ | W/m² |
| 6 | $E_{emitted} = E_{generated} - E_{consumed}$ | Net emissions from energy use | kg CO₂ |
| 7 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{GHG}}{\text{Output}}$ | Carbon footprint (per unit product) | kg CO₂eq/unit |
| 8 | $R = 0.5 \cdot \Delta T$ | Sea level rise approximation | mm/yr (per °C) |
| 9 | $\Delta F = \sum (A_x \cdot \Delta C_x)$ | Total radiative forcing | W/m² |
| 10 | $\text{ERF} = \Delta F_{\text{LW}} + \Delta F_{\text{SW}}$ | Effective Radiative Forcing | W/m² |

**Where:**
- $\lambda$ = climate sensitivity parameter (~0.5 K/(W/m²))
- $C$ = current CO₂ concentration (ppm)
- $C_0$ = reference CO₂ concentration (ppm)
- $A_x$ = radiative efficiency of gas *x*

## 2.4 The Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Energy In:** Shortwave solar radiation enters the atmosphere.
- Of 1361 W/m² striking the top of the atmosphere, ~30% is reflected (albedo).
- Net absorbed: ~240 W/m² distributed over the Earth's surface.

**Step 2 — Surface Heating:** Earth's surface absorbs this and warms to ~288 K.

**Step 3 — Outgoing Longwave Radiation (OLR):** The surface emits infrared radiation upward.

**Step 4 — Greenhouse Trapping:** GHGs absorb outgoing IR, re-radiating it both upward and downward.

**Step 5 — Net Energy Imbalance:** When absorbed energy > emitted energy, the Earth warms.

$$ \Delta E_{net} = E_{in} - E_{out} > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Warming} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Climate models (GCMs) used in IPCC reports rely on this energy balance. In energy auditing, the same radiative forcing principle is applied when calculating the embodied carbon of fossil-fuel-powered equipment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Earth's Equilibrium Temperature with Greenhouse Effect

We begin with the Stefan–Boltzmann Law applied to Earth as a blackbody.

**Step 1 — Solar flux at Earth's distance:**
The Sun emits isotropically. The total power intercepted by Earth (a disk of area $\pi R^2$) is:

$$ P_{in} = S_0 \cdot \pi R^2 $$

**Step 2 — Accounting for albedo reflection:**
Of this incident radiation, a fraction $\alpha$ is reflected back to space. The absorbed power is:

$$ P_{abs} = S_0 \cdot \pi R^2 \cdot (1 - \alpha) $$

**Step 3 — Average flux over the entire surface area ($4\pi R^2$):**
Because Earth radiates from its entire spherical surface:

$$ I_{abs} = \frac{P_{abs}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{S_0 (1 - \alpha)}{4} $$

**Step 4 — Stefan–Boltzmann equilibrium:**
Earth radiates according to $\sigma T^4$. At equilibrium:

$$ \sigma T_{eq}^4 = \frac{S_0 (1 - \alpha)}{4} $$

**Step 5 — Solving for $T_{eq}$:**

$$ T_{eq} = \left( \frac{S_0 (1 - \alpha)}{4\sigma} \right)^{1/4} $$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$ T_{eq} = \left( \frac{1361 \times (1 - 0.30)}{4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \right)^{1/4} $$

$$ T_{eq} = \left( \frac{952.7}{2.268 \times 10^{-7}} \right)^{1/4} = \left( 4.20 \times 10^{9} \right)^{1/4} $$

$$ T_{eq} \approx 254 \; \text{K} \; (-19 \; \text{°C}) $$

**Step 7 — Reconciling with the actual surface temperature (288 K):**

The greenhouse effect contributes a warming of:

$$ \Delta T_{GHG} = 288 - 254 = 34 \; \text{K} $$

This 34 K "extra" warming is *natural* and *necessary for life*. Anthropogenic GHGs are pushing this further.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Favourite:** Examiners love asking the student to derive $T_{eq}$ and then explain why the actual surface temperature is 288 K. The answer must explicitly mention the **greenhouse effect** as the cause of the 34 K difference.

## 3.2 Derivation: CO₂ Equivalent Emissions

Suppose a facility emits the following in one year:
- CO₂: 1000 tonnes
- CH₄: 5 tonnes
- N₂O: 2 tonnes

Using GWP₁₀₀ values:
- GWP(CO₂) = 1
- GWP(CH₄) = 28
- GWP(N₂O) = 273

**Step 1 — Apply the CO₂ equivalent formula for each gas:**

$$ CO_2^{eq}(CO_2) = 1000 \times 1 = 1000 \; \text{tonnes} $$

$$ CO_2^{eq}(CH_4) = 5 \times 28 = 140 \; \text{tonnes} $$

$$ CO_2^{eq}(N_2O) = 2 \times 273 = 546 \; \text{tonnes} $$

**Step 2 — Sum total CO₂ equivalent emissions:**

$$ CO_{2,eq}^{total} = 1000 + 140 + 546 = 1686 \; \text{tonnes CO}_2\text{eq} $$

**Step 3 — Convert to kg (1 tonne = 1000 kg):**

$$ CO_{2,eq}^{total} = 1.686 \times 10^6 \; \text{kg CO}_2\text{eq} $$

## 3.3 Derivation: Radiative Forcing from CO₂ Doubling

Using the **Myhre et al. (1998)** logarithmic relationship:

$$ \Delta F = 5.35 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) \quad [\text{W/m}^2] $$

**Step 1 — Identify values (doubling from 280 to 560 ppm):**

$$ C = 560 \; \text{ppm}, \quad C_0 = 280 \; \text{ppm} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$ \Delta F = 5.35 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{560}{280}\right) = 5.35 \cdot \ln(2) $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$ \Delta F = 5.35 \times 0.6931 = 3.71 \; \text{W/m}^2 $$

**Step 4 — Predicted temperature rise using climate sensitivity ($\lambda \approx 0.5$ K/(W/m²)):**

$$ \Delta T = \lambda \cdot \Delta F = 0.5 \times 3.71 = 1.86 \; \text{°C} $$

This matches IPCC's "Equilibrium Climate Sensitivity" range of **1.5 °C to 4.5 °C** per CO₂ doubling.

## 3.4 Derivation: Sea Level Rise from Thermal Expansion

A simplified steric (thermal) sea level rise formula:

$$ \Delta \text{SL} = \alpha_{thermal} \cdot \Delta T \cdot H_{ocean} $$

**Step 1 — Use values:**
- $\alpha_{thermal}$ (thermal expansion coefficient of seawater) ≈ $1.5 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1}$
- $\Delta T$ = 1 °C (warming)
- $H_{ocean}$ (mean mixed layer depth) = 1000 m

**Step 2 — Calculate:**

$$ \Delta \text{SL} = (1.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1 \times 1000 = 0.15 \; \text{m} = 150 \; \text{mm} $$

**Step 3 — Combined with ice melt (~3.4 mm/yr observed rate, accelerating):**

Projected 2100 sea level rise under RCP 8.5 (worst case) = **0.63 m to 1.01 m**.

---
