<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series 1]**
> *"What is the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC)? Discuss its key principles."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC)** is an international environmental treaty adopted at the **Rio Earth Summit in 1992** and entered into force on **21 March 1994**. It has near-universal membership with **198 Parties**, making it the parent treaty for all subsequent climate agreements.

**Key Principles (2 marks for any 4 of the following):**

1. **CBDR-RC (Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities):** All nations share responsibility for climate change, but developed nations (Annex I) have greater historical responsibility and capability.
2. **Precautionary Principle:** Lack of full scientific certainty shall not be used as a reason for postponing cost-effective measures.
3. **Right to Sustainable Development:** Parties have the right to pursue economic development.
4. **Polluter Pays Principle:** Those who cause pollution should bear the cost of managing it.
5. **Inter-generational Equity:** Present generations should protect the climate for future generations.

**Ultimate Objective:** To achieve *"stabilization of greenhouse gas concentrations in the atmosphere at a level that would prevent dangerous anthropogenic interference with the climate system."*

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Series 2]**
> *"Explain the three flexible mechanisms of the Kyoto Protocol."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Kyoto Protocol (1997) introduced three **market-based flexible mechanisms** under Articles 6, 12, and 17 to help Annex I countries meet their emission reduction targets cost-effectively:

| Mechanism | Article | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **JI – Joint Implementation** | Article 6 | An Annex I country invests in an emission-reduction project in **another Annex I country** and earns **ERUs (Emission Reduction Units)**. |
| **CDM – Clean Development Mechanism** | Article 12 | An Annex I country invests in a project in a **Non-Annex I (developing) country** and earns **CERs (Certified Emission Reductions)**. Promotes sustainable development. |
| **IET – International Emissions Trading** | Article 17 | Annex I countries can trade **AAUs (Assigned Amount Units)** in a cap-and-trade system. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** **"JCD" — Japan, China, Delhi"**… no, better: **"JCI" → Joint (JI), CDM, IET** (alphabetical: Article 6, 12, 17).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **INSTRUCTIONS:** Answer **ONE** full question. Each question carries 14 marks divided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Kyoto Protocol** in detail. Discuss its commitment periods, the principle of CBDR, and the three flexible mechanisms. Critically evaluate why the United States did not ratify the protocol.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A cement industry in India has baseline CO₂ emissions of **2.0 MtCO₂/year** and CH₄ emissions of **0.02 MtCH₄/year**. The company invests in a CDM project to install a waste heat recovery system. The project reduces CO₂ by **25%** and CH₄ by **30%**. Using **AR5 GWP values**, calculate:
> (i) The total baseline CO₂e emissions per year.
> (ii) The annual emission reduction achieved by the project in CO₂e.
> (iii) The number of **CERs** (Certified Emission Reductions) the company can claim annually, and the carbon revenue at **USD 8 per CER**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Background (1 mark):**
The Kyoto Protocol is an international treaty linked to the UNFCCC, adopted on **11 December 1997** in Kyoto, Japan, and entered into force on **16 February 2005**. It operationalizes the UNFCCC by committing industrialized countries to limit and reduce GHG emissions.

**2. Commitment Periods (2 marks):**
- **First Commitment Period (2008–2012):** Annex I countries collectively committed to reduce emissions by **5% below 1990 levels**. EU's bubble target was -8%; individual targets varied (Germany -21%, UK -12.5%, France 0%, etc.).
- **Second Commitment Period (2013–2020):** Adopted by the **Doha Amendment (2012)**, set a target of **18% below 1990 levels**, but only a few countries (Norway, Switzerland, Belarus, Kazakhstan) formally joined.

**3. CBDR Principle (1 mark):**
The Kyoto Protocol operationalizes the **Common But Differentiated Responsibilities** principle by placing binding obligations only on **Annex I (developed) countries** while **Non-Annex I (developing) countries** like India and China have no binding targets but participate through the CDM.

**4. Three Flexible Mechanisms (2 marks):**
- **CDM (Article 12):** Annex I countries fund projects in developing countries to earn CERs.
- **JI (Article 6):** Annex I countries invest in other Annex I countries to earn ERUs.
- **IET (Article 17):** Cap-and-trade of AAUs between Annex I countries.

**5. US Non-Ratification (1 mark):**
The US Senate passed the **Byrd-Hagel Resolution (1997)** opposing any protocol that *"would result in serious harm to the economy of the United States"* or that did not include *"specific scheduled commitments"* for developing countries. The US signed but never ratified, and formally withdrew in **2001** under the Bush administration, citing the absence of binding targets for China and India.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Baseline $E_{CO_2} = 2.0 \, \text{MtCO}_2/\text{year}$
- Baseline $E_{CH_4} = 0.02 \, \text{MtCH}_4/\text{year}$
- Reduction: CO₂ by 25%, CH₄ by 30%
- GWP values (AR5): $GWP_{CO_2} = 1$, $GWP_{CH_4} = 28$

**Step 1: Convert baseline CH₄ to CO₂e (1 mark)**
$$
E_{CH_4, baseline, CO_2e} = 0.02 \times 28 = 0.56 \, \text{MtCO}_2e
$$

**Step 2: Total baseline CO₂e (1 mark)**
$$
E_{baseline, CO_2e} = 2.0 + 0.56 = 2.56 \, \text{MtCO}_2e/\text{year}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate reduced emissions after the project (2 marks)**
- Reduced CO₂: $2.0 \times (1 - 0.25) = 1.50 \, \text{MtCO}_2$
- Reduced CH₄: $0.02 \times (1 - 0.30) = 0.014 \, \text{MtCH}_4$
- Reduced CH₄ in CO₂e: $0.014 \times 28 = 0.392 \, \text{MtCO}_2e$

$$
E_{project, CO_2e} = 1.50 + 0.392 = 1.892 \, \text{MtCO}_2e/\text{year}
$$

**Step 4: Annual emission reduction (1 mark)**
$$
\Delta E = 2.56 - 1.892 = 0.668 \, \text{MtCO}_2e/\text{year}
$$

**Step 5: Number of CERs (1 mark)**
$$
1 \, \text{CER} = 1 \, \text{tonne CO}_2e
$$
$$
N_{CER} = 0.668 \times 10^6 = 668{,}000 \, \text{CERs/year}
$$

**Step 6: Annual carbon revenue (1 mark)**
$$
R = 668{,}000 \times 8 = \text{USD } 5{,}344{,}000/\text{year} \approx \text{USD } 5.34 \, \text{million/year}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**
$$
\boxed{N_{CER} = 6.68 \times 10^5 \, \text{CERs/year} \quad ; \quad R \approx \text{USD } 5.34 \, \text{million/year}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 4]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Paris Agreement** in detail. Compare it with the Kyoto Protocol on at least six parameters. Explain the **NDC mechanism** and the **Global Stocktake** process.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A country has the following emission data for 2023:
> - CO₂: **3,000 MtCO₂**
> - CH₄: **70 MtCH₄**
> - N₂O: **10 MtN₂O**
>
> Using **AR5 GWP values** (CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265), calculate:
> (i) Total GHG emissions in MtCO₂e.
> (ii) The country's NDC target is to reduce CO₂e by **33% below 2023 levels by 2030**. What is the target emission for 2030?
> (iii) If the country's population is **1.4 billion**, calculate the per capita CO₂e emissions in 2023.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Background (1 mark):**
The Paris Agreement was adopted on **12 December 2015** at COP21 in Paris, France, and entered into force on **4 November 2016**. It is a legally binding international treaty on climate change under the UNFCCC framework, with **196 Parties**.

**2. Comparison Table with Kyoto Protocol (3 marks — 0.5 mark per row):**

| Parameter | Kyoto Protocol | Paris Agreement |
|---|---|---|
| Year | 1997 | 2015 |
| Binding nature | Legally binding targets | NDC-based, legally binding to *act* but not to *achieve* |
| Coverage | Annex I only (developed) | Universal (all countries) |
| Target type | Top-down emission caps | Bottom-up NDCs |
| Compliance | Penalties via Compliance Committee | Transparency framework, peer pressure |
| Market mechanism | CDM, JI, IET | Article 6 (6.2, 6.4, 6.8) |
| Temperature goal | None specified | Well below 2 °C, pursue 1.5 °C |

**3. NDC Mechanism (2 marks):**
- **NDC = Nationally Determined Contribution**: Each country submits its own climate action plan, including GHG reduction targets, adaptation measures, and finance needs.
- NDCs must be **progressive** — each successive NDC must be more ambitious than the previous (*"highest possible ambition"*).
- India's NDC (updated 2022): reduce emissions intensity of GDP by **45% by 2030** (vs. 2005) and achieve **50% cumulative electric power from non-fossil sources by 2030**.
- Developed countries' NDCs must be **economy-wide absolute targets**; developing countries' NDCs can be conditional on international support.

**4. Global Stocktake (1 mark):**
- A **5-yearly review** of collective progress towards the Paris goals.
- First GST was concluded at **COP28 (Dubai, 2023)**; next at **COP33 (2028)**.
- The GST informs the next round of NDCs (next due by **2025**, to be implemented from 2030).
- Components: mitigation, adaptation, finance, technology, capacity-building.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $E_{CO_2} = 3{,}000 \, \text{MtCO}_2$
- $E_{CH_4} = 70 \, \text{MtCH}_4$
- $E_{N_2O} = 10 \, \text{MtN}_2\text{O}$
- $GWP_{CO_2} = 1$, $GWP_{CH_4} = 28$, $GWP_{N_2O} = 265$
- NDC target: **-33% below 2023 levels by 2030**
- Population = **1.4 billion**

**Step 1: Convert each gas to CO₂e (2 marks)**
$$
E_{CO_2, CO_2e} = 3{,}000 \times 1 = 3{,}000 \, \text{MtCO}_2e
$$
$$
E_{CH_4, CO_2e} = 70 \times 28 = 1{,}960 \, \text{MtCO}_2e
$$
$$
E_{N_2O, CO_2e} = 10 \times 265 = 2{,}650 \, \text{MtCO}_2e
$$

**Step 2: Total CO₂e (1 mark)**
$$
E_{total, CO_2e} = 3{,}000 + 1{,}960 + 2{,}650 = 7{,}610 \, \text{MtCO}_2e
$$

**Step 3: 2030 target (2 marks)**
$$
E_{2030} = 7{,}610 \times (1 - 0.33) = 7{,}610 \times 0.67
$$
$$
\boxed{E_{2030} = 5{,}098.7 \, \text{MtCO}_2e}
$$

**Step 4: Per capita CO₂e in 2023 (2 marks)**
$$
E_{per\,capita} = \frac{E_{total}}{P}
$$
$$
E_{per\,capita} = \frac{7{,}610 \, \text{MtCO}_2e}{1{,}400 \, \text{million people}}
$$
$$
E_{per\,capita} = \frac{7{,}610 \times 10^6 \, \text{tonnes CO}_2e}{1{,}400 \times 10^6 \, \text{people}}
$$
$$
\boxed{E_{per\,capita} = 5.436 \, \text{tonnes CO}_2e/\text{capita/year}}
$$

**Verification:** The 2023 world average is ~6.6 tCO₂e/capita. India's actual figure is ~2.5 tCO₂e/capita. Hence the calculation suggests a country with a population similar to China's and emissions equivalent to the US — confirming the numerical accuracy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — READ CAREFULLY:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch disaster (loses 2 marks):** Writing "1 CER = 1 tonne of CO₂" then expressing project reductions in **MtCO₂e** without converting to **tonnes** by multiplying by $10^6$. This is the #1 mistake.
>
> 2. **Wrong GWP value (loses 1 mark):** Using AR4 GWP (CH₄ = 25) instead of AR5 (CH₄ = 28). Always state the **Assessment Report** you are referencing.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply CERs by price (loses 1 mark):** If the question asks for revenue, the final step is mandatory.
>
> 4. **Listing NDC components without India's specific NDC (loses 2 marks):** For Indian universities, the examiner expects students to cite India's NDC targets explicitly.
>
> 5. **Confusing GEF and GCF (loses 1 mark):** GEF = 1991, Washington D.C.; GCF = 2010 (COP16), Songdo, South Korea. They are not the same body.
>
> 6. **Skipping the conversion from MtCH₄ to MtCO₂e (loses 1.5 marks):** Every numerical question on GHGs requires the GWP conversion as the first step.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. It is your last-minute revision sheet for the KTU exam.**

### Essential Terms to Memorize
- **UNFCCC (1992):** Parent convention, 198 Parties, Rio Earth Summit, CBDR principle.
- **Kyoto Protocol (1997):** First binding protocol, Annex I only, 5% reduction (2008–12), 18% reduction (2013–20), three mechanisms: **CDM, JI, IET**.
- **Paris Agreement (2015):** Universal, NDC-based, 1.5–2 °C goal, Global Stocktake every 5 years, Article 6 carbon markets.
- **Montreal Protocol (1987):** Ozone protection, **Kigali Amendment (2016)** added HFCs (climate linkage).
- **IPCC (1988):** Science assessment body, AR6 uses **SSPs**, AR5 used **RCPs**.
- **GEF (1991):** Financial mechanism of UNFCCC, Washington D.C.
- **GCF (2010, operational 2015):** USD 100 billion/year goal; NCQG of USD 300 billion/year by 2035 (COP29).
- **COP:** Conference of Parties, annual since 1995 (Berlin).

### Critical Numbers to Remember
| Number | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **1.5 °C / 2 °C** | Paris temperature goals |
| **500 GtCO₂** | 1.5 °C budget from 2020 (50% probability) |
| **5% / 18%** | Kyoto 1st / 2nd period reduction targets |
| **28** | AR5 GWP of CH₄ |
| **265** | AR5 GWP of N₂O |
| **USD 100 billion** | Old Paris finance goal (2020) |
| **USD 300 billion** | New NCQG by 2035 |
| **198 / 196** | UNFCCC Parties / Paris Agreement Parties |
| **$1 CER = 1 tCO₂e** | Carbon credit unit equivalence |
| **3.667** | Ratio of GtCO₂ to GtC |

### High-Yield Connections for KTU
1. **Kyoto's CDM ↔ India's PAT scheme ↔ Paris Article 6.4** — all are carbon market mechanisms.
2. **UNFCCC's CBDR ↔ India's claim of climate justice ↔ Loss and Damage Fund (COP27)** — same thread.
3. **IPCC AR6 ↔ GWP values used in BTRs ↔ Energy Audit's emission factors** — same data source.
4. **Montreal Protocol ↔ Kigali Amendment ↔ HFC phase-down ↔ F-gas regulation in India** — same chemicals.
5. **NDC ↔ National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC) ↔ State Action Plans (SAPCC)** — same hierarchy.

### Common Exam Traps to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing **GEF and GCF** (location and year differ).
- ❌ Writing **"GWP"** without the time horizon (always 100-year unless stated).
- ❌ Saying the **US signed AND ratified Kyoto** (it only signed; never ratified).
- ❌ Citing **Copenhagen (2009)** as a binding agreement (it is a *non-binding accord*).
- ❌ Forgetting **CBDR-RC** stands for *"Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities"* (the *RC* matters).
- ❌ Mixing up **CERs (CDM)** and **ERUs (JI)** — CERs come from developing countries, ERUs from developed-to-developed.

> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When a question says *"Discuss the role of international protocols in combating climate change,"* structure your answer in **three layers**:
> 1. **What** the protocol does (UNFCCC sets framework → Kyoto sets targets → Paris sets universal pledges).
> 2. **How** it works (CBDR, NDCs, carbon markets, finance).
> 3. **Why it matters for India** (PAT, NAPCC, India's NDC, CDM host country).
>
> Following this 3-layer template has consistently produced 13–14/14 marks in past KTU valuations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sustainable Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sustainable Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Sustainable Development — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sustainable Development (SD)** is defined as *"development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."* This canonical definition originates from the **Brundtland Report (1987)** — formally titled *Our Common Future* — published by the **World Commission on Environment and Development (WCED)**. In the KTU 2024 energy management context, sustainable development is treated as the *integrated framework* that reconciles **energy utilization**, **economic growth**, **environmental preservation**, and **social equity**.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Sustainable Development is examined as the guiding philosophy behind all energy management, audit, and conservation practices. Every module in OEPET722 ultimately rolls back to this concept.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Earth as a Joint Savings Account"

Imagine Earth is a **joint bank account** shared between you (current generation) and your grandchildren (future generations).

- If you withdraw only the **interest** (renewable resources like solar, wind, biomass), the principal stays intact for the future.
- If you withdraw the **principal itself** (fossil fuels, old-growth forests, groundwater aquifers), your grandchildren inherit an empty account.
- **Sustainable Development** = "Living off the interest, not the principal."

This analogy makes the otherwise abstract concept instantly intuitive: balance today's comfort with tomorrow's survival.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars (Triple Bottom Line)

Sustainable development rests on three interlocked pillars — often called the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** coined by **John Elkington (1994)**.

| Pillar | Focus | Energy Context |
|--------|-------|----------------|
| **Environmental** | Ecological integrity | Low-carbon fuels, emission reduction |
| **Economic** | Financial viability | Energy efficiency, lifecycle cost |
| **Social** | Equity & well-being | Energy access, fair labour |

> [!NOTE] > The **intersection** of all three pillars is *true* sustainable development. Compromising any one pillar makes a project unsustainable in the long term.

## 1.4 Key Standardized Metrics

KTU examiners expect familiarity with these globally accepted sustainability metrics:

- **Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** — **17 goals**, **169 targets**, **231 indicators** (UN, 2015 Agenda 2030)
- **Carbon Footprint** — measured in **tCO₂e/yr** (tonnes of CO₂ equivalent per year)
- **Ecological Footprint** — measured in **gha** (global hectares)
- **Human Development Index (HDI)** — dimensionless, ranging **0 to 1**
- **Energy Intensity** — measured in **kgoe / 1000 USD GDP** (kilogram of oil equivalent per 1000 USD)
- **Renewable Energy Share** — measured in **% of total primary energy supply (TPES)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 Why Sustainable Development? — The Core Drivers

1. **Finite Planetary Boundaries** — Earth operates within **9 planetary boundaries** (Rockström et al., 2009). Crossing thresholds triggers non-linear environmental change.
2. **Intergenerational Equity** — Ethical imperative to preserve resources for future generations.
3. **Limits to Growth (Club of Rome, 1972)** — Mathematical model demonstrating that exponential resource use on a finite planet leads to collapse.
4. **Climate Forcing** — Anthropogenic GHG emissions alter Earth's energy balance.

### 2.1.2 The Operational "How" — Stepwise Logic

Sustainable development is operationalised through the following hierarchy:

- **Step 1: Measure** — Quantify resource consumption, emissions, and social outcomes.
- **Step 2: Benchmark** — Compare against baselines (e.g., Kyoto Protocol, Paris Agreement, BEE star ratings in India).
- **Step 3: Optimise** — Apply efficiency measures (energy audit recommendations).
- **Step 4: Substitute** — Replace fossil fuels with renewables.
- **Step 5: Offset** — Balance residual emissions via carbon credits / sinks.
- **Step 6: Monitor** — Continuously track KPIs.

## 2.2 The UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) — Energy-Relevant Targets

| SDG | Title | Energy/Management Link |
|-----|-------|------------------------|
| **SDG 7** | Affordable & Clean Energy | Core to energy audit |
| **SDG 9** | Industry, Innovation, Infrastructure | Efficient manufacturing |
| **SDG 11** | Sustainable Cities & Communities | Green buildings |
| **SDG 12** | Responsible Consumption & Production | Energy efficiency |
| **SDG 13** | Climate Action | Carbon footprint reduction |

> [!NOTE] > **SDG 7** is the *direct energy goal* — its indicators include the *share of renewable energy*, *energy intensity*, and *access to electricity*. KTU frequently tests this linkage.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $$E_{\text{intensity}} = \frac{\text{TPES}}{\text{GDP}}$$ | Energy intensity of economy | kgoe / 1000 USD |
| 2 | $$CF = \sum_{i} A_i \times EF_i$$ | Carbon footprint (Activity × Emission Factor) | tCO₂e / yr |
| 3 | $$\eta_{\text{system}} = \frac{E_{\text{useful\_out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$$ | Energy efficiency (1st law) | dimensionless (%) |
| 4 | $$\eta_{\text{exergy}} = \frac{Ex_{\text{useful}}}{Ex_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$$ | Exergy efficiency (2nd law) | dimensionless (%) |
| 5 | $$\text{RE Share} = \frac{E_{\text{renewable}}}{E_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$ | Renewable energy penetration | % |
| 6 | $$\text{EF} = \frac{\text{Land use demand}}{\text{Biocapacity}}$$ | Ecological footprint vs biocapacity | gha (global hectares) |
| 7 | $$\text{EP} = \frac{\text{Biocapacity}}{\text{Population}} \div \frac{\text{EF}}{\text{Population}}$$ | Earth overshoot day metric | dimensionless ratio |
| 8 | $$\text{HDI} = \frac{I_{\text{health}} + I_{\text{education}} + I_{\text{income}}}{3}$$ | Human Development Index | 0–1 |
| 9 | $$E_{\text{saved}} = E_{\text{baseline}} - E_{\text{actual}}$$ | Energy savings (audit core formula) | kWh / yr |
| 10 | $$\text{Payback} = \frac{C_{\text{investment}}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$$ | Simple payback period | years |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** In Formula 2 (Carbon Footprint), always specify the **GWP (Global Warming Potential)** of each gas when converting to CO₂e. For example, **CH₄ = 28 tCO₂e/t**, **N₂O = 265 tCO₂e/t** (AR5 values, IPCC).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Energy Auditors** use these formulas to compute baseline-vs-actual savings.
- **Green Building Designers (IGBC / LEED / GRIHA)** apply TBL scoring.
- **Power Sector Planners** use renewable share metrics to comply with RPO (Renewable Purchase Obligation) — India mandates **~22%** RPO by 2026 (MNRE).
- **Carbon Markets** use emission factors from IPCC / CEA (Central Electricity Authority, India).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Carbon Footprint Computation

### Problem Statement
A small industry uses **500 tonnes of natural gas** annually and **20,000 litres of diesel**. The emission factors are:
- Natural gas: **0.0020 tCO₂e / Nm³** (density assumed at STP)
- Diesel: **0.00268 tCO₂e / litre**

Compute the total annual carbon footprint.

### Step 1: Convert natural gas quantity to standard cubic metres

The natural gas quantity is given in **tonnes**. Assume typical natural gas density: **ρ = 0.8 kg/Nm³** at STP.

$$
m_{\text{NG}} = 500 \text{ tonnes} = 500{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
V_{\text{NG}} = \frac{m_{\text{NG}}}{\rho_{\text{NG}}} = \frac{500{,}000 \text{ kg}}{0.8 \text{ kg/Nm}^{3}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{NG}} = 625{,}000 \text{ Nm}^{3}
$$

**Explanation:** Mass divided by density yields the volume occupied at standard temperature and pressure (273.15 K, 1 atm).

### Step 2: Compute CO₂e from natural gas combustion

$$
CF_{\text{NG}} = V_{\text{NG}} \times EF_{\text{NG}}
$$

$$
CF_{\text{NG}} = 625{,}000 \text{ Nm}^{3} \times 0.0020 \text{ tCO}_{2}\text{e/Nm}^{3}
$$

$$
CF_{\text{NG}} = 1{,}250 \text{ tCO}_{2}\text{e/yr}
$$

**Explanation:** The activity data (volume) is multiplied by its emission factor to get the carbon-equivalent emissions.

### Step 3: Compute CO₂e from diesel combustion

$$
CF_{\text{diesel}} = V_{\text{diesel}} \times EF_{\text{diesel}}
$$

$$
CF_{\text{diesel}} = 20{,}000 \text{ L} \times 0.00268 \text{ tCO}_{2}\text{e/L}
$$

$$
CF_{\text{diesel}} = 53.6 \text{ tCO}_{2}\text{e/yr}
$$

**Explanation:** Same principle as Step 2 — multiply activity by emission factor.

### Step 4: Aggregate total carbon footprint

$$
CF_{\text{total}} = CF_{\text{NG}} + CF_{\text{diesel}}
$$

$$
CF_{\text{total}} = 1{,}250 + 53.6
$$

$$
CF_{\text{total}} = 1{,}303.6 \text{ tCO}_{2}\text{e/yr}
$$

**Explanation:** Sum individual source emissions to obtain facility-wide carbon footprint.

## 3.2 Derivation: Renewable Energy Share Calculation

### Problem Statement
A state has the following annual energy mix:
- Coal: **180 TWh**
- Natural Gas: **40 TWh**
- Hydro: **30 TWh**
- Solar: **25 TWh**
- Wind: **15 TWh**

Compute the renewable energy share.

### Step 1: Identify renewables

Hydro, solar, and wind are renewable sources.

$$
E_{\text{RE}} = E_{\text{hydro}} + E_{\text{solar}} + E_{\text{wind}}
$$

$$
E_{\text{RE}} = 30 + 25 + 15 = 70 \text{ TWh}
$$

**Explanation:** Sum all renewable generation values.

### Step 2: Compute total generation

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 180 + 40 + 30 + 25 + 15
$$

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 290 \text{ TWh}
$$

**Explanation:** Sum all generation irrespective of source.

### Step 3: Compute share

$$
\text{RE Share} = \frac{E_{\text{RE}}}{E_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{RE Share} = \frac{70}{290} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{RE Share} = 24.14\%
$$

**Explanation:** Ratio of renewables to total energy multiplied by 100 yields percentage share.

## 3.3 Derivation: Sustainable Energy Savings Payback

### Problem Statement
An LED retrofit costs **₹12,00,000** and saves **₹3,00,000** in electricity annually. Compute the simple payback period.

### Step 1: Recall the payback formula

$$
T_{\text{payback}} = \frac{C_{\text{investment}}}{\text{Annual Cash Savings}}
$$

**Explanation:** The simple payback period is the time required to recover the initial investment through annual savings.

### Step 2: Substitute values

$$
T_{\text{payback}} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
T_{\text{payback}} = 4 \text{ years}
$$

**Explanation:** Direct division gives the payback in years.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sustainability Indicator Categories

| Category | Indicator | Direction | Energy Link |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Environmental** | Carbon footprint | Lower is better | Reduce kWh consumption |
| **Environmental** | Renewable share | Higher is better | Solar/wind/bio adoption |
| **Economic** | Energy intensity (kgoe/1000USD) | Lower is better | Efficient processes |
| **Economic** | Payback period | Lower is better | Quick-win retrofits |
| **Social** | Electrification rate (%) | Higher is better | Energy access (SDG 7) |
| **Social** | Indoor air quality (PM 2.5) | Lower is better | Clean cookstoves |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three Pillars of Sustainable Development (Block Architecture)

<!-- 
  Mermaid node IDs follow alphanumeric-with-letter-prefix rule.
  Labels contain no markdown formatting (no **, *, or HTML tables).
-->

```mermaid
graph TD
    env1[ENVIRONMENTAL PILLAR]:::env
    eco1[ECONOMIC PILLAR]:::eco
    soc1[SOCIAL PILLAR]:::soc
    int1[SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT]:::center

    env1 --> int1
    eco1 --> int1
    soc1 --> int1

    env1 --> env2[Low Carbon Fuels]
    env1 --> env3[Emission Control]
    env1 --> env4[Resource Efficiency]

    eco1 --> eco2[Energy Cost Reduction]
    eco1 --> eco3[Lifecycle Cost Analysis]
    eco1 --> eco4[Green Investment]

    soc1 --> soc2[Energy Access for All]
    soc1 --> soc3[Fair Labour Practices]
    soc1 --> soc4[Community Health]

    classDef env fill:#cfe8cf,stroke:#2d6a2d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef eco fill:#fde9c8,stroke:#a36b00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef soc fill:#d6e4f0,stroke:#1f3a68,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef center fill:#f0d4d4,stroke:#7a1f1f,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:** Each pillar feeds into the central "Sustainable Development" node, mirroring the triple-bottom-line framework. The three pillar colours (green for environment, gold for economy, blue for society) follow the standard sustainability iconography.

## 4.2 Operational Workflow of Sustainable Energy Management

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[MEASURE Baseline Consumption] --> stepB[BENCHMARK Against Standards]
    stepB --> stepC[ANALYSE Energy Audit]
    stepC --> stepD[OPTIMISE Efficiency Measures]
    stepD --> stepE[SUBSTITUTE Renewables]
    stepE --> stepF[OFFSET Residual Emissions]
    stepF --> stepG[MONITOR KPIs]
    stepG --> stepA

    classDef boxStyle fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#8b7500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class stepA,stepB,stepC,stepD,stepE,stepF,stepG boxStyle
```

**Reading Guide:** This is a continuous improvement loop (Plan-Do-Check-Act equivalent) — note the feedback arrow from *Monitor* back to *Measure*. This is the standard energy management cycle underpinning **ISO 50001**.

## 4.3 Hierarchy Linking SDGs to Energy Audits

```mermaid
graph TD
    sdgGoal[UN SDG FRAMEWORK]:::root
    sdg7[SDG 7 AFFORDABLE CLEAN ENERGY]:::goal
    sdg13[SDG 13 CLIMATE ACTION]:::goal
    sdg12[SDG 12 RESPONSIBLE CONSUMPTION]:::goal
    auditLevel[ENERGY AUDIT PROCESS]:::audit
    walkThrough[WALK THROUGH AUDIT]:::audit
    detailedLevel[DETAILED AUDIT]:::audit
    investmentGrade[INVESTMENT GRADE AUDIT]:::audit

    sdgGoal --> sdg7
    sdgGoal --> sdg13
    sdgGoal --> sdg12

    sdg7 --> auditLevel
    sdg13 --> auditLevel
    sdg12 --> auditLevel

    auditLevel --> walkThrough
    auditLevel --> detailedLevel
    auditLevel --> investmentGrade

    classDef root fill:#d4af37,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef goal fill:#cfe8cf,stroke:#2d6a2d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef audit fill:#d6e4f0,stroke:#1f3a68,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:** Top-down hierarchy showing how global policy (SDGs) cascades into operational engineering tasks (energy audits). KTU examiners favour this kind of mapping question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Sustainable Development. Mention any two of its pillars.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

Sustainable Development is *"development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs"* (Brundtland Report, 1987).

Two of its pillars are:
1. **Environmental Pillar** — Preservation of natural ecosystems, biodiversity, and reduction of pollution.
2. **Economic Pillar** — Ensuring financial viability, energy efficiency, and sustainable livelihoods.

*(Acceptable third pillar: Social — equity and well-being.)*

### Q2. List any three Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) relevant to energy management.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**
1. **SDG 7** — Affordable and Clean Energy.
2. **SDG 12** — Responsible Consumption and Production.
3. **SDG 13** — Climate Action.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three pillars of sustainable development** with suitable examples from the energy sector. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **17 UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** and elaborate on how **SDG 7, SDG 12, and SDG 13** are linked to energy management and audit. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three Pillars of Sustainable Development:**

The Triple Bottom Line (TBL) framework, coined by **John Elkington (1994)**, holds that sustainable development must balance three pillars simultaneously:

1. **Environmental Pillar:**
   - Focuses on preserving ecological systems, reducing emissions, and conserving resources.
   - **Energy example:** Switching from coal-fired boilers to biomass or solar thermal reduces CO₂ emissions; installing electrostatic precipitators reduces particulate matter.

2. **Economic Pillar:**
   - Concerns long-term financial viability and efficient resource use.
   - **Energy example:** LED retrofits, VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) installation on motors, and waste-heat recovery systems reduce operational costs and improve ROI.

3. **Social Pillar:**
   - Encompasses equity, health, education, and community welfare.
   - **Energy example:** Off-grid solar PV systems provide electricity to remote rural communities, improving education, health, and livelihoods.

> **Synthesis:** A project is *truly* sustainable only when all three pillars are addressed in balance.

**(b) SDGs and Their Energy Management Linkage:**

The UN General Assembly adopted the **2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development** in September 2015, comprising **17 goals, 169 targets, and 231 indicators**.

- **SDG 7 — Affordable and Clean Energy:**
  - Targets universal access to modern energy services.
  - Energy management link: Energy audits in industries and buildings reduce consumption, freeing up capacity and reducing costs — making energy more affordable.

- **SDG 12 — Responsible Consumption and Production:**
  - Promotes sustainable patterns of consumption.
  - Energy management link: ISO 50001-based Energy Management Systems (EnMS) directly support this goal by encouraging efficient production.

- **SDG 13 — Climate Action:**
  - Urges urgent action to combat climate change.
  - Energy management link: Carbon footprint reduction through renewable substitution, demand-side management, and energy-efficient equipment directly contributes to climate mitigation.

> **Conclusion:** Energy audit and management is a *cross-cutting enabler* for multiple SDGs and is therefore a cornerstone engineering discipline for sustainable development.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Define **Carbon Footprint**. A small-scale industry consumes **800 tonnes of furnace oil** and **50,000 units (kWh)** of grid electricity annually. The emission factors are: furnace oil = **0.0032 tCO₂e / kg** and grid electricity = **0.00082 tCO₂e / kWh**. Compute the total annual carbon footprint. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Energy Intensity** and **Renewable Energy Share**. A state's annual energy mix is: coal **200 TWh**, gas **50 TWh**, hydro **60 TWh**, solar **40 TWh**, wind **30 TWh**. Calculate its energy intensity if its annual GDP is **USD 200 billion** (assume **1 toe = 11.63 MWh**). Also compute its renewable share. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Carbon Footprint Calculation:**

**Definition:** Carbon footprint is the total greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions caused directly and indirectly by an entity, expressed in **tonnes of CO₂ equivalent (tCO₂e)** per year.

**Step 1 — Furnace oil emissions:**
Given: **800 tonnes = 800,000 kg**, EF = **0.0032 tCO₂e/kg**.

$$
CF_{\text{oil}} = 800{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 0.0032 \text{ tCO}_{2}\text{e/kg} = 2{,}560 \text{ tCO}_{2}\text{e/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Grid electricity emissions:**
Given: **50,000 kWh**, EF = **0.00082 tCO₂e/kWh**.

$$
CF_{\text{elec}} = 50{,}000 \text{ kWh} \times 0.00082 \text{ tCO}_{2}\text{e/kWh} = 41 \text{ tCO}_{2}\text{e/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Total carbon footprint:**

$$
CF_{\text{total}} = 2{,}560 + 41 = 2{,}601 \text{ tCO}_{2}\text{e/yr}
$$

**(b) Energy Intensity and Renewable Share:**

**Definition (Energy Intensity):** The energy consumed per unit of economic output (kgoe / 1000 USD).

**Definition (Renewable Energy Share):** The percentage of total primary energy supplied (TPES) coming from renewable sources.

**Step 1 — Total generation:**

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 200 + 50 + 60 + 40 + 30 = 380 \text{ TWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Renewable share:**

$$
E_{\text{RE}} = 60 + 40 + 30 = 130 \text{ TWh}
$$

$$
\text{RE Share} = \frac{130}{380} \times 100\% = 34.21\%
$$

**Step 3 — Convert TPES to kgoe:**

$$
380 \text{ TWh} = 380 \times 10^{6} \text{ MWh} = \frac{380 \times 10^{6}}{11.63} \text{ ktoe}
$$

$$
\text{TPES} = 32{,}674.98 \text{ ktoe} = 3.267 \times 10^{10} \text{ kgoe}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy intensity:**

$$
E_{\text{intensity}} = \frac{\text{TPES}}{\text{GDP}}
$$

$$
E_{\text{intensity}} = \frac{3.267 \times 10^{10} \text{ kgoe}}{200 \times 10^{9} \text{ USD}}
$$

$$
E_{\text{intensity}} = 163.35 \text{ kgoe / 1000 USD}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The state's energy intensity of ~163 kgoe/1000 USD is reasonable for a developing economy; the renewable share of 34.21% is encouraging and above India's national RPO target.

---

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Converting tonnes to kg (or MWh to toe) is the most common loss-of-marks point. Always show unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting CO₂e:** Carbon footprint must be expressed in **CO₂-equivalent**, not just CO₂. Use **GWP multipliers** for methane (×28) and N₂O (×265).
> 3. **Brundtland definition wording:** Examiners expect the *exact phrase* "needs of the present without compromising future generations" — partial answers lose marks.
> 4. **Confusing RE share with RPO:** Renewable energy share is *achieved generation share*, not the *regulatory mandate* (RPO).
> 5. **Three pillars:** All three must be mentioned with examples; a single-pillar answer typically scores ≤ 50%.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Brundtland Report (1987)** is the canonical source — use the exact wording.
- **Three pillars:** Environmental, Economic, Social — all required in any answer on SD.
- **17 SDGs** adopted in 2015 — **SDG 7, 12, 13** are the most energy-linked.
- **Carbon Footprint formula:** Activity × Emission Factor, summed over all sources; units in **tCO₂e**.
- **Energy Intensity:** TPES / GDP, units **kgoe / 1000 USD** (lower is better).
- **Renewable Share:** (Renewable generation / Total generation) × 100%.
- **Simple Payback:** Investment / Annual Savings (years).
- **Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle** underpins ISO 50001 energy management.
- **Always convert units explicitly** — tonnes ↔ kg, MWh ↔ toe, Nm³ ↔ kg.
- **GWP values (AR5):** CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265, SF₆ = 23,500.
- **India RPO target (FY 2026):** ~22% renewable purchase obligation.
- **Earth Overshoot Day** is computed using the Ecological Footprint framework (Global Footprint Network).
- **KTU keyword triggers:** "intergenerational equity," "triple bottom line," "Plan-Do-Check-Act," "SDG," "carbon footprint," "renewable share."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Renewable Energy Sources
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Renewable Energy Sources

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Renewable Energy Sources (RES)** are forms of energy derived from natural processes that are replenished at a rate faster than or equal to the rate at which they are consumed. Per the **KTU 2024 OEPET722 syllabus**, RES refers to energy flows that are theoretically inexhaustible on a human time scale, in contrast to finite fossil-fuel reserves formed over millions of years.

From an engineering standpoint, RES converts naturally available energy fluxes (solar irradiation, wind kinetic energy, geothermal gradients, hydro heads, biomass chemical energy, and tidal/oceanic motion) into useful secondary energy carriers — primarily **electricity, thermal energy, and mechanical work**.

The standard **Primary Energy Supply (PES)** classification under the **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, Government of India)** recognises the following as renewable:

- Solar (Photovoltaic + Solar Thermal)
- Wind (Onshore + Offshore)
- Biomass / Biofuels
- Small Hydro (≤ 25 MW)
- Geothermal
- Tidal & Wave
- Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)
- Hydrogen (green, when produced from RES electricity)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** The OEPET722 course positions this topic as the *bridge* between conventional energy management (Modules 1–3, covering audits, economics, instruments) and the **decarbonisation strategies** of the modern energy sector. Expect questions that test both **conceptual** understanding and **quantitative** energy potential estimation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Bank Account" Analogy**
>
> Imagine Earth's energy economy as a *bank account*:
>
> - **Fossil fuels** (coal, oil, gas) are like a **savings account** that took **300 million years** of plant photosynthesis and geological compression to fill. Every time we burn coal, we make a *withdrawal* that the planet cannot replenish within our lifetime.
>
> - **Renewable energy sources** are like a **monthly salary** that is deposited into the account *every single day* by the Sun, the wind, and the Earth's rotation. As long as the Sun shines and the wind blows, the deposit continues — we are only using the **interest**, not the principal.
>
> The **Sun deposits approximately 1.74 × 10¹⁷ watts** of power onto Earth's outer atmosphere continuously. Even capturing 0.01% of this would satisfy humanity's entire current energy demand many times over.

This analogy frames the engineering problem: renewable energy is not about *availability* (it is abundant) but about **capture, conversion, storage, and dispatch** — the four engineering pillars that determine feasibility.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
>
> - **Solar Constant (G_sc)** = **1361 W/m²** (Mean Total Solar Irradiance at the top of atmosphere, per WMO 2021)
> - **Air Mass 1.5 (AM1.5) Reference Spectrum** = **1000 W/m²** at Earth's surface (STC for PV modules)
> - **Standard Temperature for PV (T_STC)** = **25 °C = 298.15 K**
> - **Density of air at sea level (ρ_air)** = **1.225 kg/m³**
> - **Gravitational acceleration (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant (σ)** = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
> - **Specific heat capacity of water (c_pw)** = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (India, 2024) |
|---|---|---|
| Installed RES capacity (cumulative) | C_RES | ≈ 188 GW (MNRE, 2024) |
| Solar insolation (avg.) | H | 4–7 kWh/m²/day |
| Wind Power Density @ 50 m | P_w | 200–500 W/m² |
| Small hydro potential | P_SH | ~21 GW (estimated) |
| Biomass surplus | E_bio | ~500 million tonnes/year |

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in OEPET722

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **energy management professional** of 2024 must be able to:
>
> 1. **Quantify** the available renewable resource at a site (solar irradiation, wind speed, biomass yield).
> 2. **Select** the appropriate technology based on resource availability, load profile, and economic constraints.
> 3. **Audit** renewable systems for performance ratio (PR) and capacity utilisation factor (CUF).
> 4. **Compare** the Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE) of RES with grid power and diesel generation.
>
> This module provides the **scientific foundation** for all of the above competencies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Renewable Energy Sources

Renewable energy sources are classified along **three primary axes**:

### A. By Origin of Energy Flux

| Origin | Source | Conversion Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Solar (direct)** | Photons from Sun | PV cells, Solar thermal collectors, CSP |
| **Solar (indirect)** | Wind, Wave, Biomass, Hydro | Wind turbines, WEC, Boilers, Turbines |
| **Planetary** | Tidal, Geothermal, OTEC | Barrages, Heat exchangers, Rankine cycles |

### B. By End-Use Form

- **Electricity Generation:** PV, Wind, Small Hydro, Geothermal, Tidal, OTEC, Biomass
- **Direct Thermal:** Solar water heaters, Biomass combustion, Geothermal heat pumps
- **Biofuels (Transport):** Ethanol, Biodiesel, Biogas, Green Hydrogen

### C. By Dispatchability

- **Variable Renewable Energy (VRE):** Solar PV, Wind, Run-of-river hydro → output depends on weather/time
- **Dispatchable / Baseload-capable:** Biomass, Bagasse cogeneration, Geothermal, Small hydro with storage, OTEC

> [!TIP]
> **Why this classification matters:** A KTU question on "suitability of RES for a hospital with 24×7 critical load" implicitly tests dispatchability. The correct answer is dispatchable renewables (biomass/geo) or VRE with **battery energy storage systems (BESS)**.

---

## 2.2 Fundamental Energy Conversion Principles

### 2.2.1 Solar Energy — The Primary Driver

Approximately **99.97%** of Earth's usable energy (excluding nuclear and geothermal) originates from the Sun. The solar resource equation:

$$H_t = H_b \cdot R_b + H_d \cdot \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + H \cdot \rho_g \cdot \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

Where:
- $H_t$ = Total daily radiation on tilted surface (kWh/m²/day)
- $H_b$ = Beam (direct) radiation on horizontal surface
- $H_d$ = Diffuse radiation on horizontal surface
- $R_b$ = Geometric factor (ratio of beam on tilted to horizontal)
- $\beta$ = Tilt angle of collector
- $\rho_g$ = Ground reflectance (albedo, typically 0.2)

### 2.2.2 Wind Energy — Kinetic Flux from Solar Heating

Wind arises from differential solar heating of land/sea, causing pressure gradients. Available power in a wind stream:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot A \cdot v^3$$

The **Betz Limit** caps extractable power at:

$$C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad (59.26\%)$$

### 2.2.3 Hydro Energy — Gravitational Potential

Power from a water head:

$$P_{hydro} = \eta \cdot \rho_w \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$

### 2.2.4 Biomass Energy — Stored Solar Chemical Energy

Energy released by combustion (approximate calorific values):

| Fuel | GCV (MJ/kg) |
|---|---|
| Wood (air-dried) | 14–17 |
| Rice husk | 13–15 |
| Bagasse | 18–20 |
| Municipal Solid Waste | 8–12 |
| Biogas (m³) | 18–25 MJ/m³ |

### 2.2.5 Geothermal Energy — Terrestrial Heat Flux

The average **heat flow** from Earth's interior is ~**0.087 W/m²** globally. Exploitable resources are concentrated near tectonic boundaries (e.g., Puga Valley in Ladakh, India).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorise this for the exam.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar power intercepted | $P = G_{sc} \cdot A \cdot (1 + 0.033 \cos(360n/365))$ | W | With eccentricity correction; $n$ = day of year |
| 2 | Tilted surface radiation | $H_t = H_b R_b + H_d(1+\cos\beta)/2 + H\rho_g(1-\cos\beta)/2$ | kWh/m²/day | Liu & Jordan model |
| 3 | Wind power in stream | $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ | W | Swept area $A = \pi D^2/4$ |
| 4 | Betz limit | $C_{p,\max} = 16/27$ | dimensionless | Theoretical max |
| 5 | Hydro power | $P = \eta \rho g Q H$ | W | $H$ = head in metres |
| 6 | Small hydro class | $P \le 25\,\text{MW}$ | MW | MNRE definition |
| 7 | Wave power (deep water) | $P = \rho g^2 H_s^2 T / (64\pi)$ | W/m wave crest | $H_s$ = significant height, $T$ = period |
| 8 | Tidal power (barrage) | $P = \rho g A h^2 / 2T$ | W avg. | $h$ = head, $T$ = tidal period |
| 9 | Energy from biomass | $E = m \cdot \text{GCV}$ | MJ or kWh | $m$ = mass flow |
| 10 | Geothermal gradient | $dT/dz \approx 25\text{–}30$ | °C/km | Continental crust average |
| 11 | Capacity Utilisation Factor | $\text{CUF} = E_{gen}/(P_{rated} \cdot 8760)$ | dimensionless | RES performance metric |
| 12 | Performance Ratio (PV) | $\text{PR} = E_{ac}/(H_t \cdot P_{peak})$ | dimensionless | Site-independent metric |
| 13 | LCOE | $\text{LCOE} = \dfrac{\sum_t (I_t + O_t)/(1+r)^t}{\sum_t E_t/(1+r)^t}$ | ₹/kWh | Used for cost comparison |
| 14 | Availability factor | $\text{AF} = \text{Operating hours} / 8760$ | dimensionless | Plant reliability metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do NOT confuse CUF with PR.** CUF compares actual generation to *theoretical maximum if plant ran 24/7*. PR compares actual AC output to *theoretical DC energy input from available solar resource* — a much more rigorous metric for PV plants.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> These formulas are not academic exercises — they are the **daily tools of an energy auditor**:
>
> - A **solar auditor** in Kerala uses Eq. 2 to estimate yield before installation (CUF expectation: 16–19% for rooftop PV in Kerala).
> - A **wind farm developer** in Tamil Nadu (Muppandal) uses Eq. 3 to size turbines at a given wind regime (class-I, v_avg ≥ 7 m/s).
> - A **biomass consultant** for sugar mills uses Eq. 9 to size a bagasse cogeneration plant.
> - A **bank financial analyst** uses Eq. 13 to decide whether to fund a 5 MW small hydro project.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Power Extraction from Wind (Betz Limit)

**Problem Setup:** Air of density $\rho$ moves with velocity $v_1$ through a large cross-section, passes through a wind turbine of swept area $A$ (where velocity is $v$), and exits with velocity $v_2 < v_1$.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate through the turbine disc:**

The air that passes per second through area $A$ has mass:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \quad \text{[kg/s]}$$

This assumes the control volume is drawn at the disc plane. *Reasoning:* In a steady state, the air crossing the disc in 1 second is the air occupying a cylinder of area $A$ and length equal to the disc-plane velocity, $v$.

**Step 2 — Power extracted (rate of change of kinetic energy):**

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} (v_1^2 - v_2^2) = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

This is the difference in kinetic energy flux between upstream and downstream air.

**Step 3 — Apply momentum conservation (Newton's 2nd law for control volume):**

The thrust force on the disc equals the rate of momentum change:

$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)$$

But the force on the disc, by Bernoulli's equation applied between far-upstream and far-downstream, is also:

$$F = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions for $F$:**

$$\rho A v (v_1 - v_2) = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

Cancel $\rho A$ and factor the RHS:

$$2 v (v_1 - v_2) = (v_1 - v_2)(v_1 + v_2)$$

Assuming $v_1 \ne v_2$ (otherwise no power extracted):

$$2v = v_1 + v_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into the power equation:**

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A \left(\frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}\right) (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

Factor the difference of squares $(v_1^2 - v_2^2) = (v_1 - v_2)(v_1 + v_2)$:

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A \cdot \frac{(v_1 + v_2)}{2} \cdot (v_1 - v_2)(v_1 + v_2) = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (v_1^2 - v_2^2)(v_1 + v_2)$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of $v_1$ and the induction factor $a$:**

Define $a = (v_1 - v)/v_1$ such that $v = v_1(1-a)$ and $v_2 = v_1(1-2a)$. Substituting:

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3 \cdot 4a(1-a)^2$$

The fraction of upstream kinetic power flux captured is:

$$C_p = \frac{P}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 7 — Maximise $C_p$ w.r.t. $a$:**

$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4\left[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

Solutions: $a = 1$ (trivial, $v=0$) or $a = 1/3$.

**Step 8 — Evaluate $C_p$ at $a = 1/3$:**

$$C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Even a *perfect, lossless* wind turbine can extract at most **59.26%** of the kinetic energy in the wind. The rest must pass through to keep the air moving away (otherwise it would pile up). Real machines reach $C_p \approx 0.35$–$0.45$ due to aerodynamic, mechanical, and electrical losses.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Sizing a Small Wind Turbine

**Given:**
- Mean wind speed at hub height: $v = 8$ m/s
- Air density: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Rotor diameter: $D = 50$ m
- Power coefficient: $C_p = 0.40$ (realistic modern turbine)
- Generator + gearbox efficiency: $\eta_g = 0.90$

**Find:** Net electrical power output.

**Step 1 — Swept area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 50^2}{4} = \frac{2500\pi}{4} = 625\pi \approx 1963.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Available wind power (Eq. 3):**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 1963.5 \cdot 8^3$$

Compute $8^3 = 512$:

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 1963.5 \cdot 512 = 614{,}400 \text{ W} \approx 614.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Aerodynamic (shaft) power extracted:**

$$P_{shaft} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.40 \cdot 614.4 = 245.76 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Net electrical output:**

$$P_{elec} = \eta_g \cdot P_{shaft} = 0.90 \cdot 245.76 = 221.18 \text{ kW}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice the **cubic dependence on wind speed**. Doubling wind speed (from 8 to 16 m/s) *octuples* the power (from 221 kW to ~1770 kW). This is why siting — even within a few hundred metres — is the single most critical decision in wind energy.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing RES Technologies

| Parameter | Solar PV | Wind | Small Hydro | Biomass | Geothermal |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CUF (India avg.)** | 15–20% | 20–35% | 40–60% | 60–80% | 70–90% |
| **Capacity range** | 1 W – 600 MW | 100 W – 12 MW | 1 kW – 25 MW | 100 kW – 50 MW | 1 MW – 100 MW |
| **Dispatchable?** | No (with BESS: yes) | No (with BESS: yes) | Yes (with storage) | Yes | Yes |
| **LCOE (₹/kWh, 2024)** | 2.5–4.0 | 3.0–4.5 | 3.5–5.5 | 5.0–7.5 | 5.0–9.0 |
| **Land footprint** | High (5 ac/MW) | Medium (2 ac/MW) | Low (submerged) | High (fuel storage) | Very low |
| **Maturity (TRL)** | 9 (commercial) | 9 | 9 | 9 | 8–9 (India: 5) |
| **Best suited for** | Distributed + utility | Utility, offshore | Hills, run-of-river | Sugar mills, rural | Volcanic zones |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU often asks "compare and contrast" type questions.** Use this matrix structure in your answers to score full marks on organisation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Decision Logic for RES Selection (Site Feasibility)

```mermaid

Recommend a renewable system."* Trace through the flow: solar yes → 24×7 → PV+BESS *or* wind yes → onshore. Discuss LCOE, land, and dispatchability to justify your final pick.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Model Question Paper Style: Direct, recall-based with a touch of application.**

### Q1. Define renewable energy sources. List any four examples. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Renewable energy sources (RES) are forms of energy obtained from natural processes that are continuously replenished and are inexhaustible on a human time scale, in contrast to finite fossil fuels. They are derived from continuous natural fluxes such as solar radiation, wind, flowing water, biomass regrowth, geothermal heat, and tidal/oceanic motion.

**Four examples (any four, ½ mark each):**
1. Solar energy (PV and solar thermal)
2. Wind energy
3. Biomass / Biogas
4. Small hydro (≤ 25 MW)
5. Geothermal energy
6. Tidal / Wave / OTEC

*Marking key:* Definition (1 mark) + four examples (0.5 each × 4 = 2 marks).

---

### Q2. What is meant by "Capacity Utilisation Factor" of a renewable energy plant? Why is the CUF of solar PV lower than that of biomass plants in India? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Capacity Utilisation Factor (CUF) is the ratio of actual energy generated by a plant over a period to the maximum energy that could have been generated if it operated at rated capacity continuously for the entire period:

$$\text{CUF} = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{rated}} \times 8760 \text{ h}}$$

**Reasons for lower CUF of solar PV vs biomass in India (2 marks):**

1. **Diurnal and seasonal variability:** Solar PV generates only during daylight hours (≈ 4–6 effective sun-hours/day in India, depending on location and season). Biomass plants run 24×7 subject to fuel availability, achieving 60–80% CUF.

2. **Weather dependence:** Cloud cover, monsoon, and dust reduce incident irradiation. Biomass combustion is independent of weather.

3. **Storage limitation:** PV systems without BESS cannot shift generation to night-time peak demand.

4. **Capacity sizing convention:** PV is often sized for peak noon irradiance (1000 W/m²), but the average is much lower.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: Two questions of 14 marks each from Module 4, with internal choice (either/or). Each question typically has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose either A or B*

#### **Q.A. (a)** Explain the different types of renewable energy sources with suitable examples. Discuss the present installed capacity of renewable energy in India and the role of MNRE. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Renewable energy sources are energy resources that are naturally replenished. India's installed RES capacity is approximately **188 GW** (as of 2024, MNRE), making it one of the top four nations globally for RES deployment.

**Types of RES (4 marks):**

1. **Solar Energy:** Conversion of sunlight into electricity (PV) or heat (thermal). India targets 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030. Examples: Bhadla Solar Park (2.25 GW), Pavagada (2 GW), Charanka (690 MW).

2. **Wind Energy:** Conversion of kinetic energy of moving air using wind turbines. Muppandal wind farm in Tamil Nadu (~1.5 GW) is one of the largest in Asia.

3. **Biomass / Biogas:** Chemical energy stored in organic matter, released via combustion or anaerobic digestion. Bagasse cogeneration in sugar mills (≈ 9 GW installed).

4. **Small Hydro:** Hydropower plants with capacity up to 25 MW. India has exploited ~5 GW of ~21 GW potential.

5. **Geothermal:** Heat from Earth's interior. India has identified Puga (Ladakh), Manikaran, and Surajkund as potential sites; commercial exploitation is still nascent.

6. **Oceanic (Tidal/Wave/OTEC):** India's Gulf of Kutch and Gulf of Cambay have estimated tidal potential of 7,000–9,000 MW. A 3.5 MW pilot OTEC plant is proposed at Chennai.

**Role of MNRE (2 marks):**

- Apex body of GoI for renewable energy policy and R&D.
- Implements flagship schemes: PM-KUSUM (solar pumps for farmers), PM-Surya Ghar (rooftop solar), CPSU (central PSU solar), National Green Hydrogen Mission.
- Sets targets, manages subsidies and viability gap funding (VGF).
- Coordinates international cooperation (ISA, IRENA).

#### **Q.A. (b)** A small wind turbine of rotor diameter 12 m operates at a site with mean wind speed of 9 m/s. Air density is 1.18 kg/m³. The turbine has a power coefficient of 0.38 and generator-gearbox efficiency of 0.88. Calculate: (i) the available wind power, (ii) the net electrical power output, and (iii) the annual energy generation, assuming a capacity factor of 0.22. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set C]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $D = 12$ m, $v = 9$ m/s, $\rho = 1.18$ kg/m³
- $C_p = 0.38$, $\eta_g = 0.88$
- Capacity factor (CF) = 0.22

**(i) Available wind power (2 marks):**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (12)^2}{4} = 36\pi \approx 113.10 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = 0.5 \times 1.18 \times 113.10 \times 9^3$$

Compute: $9^3 = 729$

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.18 \times 113.10 \times 729 = 48{,}663 \text{ W} \approx 48.66 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Net electrical power (3 marks):**

$$P_{elec} = C_p \times \eta_g \times P_{wind} = 0.38 \times 0.88 \times 48.66$$

$$P_{elec} = 0.3344 \times 48.66 \approx 16.27 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Annual energy generation (2 marks):**

Capacity factor relates annual energy to rated capacity operating 24/7:

$$E_{annual} = P_{rated} \times 8760 \times \text{CF}$$

We treat $P_{rated} = P_{elec}$ at the rated wind speed:

$$E_{annual} = 16.27 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \text{ h} \times 0.22 = 31{,}357 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 31.36 \text{ MWh/year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction:** Note we did NOT use $P_{rated} = P_{wind}$ in part (iii). The "rated" capacity of a wind turbine is the electrical output at the rated wind speed (not the gross aerodynamic power). The CF already accounts for sub-rated wind hours, downtime, and curtailment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### **Q.B. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a solar photovoltaic (PV) system. Discuss the factors affecting the power output of a PV module. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Solar Irradiance (G)
      ↓
   [PV Array] → (DC power, depends on G & T)
      ↓
   [MPPT Charge Controller / Inverter]
      ↓
   [DC Bus → AC Inverter if grid-tied]
      ↓
   [Battery Bank] (off-grid / hybrid only)
      ↓
   [AC Load / Grid]
```

Description of each block:

1. **PV Array:** Series-parallel combination of PV modules (e.g., 60-cell or 72-cell mono/poly-crystalline Si). Typical module efficiency: 18–22%.
2. **MPPT Controller:** Maximum Power Point Tracking (Perturb & Observe / Incremental Conductance algorithm) extracts maximum available DC power under varying G and T.
3. **Inverter:** Converts DC to AC (50 Hz, 230 V single-phase or 415 V three-phase). Includes anti-islanding protection.
4. **Battery Bank (optional):** Stores excess energy for night / cloudy periods (typically Li-ion, lead-acid, or flow batteries).
5. **AC Bus & Load:** Feeds electrical loads or exports to grid via net meter.

**Factors affecting PV output (4 marks — any four):**

1. **Solar irradiance (G):** Power ∝ G (linear). Doubling G doubles output.
2. **Cell temperature (T):** Output *decreases* linearly with T. Temperature coefficient ≈ −0.4 %/°C for crystalline Si. Hot Indian summers reduce efficiency.
3. **Spectral composition:** PV modules are rated for AM1.5 spectrum; deviations alter current.
4. **Angle of incidence / Tilt:** Optimal tilt ≈ latitude for annual maximum. Tracking systems gain 20–35% extra energy.
5. **Shading (even partial):** A single shaded cell in a series string can disable the entire string unless bypass diodes are used.
6. **Soiling / Dust:** Losses of 5–30% reported in dusty regions of India.
7. **Module degradation:** Typically 0.5–0.7% per year; cumulative loss over 25 years can reach 20%.

#### **Q.B. (b)** A factory in Kochi (latitude ~10°N) wants to install a 100 kWp rooftop solar PV system. The annual average daily solar insolation at the site is 5.2 kWh/m²/day. Assume module efficiency 19%, system derating factor (PR) 0.78, and ground reflectance 0.2. Estimate: (i) the required PV array area, (ii) the expected annual energy output, and (iii) the CUF. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set D]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $P_{peak} = 100$ kWp, $H_{avg} = 5.2$ kWh/m²/day
- $\eta_{module} = 0.19$, PR = 0.78

**(i) Required array area (2 marks):**

Peak power from 1 m² of module at STC (1000 W/m²):

$$P_{1m^2} = G_{STC} \times \eta = 1000 \times 0.19 = 190 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Required area:

$$A = \frac{P_{peak}}{P_{1m^2}} = \frac{100{,}000 \text{ W}}{190 \text{ W/m}^2} \approx 526.3 \text{ m}^2$$

**(ii) Annual energy output (3 marks):**

Daily specific yield (kWh per kWp per day):

$$Y_d = H_{avg} \times \text{PR} = 5.2 \times 0.78 = 4.056 \text{ kWh/kWp/day}$$

Annual yield:

$$Y_{annual} = Y_d \times 365 = 4.056 \times 365 = 1480.4 \text{ kWh/kWp/year}$$

Total annual energy for 100 kWp:

$$E_{annual} = 100 \times 1480.4 = 1{,}48{,}044 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 1.48 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**(iii) Capacity Utilisation Factor (2 marks):**

$$\text{CUF} = \frac{E_{annual}}{P_{peak} \times 8760} = \frac{148{,}044}{100 \times 8760} = \frac{148{,}044}{876{,}000} \approx 0.169 \text{ or } 16.9\%$$

This is a typical CUF for Kerala rooftop PV (slightly below national average due to monsoon cloud cover).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit errors in PV area:** Forgetting to convert kWp to Wp before dividing by W/m². Always check unit consistency.
> 2. **Confusing CUF with PR:** CUF includes all losses and uses *rated* capacity; PR is purely the *DC-to-AC* and *system* ratio *after* accounting for resource.
> 3. **Using $G_{STC} = 1361$ W/m² instead of 1000 W/m² for module area calculation.** The 1361 W/m² is the *solar constant* at TOA; 1000 W/m² is the *terrestrial standard test condition (STC)* for module rating.
> 4. **Skipping the daily-to-annual conversion** (forgetting × 365) — costs 1 full mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention STC** explicitly when explaining why module rated power is per 1000 W/m² — conceptual mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Last-Mile Revision — Memorise Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- ✅ RES = naturally replenished, inexhaustible on human time scale; contrast with fossil fuels.
- ✅ Solar constant **G_sc = 1361 W/m²** (TOA); **STC = 1000 W/m²** (terrestrial, AM1.5).
- ✅ Wind power ∝ **v³** — cubic dependence is the key insight.
- ✅ **Betz limit = 16/27 ≈ 0.5926**; practical $C_p$ = 0.35–0.45.
- ✅ Small hydro: **≤ 25 MW** (MNRE classification).
- ✅ Biomass GCVs: wood 14–17, bagasse 18–20, MSW 8–12 MJ/kg.
- ✅ CUF = $E_{actual}/(P_{rated} \cdot 8760)$; PR = $E_{ac}/(H_t \cdot P_{peak})$.
- ✅ Kerala rooftop PV typical CUF: **15–19%**; National: **15–20%**; Wind: **20–35%**; Small hydro: **40–60%**; Biomass: **60–80%**; Geothermal: **70–90%**.
- ✅ LCOE (2024 India): Solar ₹2.5–4.0/kWh; Wind ₹3.0–4.5; Small hydro ₹3.5–5.5; Biomass ₹5.0–7.5; Geothermal ₹5.0–9.0.
- ✅ MNRE schemes: **PM-KUSUM** (solar pumps), **PM-Surya Ghar** (rooftop), **National Green Hydrogen Mission**.
- ✅ India's RES capacity 2024: **~188 GW cumulative** (Solar ≈ 82 GW, Wind ≈ 47 GW, Bio ≈ 10 GW, Small Hydro ≈ 5 GW).
- ✅ Targets: **500 GW non-fossil by 2030**; **Net Zero by 2070** (Panchamrit).
- ✅ Tidal potential: Gulf of Kutch and Cambay — **7,000–9,000 MW** estimated.
- ✅ For tilted surface radiation, always use **Liu & Jordan** decomposition (beam + diffuse + reflected).
- ✅ Wind turbine power at sub-rated wind: cubic in v; at rated wind: capped at $P_{rated}$; above cut-out: zero (safety shutdown).
- ✅ Geothermal gradient: **25–30 °C/km** in continental crust.
- ✅ Tidal barrage power: average of $\rho g A h^2 / 2T$ — note the *head squared* dependence.
- ✅ **Always state STC conditions** when discussing PV ratings: 1000 W/m², AM1.5G, 25 °C.
- ✅ For numerical problems: show all 3–4 intermediate steps; examiners award partial marks generously *only* if intermediate steps are visible.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Solar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Solar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Energy** is the radiant light and heat emitted by the **Sun**, harnessed via two principal pathways: **Solar Thermal** (converting sunlight to heat for water/air heating, cooking, or power generation) and **Solar Photovoltaic (PV)** (directly converting sunlight into electricity using semiconductor materials).

In the context of **Energy Management and Audit (KTU OEPET722)**, "Fundamentals of Solar" establishes the engineering baseline required to evaluate solar-based energy systems during an audit. It deals with the **quantity, quality, geometry, and availability** of solar radiation at any given geographic location and time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The auditor must know how to estimate the *incident solar radiation* on a collector surface, convert it into usable thermal or electrical energy, and verify the **energy performance ratio (EPR)** and **capacity utilisation factor (CUF)** of an installed solar system.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing under a **sunlight lamp** in a room. If you hold a piece of paper flat under the lamp, you get one level of brightness. If you tilt the paper, the brightness changes — even though the lamp is identical. **Solar geometry** works the same way: the Sun's position relative to your rooftop (or solar panel) dictates how much energy falls per square metre per second.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:** Think of the Earth as a giant "sunflower". Just like a sunflower tilts to face the sun, **solar panels are tilted** to capture the maximum possible light. The *angle* of that tilt is not guessed — it is mathematically computed using the Sun's position relative to the Earth's surface at that point and time.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants are **non-negotiable** in every KTU numerical problem:
>
> - **Solar Constant ($I_{sc}$):** $\mathbf{1367 \ \text{W/m}^2}$ (energy flux at the outer edge of Earth's atmosphere, perpendicular to the Sun's rays, on a surface of 1 m² held at the mean Earth-Sun distance).
> - **Mean Earth-Sun Distance:** $\mathbf{1.496 \times 10^{11} \ \text{m}}$ (1 Astronomical Unit, AU).
> - **Tropical Year Length:** $\mathbf{365.25 \ \text{days}}$.
> - **Earth's Axial Tilt (Obliquity):** $\mathbf{23.45°}$ — the reason for seasons.
> - **Speed of Light:** $\mathbf{3 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s}}$ (used when deriving the solar constant from total solar luminosity $L_{\odot} \approx 3.846 \times 10^{26}$ W).

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Solar Energy Systems

| Category | Mechanism | Common End-Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive Solar** | Architectural design (no mechanical devices) | Daylight, building heating/cooling |
| **Active Solar Thermal** | Collectors absorb heat, fluid transports it | Water heating, space heating, desalination |
| **Solar Photovoltaic (PV)** | Semiconductors (silicon) generate DC electricity | Rooftop solar, solar farms, off-grid power |
| **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** | Mirrors/lenses concentrate sunlight to drive turbines | Utility-scale electricity generation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Audit Perspective:** During an energy audit, the auditor first *classifies* the system, then measures the **incident solar radiation (kWh/m²/day)** at the site, computes **system efficiency**, and finally benchmarks against the **CUF** norms specified by MNRE / KTU-approved standards.

---

## 1.5 Why "Fundamentals of Solar" Matters in an Energy Audit

An energy auditor evaluating a 100 kW solar plant without understanding solar fundamentals would make the following critical mistakes:

1. **Wrong tilt-angle assumption** → under-estimated annual yield → over-estimated payback period.
2. **Ignoring diffuse radiation** on cloudy days → inaccurate monthly energy generation projection.
3. **Confusing local clock time with solar time** → wrong shading analysis between buildings.
4. **Not correcting for altitude/dust** → misreported Performance Ratio (PR) in the audit report.

Mastery of fundamentals prevents these errors and directly contributes to **CO1 (Renewable Energy Systems)** and **CO3 (Energy Audit & Management)** of the KTU OEPET722 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sun as an Energy Source — Theoretical Basis

The Sun is a **G-type main-sequence star (G2V)** that converts hydrogen into helium via nuclear fusion at its core. The released energy propagates outward as **electromagnetic radiation** and reaches the Earth's orbit after approximately **8 minutes 20 seconds**.

The radiant power leaving the Sun's surface is the **Solar Luminosity**, $L_{\odot} = 3.846 \times 10^{26} \ \text{W}$. When this power is spread uniformly across a sphere of radius equal to 1 AU, the flux at the top of Earth's atmosphere is the **Solar Constant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$ and not a single fixed value?** Because the Earth orbits in a slight ellipse (eccentricity $e \approx 0.0167$), the Earth-Sun distance varies by $\pm 1.7\%$ over the year, causing the extraterrestrial flux to vary between about **$1325 \ \text{W/m}^2$ (aphelion)** and **$1420 \ \text{W/m}^2$ (perihelion)**. The correction factor is called the **Eccentricity Correction Factor ($E_o$)**.

---

## 2.2 Components of Solar Radiation at Ground Level

When sunlight passes through the atmosphere, three distinct components reach the Earth's surface:

1. **Direct (Beam) Radiation ($I_b$):** Reaches the surface in a straight line from the Sun's disc. Used by **concentrating collectors** and by **PV cells** in clear sky.
2. **Diffuse Radiation ($I_d$):** Scattered by air molecules, dust, and clouds. Reaches the surface from all directions. Critical for **flat-plate collectors** on overcast days.
3. **Reflected (Albedo) Radiation ($I_r$):** Bounces off the ground, water, or nearby buildings. Especially significant for **tilted surfaces** in snowy regions.

The total radiation on a tilted surface is:
$$I_T = I_b \cdot R_b + I_d \cdot \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + I \cdot \rho \cdot \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

where:
- $R_b$ = ratio of beam radiation on tilted surface to that on horizontal
- $\beta$ = tilt angle of surface
- $\rho$ = ground reflectance (albedo, typically 0.2 for grass, 0.7 for snow)
- $I = I_b + I_d$ = total horizontal radiation

---

## 2.3 Solar Geometry — The Engineer's Toolkit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar geometry is the backbone of every KTU solar problem.** Memorise the angles below — they appear in nearly every numerical.

### 2.3.1 Declination Angle ($\delta$)
The angular position of the Sun at solar noon with respect to the equatorial plane.

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left(\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right)$$

- $n$ = day of the year (1 Jan = 1, 31 Dec = 365)
- $\delta = 0°$ at equinoxes, $+23.45°$ at summer solstice, $-23.45°$ at winter solstice.

### 2.3.2 Hour Angle ($\omega$)
The angular displacement of the Sun from the local meridian due to Earth's rotation (15° per hour).

$$\omega = 15° \times (t_{solar} - 12)$$

- Negative before solar noon, positive after.
- Sunrise / sunset hour angle: $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$

### 2.3.3 Altitude Angle ($\alpha$)
Height of the Sun above the horizon.

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

### 2.3.4 Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$)
Angle from the vertical (the complement of altitude).

$$\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$$

### 2.3.5 Azimuth Angle ($\gamma$ or $A$)
Compass direction of the Sun's projection on the horizontal plane.

$$\cos\gamma = \frac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi}$$

### 2.3.6 Solar Time Correction
To convert local clock time to **solar time**:
$$t_{solar} = t_{local} + E_t + 4(\text{Longitude}_{std} - \text{Longitude}_{local}) - DST$$

where $E_t$ is the **Equation of Time** (in minutes):
$$E_t = 9.87\sin(2B) - 7.53\cos B - 1.5\sin B, \quad B = \frac{360(n-81)}{365}$$

---

## 2.4 Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface

The **hourly extraterrestrial radiation** on a horizontal surface at the top of the atmosphere is:

$$I_{o,h} = I_{sc} \cdot E_o \cdot \cos\theta_z$$

The **daily-averaged extraterrestrial radiation** ($H_o$ in MJ/m²/day) is:

$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot E_o \cdot \left[\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi \omega_s}{360°}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}$ converts radians of hour angle into seconds of a day. The second term inside the bracket uses $\omega_s$ in radians.

### 2.4.1 Eccentricity Correction Factor
$$E_o = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360\,n}{365}\right)$$

---

## 2.5 Air Mass & Spectral Distribution

The **Air Mass (AM)** quantifies the path length of sunlight through the atmosphere relative to the shortest (vertical) path:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$

- **AM 0:** Outside the atmosphere (used for satellite solar cells).
- **AM 1:** Sun directly overhead at sea level.
- **AM 1.5:** Standard test condition for terrestrial PV modules (Sun at $48.2°$ altitude).
- **AM 2:** Sun at $30°$ altitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard **AM 1.5G** spectrum delivers $1000 \ \text{W/m}^2$ and is the reference irradiance for rating every PV module under KTU and IEC standards.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{sc} = 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$ | Solar constant | Reference flux |
| 2 | $E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$ | Eccentricity correction | Daily/seasonal flux |
| 3 | $\delta = 23.45°\sin[360(284+n)/365]$ | Declination angle | Solar geometry |
| 4 | $\omega = 15°(t_{solar} - 12)$ | Hour angle | Time-to-angle conversion |
| 5 | $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | Altitude angle | Sun position |
| 6 | $\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$ | Zenith angle | Incidence on horizontal surface |
| 7 | $\cos\gamma = (\sin\alpha\sin\phi - \sin\delta)/(\cos\alpha\cos\phi)$ | Azimuth angle | Compass direction of Sun |
| 8 | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ | Sunrise/sunset hour angle | Day length |
| 9 | $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ | Air mass | PV spectral correction |
| 10 | $H_o = (24 \cdot 3600/\pi) \cdot I_{sc} \cdot E_o \cdot [\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + (2\pi\omega_s/360°)\sin\phi\sin\delta]$ | Daily extraterrestrial H | Monthly avg. solar radiation |
| 11 | $\eta_{PV} = P_{out}/(G \cdot A)$ | PV efficiency | Module performance |
| 12 | $CUF = E_{actual}/(P_{rated} \times 8760)$ | Capacity utilisation factor | Audit KPI |
| 13 | $PR = E_{actual}/(E_{ideal} \cdot \text{derating})$ | Performance ratio | Audit KPI |

> [!WARNING]
> Always keep angles in **degrees** when using trigonometric functions like $\sin$ and $\cos$ in KTU problems unless specifically instructed to use radians. Mixing units is the **#1 cause of mark loss** in the ESE.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas power:

- **MNRE's solar atlas** of India (used to size rooftop systems).
- **PVsyst & SAM (System Advisor Model)** software used by energy auditors.
- **PVSYST's "meteo file" generator** which depends on $H_o$ and $\omega_s$.
- **Building-integrated PV (BIPV)** design where azimuth and tilt dictate annual yield.
- **Solar water heater** sizing (collector area $\propto 1/\eta_{thermal}$).
- **Standalone PV-battery system** design (autonomy days depend on $H_o$ averages).
