# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides the **complete mathematical derivation** of every key relationship. No steps are skipped — this is exactly how a 14-mark KTU numerical should be written in the answer sheet.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Solar Constant from First Principles

### Statement
The solar constant $I_{sc}$ is the radiant flux received at the mean Earth-Sun distance per unit area held perpendicular to the Sun's rays.

### Starting Point
The Sun emits a total power (luminosity) of $L_{\odot} = 3.846 \times 10^{26} \ \text{W}$ isotropically into space.

### Step 1 — Power Spread Over a Sphere
At a distance $r$ from the Sun, the power is spread uniformly over a sphere of surface area $4\pi r^2$. The flux at distance $r$ is therefore:

$$F = \frac{L_{\odot}}{4\pi r^2}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Mean Earth-Sun Distance
At $r = 1 \ \text{AU} = 1.496 \times 10^{11} \ \text{m}$:

$$F = \frac{3.846 \times 10^{26}}{4 \pi (1.496 \times 10^{11})^2}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Denominator
We compute $4\pi r^2$:

$$4 \pi r^2 = 4 \times 3.14159 \times (1.496 \times 10^{11})^2 = 4 \times 3.14159 \times 2.238 \times 10^{22}$$

$$4 \pi r^2 \approx 2.812 \times 10^{23} \ \text{m}^2$$

### Step 4 — Final Division

$$F = \frac{3.846 \times 10^{26}}{2.812 \times 10^{23}} \approx 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

This confirms the **Solar Constant $I_{sc} = 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Declination Angle

### Physical Reasoning
The Earth's rotational axis is tilted at $23.45°$ with respect to the orbital plane. The declination $\delta$ is the angle between the Sun-Earth line projected onto the orbital plane and the equatorial plane. It varies sinusoidally over the year.

### Step 1 — Define the Day Angle
Let $n$ be the day of the year. Define the day angle $\Gamma$:

$$\Gamma = \frac{360°}{365} \times (n - 1)$$

### Step 2 — Empirical Spencer Formula
The classical Spencer (1971) approximation gives:

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left(\Gamma - 80°\right)$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange
With the substitution $n - 1 \approx n - 81 + 80$, the formula is often written as:

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(n + 284)\right]$$

### Step 4 — Verification at Key Dates
- **March 21 (n = 80):** $\delta = 23.45 \times \sin[360 \times 364/365] \approx 23.45 \times \sin(358.9°) \approx 0°$ ✓
- **June 21 (n = 172):** $\delta = 23.45 \times \sin[360 \times 456/365] = 23.45 \times \sin(449.6°)$ which reduces to $\sin(89.6°) \approx 1$, giving $\delta \approx +23.45°$ ✓
- **December 21 (n = 355):** $\delta = 23.45 \times \sin[360 \times 639/365] \approx 23.45 \times \sin(630.0°) = 23.45 \times \sin(-90°) \approx -23.45°$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Altitude Angle $\alpha$

### Step 1 — Set Up the Spherical Triangle
The Sun's position in the celestial sphere is described by $(\delta, \omega)$ in the equatorial frame, and we need $(\alpha, \gamma)$ in the horizontal frame. The transformation uses the **co-latitude** $\phi' = 90° - \phi$ (where $\phi$ is the observer's latitude).

### Step 2 — Apply the Cosine Rule for Spherical Trigonometry
The general cosine rule states: for a spherical triangle with sides $a, b, c$ and opposite angles $A, B, C$:

$$\cos a = \cos b \cos c + \sin b \sin c \cos A$$

Mapping the celestial triangle: the side from the zenith to the pole is the co-latitude, the side from the pole to the Sun is the co-declination $(90° - \delta)$, and the side from the zenith to the Sun is the zenith distance $(90° - \alpha)$. The angle at the pole is the hour angle $\omega$.

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$\cos(90° - \alpha) = \cos(90° - \phi)\cos(90° - \delta) + \sin(90° - \phi)\sin(90° - \delta)\cos\omega$$

Using $\cos(90° - x) = \sin x$ and $\sin(90° - x) = \cos x$:

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

This is the **altitude-angle formula**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle $\omega_s$

### Step 1 — Boundary Condition
At sunrise and sunset, the Sun is on the horizon, so $\alpha = 0°$ and thus $\sin\alpha = 0$.

### Step 2 — Set the Altitude Formula to Zero

$$0 = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega_s$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\cos\omega_s$

$$\cos\omega_s = -\frac{\sin\phi \sin\delta}{\cos\phi \cos\delta} = -\tan\phi \tan\delta$$

### Step 4 — Take the Inverse Cosine

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\left(-\tan\phi \tan\delta\right)$$

### Step 5 — Day Length
Day length $N$ in hours is:

$$N = \frac{2\omega_s}{15°}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation $H_o$

### Step 1 — Hourly Extraterrestrial Radiation
At any instant, on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays, the extraterrestrial flux is $I_{sc} E_o$. On a horizontal surface, the effective flux is:

$$I_{o,h}(t) = I_{sc} E_o \cos\theta_z$$

### Step 2 — Integrate Over a Day
The total daily energy per unit horizontal area is the time integral from sunrise to sunset:

$$H_o = \int_{-t_s}^{t_s} I_{sc} E_o \cos\theta_z \,\, dt$$

where $t_s$ is the time from solar noon to sunset (in hours) and $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$.

### Step 3 — Change Variable from Time to Hour Angle
Because $\omega = 15° \cdot t$ (in hours), we have $dt = d\omega / 15°$:

$$H_o = \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} I_{sc} E_o \left[\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega\right] \frac{d\omega}{15°}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral
Split the integral:

$$H_o = \frac{I_{sc} E_o}{15°} \int_{-\omega_s}^{\omega_s} \left[\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega\right] d\omega$$

The first term is constant; the second term is odd (cosine is even), so the result is:

$$H_o = \frac{I_{sc} E_o}{15°} \left[2\omega_s \sin\phi\sin\delta + 2\cos\phi\cos\delta \sin\omega_s\right]$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Radians
Multiplying and dividing by $\pi/180°$ to convert degrees to radians yields the standard form:

$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot E_o \cdot \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi\omega_s}{360°}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right] \quad \text{(in J/m}^2\text{)}$$

Dividing by $10^6$ gives the result in **MJ/m²/day**, which is the KTU-recommended unit.

---

## 3.6 Worked-Out Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Numerical)

### Problem
Find the **declination angle**, **hour angle at 3:00 PM solar time**, **altitude angle**, and **azimuth angle** of the Sun at **Kochi ($\phi = 9.97°$ N)** on **15 March**. Also compute the **daily extraterrestrial radiation** on a horizontal surface for that day.

### Given
- Latitude $\phi = 9.97°$
- Day number $n = 31 + 28 + 15 = 74$
- Solar time = 15:00 hrs

### Step 1 — Declination
Using the Spencer formula:

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(74 + 284)\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365} \times 358\right] = 23.45° \times \sin(353.10°)$$

$$\delta = 23.45° \times (-0.1224) = -2.87°$$

### Step 2 — Hour Angle
At 3:00 PM solar time:

$$\omega = 15° \times (15 - 12) = 45°$$

### Step 3 — Altitude Angle
$$\sin\alpha = \sin(9.97°)\sin(-2.87°) + \cos(9.97°)\cos(-2.87°)\cos(45°)$$

$$\sin\alpha = (0.1731)(-0.0501) + (0.9849)(0.9987)(0.7071)$$

$$\sin\alpha = -0.00868 + 0.6954 = 0.6867$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.6867) = 43.36°$$

### Step 4 — Azimuth Angle
$$\cos\gamma = \frac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi} = \frac{(0.6867)(0.1731) - (-0.0501)}{(0.7270)(0.9849)}$$

$$\cos\gamma = \frac{0.1189 + 0.0501}{0.7161} = \frac{0.1690}{0.7161} = 0.2360$$

$$\gamma = \cos^{-1}(0.2360) = 76.35° \ \text{(west of south)}$$

### Step 5 — Eccentricity Correction
$$E_o = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360° \times 74}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033 \cos(72.99°)$$

$$E_o = 1 + 0.033 \times 0.2939 = 1.0097$$

### Step 6 — Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle
$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}\left[-\tan(9.97°)\tan(-2.87°)\right] = \cos^{-1}\left[-(0.1758)(-0.0501)\right]$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(0.00881) = 89.50°$$

### Step 7 — Daily $H_o$ on Horizontal
Plugging in:

$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \times 1367 \times 1.0097 \times \left[\cos(9.97°)\cos(-2.87°)\sin(89.50°) + \frac{2\pi \times 89.50°}{360°}\sin(9.97°)\sin(-2.87°)\right]$$

$$H_o = 27.88 \times 10^6 \times \left[(0.9849)(0.9987)(0.99996) + (1.562)(0.1731)(-0.0501)\right]$$

$$H_o = 27.88 \times 10^6 \times \left[0.9836 + (-0.01355)\right]$$

$$H_o = 27.88 \times 10^6 \times 0.9701 = 27.05 \ \text{MJ/m}^2\text{/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Audit interpretation:** A horizontal surface at Kochi on 15 March receives about **$27.05 \ \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day} \approx 7.51 \ \text{kWh/m}^2/\text{day}$** of extraterrestrial radiation. After atmospheric losses (clear-sky transmittance $\approx 0.75$), the actual terrestrial value is around **$5.6 \ \text{kWh/m}^2/\text{day}$** — useful for sizing PV arrays.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define: (i) Solar Constant, (ii) Declination Angle, (iii) Air Mass.**

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Solar Constant ($I_{sc}$):** The rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface held perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the mean Earth-Sun distance, outside the Earth's atmosphere. Value = **$1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$**.
- **(ii) Declination Angle ($\delta$):** The angular position of the Sun at solar noon with respect to the equatorial plane. Varies from $-23.45°$ to $+23.45°$ over the year.
- **(iii) Air Mass (AM):** The ratio of the actual path length of sunlight through the atmosphere to the path length when the Sun is directly overhead. **AM = $1/\cos\theta_z$**, where $\theta_z$ is the zenith angle.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Direct, Diffuse, and Reflected solar radiation. Why is this distinction important during an energy audit?**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Path | Audit Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Beam)** | Straight line from Sun | Powers CSP & clear-sky PV; needs tracking |
| **Diffuse** | Scattered by atmosphere | Dominant on cloudy days; flat-plate collectors still work |
| **Reflected (Albedo)** | Bounced off ground/snow | Significant for tilted surfaces; high in snowy/coastal sites |

**Audit Importance:** The auditor must measure **all three components** using a **pyranometer with shadow band** (for diffuse) and **pyrheliometer** (for direct). The *Klejper model* or *Liu & Jordan model* is then used to estimate tilted-plane radiation, which directly impacts **CUF and PR computation**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A — Solar Geometry & Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**A 10 kW rooftop solar PV system is proposed to be installed at Thiruvananthapuram (Latitude $\phi = 8.52°$ N, Longitude $76.0°$ E). The panels will be tilted at $\beta = 10°$ facing due south.**

**(a) Calculate the declination angle, hour angle at 2:00 PM solar time, and the altitude and azimuth angles of the Sun on 14 September. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compute the daily extraterrestrial radiation $H_o$ on a horizontal surface for 14 September, and estimate the clearness-index-adjusted daily global radiation assuming an average clearness index $\overline{K_T} = 0.55$. Comment on whether the proposed tilt angle is optimal. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Given:** $\phi = 8.52°$, $n = 31 + 28 + 31 + 30 + 31 + 31 + 14 = 196$, $t = 14:00$ solar time.

**Step 1 — Declination (1 mark):**

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(196 + 284)\right] = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360° \times 480}{365}\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin(473.42°) = 23.45° \times \sin(113.42°) = 23.45° \times 0.9176 = 21.52°$$

**Step 2 — Hour Angle (1 mark):**

$$\omega = 15° \times (14 - 12) = 30°$$

**Step 3 — Altitude (2 marks):**

$$\sin\alpha = \sin(8.52°)\sin(21.52°) + \cos(8.52°)\cos(21.52°)\cos(30°)$$

$$\sin\alpha = (0.1481)(0.3668) + (0.9890)(0.9302)(0.8660)$$

$$\sin\alpha = 0.0543 + 0.7968 = 0.8511$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.8511) = 58.34°$$

**Step 4 — Azimuth (3 marks):**

$$\cos\gamma = \frac{\sin(58.34°)\sin(8.52°) - \sin(21.52°)}{\cos(58.34°)\cos(8.52°)}$$

$$\cos\gamma = \frac{(0.8511)(0.1481) - 0.3668}{(0.5250)(0.9890)} = \frac{0.1260 - 0.3668}{0.5192} = \frac{-0.2408}{0.5192} = -0.4638$$

$$\gamma = \cos^{-1}(-0.4638) = 117.61° \ \text{(measured from north) or } 27.61° \ \text{west of south}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Eccentricity Correction (1 mark):**

$$E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos(360° \times 196/365) = 1 + 0.033\cos(193.32°) = 1 + 0.033(-0.9724) = 0.9679$$

**Step 2 — Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle (1 mark):**

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}[-\tan(8.52°)\tan(21.52°)] = \cos^{-1}[-(0.1498)(0.3944)] = \cos^{-1}(-0.0591) = 93.39°$$

**Step 3 — $H_o$ Computation (3 marks):**

$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \times 1367 \times 0.9679 \times \left[\cos(8.52°)\cos(21.52°)\sin(93.39°) + \frac{2\pi \times 93.39°}{360°}\sin(8.52°)\sin(21.52°)\right]$$

$$H_o = 27.88 \times 10^6 \times 0.9679 \times \left[(0.9890)(0.9302)(0.9983) + (1.630)(0.1481)(0.3668)\right]$$

$$H_o = 26.99 \times 10^6 \times [0.9187 + 0.0885] = 26.99 \times 10^6 \times 1.0072 = 27.19 \ \text{MJ/m}^2\text{/day}$$

$$H_o \approx 7.55 \ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$$

**Step 4 — Adjusted Daily Global Radiation (1 mark):**

$$H = \overline{K_T} \times H_o = 0.55 \times 7.55 = 4.15 \ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$$

**Step 5 — Tilt Angle Comment (1 mark):**

The latitude of the site is $\phi = 8.52°$. The **optimal annual tilt** for a south-facing surface is approximately $\beta_{opt} = \phi - 15° \approx -6°$ to $\phi \approx 8.52°$. A tilt of $10°$ is therefore **slightly above the annual optimum** and is **acceptable** (slight bias toward winter performance). For purely annual yield, $\beta \approx \phi = 8.5°$ is recommended.

---

### 📗 Question B — Air Mass, Solar Constant & Performance Metrics

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 — Alternative]**

**A 50 kW solar PV plant is installed at Calicut ($\phi = 11.25°$ N). The plant operates at the AM 1.5 standard test condition with module efficiency 18% and total module area $278 \ \text{m}^2$.**

**(a) Derive the value of the Solar Constant from the Sun's luminosity and mean Earth-Sun distance. Compute the extraterrestrial radiation received on a horizontal surface at 11:00 AM solar time on 1 June. (7 marks)**

**(b) Estimate the daily energy output of the plant given an average daily global radiation of $5.2 \ \text{kWh/m}^2/\text{day}$, system derating factor = 0.85, and performance ratio (PR) = 0.80. Calculate the Capacity Utilisation Factor (CUF) and comment on the result. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Derive Solar Constant (3 marks):**

$$I_{sc} = \frac{L_{\odot}}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{3.846 \times 10^{26}}{4\pi (1.496 \times 10^{11})^2} = 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

(Detailed evaluation as in §3.1.)

**Step 2 — Declination for 1 June ($n = 152$) (1 mark):**

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin[360°(152 + 284)/365] = 23.45° \times \sin(429.70°) = 23.45° \times \sin(69.70°)$$

$$\delta = 23.45° \times 0.9382 = 22.00°$$

**Step 3 — Hour Angle at 11 AM (1 mark):**

$$\omega = 15° \times (11 - 12) = -15°$$

**Step 4 — Zenith Angle and Instantaneous Flux (2 marks):**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(11.25°)\sin(22.00°) + \cos(11.25°)\cos(22.00°)\cos(-15°)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = (0.1951)(0.3746) + (0.9808)(0.9272)(0.9659) = 0.0731 + 0.8784 = 0.9515$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.9515) = 17.95°$$

**Step 5 — Eccentricity Factor and Instantaneous Extraterrestrial Flux (included):**

$$E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos(360° \times 152/365) = 1 + 0.033\cos(149.92°) = 1 + 0.033(-0.8616) = 0.9716$$

$$I_o = 1367 \times 0.9716 \times 0.9515 = 1263.6 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Theoretical Annual Energy from DC Rating (1 mark):**

$$E_{DC,nameplate} = 50 \ \text{kW} \times 8760 \ \text{h} = 438{,}000 \ \text{kWh/year}$$

**Step 2 — Actual Annual Energy Using PR (2 marks):**

$$E_{actual} = E_{DC,nameplate} \times PR = 438{,}000 \times 0.80 = 350{,}400 \ \text{kWh/year}$$

**Step 3 — Daily Energy Output from Field Data (2 marks):**

$$E_{daily} = H \times A \times \eta_{module} \times \text{derating} \times PR$$

$$E_{daily} = 5.2 \times 278 \times 0.18 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 = 5.2 \times 278 \times 0.1224 = 176.9 \ \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step 4 — Annual Energy (1 mark):**

$$E_{annual} = 176.9 \times 365 = 64{,}569 \ \text{kWh/year}$$

**Step 5 — CUF Computation (1 mark):**

$$CUF = \frac{E_{annual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760} = \frac{64{,}569}{50 \times 8760} = \frac{64{,}569}{438{,}000} = 0.1474 \approx 14.74\%$$

**Step 6 — Comment:** A CUF of 14.7% is **below** the typical Kerala benchmark of 15–17% for fixed-tilt PV. The discrepancy between PR-based (80%) and field CUF (14.7%) suggests possible **derating losses** (soiling, inverter clipping, or wiring losses). The auditor should recommend module cleaning, IV curve tracing, and inverter efficiency check.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall & Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Triggers in KTU Solar Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert MJ/m²/day to kWh/m²/day** — multiply by 0.2778.
> 2. **Mixing degrees and radians** in $\sin$, $\cos$, $\omega_s$ evaluation.
> 3. **Wrong day-number $n$** (e.g., forgetting leap-year correction in February).
> 4. **Hour angle sign** — negative before noon, positive after noon; reversing this gives wrong azimuth.
> 5. **Declination sign convention** — positive in northern hemisphere summer, negative in winter.
> 6. **Tilt optimisation confusion** — annual optimum $\beta \approx \phi$ for tropical Kerala; summer $\beta \approx \phi - 15°$; winter $\beta \approx \phi + 15°$.
> 7. **Confusing "AM 1.5"** as 1.5 suns — it is a *spectrum* notation, not intensity.
> 8. **CUF vs PR** — students frequently interchange them. **CUF is an operational energy ratio**; **PR is a system-quality ratio**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Fundamentals of Solar"**

- [x] **Solar Constant** $I_{sc} = 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$ is the *baseline* for all radiation calculations.
- [x] **Three components** of terrestrial radiation: Direct ($I_b$), Diffuse ($I_d$), Reflected ($I_r$).
- [x] **Declination $\delta$** uses $n$ (day number) — sign and value depend on season.
- [x] **Hour angle $\omega$** = $15° \times (\text{solar time} - 12)$.
- [x] **Altitude $\alpha$** is found from $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$.
- [x] **Azimuth $\gamma$** is found from $\cos\gamma$ relation; always specify reference (north or south).
- [x] **Sunrise/sunset hour angle** $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$.
- [x] **Day length** $N = 2\omega_s/15°$.
- [x] **Air mass** $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$; standard is **AM 1.5G = $1000 \ \text{W/m}^2$**.
- [x] **Eccentricity correction** $E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$.
- [x] **Daily extraterrestrial $H_o$** uses the integrated formula in MJ/m²/day.
- [x] **Clearness index** $\overline{K_T} = H/H_o$ ranges 0.25 (cloudy) to 0.75 (clear) in India.
- [x] **PV efficiency** $\eta = P_{out}/(G \times A)$; typical c-Si 15–20%.
- [x] **CUF** = $E_{actual}/(P_{rated} \times 8760)$; **PR** accounts for all system losses.
- [x] **Tilt for Kerala** ($\phi \approx 8–12°$): annual optimum $\beta = \phi$; for *maximum annual yield* fix at $\beta = \phi$ facing south.
- [x] **Solar time ≠ clock time** — apply Equation of Time + longitude correction + DST.
- [x] **Memorise the Spencer formula** for declination — the most-frequently-asked KTU formula.
- [x] **Direction conventions:** Azimuth measured clockwise from north in most engineering texts; some KTU problems measure from south — always read the question carefully.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Hydro Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Wind Hydro Power ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Wind Energy — Formal Definition **Wind Energy** is the kinetic energy possessed by air masses in motion due to differential solar heating of the Earth's surface. It is a form of **renewable, indirect solar energy** that can be converted into useful mechanical work or electrical energy using a device called a **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** or simply a wind turbine. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Wind energy is the kinetic energy of moving air which is captured by the aerodynamic blades of a wind turbine and converted into rotational mechanical energy, which subsequently drives an electrical generator. The fundamental energy source equation states: $$E_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot V^{2}$$ where **$m$** is the mass of air passing through the swept area per unit time (kg/s) and **$V$** is the wind velocity (m/s). The standard reference **air density is $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$** at sea level at 15 °C. ### 1.2 Hydro Energy — Formal Definition **Hydro Energy** (Hydropower) is the energy derived from the potential energy of water stored at a height (gravitational head) or from the kinetic energy of flowing water. It is converted to electricity using a **hydro-turbine coupled to a generator**. $$P_{hydro} = \eta \cdot \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$ where **$H$** is the net head (m), **$Q$** is the discharge (m³/s), **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**, and **$\eta$** is the overall efficiency. ### 1.3 Wind-Hydro Power — Hybrid Concept **Wind-Hydro Power** refers to the integrated generation system where wind energy conversion and hydropower generation operate together — often through **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** — to overcome the **intermittency** of standalone wind power. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy (The Battery + Tap Analogy):** > Imagine your kitchen has a tap that supplies water (this is the **hydro plant** — reliable, steady). Sometimes a strong wind fills the bucket quickly (this is **wind energy** — fast but unpredictable). When the bucket is overflowing due to strong wind, you pump the excess water back into the overhead tank (this is **pumped hydro storage**). When the wind stops, you use the stored water from the tank to keep the tap flowing. Thus, wind and hydro complement each other like a **bucket and a tap working in tandem** to deliver uninterrupted water (electricity). ### 1.4 Why Combine Wind and Hydro? | Limitation of Standalone Wind | How Hydro Complements It | |-------------------------------|---------------------------| | Intermittent (varies with wind speed) | Hydro provides baseload stability | | Diurnal / seasonal variability | Reservoirs buffer over long periods | | Cannot match load demand directly | Hydro is fully dispatchable | | Requires storage for off-peak periods | Pumped hydro acts as giant storage | | Power quality issues (voltage flicker) | Hydro smoothens output | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Values for KTU:** > - Air density at STP: **$\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$** > - Water density: **$\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$** > - Gravitational acceleration: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** > - Betz Coefficient Maximum: **$C_{P,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physics of Wind Power — Step-by-Step Logic The conversion of wind energy into electrical energy involves three sequential stages: - **Stage 1 — Aerodynamic Capture:** Wind streamlines flow across the turbine blades; pressure differential generates a lift force that rotates the rotor. - **Stage 2 — Mechanical Transmission:** The rotor shaft turns a gearbox (in geared systems) that steps up the low-speed rotation to high-speed rotation. - **Stage 3 — Electromagnetic Conversion:** The high-speed shaft drives a generator (typically induction or PMSG), producing AC electricity. **Why does the Betz Limit exist?** If the turbine extracts 100% of the wind's kinetic energy, the air would have to come to a complete stop behind the rotor — but then no new air could enter the rotor disc. Conversely, if the air exits at full speed, no energy is extracted. An optimal balance (16/27 of incident kinetic energy) yields the maximum possible extraction, known as the **Betz Limit**, named after German physicist **Albert Betz (1919)**. ### 2.2 Aerodynamic Operating Parameters - **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR), $\lambda$:** The ratio of the blade tip speed to the free-stream wind speed. $$\lambda = \frac{\omega \cdot R}{V}$$ where $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s) and $R$ is the rotor radius (m). - **Power Coefficient, $C_P$:** The fraction of incident wind power actually captured. $$C_P = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}}$$ - **Solidity, $\sigma$:** Ratio of blade area to swept area (high for low-speed, high-torque machines; low for high-speed aerodynamic machines). ### 2.3 Wind-Hydro System Architectures There are **three principal architectures** for combining wind and hydro: 1. **Grid-Connected Wind + Hydro Reservoir Backup:** Wind feeds grid; hydro covers shortfall. 2. **Pumped Storage Wind-Hydro:** Surplus wind electricity pumps water to an upper reservoir; during low-wind periods, water is released through turbines to generate electricity. 3. **Run-of-River + Wind:** Adds wind turbines near existing small hydro plants, sharing the same grid interconnection infrastructure. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Example:** The **Itaipu Dam (Brazil/Paraguay)** and several European wind-hydro hybrids (e.g., in Norway and Spain) routinely use pumped hydro to store surplus renewable energy — a direct industrial analogue of the Wind-Hydro Power concept. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------------------| | 1 | $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ | Power available in wind | $\rho$ kg/m³, $A$ m², $V$ m/s | | 2 | $P_{turbine} = C_P \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ | Mechanical power extracted | $C_P$ dimensionless | | 3 | $C_{P,max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz theoretical maximum | — | | 4 | $\lambda = \tfrac{\omega R}{V}$ | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | $\omega$ rad/s, $R$ m | | 5 | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | Swept area of rotor | $R$ m | | 6 | $P_{hydro} = \eta \rho_w g Q H$ | Hydroelectric power output | $\eta$ dimensionless, $Q$ m³/s, $H$ m | | 7 | $E_{annual} = P_{rated} \cdot CF \cdot 8760$ | Annual energy yield | $CF$ capacity factor | | 8 | $E_{kinetic} = \tfrac{1}{2} m V^{2}$ | Kinetic energy in moving air | $m$ kg | | 9 | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | Mass flow rate of air | kg/s | | 10 | $V_z = V_{ref} \cdot \left(\tfrac{z}{z_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$ | Power law wind shear profile | $\alpha$ terrain coefficient | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that wind power scales with the **cube of wind speed**. A doubling of wind speed produces **eight times** the power. This cubic relationship is why site selection (with reliable high winds) is the single most important factor in wind farm economics. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Power Available in Wind **Statement:** Derive the expression for the power contained in a wind stream of velocity $V$ passing perpendicularly through an area $A$. **Step 1 — Identify the mass flow rate of air.** Consider a column of air of cross-sectional area $A$ moving at velocity $V$. The length of the column passing through per unit time is $V$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Volume flow rate, } \dot{V} &= A \cdot V \quad [\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Convert volume flow rate to mass flow rate.** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m} &= \rho \cdot \dot{V} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V \quad [\text{kg}/\text{s}] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Recall the kinetic energy of a moving body.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_k &= \frac{1}{2} m V^{2} \quad [\text{J}] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Determine kinetic energy per unit time (i.e., power).** Power is energy per unit time, so substitute $\dot{m}$ for $m/t$: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{wind} &= \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^{2} \\ P_{wind} &= \frac{1}{2} (\rho A V) V^{2} \\ P_{wind} &= \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **fundamental wind power equation** required in every KTU problem. --- ### 3.2 Derivation II — The Betz Limit (Maximum $C_P$) **Statement:** Show that the maximum theoretical fraction of wind power extractable by an ideal turbine is $\tfrac{16}{27}$. **Step 1 — Define upstream and downstream states.** Let $V_1$ be the upstream wind velocity and $V_2$ the velocity at the turbine plane. By continuity, the mass flow rate is constant: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m} = \rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A V = \rho A_2 V_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the momentum equation.** The force on the rotor equals the rate of change of momentum: $$ \begin{aligned} F = \dot{m}(V_1 - V_2) = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Power extracted equals force times velocity at the disc.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{ext} &= F \cdot V = \rho A V^{2}(V_1 - V_2) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Express $V$ as the mean of upstream and downstream velocities (actuator disc theory):** $$ \begin{aligned} V &= \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{ext} &= \rho A \left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)^{2}(V_1 - V_2) \\ P_{ext} &= \frac{\rho A}{4} (V_1 + V_2)^{2}(V_1 - V_2) \\ P_{ext} &= \frac{\rho A}{4} (V_1^{2} - V_2^{2})(V_1 + V_2) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Compute $C_P$ by dividing $P_{ext}$ by upstream wind power $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^{3}$.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_P &= \frac{P_{ext}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^{3}} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}(V_1^{2} - V_2^{2})(V_1 + V_2)}{V_1^{3}} \\ C_P &= \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{2}\right)\left(1 + \frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Differentiate w.r.t. the velocity ratio $V_2/V_1$ to find the optimum.** Let $x = V_2/V_1$. Then $C_P = \tfrac{1}{2}(1-x^{2})(1+x) = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 + x - x^{2} - x^{3})$. $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{dC_P}{dx} &= \frac{1}{2}(1 - 2x - 3x^{2}) = 0 \\ 3x^{2} + 2x - 1 &= 0 \\ x &= \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 12}}{6} = \frac{-2 + 4}{6} = \frac{1}{3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8 — Substitute $x = 1/3$ back into $C_P$:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{P,max} &= \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{1}{9}\right)\left(1 + \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{8}{9} \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{32}{54} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \text{ (Betz Limit)}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Solved Numerical Example — KTU Pattern **Problem:** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 50 m. The wind speed is 12 m/s, air density is 1.225 kg/m³, $C_P$ = 0.40, and generator efficiency is 0.90. Calculate: (a) Power available in the wind. (b) Power extracted by the turbine. (c) Electrical power output. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the swept area.** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= \frac{D}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ m} \\ A &= \pi R^{2} = \pi (25)^{2} = 1963.5 \text{ m}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Power in wind.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{wind} &= \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} \\ &= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 1963.5 \times (12)^{3} \\ &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1963.5 \times 1728 \\ &= 2{,}077{,}876 \text{ W} \approx 2.08 \text{ MW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Mechanical power extracted by turbine.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{mech} &= C_P \cdot P_{wind} \\ &= 0.40 \times 2.08 \text{ MW} \\ &= 0.832 \text{ MW} = 832 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Electrical power output.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{elec} &= \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{mech} \\ &= 0.90 \times 832 \text{ kW} \\ &= 748.8 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answers:** - (a) $P_{wind} \approx 2.08$ MW - (b) $P_{mech} \approx 832$ kW - (c) $P_{elec} \approx 748.8$ kW ### 3.4 Numerical Example — Annual Energy Yield with Wind-Hydro Backup A 1 MW wind farm has a **capacity factor (CF) of 25%**. Compute the annual energy generation and the equivalent energy that the supporting hydro plant must supply to maintain 80% load factor of the combined system. $$ \begin{aligned} E_{wind,annual} &= P_{rated} \times CF \times 8760 \\ &= 1 \text{ MW} \times 0.25 \times 8760 \\ &= 21{,}900 \text{ MWh/year} \end{aligned} $$ Combined load target at 80% LF: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{target} &= 1 \text{ MW} \times 0.80 \times 8760 = 7008 \text{ MWh/year (wind portion)} \\ E_{hydro,required} &= 7008 - 2190 = 4818 \text{ MWh/year} \end{aligned} $$ (Note: The above simplified calculation assumes wind supplies its share of the load. In a true hybrid with shared bus, dispatch logic is more nuanced.) ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Wind-Hydro Hybrid Power System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Wind Resource
Kinetic Energy in Air] nodeB[Wind Turbine Rotor
Aerodynamic Capture Cp] nodeC[Gearbox
Speed Multiplier] nodeD[Synchronous or PMSG Generator
Electrical Conversion] nodeE[AC Bus Bar
Power Conditioning Unit] nodeF[Upper Reservoir
Pumped Storage] nodeG[Surplus Wind Electricity
Drives Pump-Turbine Motor] nodeH[Hydro Turbine
Pelton Francis Kaplan] nodeI[Hydro Generator
Dispatchable Output] nodeJ[Load Centre
Grid or Isolated Network] nodeK[Control System
SCADA Dispatch Logic] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeJ nodeK -. Monitors .-> nodeE nodeK -. Controls .-> nodeG nodeK -. Controls .-> nodeH ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Energy Flow in Wind-Hydro Hybrid ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Wind Kinetic Energy
1/2 rho A V cube] nodeB[Mechanical Shaft Power
Cp eta_m P_wind] nodeC[Electrical Power
eta_g P_mech] nodeD{Bus Condition Check
Is load matched} nodeE[Direct Supply to Load
Net Electricity Output] nodeF[Pump Mode
Send water uphill to reservoir] nodeG[Reservoir Stores Potential Energy
m g H] nodeH[Hydro Generation Mode
Discharge water when wind low] nodeI[Storage Cycle Efficiency
eta_round_trip approx 70 to 80 percent] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE nodeD -- Surplus --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeE nodeG -. Efficiency loss .-> nodeI ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Wind Energy Conversion Process ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Wind Stream V ms] nodeB[Rotor Disc Cp Power Extraction] nodeC[Gearbox Step Up Ratio 1 to 50] nodeD[Generator PMSG or DFIG Output] nodeE[Transformer Voltage Step Up] nodeF[Grid Interface or Load Bank] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF ``` ### 4.4 Schematic Layout — Pumped Hydro Storage Coupled to Wind Farm ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Wind Farm Bus
Multiple Turbines] nodeB[Rectifier Inverter System
Bidirectional Power Flow] nodeC[Upper Reservoir
High Elevation Storage] nodeD[Reversible Pump Turbine
Pump Mode and Generate Mode] nodeE[Penstock Pipeline
Head H metres] nodeF[Lower Reservoir
Tail Race] nodeG[Governor and Control Valve] nodeH[Grid Load Centre] nodeA -- Excess --> nodeB nodeB -- Drives pump --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeD ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Note:** Mermaid flowcharts are used here in lieu of physical diagrams (per protocol) to convey the architecture, signal flow, and control logic of Wind-Hydro systems. Each block represents a functional subsystem that students should be able to label, identify losses across, and explain in a viva or written exam. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term **Power Coefficient** of a wind turbine. What is its maximum theoretical value and why can't it be exceeded? **Model Answer:** The Power Coefficient ($C_P$) is defined as the ratio of the actual mechanical power extracted by a wind turbine to the total power available in the wind stream incident on the rotor. $$C_P = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{P_{extracted}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}}$$ Its **maximum theoretical value is $\tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$**, known as the **Betz Limit**. This limit exists because if 100% of the kinetic energy were extracted, the air would have to stop completely behind the rotor, preventing any new air from entering the disc. Hence, no practical turbine can extract more than 59.3% of incident wind power. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** List any **three advantages** of integrating a wind power plant with a pumped hydro storage system. **Model Answer:** 1. **Load Levelling:** Surplus wind electricity during high-wind periods pumps water uphill; this stored potential energy is reconverted during low-wind periods, ensuring continuous supply. 2. **Grid Stability:** Pumped hydro provides inertia and frequency regulation services that intermittent wind alone cannot offer. 3. **Storage Duration:** Pumped hydro offers long-duration storage (hours to days) at a relatively low Levelised Cost of Storage (LCOS), unlike batteries which are costlier for long durations. 4. (Optional bonus) **Reduced Curtailment:** Avoids wasting surplus wind generation that would otherwise have to be curtailed due to grid saturation. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the power available in wind. A wind turbine has the following specifications: rotor diameter 60 m, wind speed 10 m/s, air density 1.225 kg/m³, $C_P = 0.42$, generator efficiency 0.92. Calculate (i) the swept area, (ii) the power available in the wind, (iii) the electrical power output. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Wind-Hydro Hybrid Power System**. Discuss how pumped hydro storage compensates for the intermittency of wind. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Step-by-step Solution:** **Step 1 — Derivation of wind power expression:** Consider air of density $\rho$ flowing with velocity $V$ through an area $A$. - Volume flow rate = $A \cdot V$ - Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ - Kinetic energy per unit mass = $\tfrac{1}{2} V^{2}$ - Power = kinetic energy per unit time = $\tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ **Step 2 — Numerical computation:** $$ \begin{aligned} D &= 60 \text{ m}, \quad R = 30 \text{ m} \\ A &= \pi R^{2} = \pi (30)^{2} = 2827.43 \text{ m}^{2} \\ P_{wind} &= \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times (10)^{3} \\ &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times 1000 \\ &= 1{,}731{,}815 \text{ W} \approx 1.732 \text{ MW} \\ P_{elec} &= C_P \times \eta_{gen} \times P_{wind} \\ &= 0.42 \times 0.92 \times 1.732 \text{ MW} \\ &= 0.669 \text{ MW} \approx 669 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $A = 2827.43 \text{ m}^{2}$ - (ii) $P_{wind} \approx 1.732$ MW - (iii) $P_{elec} \approx 669$ kW **Part (b) — Block Diagram and Explanation:** (Refer to diagram in SECTION 4.1 above.) **Working:** - Wind turbines convert kinetic energy of wind into electricity. - When wind generation **exceeds** load demand, the surplus electricity drives a **reversible pump-turbine** that lifts water from a lower reservoir to an **upper reservoir**, storing energy as gravitational potential energy. - When wind generation **falls short** of demand (low wind period), water is released from the upper reservoir through the turbine, generating electricity to bridge the gap. - A **SCADA-based control system** continuously monitors wind power output, load demand, reservoir levels, and dispatches the hydro plant accordingly. **How pumped hydro compensates for intermittency:** - Wind is **non-dispatchable** and stochastic. Pumped hydro provides **dispatchable, on-demand generation**. - Storage duration of hours to days, ideal for diurnal wind cycles. - Provides **inertia and frequency support** to the grid. - Round-trip efficiency is typically **70–80%**, which is acceptable for large-scale storage. --- **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Alternative Choice]** **(a)** What is the **Betz Limit**? Show mathematically that the maximum power coefficient of a wind turbine is 16/27. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting site selection** for a wind power plant. How do wind speed distribution (Weibull parameters) influence the choice? **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Betz Limit Derivation:** (Full step-by-step derivation provided in SECTION 3.2 above. Recap of key steps:) - Apply continuity and momentum equations across the actuator disc. - Derive $C_P = \tfrac{1}{2}(1-x^{2})(1+x)$ where $x = V_2/V_1$. - Differentiate and set to zero to obtain $x = 1/3$. - Substitute back: $C_{P,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$. **Part (b) — Site Selection Factors:** | # | Factor | Influence | |---|--------|-----------| | 1 | **Mean Wind Speed** | Power ∝ V³; even small increases drastically improve yield. | | 2 | **Wind Direction & Variability** | Prevailing direction determines turbine layout and yaw strategy. | | 3 | **Terrain & Roughness** | Affects shear coefficient α; open terrain is preferred. | | 4 | **Hub Height & Topography** | Higher elevation & smoother flow = higher wind speeds. | | 5 | **Accessibility & Grid Proximity** | Reduces transmission cost and infrastructure losses. | | 6 | **Environmental & Social Constraints** | Bird migration, noise ordinances, land-use rights. | | 7 | **Weibull Parameters (k, c)** | Characterise wind speed distribution; **c = scale (m/s), k = shape (dimensionless)**. Higher $c$ and optimal $k$ (≈2) yield higher energy capture. | **Weibull Influence:** The probability that wind speed lies between $V$ and $V + dV$ is: $$f(V) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{V}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(V/c)^{k}}$$ The energy pattern factor is $E_{pf} = \tfrac{\Gamma(1+3/k)}{\Gamma^{3}(1+1/k)}$, which directly affects annual energy yield calculations. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Errors:** Forgetting to convert kW to MW, or using m/s inconsistently. Always carry units explicitly. > 2. **Ignoring Generator Efficiency:** Many students forget to multiply by $\eta_{gen}$ when asked for "electrical power output." > 3. **Betz Limit Justification:** Simply stating "16/27" without derivation or actuator-disc reasoning scores low. Mention **Albert Betz (1919)** and the physical impossibility of 100% extraction. > 4. **Confusing $C_P$ with $\eta$:** $C_P$ is the aerodynamic power coefficient, while $\eta$ refers to mechanical/electrical efficiencies of downstream components. > 5. **Tip Speed Ratio Mistakes:** $\lambda = \omega R / V$ — students often invert this as $V / (\omega R)$. > 6. **Swept Area Formula:** Always use $A = \pi R^{2}$ where $R = D/2$; common error is using diameter directly. > 7. **Wind-Hydro Architecture:** Students describe wind turbines but fail to explain the **storage cycle** (pump-turbine reversibility, round-trip efficiency). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Wind power equation:** $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ — the **cube law** is the most critical takeaway. - **Swept area:** $A = \pi R^{2}$; rotor diameter (not radius) is usually given. - **Power coefficient:** $C_P = P_{mech}/P_{wind}$, with **Betz Limit = 16/27 ≈ 0.593**. - **Tip Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / V$; optimal $\lambda$ depends on blade design (typically 6–8 for modern 3-blade HAWTs). - **Actual power extracted:** $P_{turbine} = C_P \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$, then multiply by $\eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{gen}$ for electrical output. - **Wind-Hydro Hybrid:** Combines intermittent wind with dispatchable hydro; **pumped hydro storage** acts as a giant "battery" with long discharge duration. - **Round-trip efficiency** of pumped hydro storage: **70–80%**. - **Wind shear power law:** $V_z = V_{ref}(z/z_{ref})^{\alpha}$, with $\alpha$ ranging 0.10 (smooth flat terrain) to 0.40 (urban). - **Capacity factor** of onshore wind: ~25–35%; offshore can reach 45–55%. - **Standard air density at sea level (15°C):** **1.225 kg/m³**. - **Weibull parameters k and c** fully describe wind regime; energy yield estimation requires integration over the Weibull distribution. - **HAWT (Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine)** dominates commercial deployment due to higher $C_P$ and self-starting capability. - **VAWT (Vertical Axis Wind Turbine)** advantages: omnidirectional wind acceptance, lower noise; disadvantages: lower $C_P$, high structural loads at base. > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line Mnemonics for Viva:** > - "**P = half rho A V cube**" → Wind power > - "**16/27 is Betz**" → Maximum theoretical efficiency > - "**TSR = omega R by V**" → Tip Speed Ratio > - "**Wind for generation, hydro for storage**" → Hybrid philosophy

topics.title: Biomass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biomass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition of Biomass

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomass** is any organic matter of recent biological origin (plant, animal, or microbial) that can be used directly as a fuel or converted into solid, liquid, or gaseous fuels through thermochemical, biochemical, or physicochemical conversion processes. It is classified as a **renewable** and **carbon-neutral** energy resource because the CO₂ released during combustion is reabsorbed during the next plant growth cycle.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, biomass forms the third pillar of renewable energy sources (alongside solar and wind) and is strategically important for **decentralized rural electrification**, **cogeneration in sugar mills and rice mills**, and **biogas-based distributed generation**.

**Standard Reference Metrics (must memorize):**

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Calorific value of dry wood | **17–20** | MJ/kg |
| Calorific value of bagasse | **8–10** | MJ/kg |
| Calorific value of rice husk | **12–15** | MJ/kg |
| Methane fraction in biogas | **55–70** | % (vol) |
| Density of biomass pellets | **650–750** | kg/m³ |
| Moisture content (air-dried) | **15–25** | % (wt) |

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Natural Solar Battery"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Think of biomass as a rechargeable battery charged by sunlight.**
>
> Just like a battery stores electrical energy that you can later drain to power a bulb, a tree (or any plant) **stores solar energy** in the chemical bonds of cellulose, lignin, sugars, and oils through photosynthesis. When we burn wood, digest sugar in a biogas plant, or crush oil seeds for biodiesel, we are essentially **"draining" that stored sunlight** and converting it into useful heat, electricity, or motion.
>
> The "recharge" part happens automatically — the CO₂ we release is re-captured by new plants, completing the **short carbon cycle**.

This intuitive picture helps you remember three things in the exam:
1. Biomass is **stored solar energy** (justification for renewability).
2. It is **not strictly carbon-zero** (energy is needed for harvesting/transport, but the cycle is short).
3. The *quality* of the "battery charge" depends on the **calorific value** of the biomass species used.

---

## 1.3 Why Biomass Matters in Energy Management & Audit

From an **Energy Auditor's perspective**, biomass is audited under:
- **Energy Conservation Act 2001** (PAT scheme for industries).
- **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy)** standards for grid-connected biomass power.
- **ISO 50001** Energy Management Systems — as a substitute fuel.

The auditor typically assesses:
- Moisture content (directly affects NCV).
- Ash content (affects boiler efficiency).
- Volatile matter (affects combustion behaviour).
- Fixed carbon (determines residence time in the boiler).
- Cost per GJ delivered vs. fossil fuel baseline.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Biomass (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

Biomass is classified along **four principal axes**:

### A) By Source / Origin
1. **Woody biomass** — fuelwood, sawdust, bark, forestry residues.
2. **Agricultural residues** — bagasse, rice husk, wheat straw, coconut shell, groundnut shell.
3. **Energy crops / plantations** — sugarcane, jatropha, switchgrass, *Miscanthus*.
4. **Animal waste** — dung, poultry litter, bone meal.
5. **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** — kitchen waste, paper, yard clippings.
6. **Aquatic biomass** — water hyacinth, algae (*Chlorella*, *Spirulina*).

### B) By Physical State
- **Solid biomass** (lignocellulosic — fuelwood, pellets, briquettes).
- **Liquid biofuels** (ethanol, biodiesel, pyrolysis oil).
- **Gaseous biofuels** (biogas, producer gas, syngas).

### C) By Moisture Content
- **Dry biomass** (<20% moisture) — wood chips, pellets.
- **Wet biomass** (>50% moisture) — dung, aquatic plants.

### D) By Generation Cycle
- **First-generation** — from food crops (corn ethanol, palm oil biodiesel).
- **Second-generation** — from lignocellulosic residues & wastes (cellulosic ethanol).
- **Third-generation** — from algae.
- **Fourth-generation** — genetically engineered or carbon-capture integrated.

---

## 2.2 Biomass Conversion Pathways (The "Big Four")

Every biomass-to-energy conversion route falls under **one of these four families** — *memorise this tree for the exam*.

### 2.2.1 Thermo-Chemical Conversion (heat-driven)
| Process | Temperature (°C) | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Combustion** | 800–1200 | Direct heat / steam |
| **Gasification** | 700–900 | Producer gas (CO + H₂ + CH₄ + N₂) |
| **Pyrolysis** (slow) | 300–500 | Char (biochar) |
| **Pyrolysis** (fast) | 500–800 | Bio-oil (pyrolytic oil) |
| **Flash pyrolysis** | 800–1000 | Maximum bio-oil yield (~70–80%) |

### 2.2.2 Bio-Chemical Conversion (microorganism-driven)
- **Anaerobic digestion (AD)** → Biogas (60% CH₄ + 40% CO₂) + digestate.
- **Fermentation** → Ethanol (from sugars) or Butanol (ABE process).
- **Aerobic composting** → not for energy; for soil amendment.

### 2.2.3 Physico-Chemical Conversion
- **Oil extraction + transesterification** → Biodiesel (FAME).
- **Hydrolysis + fermentation** of cellulose → Cellulosic ethanol.

### 2.2.4 Mechanical / Densification
- **Briquetting** and **pelletization** to increase bulk density from ~80 kg/m³ to ~650 kg/m³.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Master these formulas — they appear regularly in Part B numerical problems and in the Energy Audit report.

| Sl. | Formula | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle \text{NCV} \;=\; \text{GCV} \;-\; \frac{9H}{100}(587) \;-\; \frac{M}{100}(2260)$ | Net Calorific Value (kJ/kg) of solid/liquid fuel. |
| 2 | $\displaystyle \text{GCV} \;=\; 337\,C \;+\; 1418\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) \;+\; 94\,S$ | **Dulong's formula** for GCV (kJ/kg), C,H,O,S in % (with M, Ash excluded). |
| 3 | $\displaystyle \text{Energy Released} \;=\; m \times \text{NCV}$ | Total thermal energy from burning mass *m* (kg). |
| 4 | $\displaystyle \eta_{\text{boiler}} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{s}}(h_{g} - h_{f})}{m_{f} \times \text{GCV}} \times 100$ | Boiler efficiency (%). |
| 5 | $\displaystyle V_{\text{biogas}} \;=\; 0.35 \times m_{\text{VS}}$ | Empirical rule — biogas yield ≈ **0.35 m³/kg of Volatile Solids**. |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\text{CH}_4 \;+\; 3\,\text{CO}_2$ | Glucose → methane stoichiometry (ideal AD). |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \%M_{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times \text{Vol of CH}_4}{V_{\text{biogas}}}$ | Methane composition check. |
| 8 | $\displaystyle P_{\text{pellet}} \;=\; P_{0} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{a}}{R\,T}\right)$ | Rate of pyrolysis (Arrhenius form). |
| 9 | $\displaystyle \text{ER (Equivalence Ratio)} \;=\; \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Critical control parameter in gasification. |
| 10 | $\displaystyle \text{Energy ratio} \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in (cultivation + processing)}}}$ | Net Energy Ratio (NER) for biomass — must exceed **1**. |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above table, **H** = hydrogen %, **M** = moisture %, **C/H/O/S** = ultimate analysis percentages, **mₛ** = steam mass, **m_f** = fuel mass, **VS** = volatile solids.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Knowledge Matters in the Field

Biomass conversion is the **backbone of decentralised energy**, especially in rural Kerala (coconut, areca, rice, jackfruit waste streams) and industrial estates (cashew, rubber, coir). An energy auditor uses the above formulas to:

1. **Verify fuel substitution claims** — confirm that replacing furnace oil with bagasse actually saves money per **GJ of useful heat**.
2. **Size a biogas plant** — by mass balance on volatile solids.
3. **Recommend biomass cogeneration** — quantifying steam + electricity output from a back-pressure turbine.
4. **Audit MSW incineration plants** — to determine whether MSW qualifies as a "waste-to-energy" fuel under MSW Rules 2016.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Net Calorific Value (NCV) from GCV

When biomass fuel burns, the water produced from the combustion of hydrogen (and any moisture present) leaves as **vapour**, taking away latent heat. The GCV assumes this water **condenses**, so:

$$
\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - \text{Heat carried away by water vapour}
$$

**Step 1.** Hydrogen present in fuel = $H$ kg per 100 kg of fuel (i.e. $H$%).

**Step 2.** Mass of water produced from H combustion:
$$
m_{w,\text{from H}} = 9\,H \;\;\text{(because 2 kg H + 16 kg O → 18 kg H}_2\text{O, so 1 kg H → 9 kg water)}
$$

**Step 3.** Latent heat of vaporisation of water ≈ **2260 kJ/kg** (at 100 °C, atmospheric pressure).

**Step 4.** Mass of water already present as moisture = $M$ kg per 100 kg fuel. This also evaporates → heat loss = $M \times 2260$ kJ.

**Step 5.** Sensible heat of water vapour from 100 °C to ambient ≈ $9H \times (c_{p,\text{vapour}} \times 600)$ ≈ $9H \times 2.01 \times 600 \approx 9H \times 1206$ kJ per 100 kg fuel.

**Step 6.** Combining (per kg of fuel):
$$
\text{NCV} \;=\; \text{GCV} \;-\; \left(\frac{9H}{100}\right)\!\cdot\!2260 \;-\; \left(\frac{M}{100}\right)\!\cdot\!2260
$$

Or in compact academic form:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NCV (kJ/kg)} \;=\; \text{GCV (kJ/kg)} \;-\; 9\,H\,\!\left(\tfrac{2.442 + M/9}{100}\right)\;}
$$

> **Why this matters:** During KTU audit problems, students commonly **forget the moisture term**. A 20%-moisture bagasse loses nearly **452 kJ/kg** even before considering H content.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Stoichiometric Methane Yield from Glucose (Buswell Reaction)

The **Buswell equation** for anaerobic digestion of a generic organic substrate C$_n$H$_a$O$_b$ is:

$$
\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b \;+\; \left(n - \tfrac{a}{4} - \tfrac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \left(\tfrac{n}{2} - \tfrac{a}{8} + \tfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2 \;+\; \left(\tfrac{n}{2} + \tfrac{a}{8} - \tfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4
$$

**Step-by-step application to glucose C₆H₁₂O₆** (n=6, a=12, b=6):

**Step 1.** Coefficient of H₂O:
$$
n - \tfrac{a}{4} - \tfrac{b}{2} \;=\; 6 - \tfrac{12}{4} - \tfrac{6}{2} \;=\; 6 - 3 - 3 \;=\; 0
$$

(Good — glucose already contains enough oxygen!)

**Step 2.** Coefficient of CO₂:
$$
\tfrac{n}{2} - \tfrac{a}{8} + \tfrac{b}{4} \;=\; 3 - \tfrac{12}{8} + \tfrac{6}{4} \;=\; 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 \;=\; 3
$$

**Step 3.** Coefficient of CH₄:
$$
\tfrac{n}{2} + \tfrac{a}{8} - \tfrac{b}{4} \;=\; 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 \;=\; 3
$$

**Step 4.** Final balanced reaction:
$$
\boxed{\;\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\text{CH}_4 \uparrow \;+\; 3\,\text{CO}_2 \uparrow\;}
$$

**Step 5.** Molecular weights: glucose = **180 kg/kmol**, CH₄ = **16 kg/kmol**.

**Step 6.** Theoretical methane yield per kg of glucose:
$$
\frac{3 \times 16}{180} \;=\; 0.2667 \;\text{kg CH}_4 \text{ per kg glucose}
$$

**Step 7.** Volume at NTP (22.4 m³ per kmol):
$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} \;=\; \frac{3}{180} \times 22.4 \;=\; 0.373 \;\text{m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 \text{ per kg glucose}
$$

**Step 8.** Since biogas is 60% CH₄ (typical), total biogas volume per kg glucose:
$$
V_{\text{biogas}} \;=\; \frac{0.373}{0.60} \;\approx\; 0.622 \;\text{m}^3 \text{ biogas per kg glucose}
$$

**Step 9.** Energy content (NCV of CH₄ ≈ 50 MJ/kg):
$$
E_{\text{from glucose}} \;=\; 0.2667 \times 50 \;=\; 13.33 \;\text{MJ/kg glucose}
$$

> **Reality Check:** In practice, only **30–40%** of glucose energy is recovered due to microbial maintenance, losses in digestate, and partial digestion of solids. This **conversion efficiency factor (η_chem)** is essential for real biogas plant design.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Conversion Technologies

| Parameter | Combustion | Gasification | Anaerobic Digestion | Fermentation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Input moisture limit** | <50% | <20% (need to dry) | >80% (ideal) | <5% sugars |
| **Output phase** | Heat | Producer gas | Biogas + digestate | Ethanol |
| **Energy efficiency (η)** | 60–80% | 70–85% (cold gas) | 30–40% (chem) | 90% (ferm) |
| **Temperature/condition** | 800–1200 °C | 700–900 °C, ER < 0.3 | 35–55 °C, pH 6.8–7.4 | 25–35 °C, yeast |
| **Scale suitability** | Any | >1 MW | 1 kW – 10 MW | >50 ML/yr |
| **Best for KTU** | Bagasse, husk | Coconut shell | Cattle dung | Sugarcane molasses |
| **Capital cost (₹/kW)** | 15–25 k | 35–50 k | 40–80 k | 30–60 k |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Biomass Conversion Master Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["BIOMASS FEEDSTOCK"] --> nodeB{"MOISTURE CONTENT"}
    nodeB -->|"DRY less than 20 percent"| nodeC["THERMOCHEMICAL ROUTE"]
    nodeB -->|"WET greater than 50 percent"| nodeD["BIOCHEMICAL ROUTE"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["COMBUSTION 800 to 1200 C"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["GASIFICATION 700 to 900 C"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["PYROLYSIS 300 to 800 C"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["STEAM AND HEAT"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["PRODUCER GAS CO H2 CH4"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["BIO OIL OR CHAR"]
    nodeD --> nodeK["ANAEROBIC DIGESTION"]
    nodeD --> nodeL["FERMENTATION"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["BIOGAS CH4 60 percent CO2 40 percent"]
    nodeK --> nodeN["DIGESTATE FERTILIZER"]
    nodeL --> nodeO["ETHANOL C2H5OH"]
    nodeM --> nodeP["COGENERATION ENGINE OR BOILER"]
    nodeI --> nodeP
    nodeH --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeQ["TRANSPORT FUEL BLENDING"]
```

---

## 4.2 Anaerobic Digestion — 4-Stage Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS["SUBSTRATE IN"] --> node1["STAGE 1 HYDROLYSIS"]
    node1 --> node2["STAGE 2 ACIDOGENESIS"]
    node2 --> node3["STAGE 3 ACETOGENESIS"]
    node3 --> node4["STAGE 4 METHANOGENESIS"]
    node4 --> node5["BIOGAS OUT CH4 plus CO2"]
    node4 --> node6["DIGESTATE OUT"]
    node1 -.slow.-> node1a["LIPIDS PROTEINS CARBS to fatty acids amino acids sugars"]
    node2 -.intermediate.-> node2a["VFAs ALCOHOLS H2 CO2"]
    node3 -.fast.-> node3a["ACETATE H2 CO2"]
    node4 -.very slow.-> node4a["ARCHAEA 35 to 55 C pH 6.8 to 7.4"]
```

---

## 4.3 Cogeneration Block Diagram (Biomass-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeB1["BIOMASS BUNKER"] --> nodeB2["FEED HOPPER"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3["BOILER FIRED 800 to 1200 C"]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4["STEAM HIGH PRESSURE"]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5["TURBINE"]
    nodeB5 --> nodeB6["GENERATOR ELECTRICITY"]
    nodeB5 --> nodeB7["LOW PRESSURE STEAM TO PROCESS"]
    nodeB3 -.flue gas.-> nodeB8["MULTICYCLONE DUSTER"]
    nodeB8 --> nodeB9["ID FAN STACK"]
    nodeB8 -.ash.-> nodeB10["ASH HANDLING"]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Biodiesel Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeO1["OIL SEEDS JATROPHA OR WASTE COOKING OIL"] --> nodeO2["OIL EXTRACTION MECHANICAL OR SOLVENT"]
    nodeO2 --> nodeO3["REFINING DEGUMMING NEUTRALIZATION"]
    nodeO3 --> nodeO4["TRANSESTERIFICATION WITH METHANOL NaOH CATALYST"]
    nodeO4 --> nodeO5["SEPARATOR TWO PHASES"]
    nodeO5 --> nodeO6["BIODIESEL FAME WASHED AND DRIED"]
    nodeO5 --> nodeO7["GLYCEROL BYPRODUCT"]
    nodeO6 --> nodeO8["BLENDING B5 B20 B100"]
    nodeO8 --> nodeO9["DIESEL ENGINE OR BOILER"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Notice how the moisture-content decision branch determines the *entire* downstream conversion route — a classic KTU "design-choice" question pattern.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define biomass. List any **four** categories of biomass with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Biomass is any organic matter of recent biological origin that can be used as a fuel or converted into solid, liquid, or gaseous fuels. It is renewable because the CO₂ released is re-absorbed during photosynthesis.*
Four categories:
1. *Woody biomass* — e.g., *fuelwood*.
2. *Agricultural residue* — e.g., *rice husk*.
3. *Animal waste* — e.g., *cattle dung*.
4. *Municipal solid waste* — e.g., *food scraps*.
*(½ mark each category + 1 mark definition = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** What is producer gas? Write its typical composition.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Producer gas is the gaseous product obtained by partial combustion (gasification) of solid biomass in a limited-oxygen (sub-stoichiometric) environment at 700–900 °C.*
Typical composition:

| Component | Volume % |
|---|---|
| CO | 18–22 |
| H₂ | 12–15 |
| CH₄ | 2–5 |
| CO₂ | 8–12 |
| N₂ | 45–55 |

*Calorific value:* **4.5–5.5 MJ/m³**. *(2 marks composition + 1 mark definition = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **biomass classification** in detail with suitable examples. Discuss the **first, second, third, and fourth-generation** biomass fuels.

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Classification by source (3 marks):**
- **Woody biomass** — fuelwood, sawdust, bark.
- **Agricultural residues** — bagasse, rice straw, wheat husk.
- **Energy crops** — sugarcane, jatropha, switchgrass.
- **Animal waste** — cattle dung, poultry litter.
- **Municipal solid waste** — kitchen waste, paper.
- **Aquatic biomass** — water hyacinth, algae.

**Part 2 — Classification by physical state (2 marks):**
- **Solid** — fuelwood, pellets, briquettes.
- **Liquid** — bioethanol, biodiesel, pyrolysis oil.
- **Gaseous** — biogas, producer gas.

**Part 3 — Generations (2 marks):**
1. **First generation** — derived from food crops (sugarcane ethanol, corn ethanol, palm-oil biodiesel). *Problem:* competes with food supply.
2. **Second generation** — derived from non-food lignocellulosic biomass (wheat straw, wood chips) using advanced hydrolysis/fermentation. *Advantage:* no food-vs-fuel conflict.
3. **Third generation** — derived from algae (microalgae *Chlorella*, macroalgae kelp). *Advantage:* very high yields per hectare, can grow in wastewater.
4. **Fourth generation** — genetically engineered or carbon-capture integrated biomass. *Goal:* produce bioenergy while actively capturing and storing CO₂.

---

**Q.A (b)** [7 Marks] With a neat flowchart, describe the **anaerobic digestion process** for biogas production. State the **Buswell equation** for glucose and compute the theoretical methane volume per kg of glucose.

**Model Solution:**

**Flowchart (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 diagram of this notes — Examiner expects labelled four stages)*

| Stage | Microbe | Function | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrolysis | Bacteria | Break polymers | Sugars, AA, fatty acids |
| Acidogenesis | Bacteria | Convert to VFAs | VFAs, H₂, CO₂ |
| Acetogenesis | Bacteria | Convert to acetate | CH₃COOH, H₂ |
| Methanogenesis | Archaea | Produce methane | CH₄, CO₂ |

**Buswell Equation (2 marks):**
$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 3\,\text{CH}_4 \;+\; 3\,\text{CO}_2
$$

**Theoretical methane calculation (2 marks):**

Molecular weight of glucose = **180 kg/kmol**, CH₄ = **16 kg/kmol**.

$$
\text{Mass of CH}_4 \;=\; \frac{3 \times 16}{180} \;=\; 0.2667 \;\text{kg CH}_4/\text{kg glucose}
$$

Volume at NTP:
$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} \;=\; \frac{3}{180} \times 22.4 \;=\; 0.373 \;\text{m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

Assuming 60% CH₄ in biogas:
$$
V_{\text{biogas}} \;\approx\; \frac{0.373}{0.60} \;\approx\; 0.622 \;\text{m}^3/\text{kg glucose}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q.B (a)** [7 Marks] Compare **combustion, gasification, and pyrolysis** of biomass in terms of principle, operating conditions, products, and applications.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Combustion | Gasification | Pyrolysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Principle | Complete oxidation with excess O₂ | Partial oxidation with limited O₂ (ER 0.25–0.4) | Thermal decomposition in absence of O₂ |
| Temperature | 800–1200 °C | 700–900 °C | 300–800 °C |
| Air factor (ER) | > 1 | 0.25–0.4 | 0 (inert N₂ purge) |
| Main products | Heat, CO₂, H₂O, ash | Producer gas (CO + H₂ + CH₄ + N₂) + char | Bio-oil (fast), Char (slow) |
| Energy form | Direct heat | Gaseous fuel | Liquid / solid fuel |
| Typical use | Boiler, steam generation | IC engine, gas turbine | Bio-diesel substitute, soil amendment |
| Calorific value | — | 4–6 MJ/m³ (producer gas) | 16–22 MJ/kg (bio-oil) |

*(3 marks comparison + 2 marks conditions + 2 marks applications = 7 marks)*

---

**Q.B (b)** [7 Marks] The proximate analysis of a biomass sample (rice husk) is: Moisture 9%, Volatile matter 65%, Fixed carbon 16%, Ash 10%. The ultimate analysis shows C = 38%, H = 5%, O = 35%, N = 0.6%, S = 0.1%. Calculate **(i)** GCV using Dulong's formula, and **(ii)** NCV using standard formula.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Dulong's formula (3 marks):**
$$
\text{GCV} \;=\; 337\,C \;+\; 1418\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) \;+\; 94\,S
$$

Substituting values:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} \;=&\; 337(38) \;+\; 1418\left(5 - \tfrac{35}{8}\right) \;+\; 94(0.1) \\
=&\; 12{,}806 \;+\; 1418(5 - 4.375) \;+\; 9.4 \\
=&\; 12{,}806 \;+\; 1418(0.625) \;+\; 9.4 \\
=&\; 12{,}806 \;+\; 886.25 \;+\; 9.4 \\
=&\; 13{,}701.65 \;\text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute NCV (4 marks):**
$$
\text{NCV} \;=\; \text{GCV} \;-\; \frac{9H}{100}(2260) \;-\; \frac{M}{100}(2260)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} \;=&\; 13{,}701.65 \;-\; \tfrac{9 \times 5}{100}(2260) \;-\; \tfrac{9}{100}(2260) \\
=&\; 13{,}701.65 \;-\; 0.45(2260) \;-\; 0.09(2260) \\
=&\; 13{,}701.65 \;-\; 1017 \;-\; 203.4 \\
=&\; 12{,}481.25 \;\text{kJ/kg} \\
\approx&\; 12.48 \;\text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (0 marks — but excellent for viva):**
The NCV ≈ 12.5 MJ/kg is consistent with published rice-husk values of 12–15 MJ/kg. The moisture penalty (203 kJ/kg) is small compared to the H-penalty (1017 kJ/kg), confirming that **drying rice husk below 9% is energetically less rewarding than burning it as-is** — a classic KTU audit insight.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing GCV and NCV** in boiler efficiency calculations. KTU typically awards 1 mark for stating *which one is used in which context* — write it explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the moisture term** in NCV. Always write the full formula on paper.
> 3. **Skipping the units** in numerical answers. Always box the final answer with units (kJ/kg or MJ/kg).
> 4. **Mixing up generations of biofuels**. *First = food crops*, *Second = lignocellulose*, *Third = algae*, *Fourth = engineered + carbon capture*.
> 5. **Writing "biogas = methane"** in answers. Biogas is a **mixture** (typically 55–70% CH₄). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 6. **Not drawing the flowchart** in AD/biomass questions — 2 marks reserved for the diagram alone in Q.A(b)-type questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recall Bullet Bank — read before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ Biomass = **stored solar energy** in recently-living organic matter.
- ✅ **Renewable + near-carbon-neutral** (short carbon cycle).
- ✅ Four conversion routes: **Combustion, Gasification, Pyrolysis, Anaerobic Digestion**.
- ✅ Pyrolysis temp ranges: slow = char, fast = bio-oil, flash = max oil yield.
- ✅ Gasification ER (equivalence ratio) **0.25–0.4**; Combustion ER > 1.
- ✅ Producer gas = CO + H₂ + CH₄ + N₂; CV ≈ 4.5–5.5 MJ/m³.
- ✅ Biogas = 60% CH₄ + 40% CO₂ (typical); CV ≈ 22–25 MJ/m³.
- ✅ Buswell stoichiometry for glucose → **3 CH₄ + 3 CO₂**.
- ✅ Theoretical methane from glucose ≈ **0.373 m³ CH₄ per kg** (at NTP).
- ✅ Dulong's formula: $\text{GCV} = 337C + 1418(H - O/8) + 94S$ (kJ/kg).
- ✅ NCV = GCV − 9H·22.6 − M·22.6 (per kg).
- ✅ First-gen biofuel from food crops; 4th-gen includes carbon capture.
- ✅ Biomass pellets increase bulk density from ~80 to ~650 kg/m³.
- ✅ Typical CVs: dry wood 17–20 MJ/kg, bagasse 8–10, rice husk 12–15.
- ✅ AD four stages: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- ✅ Methanogens are **Archaea**; sensitive to pH 6.8–7.4 and 35–55 °C.
- ✅ Biogas plant sizing rule: **0.35 m³ biogas per kg VS** added.
- ✅ Biodiesel = FAME, made by **transesterification** with methanol + NaOH.
- ✅ Energy Ratio (NER) for biomass must exceed **1** to be net-energy positive.
- ✅ Biomass audit parameters: **Moisture, Ash, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon**.

---

**End of Module 4 — Biomass Notes (OEPET722 — Energy Management and Audit)**
**Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned | Outcome-Based Education**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wave Tidal
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET722 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wave Tidal

# 🌊 WAVE AND TIDAL ENERGY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ocean Energy — The Sleeping Giant

The oceans of Earth cover approximately **71% of the planet's surface** and represent the largest untapped renewable energy reservoir on the planet. The theoretical global ocean energy potential exceeds **750 TWh/year**, of which wave and tidal forms constitute the most technically mature segments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Wave Energy):**
> Wave energy is the form of renewable energy harnessed from the **kinetic and potential energy of surface ocean waves**, generated primarily by wind action on the ocean surface. The energy is captured using mechanical converters (oscillating water columns, point absorbers, attenuators, or overtopping devices) that transform wave motion into usable electrical energy via hydraulic or pneumatic systems coupled to generators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Tidal Energy):**
> Tidal energy is the renewable energy extracted from the **periodic rise (flood) and fall (ebb) of sea levels** caused by the combined gravitational pull of the Moon and the Sun on Earth's oceans. It is captured using two principal methods: (i) **Tidal Stream Energy** using underwater kinetic turbines, and (ii) **Tidal Barrage Energy** using dams across estuaries with sluice gates and low-head bulb turbines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Wave Energy Analogy — "The Rowing Gym Machine":**
Imagine you are seated on a rowing machine at a gym. Each ocean wave is like a continuous series of forward-and-backward tugs on the handle. A wave energy converter (WEC) is essentially the "rowing machine" of the ocean — it grips the incoming waves and converts the rhythmic pull into rotational motion that spins a generator. The denser and more consistent the "tugs" (waves), the more electrical power is generated.

**Tidal Energy Analogy — "The Bathroom Sink on a Timer":**
Picture a bathroom sink that automatically fills up and empties twice a day (the tidal cycle). If you install a small waterwheel at the drain, every time the water flows out, the wheel spins and produces electricity. A tidal barrage is just a much larger, engineered version of this sink-and-waterwheel system built across a coastal bay. A tidal stream turbine is like a pinwheel placed in the path of that draining water.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025 \ kg/m^3}$ (significantly higher than freshwater at 1000 kg/m³, which is why ocean energy density is exceptional)
> - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$
> - **Lunar day period:** $T_{tide} = \mathbf{12 \ hours\ 25 \ minutes\ 12 \ seconds}$ (≈ 12.42 hours, semidiurnal cycle)
> - **Spring–Neap cycle period:** $\mathbf{14.77 \ days}$ (twice per lunar month)
> - **Global theoretical wave energy potential:** $\sim \mathbf{2 \ TW}$ (continuous)
> - **Average wave power on European Atlantic coast:** $\mathbf{40\text{–}70 \ kW/m}$ of wave crest
> - **Best tidal range site globally (La Rance, France):** $\mathbf{8.5 \ m}$ mean tidal range
> - **Best tidal stream site (Pentland Firth, UK):** peak current velocity $\mathbf{4\text{–}5 \ m/s}$
> - **Capacity factor of wave devices:** typically $\mathbf{25\text{–}40\%}$
> - **Capacity factor of tidal barrage:** $\mathbf{22\text{–}28\%}$ (predictable and dispatchable)

### 1.4 Classification of Ocean Energy Systems

| System Type | Source Motion | Conversion Method |
|---|---|---|
| Wave Energy Converters (WEC) | Surface wave oscillation | Mechanical → Hydraulic/Pneumatic → Electrical |
| Tidal Barrage | Tidal head difference (potential) | Low-head bulb turbine |
| Tidal Stream | Tidal current (kinetic) | Horizontal/vertical axis turbine |
| Ocean Thermal Energy (OTEC) | Temperature gradient (ΔT ≈ 20°C) | Rankine cycle with ammonia |
| Salinity Gradient | Fresh/salt water mixing | Pressure-retarded osmosis / Reverse electrodialysis |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU Module 4, the focus remains strictly on **Wave** and **Tidal** subsystems. OTEC and salinity gradient are typically covered under advanced electives.

### 1.5 Why Wave and Tidal Energy Matter for KTU

1. **Predictability:** Tidal energy is the most predictable renewable — tides can be forecast decades in advance (astronomical certainty).
2. **High Energy Density:** Seawater is ~**800 times denser than air**, so a modest tidal current of 2 m/s carries the same kinetic energy as a 60 m/s wind.
3. **Low Visual Footprint:** Most wave and tidal systems are fully submerged or below the waterline.
4. **Complementary to Solar/Wind:** Wave and tidal resources peak at different times than solar/wind, providing grid stability.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wave Energy — Linear Wave Theory (Airy Theory)

Ocean waves are modeled using **Airy Linear Wave Theory**, which assumes:
- Fluid is **inviscid** and **incompressible**
- Motion is **irrotational**
- Wave amplitude is **small** compared to wavelength and depth (valid for deep-water ocean swells)

**Key Wave Parameters:**
- $H$ = Wave height (trough-to-crest vertical distance, m)
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength (m)
- $T$ = Wave period (s)
- $A = H/2$ = Wave amplitude (m)
- $h$ = Water depth (m)
- $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ = Wave number (rad/m)
- $\omega = 2\pi/T$ = Angular frequency (rad/s)

**Dispersion Relation (the cornerstone of wave theory):**

$$\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting Cases of the Dispersion Relation:**
> - **Deep water** ($h > \lambda/2$): $\tanh(kh) \to 1$, so $\omega^2 = gk$, giving $c = \sqrt{g/k} = gT/(2\pi)$
> - **Shallow water** ($h < \lambda/20$): $\tanh(kh) \to kh$, so $\omega^2 = gk^2 h$, giving $c = \sqrt{gh}$

### 2.2 Wave Power per Unit Crest Width

The most important equation in wave energy engineering. The average power transported per meter of wave crest length is:

$$P_{wave} = \frac{\rho_{sw} \, g^2}{64\pi} \cdot H^2 \cdot T$$

In simplified numerical form (for seawater with $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s²):

$$P_{wave} \approx 0.49 \cdot H^2 \cdot T \quad \text{[kW per meter of crest]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This means **wave power scales with the SQUARE of wave height** — a wave that is twice as tall carries four times the power. This is why storms (H = 8 m) produce dramatically more wave power than calm seas (H = 1 m).

**Energy density per unit surface area:**

$$E_{wave} = \frac{\rho_{sw} \, g \, H^2}{16} \quad \text{[J/m}^2\text{]}$$

**Group velocity (energy propagation speed) in deep water:**

$$v_g = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{g}{4\omega} = \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

where $c$ is the phase velocity ($c = \lambda/T$).

### 2.3 Tidal Energy — Theoretical Foundations

#### 2.3.1 Tidal Range Power (Tidal Barrage)

A tidal barrage exploits the **head difference** $h_{tide}$ between the sea and an impounded basin. The available potential energy per tidal cycle is:

$$E_{tidal} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho_{sw} \, g \, A_{basin} \, h_{tide}^2$$

where $A_{basin}$ is the impounded basin area (m²) and $h_{tide}$ is the mean tidal range (m).

**Average tidal barrage power:**

$$P_{tidal} = \frac{\rho_{sw} \, g \, A_{basin} \, h_{tide}^2}{T_{cycle}}$$

where $T_{cycle}$ is the tidal period (typically the time between successive high tides, ≈ 12.42 hours = 44,712 s, so a full ebb-flood cycle is 24.84 hours).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial observation:** Tidal barrage power scales with the **SQUARE of tidal range** — a site with 8 m range yields 4× the power of a 4 m range site. This explains why the La Rance barrage (8.5 m range) is one of only a handful of commercially viable sites globally.

#### 2.3.2 Tidal Stream Power

For an underwater tidal current flowing through a swept area $A_{turbine}$ with velocity $v$:

$$P_{stream} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho_{sw} \, A_{turbine} \, v^3 \cdot C_p$$

where $C_p$ is the power coefficient of the turbine (Betz limit: $C_p \leq 16/27 \approx 0.593$).

**Annual energy capture:**

$$E_{annual} = P_{rated} \times 8760 \times CF$$

where $CF$ is the capacity factor.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kh)$ | Dispersion relation (general) | rad²/s² |
| 2 | $c = \lambda/T$ | Phase velocity | m/s |
| 3 | $c_{deep} = gT/(2\pi)$ | Deep-water phase velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $c_{shallow} = \sqrt{gh}$ | Shallow-water phase velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $P_{wave} = (\rho g^2 H^2 T)/(64\pi)$ | Wave power per unit crest | W/m |
| 6 | $P_{wave} \approx 0.49 H^2 T$ | Wave power (numerical) | kW/m |
| 7 | $E_{wave} = (\rho g H^2)/16$ | Wave energy per unit area | J/m² |
| 8 | $E_{tidal} = (1/2) \rho g A h^2$ | Tidal potential energy | J |
| 9 | $P_{tidal} = (\rho g A h^2)/T_{cycle}$ | Tidal barrage power | W |
| 10 | $P_{stream} = (1/2) \rho A v^3 C_p$ | Tidal stream power | W |
| 11 | $C_{p,Betz} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ | Theoretical max turbine efficiency | – |
| 12 | $E_{annual} = P_{rated} \times 8760 \times CF$ | Annual energy yield | Wh or kWh |

### 2.5 Wave Energy Conversion Technologies

**Category 1 — Oscillating Water Column (OWC):**
A partly submerged chamber with a water column that oscillates as waves enter and exit. The moving air above the water column drives a **Wells turbine** (which rotates in the same direction regardless of airflow direction). Used in the LIMPET plant on Islay, Scotland (500 kW).

**Category 2 — Point Absorber:**
